Colour the chessboard-like 8×8 grid black and white so that every horizontal or vertical neighbour has the opposite colour.  
Every edge of the grid therefore joins one black and one white square.

Let  

 d(v) = degree of square v (2 for a corner, 3 for an edge square, 4 for an interior square)  
 c(v) = (#edges on which v has the larger number) − (#edges on which v has the smaller number)

For each edge one square contributes +1 and the other −1 to the corresponding c(·), so  
∑ c(v)=0 and |c(v)|≤d(v) with c(v)≡d(v) (mod 2).

The total sum of absolute differences is  

 M = Σ_edges |a – b| = Σ_v c(v)·a(v).

To maximise M we want the largest numbers to have the largest positive coefficients and the smallest numbers the largest negative coefficients; thus we should take

 c(v)=+d(v) for one colour class, c(v)=−d(v) for the other.

Without loss of generality let every black square contain a larger number than each adjacent white square; then each black square contributes +d(v) and each white square −d(v).

Degree distribution per colour  
 Corners: 2 squares, degree 2  
 Edge (non-corner): 12 squares, degree 3  
 Interior: 18 squares, degree 4.

Assign numbers 33–64 to the 32 black squares, largest to highest degree:

 Interior (18 squares, d=4): 64,…,47  
 Edge  (12 squares, d=3): 46,…,35  
 Corners ( 2 squares, d=2): 34,33

Assign numbers 1–32 to the 32 white squares, smallest to highest degree:

 Interior (18 squares, d=4): 1,…,18  
 Edge  (12 squares, d=3): 19,…,30  
 Corners ( 2 squares, d=2): 31,32

Now compute the contribution.

Black squares  
 Sum of values 47–64 = 999 → 4·999 = 3996  
 Sum of values 35–46 = 486 → 3·486 = 1458  
 Values 33+34 = 67    → 2·67  = 134  
 Total (black) = 3996 + 1458 + 134 = 5588

White squares  
 Sum of values 1–18  = 171 → 4·171 = 684  
 Sum of values 19–30 = 294 → 3·294 = 882  
 Values 31+32 = 63    → 2·63  = 126  
 Total (white) = 684 + 882 + 126 = 1692

Therefore  

 M_max = 5588 – 1692 = 3896.

Checking: minimal black number (33) exceeds maximal white number (32), so every edge indeed has the black square larger, and the arrangement is attainable.

Finally, the required remainder is  

 3896 mod 1000 = 896.

\boxed{896}

Step 1.  Interpret M.

For every horizontal or vertical pair of adjacent cells we add  
|a – b|.  
In an 8 × 8 grid there are

56 horizontal + 56 vertical = 112 adjacent pairs.

Label the cell in row i, column j by its parity  
P(i,j) = (i + j) mod 2.  
Because adjacent cells always have opposite parity, the 64 cells form the two parts of a bipartite graph:

32 even–parity cells, 32 odd–parity cells.

Step 2.  Rewrite M with degrees.

Let d(c) be the degree (number of neighbours) of cell c in the grid:
d = 4 for interior cells (36 of them)  
d = 3 for edge–but–not–corner cells (24 of them)  
d = 2 for the four corners.

Put the 32 even cells in the set E and the 32 odd cells in the set O.  
For an edge joining an even cell e and an odd cell o its contribution is |ae – ao| = ae – ao
because we will force ae ≥ ao.  Hence

M = Σ(e∈E) d(e) · ae  −  Σ(o∈O) d(o) · ao.     (1)

The coefficient of each variable is its degree (positive for E, negative for O).

Step 3.  Choose where to place the numbers 1,…,64.

Equation (1) is linear: to maximise it we
• assign the largest numbers to the largest positive coefficients;  
• assign the smallest numbers to the largest-magnitude negative coefficients.  

Thus:

Even cells (positive weights)  
 degree 4 (18 cells): 64,63,…,47  
 degree 3 (12 cells): 46,45,…,35  
 degree 2 ( 2 cells): 34,33

Odd cells (negative weights)  
 degree 4 (18 cells):  1, 2,…,18  
 degree 3 (12 cells): 19,20,…,30  
 degree 2 ( 2 cells): 31,32

Any arrangement respecting these multisets attains the same value, and by the rearrangement inequality none can do better.

Step 4.  Compute M.

Even cells  
 Σ(deg·value) = 4·(47+…+64) + 3·(35+…+46) + 2·(33+34)

 47+…+64 = 18·(47+64)/2 = 18·55.5 = 999  
 35+…+46 = 12·(35+46)/2 = 12·40.5 = 486

 ⇒ 4·999 = 3996  
 ⇒ 3·486 = 1458  
 ⇒ 2·(33+34)=2·67 = 134  

 Total even contribution = 3996 + 1458 + 134 = 5588.

Odd cells  
 1+…+18 = 18·(1+18)/2 = 171  
 19+…+30 = 12·(19+30)/2 = 294

 4·171 = 684  
 3·294 = 882  
 2·(31+32)=2·63 = 126  

 Total odd contribution = 684 + 882 + 126 = 1692.

Therefore  

M_max = 5588 − 1692 = 3896.

Step 5.  Remainder modulo 1000.

3896 mod 1000 = 896.

\boxed{896}

Step 1.  Count the edges and the degrees  
In an 8 × 8 grid every cell is adjacent to the ones directly above, below, to the left and to the right (when they exist).  
Number of horizontal edges = 8 rows × 7 = 56  
Number of vertical   edges = 7 × 8 columns = 56  
Total edges = 112.

Colour the board like a chessboard.  Then every edge joins one black and one white square, so the grid graph is bipartite.

For each vertex let d(v) be its degree (number of adjacent squares).  
• 18 black and 18 white interior squares have degree 4  
• 12 black and 12 white edge–but–not–corner squares have degree 3  
• 2 black and 2 white corner squares have degree 2.

Step 2.  Reformulate the sum  
If a black square B contains the number b and a white neighbour W contains w with b > w, the contribution of that edge to M is b − w.  
Because we shall put every larger number on one colour and every smaller number on the other, every edge will indeed run from a larger to a smaller number and

  M = Σblack d(B)·b − Σwhite d(W)·w.  (†)

Thus maximising M amounts to
1. putting the 32 larger numbers on, say, the black squares and the 32 smaller on the white squares;
2. within each colour, giving the higher-degree squares the more extreme numbers.

Step 3.  Assign numbers in the optimal way  

Numbers 1,…,32 will go on white squares, 33,…,64 on black squares.

Black squares  
• 18 degree-4 squares get the 18 largest numbers 47,…,64  
• 12 degree-3 squares get 35,…,46  
• 2  degree-2 squares get 33,34

White squares  
• 18 degree-4 squares get 1,…,18  
• 12 degree-3 squares get 19,…,30  
• 2  degree-2 squares get 31,32

Step 4.  Evaluate M with formula (†)

Black side  
Sum of the 18 numbers 47–64: S₁ = 18(47+64)/2 = 999 → contribution 4S₁ = 3996  
Sum of the 12 numbers 35–46: S₂ = 12(35+46)/2 = 486 → contribution 3S₂ = 1458  
Sum of 33 and 34:      S₃ = 67        → contribution 2S₃ = 134  
Total black contribution = 3996 + 1458 + 134 = 5588.

White side  
Sum of 1–18:     T₁ = 18(1+18)/2 = 171 → contribution 4T₁ = 684  
Sum of 19–30:     T₂ = 12(19+30)/2 = 294 → contribution 3T₂ = 882  
Sum of 31 and 32:   T₃ = 63       → contribution 2T₃ = 126  
Total white contribution = 684 + 882 + 126 = 1692.

Therefore  
M_max = 5588 − 1692 = 3896.

Step 5.  Remainder modulo 1000  
3896 ≡ 896 (mod 1000).

Hence the desired remainder is  
\boxed{896}.

Step 1.  The grid is bipartite  
Color the board like a chessboard.  
Every edge joins a black square and a white square.

Step 2.  Replace every number by as many “copies’’ as its degree  
A corner has degree 2, an edge (non-corner) degree 3, an interior square degree 4.  
Thus there are  

• 4 squares of degree 2  
• 24 squares of degree 3  
• 36 squares of degree 4  

so 4·2 + 24·3 + 36·4 = 224 copies in all (two copies are consumed by every edge, so 224 = 2·112).

Step 3.  Turning M into a pairing problem  
Give every edge the two copies that correspond to its two end-points; the edge contributes the absolute difference of the two numbers on those copies.  
Hence the 224 copies are split into 112 pairs, one pair per edge, and  

M = sum over the 112 pairs of |large − small|.

Step 4.  A universal upper bound  
Arrange the 224 copies in non-decreasing order  
b1 ≤ b2 ≤ … ≤ b224.  
By the rearrangement inequality,

|b1−b224| + |b2−b223| + … + |b112−b113| ≥ M

so

M ≤ (b224 + … + b113) − (b1 + … + b112). (★)

Thus the maximum possible M is exactly the difference between
the sum of the largest 112 copies and the sum of the smallest 112 copies.

Step 5.  How to maximise the two half-sums  
To maximise (★) we want as many very large copies as possible among the largest 112
and as many very small copies among the smallest 112.  
Therefore give degree 4 to the 18 largest and 18 smallest integers,
degree 2 to the 4 middle integers,
and degree 3 to the remaining 24 integers:

• degree 4: 1–18 and 47–64 (72 + 72 = 144 copies)  
• degree 3: 19–30 and 35–46 (36 + 36 = 72 copies)  
• degree 2: 31, 32, 33, 34 (8 copies)

Step 6.  The two required half-sums  
Smallest 112 copies  
= 4(1+…+18) + 3(19+…+30) + 2·31 + 2·32  
= 4·171 + 3·294 + 2·31 + 2·32  
= 684 + 882 + 62 + 64  
= 1692.

Largest 112 copies  
= 4(47+…+64) + 3(35+…+46) + 2·33 + 2·34  
= 4·999 + 3·486 + 66 + 68  
= 3996 + 1458 + 134  
= 5588.

Step 7.  The absolute maximum  
M_max = 5588 − 1692 = 3896.

Step 8.  Achieving the bound  
Place the 32 small numbers 1–32 on the black squares
and the 32 large numbers 33–64 on the white squares, with the
larger numbers (and also the very small ones) situated in interior squares,
the four middle numbers 31–34 at the corners, etc., exactly matching the degree allocation
used above.  
Because every white number exceeds every adjacent black number, every edge contributes
“large – small”, and the value 3896 is indeed attained, so it is the true maximum.

Step 9.  Required remainder  
3896 mod 1000 = 896.

\boxed{896}

Label the 64 squares of the \(8\times 8\) board black and white as on a chessboard; every horizontal or vertical edge joins one black and one white square.  
Let  

• \(W\) be the set of white squares, \(B\) the set of black squares,  
• \(d(v)\in\{2,3,4\}\) the degree (number of neighbours) of a square \(v\).

For any filling of the board with the numbers \(1,2,\dots ,64\) the quantity to be maximised is  

\[
M=\sum_{\text{edges }(w,b)}|a(w)-a(b)| .
\]

1.  Separating large and small numbers.  
   Replace every entry \(k\) by \(65-k\); this does not change any absolute difference, hence does not change \(M\).  
   Therefore there exists a maximal arrangement in which every white number exceeds every black number.  
   In that situation each edge contributes \(a(w)-a(b)\) (no absolute value is necessary) and

   \[
   M=\sum_{w\in W}d(w)\,a(w)-\sum_{b\in B}d(b)\,a(b).
   \tag{1}
   \]

   The coefficients \(d(w)\) are positive, the coefficients \(-d(b)\) negative.

2.  Using rearrangement.  
   Write the 64 coefficients occurring in (1):

   • \(+4\): 18 times (white interior squares)  
   • \(+3\): 12 times (white edge squares)  
   • \(+2\):  2 times (white corner squares)  
   • \(-2\):  2 times (black corner squares)  
   • \(-3\): 12 times (black edge squares)  
   • \(-4\): 18 times (black interior squares).

   To maximise the sum (1) we pair the largest numbers with the largest positive
   coefficients and the smallest numbers with the largest negative coefficients
   (rearrangement inequality).

   Thus put  

   • \(64,63,\dots ,47\) (18 numbers) in the white interior squares,  
   • \(46,45,\dots ,35\) (12 numbers) in the white edge squares,  
   • \(34,33\) in the white corner squares,  

   • \(1,2,\dots ,18\) in the black interior squares,  
   • \(19,20,\dots ,30\) in the black edge squares,  
   • \(31,32\) in the black corner squares.

3.  Computing \(M\).

   White contribution  
   \(4(64+\cdots +47)=4\cdot 999=3996\)  
   \(3(46+\cdots +35)=3\cdot 486=1458\)  
   \(2(34+33)=2\cdot 67=134\)

   Black contribution  
   \(4(1+\cdots +18)=4\cdot 171=684\)  
   \(3(19+\cdots +30)=3\cdot 294=882\)  
   \(2(31+32)=2\cdot 63=126\)

   Hence  
   \(M= (3996+1458+134)-(684+882+126)=5588-1692=3896\).

4.  Maximality.  
   Steps 1 and 2 show that any arrangement yielding a larger value than 3896 would contradict the rearrangement inequality, hence 3896 is the maximum.

Finally

\[
M_{\max}=3896\quad\Longrightarrow\quad M_{\max}\pmod{1000}=896.
\]

\boxed{896}

Step 1.  Place the triangle in the plane  
P : z = 0 with
A = (0,0,0), B = (6,0,0), C = (0,4,0).

Step 2.  Find D, the reflection of the centroid G across line BC.
Centroid G = (2, 4⁄3, 0).  
Line BC has direction v = (–6, 4).  
Foot of perpendicular from G to BC:

BG = (–4, 4⁄3)  
BG·v = 24 + 16⁄3 = 88⁄3, |v|² = 52  
Projection factor = (88/3)/52 = 22/39

H = B + (22/39)v = (34/13, 88/39, 0)

D = 2H – G = (42/13, 124/39, 0).

Step 3.  Centres of the four tangent spheres (all above P):

O₁ = (0,0,1) radius 1  
O₂ = (6,0,2) radius 2  
O₃ = (0,4,3) radius 3  
O₄ = (42/13, 124/39, r) radius r

Step 4.  Let the second plane be  
T : ax + by + cz + d = 0  
with the normal normalised so that a²+b²+c² = 1.  
The signed distance from each centre to T equals its radius, giving

a·0 + b·0 + c·1 + d = 1  
a·6 + b·0 + c·2 + d = 2  
a·0 + b·4 + c·3 + d = 3  
a·(42/13) + b·(124/39) + c·r + d = r.

Step 5.  Solve the first three equations.

d = 1 – c  
6a + c = 1  ⇒  a = (1–c)/6  
2b + c = 1  ⇒  b = (1–c)/2.

Step 6.  Substitute a, b, d in the fourth equation:

(42/13)(1–c)/6 + (124/39)(1–c)/2 + (1–c) + cr = r  
(1 – c)(7/13 + 62/39 + 1) + cr = r  
(1 – c)(122/39) + cr = r  
r(1 – c) = (1 – c)(122/39).

Since 1 – c ≠ 0 (otherwise T would coincide with P), we have

r = 122/39.

Step 7.  Determine the value of c from the normalising condition.

a = (1–c)/6, b = (1–c)/2.

(1–c)²(1/36 + 1/4) + c² = 1  
(1–c)²·5/18 + c² = 1  
23c² – 10c – 13 = 0  
c = 1  (discarded)  or  c = –13/23.

Thus a = 6/23, b = 18/23, c = –13/23, d = 36/23 gives the required plane, and r = 122/39 indeed satisfies the tangency condition.

Step 8.  r = 122/39 with relatively prime integers 122 and 39; hence

m + n = 122 + 39 = 161.

\boxed{161}

Step 1.  Set up coordinates  
Put plane 𝓟 in the xy–plane (z = 0).  Choose  
A = (0,0,0), B = (6,0,0), C = (0,4,0).  
The centroid of ΔABC is  
G = ( (0+6+0)/3 , (0+0+4)/3 , 0 ) = (2, 4/3 , 0).

Step 2.  Find D, the reflection of G across line BC  
Line BC has equation 2x + 3y – 12 = 0.  
Using the reflection formula across ax + by + c = 0,

 a = 2, b = 3, c = –12, a² + b² = 13, 
 ax₀ + by₀ + c = 2(2) + 3(4/3) – 12 = –4.

Then

x_D = 2 − 2a(ax₀+by₀+c)/(a²+b²) = 2 + 16/13 = 42/13  
y_D = 4/3 − 2b(ax₀+by₀+c)/(a²+b²) = 4/3 + 24/13 = 124/39  

so D = (42/13, 124/39, 0).

Step 3.  Centers of the first three spheres  
They lie a distance equal to their radii above plane 𝓟:

O₁ = (0,0,1),  O₂ = (6,0,2),  O₃ = (0,4,3).

Step 4.  Equation of plane 𝓣  
Write 𝓣 as z = ux + vy + w.  Let S = √(1+u²+v²) (the length of its normal).

For a sphere with centre (x_i, y_i, r_i) to be tangent to 𝓣 and on the same side as the others we need  

|r_i − ux_i − vy_i − w| / S = r_i.

Choosing the consistent (negative) sign of the numerator gives

ux_i + vy_i + w = r_i(1 + S)  (⋆)

Applying (⋆) to the first three spheres (radii 1,2,3) yields

1) w = 1 + S  
2) 6u + w = 2(1 + S) ⇒ u = (1 + S)/6  
3) 4v + w = 3(1 + S) ⇒ v = (1 + S)/2.

Now compute S:

S² = 1 + u² + v²  
      = 1 + (1+S)²/36 + (1+S)²/4  
      = 1 + (1+S)²·(10/36)  
      = 1 + (1+S)²·(5/18).

Let t = S.  Then

t² = 1 + (1+t)²·5/18  
⇒ (after simplification) 13(1+t)² − 36(1+t) = 0  
⇒ 1+t = 36/13  (because 1+t ≠ 0)  
⇒ S = t = 23/13.

Consequently

u = (1+S)/6 = 6/13, v = (1+S)/2 = 18/13, w = 1+S = 36/13,

so plane 𝓣 is  z = (6/13)x + (18/13)y + 36/13.

Step 5.  The fourth sphere  
Its centre is O₄ = (42/13, 124/39, r).  
Apply (⋆):

u x_D + v y_D + w = r(1 + S).

Compute the left side:

u x_D = (6/13)(42/13) = 252/169  
v y_D = (18/13)(124/39) = 744/169  
w       = 36/13          = 468/169  

Sum: 252/169 + 744/169 + 468/169 = 1464/169.

Also 1 + S = 1 + 23/13 = 36/13.

Hence

r(36/13) = 1464/169  
⇒ r = 1464/169 · 13/36  
⇒ r = 1464 / 468  
⇒ r = 122/39 (in lowest terms).

Step 6.  Answer  
r = 122/39, so m + n = 122 + 39 = 161.

\boxed{161}

Place plane 𝒫 as the xy–plane (z = 0) and orient the right triangle so that  
A = (0,0,0), B = (6,0,0), C = (0,4,0).

1.  Find the reflection point D.

   • Centroid G = (2, 4/3).  
   • Line BC has direction (−6,4) and unit normal n̂ = (2/√13, 3/√13).  
   • Reflection: D = G + 8/√13 · n̂  
     ⟹ D = (42/13, 124/39, 0).

2.  Coordinates of the sphere centres (all above 𝒫):

   A′ = (0,0,1), B′ = (6,0,2), C′ = (0,4,3), D′ = (42/13, 124/39, r).

3.  Let the second tangent plane 𝒯 have unit normal (α,β,γ) and satisfy  
   αx + βy + γz = δ.

   Tangency to the four spheres gives the four linear equations  
   γ − δ = 1  
   6α + 2γ − δ = 2  
   4β + 3γ − δ = 3  
   αx_D + βy_D + γr − δ = r.

   From the first three,
      6α + γ = 1, 2β + γ = 1, δ = γ − 1  
      ⇒ α = (1−γ)/6, β = (1−γ)/2.

4.  Substitute into the equation for D:

      r(γ − 1) + αx_D + βy_D − γ + 1 = 0  
      αx_D + βy_D = 7(1−γ)/13 + 62(1−γ)/39 = 83(1−γ)/39.

      Thus r(γ − 1) = −122(1−γ)/39  
      ⇒ r = 122/39 (since γ ≠ 1).

5.  Determine γ by imposing ‖(α,β,γ)‖ = 1:

      (1−γ)^2(1/36 + 1/4) + γ² = 1  
      (1−γ)²·5/18 + γ² = 1  
      23γ² − 10γ − 13 = 0  
      γ = 1 or γ = −13/23.

      γ = 1 makes plane 𝒯 coincide with 𝒫, impossible, so γ = −13/23.  
      With this value, α = 6/23, β = 18/23, δ = −36/23, and plane 𝒯 is
      6x + 18y − 13z + 36 = 0.

      Plugging D′ confirms its distance to 𝒯 equals r = 122/39, so the configuration is valid.

Hence the unknown radius is

      r = 122/39 = m/n  with m = 122, n = 39.

m + n = 122 + 39 = 161.

Place plane 𝒫 as the xy-plane (z = 0) and put  
A = (0,0,0) , B = (6,0,0) , C = (0,4,0).

Then AB = 6, AC = 4 and ∠BAC = 90°, as required.

1.  Coordinates of the centroid  
 G = ((0+6+0)/3 , (0+0+4)/3 , 0) = (2 , 4/3 , 0).

2.  Reflect G across BC to get D.  
 Line BC: 2x + 3y – 12 = 0.  
 Projection H of G on BC is the solution of  

  y – 4/3 = (3/2)(x – 2) and 2x + 3y – 12 = 0,  

 giving H = (34/13 , 88/39 , 0).  
 Since H is the midpoint of GD,  

 D = 2H – G = (42/13 , 124/39 , 0).

3.  Centres of the four tangent spheres (they sit above 𝒫):  
 A₁ = (0,0,1) radius 1  
 B₁ = (6,0,2) radius 2  
 C₁ = (0,4,3) radius 3  
 D₁ = (42/13 , 124/39 , r) radius r

4.  Let the second plane 𝒯 have unit normal n̂ = (nₓ,n_y,n_z) and satisfy  
 n̂·P = h for points on 𝒯.  
 For each sphere centre Pᵢ with radius Rᵢ,

  n̂·Pᵢ = h + Rᵢ  (i = 1,2,3,4)  (∗)

   Using A₁, B₁, C₁ (R = 1,2,3):

 n_z = h + 1     (1)  
 6nₓ + 2n_z = h + 2  (2)  
 4n_y + 3n_z = h + 3  (3)

   Eliminating h gives  

 6nₓ + n_z = 1    (4)  
 2n_y + n_z = 1    (5)

   From (4) and (5): 3nₓ = n_y.  
   Impose |n̂| = 1:

 nₓ² + n_y² + n_z² = 1  
 ⇒  nₓ² + (3nₓ)² + (1 – 6nₓ)² = 1  
 ⇒  –12nₓ + 46nₓ² = 0  
 ⇒  nₓ = 0 or nₓ = 6/23.

   nₓ = 0 would make 𝒯 coincide with 𝒫, so take nₓ = 6/23.

 Then n_y = 3nₓ = 18/23, n_z = 1 – 6nₓ = –13/23,  
 and h = n_z – 1 = –36/23.

5.  Apply (∗) to D₁ to determine r:

 n̂·D₁ = h + r  
 ⇒ (6/23)(42/13) + (18/23)(124/39) + (–13/23)r = –36/23 + r

 First two terms: 252/299 + 744/299 = 996/299  
 Add h: 996/299 + 36/23 = 1464/299.

 Thus 1464/299 – (36/23)r = 0  
 ⇒ r = (1464/299)·(23/36) = 33672/10764 = 122/39.

Therefore r = 122/39 = 3 122⁄39, so m + n = 122 + 39 = 161.

Place plane 𝒫 as the coordinate plane z = 0 and put  
A = (0,0,0), B = (6,0,0), C = (0,4,0).

        O₃ = (0,4,3) (radius 3)

    Tangency to 𝒯 yields

Step 1.  Set up a coordinate system

A = (0,0,0) , B = (6,0,0) , C = (0,4,0)

Centers of the first three spheres (tangent to 𝒫 at A, B, C and lying on the positive-z side):

O₂ = (6,0,2) (radius 2)  
O₃ = (0,4,3) (radius 3)

Vector BC = (–6,4).  
w = G – B = (–4,4/3).  
t = (w·BC)/(‖BC‖²) = (88/3)/52 = 22/39.

Projection P of G on BC:
P = B + t·BC = (34/13 , 88/39).

Therefore D = 2P – G = (42/13 , 124/39 , 0).

Let the (unit) normal of 𝒯 be n = (α,β,γ) and its equation be  
αx + βy + γz + δ = 0, with α²+β²+γ² = 1.

Because each sphere is tangent to 𝒯, the signed distance from every center Oᵢ to 𝒯 equals its radius rᵢ:

αx_i + βy_i + γz_i + δ = rᵢ  (i = 1,2,3,4).

Using O₁, O₂, O₃ we get

(1) γ + δ = 1  
(2) 6α + 2γ + δ = 2  
(3) 4β + 3γ + δ = 3.

From (1): δ = 1 – γ.  
Then (2) ⇒ 6α + γ = 1, so α = (1 – γ)/6.  
(3) ⇒ 4β + 2γ = 2 ⇒ β = (1 – γ)/2.

With α,β expressed through γ, apply the unit–length condition:

α² + β² + γ² = 1  
(1 – γ)²(1/36 + 1/4) + γ² = 1  
(5/18)(1 – 2γ + γ²) + γ² = 1

18×: 23γ² – 10γ – 13 = 0  
γ = 1 or γ = –13/23.  
γ = 1 would make α = β = 0 and cannot satisfy the different radii, so

γ = –13/23, α = 6/23, β = 18/23, δ = 36/23.

Hence n = (6/23, 18/23, –13/23).

Step 4.  Impose tangency for the fourth sphere  

Let its radius be r and its center O₄ = (x_D, y_D, r) with (x_D, y_D) = (42/13, 124/39).

Plugging into αx + βy + γz + δ = r:

αx_D + βy_D + γr + δ = r.

Compute αx_D + βy_D:

αx_D = (6/23)(42/13) = 252/299  
βy_D = (18/23)(124/39) = 744/299

Substituting and using γ, δ:

996/299 + (–13/23)r + 36/23 = r.

Put everything over 299 (since 23·13 = 299):

996/299 – 169r/299 + 468/299 = r  
⇒ 1464 – 169r = 299r  
⇒ 468r = 1464  
⇒ r = 1464/468 = 122/39.

Step 5.  Answer

The required radius is r = 122/39, already in lowest terms, so m + n = 122 + 39 = 161.

Step 1.  Express S₍ᵣ₎ modulo 503 with Lucas’ theorem  

Write 10000 in base 503:
10000 = 19·503 + 443.

For any k = 502m + r (0 ≤ k ≤ 10000, 0 ≤ r ≤ 501, m ≥ 0)  
let k = a₁·503 + a₀ with 0 ≤ a₀ ≤ 502.  
Since 502 ≡ –1 (mod 503),

k = 502m + r = 503m – m + r = 503m + (r – m).

Thus  
a₀ = r – m   (because 0 ≤ m ≤ 19, r – m ≥ 0),  
a₁ = m.

Lucas’ theorem gives, modulo 503,

C(10000, k) ≡ C(19, a₁)·C(443, a₀).

Hence

S_r = Σ_{m=0}^{min(r,19)} C(19, m)·C(443, r–m)  (mod 503). (1)

Step 2.  Recognise a binomial convolution  

The right‐hand side of (1) is the coefficient of x^r in  
(1+x)^{19}·(1+x)^{443} = (1+x)^{462}.

Therefore, for 0 ≤ r ≤ 501,

S_r ≡ C(462, r) (mod 503). (2)

Step 3.  Locate the zeros of C(462, r) modulo 503  

Because 462 < 503, none of the factorials 462!, r!, (462 – r)! contains the prime 503.  
Thus, for 0 ≤ r ≤ 462, the binomial coefficient C(462, r) is not divisible by 503.

For r > 462, C(462, r) = 0 outright.  
Those indices are r = 463, 464, …, 501 – a total of

501 – 462 = 39 values.

Step 4.  Conclusion  

By (2), S_r is a multiple of 503 exactly for those 39 indices r > 462.

Let p = 503.
The multiplicative group Fₚ* is cyclic of order p−1 = 502, so it possesses a primitive 502-nd root of unity g.  
Put ω = g and write

(1 + x)^{10000}= Σ_{k=0}^{10000} {10000 \choose k} x^{k} .

1.  Root–of–unity filter  
For 0 ≤ r ≤ 501,

S_r = Σ_{m≥0}{10000 \choose 502m+r}
    ≡ (1/502) Σ_{j=0}^{501}(1+ω^{j})^{10000} ω^{-jr}     (mod 503)

because the inner sum of coefficients having exponents ≡ r (mod 502) is the discrete Fourier transform of the sequence (1+ω^{j})^{10000}.
Since 502≡−1 (mod 503), 1/502 ≡ −1 (mod 503); define

B_r = Σ_{j=0}^{501}(1+ω^{j})^{10000} ω^{-jr}  so  S_r ≡ −B_r (mod 503).

Hence S_r is divisible by 503 iff B_r ≡ 0 (mod 503).

2.  Simplifying (1+ω^{j})^{10000}  
Because ω^{251}=−1 and, for all non-zero α∈Fₚ, α^{502}=1,

(1+ω^{j})^{10000}= (1+ω^{j})^{502·19+462}= (1+ω^{j})^{462}.

If j=251, 1+ω^{j}=0, so that term is 0.
Thus, with T_j = (1+ω^{j})^{462},

T_j = 0  (j=251) ,
T_j = (1+ω^{j})^{462}= (1+ω^{j})^{−40}  (j≠251) , because 462≡−40 (mod 502).

Therefore

B_r = Σ_{j=0}^{501}T_j ω^{-jr}
    = Σ_{j≠251}(1+ω^{j})^{−40} ω^{-jr}.

3.  Converting the sum to a field sum  
Let X = ω^{j}.  As j runs through 0,…,501 (j≠251) the non-zero field elements X∈Fₚ* with X≠−1 are taken once each.  Set Y = 1+X; then Y ranges over all non-zero elements of Fₚ (because X=−1, giving Y=0, is omitted).

B_r = Σ_{Y∈Fₚ*} Y^{−40}(Y−1)^{−r}
    = Σ_{Y∈Fₚ*} Y^{502−40}(Y−1)^{502−r}
    = Σ_{Y∈Fₚ*} Y^{462}(Y−1)^{502−r} .

4.  Expanding (Y−1)^{502−r} and using Σ_{Y∈Fₚ*}Y^t  
Write n = 502−r (0≤r≤501).  Expand:

(Y−1)^{n} = Σ_{k=0}^{n} (−1)^k {n \choose k} Y^{n−k}.

Thus

B_r = Σ_{k=0}^{n} (−1)^k {n \choose k} Σ_{Y∈Fₚ*} Y^{462+n−k}.

Because Σ_{Y∈Fₚ*}Y^t = 0 unless t ≡ 0 (mod 502), in which case the sum equals 502 (=−1 mod 503).  
So a term contributes only when

462 + n − k ≡ 0 (mod 502)  ⇔ k = 462 − r.

Such k satisfies 0 ≤ k ≤ n exactly when r ≤ 462.  Hence

If r > 462: B_r = 0.

If 0 ≤ r ≤ 462:
 n = 502−r, k = 462−r = n−40,
 B_r = 502 (−1)^k {n \choose k}
       = (−1) (−1)^k {502−r \choose 40}
       = (−1)^{463−r} {502−r \choose 40}  (mod 503).

5.  When is B_r ≡ 0 ?  
For 0 ≤ r ≤ 462 we must check whether {502−r \choose 40} is 0 (mod 503).  
But 0 ≤ 40 ≤ 502 − r < 503, so both arguments of the binomial coefficient are less than p.  By Lucas’s/Kummer’s theorem no carry occurs when adding 40 and (502−r−40); hence 503 does not divide the binomial coefficient.  Therefore B_r ≡ 0 does not occur in this range.

Consequently

• For r = 463,464,…,501 (39 values) B_r ≡ 0;  
• For r = 0,1,…,462        B_r ≠ 0.

Because S_r ≡ −B_r (mod 503), the same set of r’s gives S_r ≡ 0 (mod 503).

Hence exactly 39 of the numbers S_0, S_1, … , S_{501} are multiples of 503.

 (462 choose r) ≠ 0 (mod 503),

Exactly 39 of the 502 sums are divisible by 503.

P(x)\equiv R(x)=\sum_{r=0}^{501}S_rx^{r}\pmod{x^{502}-1},

so R(x) is the unique polynomial of degree ≤501 whose coefficient of x^{r} equals S_{r}.

Hence we only have to study R(x) modulo the prime 503.

Step 2.  Evaluate R(x) modulo 503.

For every non-zero a∈𝔽_{503} we have a^{502}=1, so

(1+a)^{10000}=(1+a)^{10000\bmod 502}          in 𝔽_{503}.

(1+a)^{10000}=(1+a)^{462}\qquad(\forall\,a∈𝔽_{503}^{\times}).

Thus the polynomial

H(x)=(1+x)^{10000}-(1+x)^{462}

vanishes at each of the 502 non–zero elements of 𝔽_{503}.

(1+x)^{10000}\equiv(1+x)^{462}\pmod{503,\;x^{502}-1}.

But (1+x)^{462} already has degree 462≤501, so it is exactly the remainder:

R(x)≡(1+x)^{462}\pmod{503}.

Let the (directed) side–vectors of the pentagon be  

v_k = s_k u_k, k = 0,1,2,3,4,

where  

• s_k = |v_k| is the length of the k-th side,  
• u_k = e^{ikθ} is a unit vector,  
• θ = 72° = 2π/5 (because consecutive sides turn through the exterior angle).

Thus  

|u_k| = 1, u_{k+1}·u_k = cos θ = c, with c = cos 72° = (√5 – 1)/4.

1.  Given data for the sides  
   Σ s_k² = 308.  (1)

2.  Each diagonal is v_k + v_{k+1}; hence  

   |v_k + v_{k+1}|² = s_k² + s_{k+1}² + 2c s_k s_{k+1}.  

   Summing over k = 0…4,

   Σ |v_k + v_{k+1}|² = 2Σ s_k² + 2c Σ s_k s_{k+1} = 800.  

   Using (1):

   2·308 + 2c Σ s_k s_{k+1} = 800  
   ⟹ Σ s_k s_{k+1} = 92/c = 92 / ( (√5 – 1)/4 ) = 92(√5 + 1).  (2)

   (A_0, A_1 will denote (1) and (2) below.)

3.  Closure of the pentagon: Σ v_k = 0.  
   In complex form: Σ s_k e^{ikθ} = 0 ⇒

   A_0 + 2cos θ·A_1 + 2cos 2θ·A_2 = 0, where  
   A_0 = Σ s_k² = 308,  
   A_1 = Σ s_k s_{k+1} = 92(√5 + 1),  
   A_2 = Σ s_k s_{k+2}  (unknown).

   Since cos 2θ = cos 144° = –(√5 + 1)/4,

   308 + 2·( (√5 – 1)/4 )·92(√5 + 1) + 2·( –(√5 + 1)/4 )·A_2 = 0.

   The middle term simplifies to 184, so

   308 + 184 – ( (√5 + 1)/2 ) A_2 = 0  
   ⟹ A_2 = 984 / (√5 + 1) = 246(√5 – 1).  (3)

4.  Perimeter P.  
   P = Σ s_k ⇒ P² = Σ s_k² + 2Σ_{i<j} s_i s_j.  
   The unordered cross-sum equals A_1 + A_2, so

   P² = A_0 + 2A_1 + 2A_2
       = 308 + 2·92(√5 + 1) + 2·246(√5 – 1)
       = 308 + 184(√5 + 1) + 492(√5 – 1)
       = 676√5.

Thus the square of the perimeter is 676√5 = m√n with m = 676 and n = 5 (square-free).

Therefore, m + n = 676 + 5 = 681.

\boxed{681}

Let the sides of the equi-angular pentagon, taken consecutively, be  

s₀ , s₁ , s₂ , s₃ , s₄ .

Write the side–vectors in the complex plane:

v₀ = s₀ ,
v₁ = s₁ ζ,
v₂ = s₂ ζ²,
v₃ = s₃ ζ³,
v₄ = s₄ ζ⁴,

where ζ = e^{2πi/5}; then |ζ| = 1 and the argument of ζ is the exterior angle 72°.

1.  Closure condition  
   Σ v_i = 0  ⇒  Σ s_i ζ^{i} = 0. (1)

2.  Sums involving the side lengths  
   A  = Σ s_i² = 308 (given)  
   B  = Σ s_i s_{i+1}.  
   C  = Σ s_i s_{i+2}.  

3.  Squares of the five diagonals  
   The diagonal from vertex i to i+2 is the vector
   d_i = v_i + v_{i+1}.  
   Hence  
   |d_i|² = s_i² + s_{i+1}² + 2s_i s_{i+1} cos 72°.  

   Summing over the five diagonals,

   Σ|d_i|² = 2A + 2 cos72° B = 800 (given).

   cos72° = (√5 – 1)/4, so

        800 = 2·308 + 2·(√5–1)/4 · B
     ⇒   184 = (√5–1)/2 · B
     ⇒   B  = 92(√5 + 1).                     (2)

4.  Using the closure condition  
   Take the modulus squared of (1):

        0 = |Σ s_i ζ^{i}|²
          = A + 2 Σ_{i<j} s_i s_j cos[72°(j–i)].

   For pairs with separation 1 we have cos 72° = c₁ = (√5–1)/4;  
   for separation 2 we have cos 144° = c₂ = –(√5+1)/4.

   Therefore

        0 = A + 2(c₁ B + c₂ C).

   Substitute A = 308 and B from (2):

        0 = 308 + 2·(√5–1)/4 · 92(√5+1) + 2·c₂ C
          = 308 + 184 + 2c₂ C
          = 492 + 2c₂ C.

     With c₂ = –(√5+1)/4 we get

        C = –492 / (2c₂)
          = 984 / (√5 + 1)
          = 246(√5 – 1).                      (3)

5.  The perimeter  
   P = Σ s_i ; so P² = A + 2 Σ_{i<j} s_i s_j
                     = A + 2(B + C).

   Using A = 308, (2) and (3):

        B + C = 92(√5+1) + 246(√5–1)
               = 338√5 – 154.

        P² = 308 + 2(338√5 – 154)
            = 308 + 676√5 – 308
            = 676√5.

Thus

   P² = 676 √5 = m √n with  m = 676 , n = 5  (n square-free).

Required sum:

   m + n = 676 + 5 = 681.

\boxed{681}

Let the consecutive side–vectors of the pentagon be  
v₁ , v₂ , … , v₅  in the complex plane, with directions differing by the constant exterior angle 72°.  
Hence

vₖ = sₖ e^{i(k-1)·72°}, k = 1,2,…,5,

where sₖ = |vₖ| is the k-th side length.

Important facts.

1.  Sum of squared sides  
 Σ sₖ² = 308.                       (given)

2.  Each diagonal joins two vertices two edges apart, so its vector is vₖ + v_{k+1}.  
 Therefore

 Σ(diagonal)² = Σ |vₖ + v_{k+1}|²  (k mod 5) = 800.      (given)

3.  Because the polygon is equiangular, the angle between consecutive sides is 72°, so  
 Re(vₖ conj v_{k+1}) = sₖ s_{k+1} cos 72° with  
 cos 72° = (√5 – 1)/4 ≡ c.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
A.  Using the diagonal sum

|vₖ + v_{k+1}|² = sₖ² + s_{k+1}² + 2c sₖ s_{k+1}

Sum over k = 1…5:

800 = 2 Σ sₖ² + 2c Σ sₖ s_{k+1}
      = 2·308 + 2c A, where A = Σ sₖ s_{k+1}

 ⇒ A = 92/c = 92(√5 + 1).                     (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.  The closing (Σ vₖ = 0) condition

0 = |Σ vₖ|² = Σ sₖ² + 2 Σ_{i<j} s_i s_j cos(72°(j–i))

Among the 10 unordered pairs,
• 5 are adjacent (difference 1 or 4): coefficient cos 72° =  c.  
• 5 are two apart (difference 2 or 3): coefficient cos 144° = –cos 36° = –(√5+1)/4 ≡ –d.

Let
A = Σ sₖ s_{k+1} (as above)  
B = Σ sₖ s_{k+2} (indices mod 5)

Then

0 = Σ sₖ² + 2(cA – dB)
  = 308 + 2cA – 2dB.

Because cA = (√5–1)/4 · 92(√5+1) = 46·4 = 92  
 → 2cA = 184.

Thus 0 = 308 + 184 – 2dB ⇒ 2dB = 492

 ⇒ B = 246/d = 246(√5 – 1).                  (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.  The perimeter

P = Σ sₖ, so P² = Σ sₖ² + 2Σ_{i<j} s_i s_j = Σ sₖ² + 2(A + B).

Compute:

A + B = 92(√5 + 1) + 246(√5 – 1)
       = 338√5 – 154.

Hence

P² = 308 + 2(338√5 – 154)
    = 308 + 676√5 – 308
    = 676√5.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
D.  Final form

P² = 676√5 = m√n with m = 676, n = 5 (n is square-free).

Therefore m + n = 676 + 5 = 681.

\boxed{681}

Let the sides of the equiangular pentagon be  
s₀, s₁, s₂, s₃, s₄   (indices taken mod 5).

1.  Geometry in the complex plane  
   Place the pentagon so that the unit vectors in the directions of the five sides are  
   uₖ = e^{i(θ₀+72°k)},  k = 0,…,4.  
   Then the side vectors are sₖuₖ and the pentagon closes:  

        ∑_{k=0}^{4} sₖuₖ = 0.                (1)

2.  Sums involving the sides  
   a)  Given data  

        ∑ sₖ² = 308.                             (2)

   b)  The five diagonals are the vectors  
        dₖ = sₖuₖ + s_{k+1}u_{k+1}.  
       Their squared lengths are  

        |dₖ|² = sₖ² + s_{k+1}² + 2sₖs_{k+1}cos72°.  

       With cos 72° = (√5 − 1)/4 ≡ c, the given sum of the squares of the diagonals is  

        800 = ∑|dₖ|²  
            = 2∑sₖ² + 2c∑sₖs_{k+1}.  

       Hence  

        ∑ sₖs_{k+1} = (800 − 2·308)/(2c) = 92(√5 + 1).    (3)

3.  Using the closure condition  
   Taking the squared modulus of (1) gives  

        0 = ∑sₖ² + 2c∑sₖs_{k+1} − 2c'∑sₖs_{k+2},  

   where c' = cos 36° = (√5 + 1)/4 and cos 144° = −c'.  
   Denote  

        S₁ = ∑sₖs_{k+1},      S₂ = ∑sₖs_{k+2}.  

   Then  

        2c'S₂ = ∑sₖ² + 2cS₁.  

   Using (2) and (3):  

        cS₁ = [(√5 − 1)/4]·92(√5 + 1) = 92,  
        S₂ = [308 + 2·92] / (2c') = 246(√5 − 1).             (4)

4.  The perimeter  
   P = ∑sₖ, so  

        P² = ∑sₖ² + 2∑_{i<j}sₖsⱼ  
            = ∑sₖ² + 2S₁ + 2S₂.  

   Substitute (2), (3) and (4):  

        P² = 308 + 2·92(√5 + 1) + 2·246(√5 − 1)  
            = 308 + 184√5 + 184 + 492√5 − 492  
            = 676√5.

5.  Final form  
   P² = 676√5 = m√n with m = 676, n = 5 (n square-free).  
   Therefore m + n = 676 + 5 = 681.

\boxed{681}

Let the five directed side–vectors of the pentagon be  

eₖ = sₖ ωᵏ (k = 0,1,2,3,4),

where  

• sₖ > 0 is the length of the k-th side,  
• ω = e^{i 72°} = e^{2πi/5} is the primitive 5-th root of unity  
  (because the exterior angle of an equiangular pentagon is 72°).

Since the polygon is closed,  

Σ_{k=0}^{4} eₖ = Σ_{k=0}^{4} sₖ ωᵏ = 0.  (1)

Define the discrete Fourier sums  

S_r = Σ_{k=0}^{4} sₖ ω^{rk} (r = 0,1,2,3,4).

Then (1) gives S₁ = 0; taking conjugates yields S₄ = 0 as well.

Important quantities in terms of the S_r:

1.  Sum of squares of the sides  
    Σ sₖ² = 1/5 Σ |S_r|² = 1/5(S₀² + |S₂|² + |S₃|²).  
    Because |S₂| = |S₃|, this is  
    Σ sₖ² = 1/5(S₀² + 2|S₂|²).  (2)

2.  Sum of the products of adjacent sides  
    T = Σ sₖ s_{k+1} = 1/5 Σ |S_r|² ω^{r}  
      = 1/5(S₀² + |S₂|²(ω² + ω³)).  (3)

   Since ω² + ω³ = 2 cos 144° = –φ, where φ = (1+√5)/2, eq. (3) becomes  
   T = 1/5 (S₀² – φ |S₂|²).  (4)

Given data  

Σ sₖ² = 308,  Σ diagonals² = 800.

For a diagonal dₖ joining the ends of sides sₖ and s_{k+1},

dₖ² = sₖ² + s_{k+1}² – 2sₖ s_{k+1} cos 108°,  
with cos 108° = (1–√5)/4.

Hence  
Σ dₖ² = 2Σ sₖ² – 2 cos 108° T  
800 = 2·308 – 2·(1–√5)/4 · T  
⇒ T = 92(1+√5) = 184 φ.  (5)

Put (2) and (4) into (5).

Let X = S₀², Y = |S₂|².

From (2): X + 2Y = 5·308 = 1540.  (6)  
From (4) & (5): X – φY = 5·184 φ = 920 φ.  (7)

Solve (6)–(7):

Y(2+φ) = 1540 – 920 φ  
 ⇒ Y = (1540 – 920 φ)/(2+φ).  

Substituting into (6) gives  

X = 1540 – 2Y = 3380(1+√5)/(5+√5) = 676√5.

But S₀ = Σ sₖ is the perimeter P, so  

P² = X = 676√5 = m√n  with m = 676, n = 5.

Since n is square-free, the required sum is  

m + n = 676 + 5 = 681.

\boxed{681}

Let the sides of the equiangular pentagon be the vectors  

a₁ , a₂ , a₃ , a₄ , a₅     (taken in order round the polygon).

Because the pentagon is equiangular, each side is obtained from the
preceding one by a rotation of 72° ( = 2π⁄5 ).  
Choose a₁ to lie on the positive real axis and write

aᵢ = lᵢ w^{\,i-1}, w = e^{i72°}, lᵢ = |aᵢ|  (i = 1,…,5).

Thus |aᵢ| = lᵢ are the side–lengths of the pentagon and

∑ lᵢ² = 308.  (1)

------------------------------------------------
1.  The diagonal lengths  

The five diagonals are

AC = |a₁+a₂| , BD = |a₂+a₃| , CE = |a₃+a₄| , DA = |a₄+a₅| , EB = |a₅+a₁| .

Hence

∑ diagonal² = Σ |aᵢ + a_{i+1}|²  (indices mod 5)

 = Σ (|aᵢ|² + |a_{i+1}|² + 2 Re(aᵢ ā_{i+1}))  
 = 2Σ|aᵢ|² + 2ΣRe(aᵢ ā_{i+1}).

Given ∑ diagonal² = 800 and (1), we get

2·308 + 2ΣRe(aᵢ ā_{i+1}) = 800  
⇒ ΣRe(aᵢ ā_{i+1}) = 92.  (2)

But aᵢ ā_{i+1} = lᵢl_{i+1} w^{\,i-1}\,w^{-i} = lᵢl_{i+1} e^{-i72°}.  
Hence Re(aᵢ ā_{i+1}) = lᵢl_{i+1} cos72°.

Let c = cos72° = (√5-1)/4. Then (2) gives

Σ lᵢl_{i+1} = 92 / c = 92(√5+1).  (3)

------------------------------------------------
2.  The closure condition  

Because the pentagon closes,  

a₁ + a₂ + a₃ + a₄ + a₅ = 0  
⇒ Σ lᵢ w^{\,i-1} = 0.

Take mod–squared to obtain  

0 = |Σ lᵢw^{\,i-1}|² = Σ lᵢ² + Σ_{k=1}^4 w^{k} Σ lᵢl_{i-k}.

Grouping conjugate pairs gives the real equation  

0 = S + 2cos72°·T₁ + 2cos144°·T₂,  (4)

where  
S  = Σ lᵢ² = 308,  
T₁ = Σ lᵢl_{i+1}  (adjacent products) = value in (3),  
T₂ = Σ lᵢl_{i+2}  (products at distance 2).

Since cos72° = c and cos144° = –cos36° = –C with C = (√5+1)/4, (4) becomes

0 = S + 2cT₁ – 2C T₂  
⇒ T₂ = (S + 2cT₁)/(2C).

Insert   S = 308, T₁ = 92(√5+1), c = (√5-1)/4, C = (√5+1)/4 :

2cT₁ = (√5-1)·92(√5+1)/2 = 184,  
S + 2cT₁ = 308 + 184 = 492,

T₂ = 492 / [(√5+1)/2] = 984/(√5+1) = 246(√5-1).  (5)

------------------------------------------------
3.  The perimeter  

Let P be the perimeter;  P = Σ lᵢ.  Then

P² = (Σ lᵢ)² = Σ lᵢ² + 2Σ_{i<j} lᵢl_j
     = S + 2(T₁ + T₂).

Using S, (3) and (5):

T₁ + T₂ = 92(√5+1) + 246(√5-1)  
         = (92+246)√5 + (92-246)  
         = 338√5 – 154,

P² = 308 + 2(338√5 – 154)  
     = 308 + 676√5 – 308  
     = 676√5.

------------------------------------------------
4.  Final form  

P² = 676√5 = m√n with n square-free.

Thus   m = 676, n = 5  ⇒ m + n = 676 + 5 = 681.

\boxed{681}

Let the directed sides of the pentagon be the complex vectors

v_k = s_k ω^{k-1}  for k = 1,2,3,4,5 ,

where  
 s_k > 0 is the length of side k,  
 ω = e^{2π i /5} is the 72° rotation (since the exterior angle of an equiangular pentagon is 72°).

Because the pentagon closes,  
Σ v_k = 0.                                                                        (1)

1.  Sums involving the side lengths  
 a) Sum of the squares of the sides  
  Σ s_k² = 308.                                                                (2)

 b) Diagonal from vertex k to k+2 is v_k + v_{k+1}; its length-squared is |v_k + v_{k+1}|².  
  Sum of squares of the five diagonals is given to be 800:

  Σ |v_k + v_{k+1}|² = 800.                                            (3)

2.  Translating (3)

|v_k + v_{k+1}|² = |v_k|² + |v_{k+1}|² + 2 Re(v_k \overline{v_{k+1}}).

Sum over k:

800 = 2Σ|v_k|² + 2Σ Re(v_k \overline{v_{k+1}})  
   = 2·308 + 2Σ Re(v_k \overline{v_{k+1}}).

Hence Σ Re(v_k \overline{v_{k+1}}) = 92.                               (4)

But v_k \overline{v_{k+1}} = s_k s_{k+1} ω^{-1} whose real part is s_k s_{k+1} cos72°.  
Let c = cos72° = (√5 − 1)/4.  Then (4) gives

c Σ s_k s_{k+1} = 92  ⇒ Σ s_k s_{k+1} = 92(√5 + 1).                (5)

Call this adjacent product sum A.

3.  Using the closure condition

Let B = Σ s_k s_{k+2} (sum of products of non-adjacent pairs).

Compute |Σ v_k|² using (1):

0 = |Σ v_k|² = Σ s_k² + Σ_{d=1}^4 ω^d C_d ,

where C_d = Σ s_k s_{k+d}.  
Because of 5-fold symmetry: C_1 = C_4 = A,  C_2 = C_3 = B.

Thus

0 = 308 + (ω + ω⁴)A + (ω² + ω³)B.

Now  
ω + ω⁴ = 2cos72° = 2c ,  
ω² + ω³ = 2cos144° = −2cos36° = −2d, where d = cos36° = (√5 + 1)/4.

Therefore

0 = 308 + 2Ac − 2Bd  ⇒ Bd − Ac = 154.                        (6)

From (5), A = 92(√5 + 1); with c = (√5 − 1)/4 we get

Ac = 92(√5 + 1)·(√5 − 1)/4 = 92.                               (7)

Insert (7) into (6):

Bd = 154 + 92 = 246  ⇒ B = 246/d = 246(√5 − 1).             (8)

4.  The perimeter

Let P = Σ s_k.  Then

P² = (Σ s_k)² = Σ s_k² + 2Σ_{i<j} s_i s_j  
   = 308 + 2(A + B)                                                (9)

Using (5) and (8):

2A = 184(√5 + 1), 2B = 492(√5 − 1).

Plug into (9):

P² = 308 + 184(√5 + 1) + 492(√5 − 1)  
   = 308 + 184√5 + 184 + 492√5 − 492  
   = (308 + 184 − 492) + (184 + 492)√5  
   = 0 + 676√5  
   = 676√5.

Hence P² = 676√5 = m√n with m = 676, n = 5.

Since 5 is square-free, m + n = 676 + 5 = 681.

\boxed{681}

Think of the 10 × 10 board as being eaten away one loop at a time.

A loop can remove  

• one horizontal “strip’’ 2 × w from the current top edge,  
• one horizontal strip from the current bottom edge,  
• one vertical strip h × 2 from the current left edge,  
• one vertical strip from the current right edge, or  
• a complete “ring’’ 1 cell thick round the whole current rectangle (this removes the whole perimeter and shrinks both its height and width by 2).

Starting with the 10 × 10 square and removing exactly five such layers must leave no cells at all.  
Denote by

 T, B, L, R the numbers of horizontal/vertical strips removed from the four sides  
 S     the number of complete rings removed.

Each horizontal strip decreases the height by 2, each vertical strip decreases the width by 2, and each ring decreases both by 2.  
After 5 layers have been removed we need the height or the width (or both) to have become 0.

A little case–work shows that only the following six “patterns’’ of layers are possible:

1. five horizontal strips → five 2 × 10 loops (rows 1–2, 3–4, …, 9–10)  
2. five vertical strips  → five 10 × 2 loops (columns 1–2, 3–4, …, 9–10)  

3. 1 outer 10 × 10 ring + 4 horizontal 2 × 8 strips inside it  
4. 1 outer 10 × 10 ring + 4 vertical 8 × 2 strips inside it  

5. 10 × 10 ring, 8 × 8 ring, 3 horizontal 2 × 6 strips in the 6 × 6 square  
6. 10 × 10 ring, 8 × 8 ring, 3 vertical 6 × 2 strips in the 6 × 6 square  

7. 10 × 10, 8 × 8, 6 × 6 rings, 2 horizontal 2 × 4 strips in the 4 × 4 square  
8. 10 × 10, 8 × 8, 6 × 6 rings, 2 vertical 4 × 2 strips in the 4 × 4 square  

9. 10 × 10, 8 × 8, 6 × 6, 4 × 4 rings and the single 2 × 2 loop in the centre  

10. five concentric rings: 10 × 10, 8 × 8, 6 × 6, 4 × 4, 2 × 2.

Every one of these patterns really can be carried out and they are all plainly different, while no other pattern is possible once the bookkeeping of what is removed at every step is enforced.

Hence the required number of partitions of the 10 × 10 grid into exactly five cell-loops is

\boxed{10}

Call a loop “thin” if one of its side–lengths equals 2; then the loop contains all the cells of its 2 × k (or k × 2) rectangle.  
A loop whose two side–lengths are ≥3 is a “frame”; its interior is another rectangle that still has to be tiled by the remaining loops.  

Because every loop is the perimeter of a rectangle and the five rectangles must be pairwise disjoint and cover the whole 10 × 10 board, they must be laminar: every two rectangles are either disjoint or one is contained in the other.  
Hence a tiling is completely described by

• how many strips two‐rows (or two‐columns) thick are peeled from each of the four sides, and  
• how many nested frames (possibly 0) are put into the remaining middle rectangle.

Write  

h = number of horizontal 2 × 10 strips removed (0,1 or 2),  
v = number of vertical 10 × 2 strips removed (0,1 or 2).

After removing those border strips the remaining middle rectangle has size  
(10–2h) × (10–2v) .

Let t = 5 – (h + v) be the number of loops that still have to be placed in that middle rectangle.  
Inside a rectangle of size R × C (R ≥ 2, C ≥ 2) the only possible numbers of loops are

• R⁄2   thin horizontal strips (if t = R⁄2),  
• C⁄2   thin vertical strips  (if t = C⁄2), or  
• 1 frame of size R × C followed by the above rules for the rectangle (R–2) × (C–2).

Carrying this out for every admissible pair (h , v) gives the following totals.

h + v = 0  
 Middle rectangle 10 × 10.  
 The possible distributions of the remaining 5 loops give 8 tilings.

h + v = 1 (two symmetric sub-cases)  
 Middle rectangle 8 × 10 (or 10 × 8) with 4 loops to place.  
 There are 16 tilings for each orientation → 32.

h + v = 2  
 If {h , v} = {2,0} or {0,2}: middle rectangle 6 × 10 (or 10 × 6) with 3 loops → 1 + 1 = 2 tilings.  
 If h = v = 1 the middle rectangle is 8 × 8, but it cannot be tiled with exactly 3 loops.

h + v ≥ 3 impossible (too few loops would remain for the middle rectangle).

Adding the two completely‐thin tilings (five horizontal 2 × 10 strips, five vertical 10 × 2 strips) gives

 2  (all thin)  
+ 8  
+32  
+ 2  
= 42 distinct tilings.

Therefore the 10 × 10 board can be partitioned into 5 cell loops in exactly

\boxed{42}

different ways.

Call a rectangle of cells h × w (h, w ≥ 2) a loop if it consists only of the cells on its perimeter
(2h+2w−4 cells).  
If the loop’s interior (h−2) × (w−2) is empty (i.e. h=2 or w=2) we call it a leaf loop.

Every cell of the 10 × 10 grid has to belong to exactly one loop, so:

1.  The 10 × 10 outer border must itself be a loop; otherwise the four corner cells could not
    be covered.  
    That already uses one of the five required loops and leaves an 8 × 8 interior
    rectangle that must be filled with the remaining four loops.

2.  We now have to count the partitions of an 8 × 8 rectangle into exactly 4 loops.
    Remove the outer loop of that 8 × 8 rectangle; the remaining 6 × 6 interior must be
    covered with 3 loops.
    There are two fundamentally different ways to proceed:

    A.  No splitting – keep nesting.
        8 × 8 → 6 × 6 → 4 × 4 → 2 × 2
        gives the single fully-nested chain
        (10×10, 8×8, 6×6, 4×4, 2×2).

    B.  Split the 8 × 8 interior once, either vertically or horizontally, into strips that are
        themselves loops of height 8 or width 8.

        Let the strip widths (if we split vertically) be
        w₁,…,w_k  (k ≥ 2,  each w_i ≥ 2,  w₁+…+w_k = 8).
        Each such strip is an 8 × w_i loop; its interior is
        6 × (w_i−2).  
        The table shows how many additional loops Li each strip must contribute so
        that the total number of loops is 4:

        • w_i = 2  →  interior empty   →  L_i = 0  
        • w_i = 4  →  interior 6×2      →  exactly one more loop (the 6×2 loop)   →  L_i = 1  
        • w_i = 6  →  interior 6×4      →  needs exactly two more loops
          (either 6×4 & 4×2 concentric or two 6×2 strips)   →  L_i = 2  
        • w_i = 3 or 5 produce an interior of width 1 or 3, impossible.

        Enumerating the allowed compositions gives

        k=2  
          (2,6) and (6,2)   → 2 orientations × 2 internal possibilities = 4  
          (4,4)             → 1 possibility

        k=3  
          (4,2,2) in 3 orders                         → 3 possibilities

        k=4  
          (2,2,2,2)                                   → 1 possibility

        Total for a vertical first split = 9.

        A horizontal first split is completely symmetric (interchanging the roles of
        height and width) and gives another 9 possibilities.

3.  Adding everything:

    • purely nested chain                      1  
    • first split vertical                     9  
    • first split horizontal                   9  

    Total partitions of the 8 × 8 interior into 4 loops = 19.

So the 10 × 10 grid can be partitioned into 5 cell loops in exactly 19 different ways.

\boxed{19}

Think of building the five loops one‐by‐one, always working from the outside of the yet–uncovered rectangle in toward the centre.

At any moment the uncovered part of the board is a rectangle W × H whose side–lengths are even.  
A new loop can be of only three possible types

S (“shell”) – the 1–cell–thick border of the present rectangle  
       removes one cell from ​each​ of the four sides, so W and H are each reduced by 2;

L (“left stripe”) – the whole 2–column strip along the left side  
       removes two columns, so W is reduced by 2, H is unchanged;

R (“right stripe”) – the whole 2–column strip along the right side  
       again reduces W by 2, H is unchanged;

(Two analogous horizontal stripes – “top” and “bottom’’ – are obtained by exchanging the roles of
W and H; they will be counted later by symmetry.)

----------------------------------------------------
1.  What must happen to the side–lengths?

To cover the 10 × 10 board we must in total eliminate ten columns and ten rows.
Writing

s = # of S–loops,   l = # of L–loops,   r = # of R–loops,

the final side–lengths are

W = 10 – 2(s + l + r) ,  H = 10 – 2s.

Because exactly five loops are used,
  s + l + r = 5.   (1)

The rectangle is completely eaten as soon as EITHER W or H becomes 0.  
There are two exclusive possibilities.

 A.  W = 0.  Then s + l + r = 5 (already true) and necessarily H > 0, i.e. no horizontal stripes
    are present.  That means l + r can be anything with

    s + l + r = 5 and l, r ≥ 0.  (2)

 B.  H = 0.  By symmetry this is the same situation with the roles of rows and columns swapped
    (i.e. with “top’’ and “bottom’’ stripes in place of L, R).

----------------------------------------------------
2.  Building the loops layer by layer

Going from the outside in, every layer is independently chosen from
{ L, R, S } (case A) or from { T, B, S } (case B),
and any order works – the width and height never become negative until the
last loop is laid because
each layer removes at most two cells from each dimension.

Hence, in case A the five layers are simply a word of length 5 in the
three–letter alphabet { L, R, S }.  The number of such words is

3^5 = 243.

In case B the five layers are a word of length 5 in the alphabet
{ T, B, S }; again there are 3^5 = 243 words.

----------------------------------------------------
3.  Removing the overcount

The word S S S S S (five concentric shells 10×10, 8×8, 6×6, 4×4, 2×2)
has been counted in both cases, so it must be subtracted once.

----------------------------------------------------
4.  Total number of partitions

243 + 243 – 1 = 485.

----------------------------------------------------
Therefore there are

\boxed{485}

different ways to partition the 10 × 10 board into five cell loops.

Call a loop “thin” if one of its side–lengths equals 2; otherwise it is “thick.”  
In a thin loop all the cells of its \(a \times b\) rectangle belong to the loop (because the “interior’’ is empty); in a thick loop only the cells on the border of its rectangle are used, leaving an empty rectangular hole that must be filled by other loops placed completely inside it.

Let us partition the \(10\times10\) board into five disjoint loops.

-------------------------------------------------
1.  Partitions containing a thick loop  
-------------------------------------------------
•  If a thick loop is present, every cell strictly inside it must be covered by other loops, so those loops must be nested completely inside each other.

•  Because every new thick loop must lie two cells farther inside than the previous one, the only possible nest inside the \(10\times10\) square is  
 \(10\times10,\; 8\times8,\; 6\times6,\; 4\times4,\; 2\times2\).

•  This gives exactly five loops and covers the whole board:
 36 + 28 + 20 + 12 + 4 = 100 cells.

•  No alternative dimensions are possible (any change would make one dimension 0 or negative before five loops are used).  

Hence there is exactly 1 partition that contains a thick loop: the five concentric square loops.

-------------------------------------------------
2.  Partitions with only thin loops  
-------------------------------------------------
Every thin loop has one side–length 2.  If all loops are thin, each loop is a solid \(2\times k\) or \(k\times2\) rectangle.

(a) Suppose some loop is \(2\times k\) (height 2).  
   •  That loop occupies two whole rows.  
   •  No two different loops can share a row (otherwise we could not cover the remaining 8 rows using at most the remaining 4 loops, each of height 2).  
   •  Thus each pair of rows must be covered by exactly one loop; there are five such pairs, so each loop must span the full width of 10 columns.  
   •  Consequently the only possible horizontal partition is the five \(2\times10\) stripes.

(b) By the same reasoning, if there is a loop \(k\times2\) (width 2) then every loop must be width 2 and height 10, giving five vertical \(10\times2\) stripes.

No mixture of horizontal and vertical thin loops is possible because a \(2\times k\) rectangle and a \(k\times2\) rectangle would overlap.

Thus there are exactly 2 partitions made only of thin loops:  
•  five horizontal \(2\times10\) stripes,  
•  five vertical \(10\times2\) stripes.

-------------------------------------------------
3.  Total number of partitions  
-------------------------------------------------
Adding the one concentric-square partition to the two stripe partitions,

total number of ways = 1 + 2 = 3.

\boxed{3}

Think of the 10 × 10 board as the outside of a rectangle that we repeatedly “peel” with $1$-cell–thick rectangular loops.  
At every step exactly one loop is removed.  Three different kinds of peels can occur.

F  : a “frame’’ – it removes one cell from every side of the current rectangle,  
  so the height and the width are both reduced by 2;

L,R: a vertical 2-column stripe on the left (L) or right (R) side – it shortens the
  width by 2 but leaves the height unchanged;

T,B: a horizontal 2-row stripe on the top (T) or bottom (B) – it shortens the
  height by 2 but leaves the width unchanged.

Five peels must remove the whole 10 × 10 board.

1.  All five peels are frames  
 Every step is an F, giving the five concentric
 10 × 10, 8 × 8, 6 × 6, 4 × 4 and 2 × 2 loops.
 There is exactly 1 such partition.

2.  The board is exhausted in the width direction  
 Let $f$ be the number of frames and $v$ the number of vertical stripes
 ($L$ or $R$); then
   $f+v=5,\qquad v\ge 1,\qquad h=0.$  
 After the $f$ frames have been taken, the height is $10-2f\;( \ge 2)$, so the
 stripes are always legal.

 Between the $f$ frames there are $m=f+1$ “levels’’ (height $10,\,10-2,\dots,10-2f$).
 Write $s_0,s_1,\dots,s_f$ for the numbers of vertical stripes removed at those
 levels ($s_0+\dots+s_f=v$).  In a level with $s_k$ stripes we may choose any
 number $L_k$ of them to be on the left side and the remaining $R_k=s_k-L_k$
 on the right; that gives $s_k+1$ possibilities.

 Hence, for a fixed $f$ the number of partitions is  
   S$(v,m)=\displaystyle\sum_{s_0+\dots+s_f=v}\prod_{k=0}^{f}(s_k+1).$  
 A standard stars-and-bars generating-function argument shows  
   S$(v,m)=\binom{v+2m-1}{2m-1}.$  

 Here $v=5-f$ and $m=f+1$, so the count for each $f$ is

   $\displaystyle N_f=\binom{(5-f)+2(f+1)-1}{2(f+1)-1}
   =\binom{6+f}{2f+1}.$

 f = 0  1  2  3  4        $N_f$ = 6, 35, 56, 36, 10

 Adding them gives 143 partitions that finish by shrinking the width to 0
 (vertical-stripe type).

3.  The board is exhausted in the height direction  
 By symmetry (rotate the board $90^\circ$), the same
 143 partitions arise, now using horizontal stripes instead of vertical ones.

Finally

 All–frame type     1  
 Vertical-stripe type 143  
 Horizontal-stripe type 143  

 Total   $=1+143+143=287$

\boxed{287}

Let F(n) be the number of ways to partition a 2n × 2n grid into n cell-loops.

1.  Base case  
   For n = 1 the grid is 2 × 2 and the only possible loop is the 2 × 2 loop itself, so  
   F(1) = 1.

2.  Two families of partitions of a 2n × 2n grid  

   a)  The “stripe’’ tilings  
       – n horizontal 2 × 2n loops (one pair of rows per loop), or  
       – n vertical 2n × 2 loops (one pair of columns per loop).  
       These give exactly 2 partitions and they never mix, because horizontal and
       vertical stripes would overlap.

   b)  Partitions that contain the outer 2n × 2n loop  
       Remove that outer loop; what remains is a (2n−2) × (2n−2) grid that still has
       to be partitioned into n−1 loops.  The number of ways to do this is F(n−1).

   Hence for every n ≥ 2  

          F(n) = 2 + F(n−1).

3.  Solving the recurrence  
   With F(1)=1,

      F(2) = 2 + 1 = 3  
      F(3) = 2 + 3 = 5  
      F(4) = 2 + 5 = 7  
      F(5) = 2 + 7 = 9.

4.  n = 5 (the 10 × 10 grid)  
   Therefore there are F(5) = 9 distinct partitions of a 10 × 10 grid into 5
   cell-loops:

   • 5 horizontal 2 × 10 loops;  
   • 5 vertical 10 × 2 loops;  
   • and, with the outer 10 × 10 loop present, the 7 possibilities obtained by
     recursively inserting any of the 4-loop partitions of the 8 × 8 interior:
       – 4 horizontal 2 × 8 stripes,  
       – 4 vertical 8 × 2 stripes,  
       – the nested set 8 × 8, 6 × 6, 4 × 4, 2 × 2,  
       – 8 × 8 loop + 3 horizontal 2 × 6 stripes,  
       – 8 × 8 loop + 3 vertical 6 × 2 stripes,  
       – 8 × 8 loop + 6 × 6 loop + 2 horizontal 2 × 4 stripes,  
       – 8 × 8 loop + 6 × 6 loop + 2 vertical 4 × 2 stripes.

   Together these 2 + 7 = 9 partitions exhaust all possibilities.

Answer: \boxed{9}

First shrink every dimension by a factor ​2.  
A \(10\times 10\) board of unit squares becomes a \(5\times 5\) board of
“blocks’’, each block representing a \(2\times 2\) group of unit squares.

In this coarse \(5\times 5\) board every cell–loop

• is the whole boundary of a rectangle whose sides are lattice–parallel,  
• has thickness one block,  
• and is either  
  – a “stripe’’ (its height or its width is one block, so it encloses no
   interior blocks), or  
  – a “frame’’ (both height and width are at least two blocks, so it
   encloses another, smaller rectangle).

Because the original grid must be split into exactly five disjoint
cell–loops, the corresponding \(5\times 5\) board must be split into
exactly five disjoint stripes/frames whose union is the whole board.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1 – possible outer structures
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Let \(k\;(0\le k\le 5)\) be the number of concentric frames, starting
from the outside.  
After \(k\) frames are removed the remaining inner rectangle has side
length \(5-k\) blocks.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2 – filling the last inner rectangle
---------------------------------------------------------------------
If \(5-k>0\) the remaining \((5-k)\times(5-k)\) rectangle must be
covered with stripes because only stripes contain no interior blocks.
A horizontal covering needs \((5-k)\) horizontal stripes, while a
vertical covering needs \((5-k)\) vertical stripes; in either case the
number of stripes is exactly \(5-k\), so, together with the \(k\) outer
frames, the total number of loops is \(k+(5-k)=5\).

Thus for each \(k=0,1,2,3,4\)

 •  there are two possibilities (horizontal or vertical stripes).

For \(k=5\) the whole board is exactly the five nested frames
(the sizes are \(5,4,3,2,1\) blocks, i.e. \(10,8,6,4,2\) unit squares),
so there is one additional possibility.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3 – eliminating the double count for \(k=4\)
---------------------------------------------------------------------
For \(k=4\) the inner rectangle has side length \(1\) block, and “a
horizontal stripe’’ and “a vertical stripe’’ are the same \(1\times1\)
block; hence the two formal choices coincide and give only
one genuine partition.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4 – total count
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Total number of partitions

 = (for \(k=0,1,2,3\) : \(2+2+2+2\))  
 + (for \(k=4\)   : \(1\))  
 + (for \(k=5\)   : \(1\))

 = \(8+1+1 = 10\).

Hence there are

\[
\boxed{10}
\]

distinct ways to partition a \(10\times 10\) grid into exactly five
cell–loops.

Let the common difference of the arithmetic sequence be the (integer) value d > 0.  
General term:

a_n = 4 + (n−1)d.

Because both 24 and 34 are terms, there exist positive integers k and m such that

4 + (k−1)d = 24, 4 + (m−1)d = 34.

Subtracting gives
34 − 24 = (m − k)d  ⇒ 10 = (m − k)d,
so d must be a positive divisor of 10:

d ∈ {1, 2, 5, 10}.

Check that each d indeed yields integer indices k, m ≥ 1:

d = 1 → k = 21, m = 31  
d = 2 → k = 11, m = 16  
d = 5 → k = 5,  m = 7  
d = 10 → k = 3,  m = 4.

Thus exactly four arithmetic sequences satisfy the conditions.

For each sequence, the 10th term is

a_10 = 4 + 9d.

Compute and add:

d = 1  → a_10 = 13  
d = 2  → a_10 = 22  
d = 5  → a_10 = 49  
d = 10 → a_10 = 94  

Sum = 13 + 22 + 49 + 94 = 178.

\boxed{178}

4 + (m-1)d = 24 and 4 + (n-1)d = 34.

Hence  
(m-1)d = 20 and (n-1)d = 30,

so d must divide both 20 and 30.  
Therefore d is a positive common divisor of 20 and 30:

d ∈ {1, 2, 5, 10}.

a_10 = 4 + 9d.

d = 5 → a_10 = 4 + 9(5) = 49  
d = 10 → a_10 = 4 + 9(10) = 94

Sum of all 10th terms: 13 + 22 + 49 + 94 = 178.

Step 1.  Fix a convenient coordinate system  
A  = (0,0) , E = (20,0)   (so AE = 20 and lies on the x–axis).  
Because ∠A = 90° the side AB is perpendicular to AE, so let  

  B = (0,b) with b = AB  (b is an integer we are asked to find).

Step 2.  Locate C  
At B the interior angle is 45° and BC = 14√2.  
Taking the 45° turn toward the right keeps B,C,D on the same side of AE, giving  

  C = (14, b – 14).

(With b > 14, C is still above AE, so B,C,D are all above the x-axis.)

Step 3.  Locate D and use ∠E = 90°  
Since ∠E = 90° and AE is horizontal, ED must be vertical.  
Put  

  D = (20, h) with h = DE and 0 < h < b (because DE < AB).

Step 4.  Use ∠D = 45°  
At D the interior angle between DC and DE is 45°.  
DE is the downward vector (0, –h).  
DC = C – D = (14 – 20 , (b – 14) – h) = ( –6 , b – 14 – h).

Let t = h – (b – 14)  (> 0 so that the angle is interior).  
Then DC = ( –6 , –t ) with |DC| = √(6² + t²).

The cosine of the 45° interior angle gives  
      cos 45° = (–DC·DE)/( |DC| |DE| )  
       = ( –( –t ) h ) / ( |DC| h ) = t / |DC| = √2/2.

Hence |DC| = √2 t, and substituting |DC|² = 36 + t² gives  
 36 + t² = 2t²  ⇒ t² = 36  ⇒ t = 6.

Therefore h = b – 14 + 6  =  b – 8, so

  D = (20 , b – 8) and DE = h = b – 8.

(The condition DE < AB is now b – 8 < b, automatically true.)

Step 5.  Verify the remaining lengths  
CD = √(6² + 6²) = 6√2, which is positive,  
BC = 14√2 by construction, and all vertices are distinct with B,C,D above AE.  
The pentagon is simple and non-convex (the cross-product signs change at C).

Step 6.  Area of the pentagon  
Using the shoelace formula for A(0,0), B(0,b), C(14, b–14), D(20, b–8), E(20,0):

Σ x_i y_{i+1} = 14b – 112  
Σ y_i x_{i+1} = 54b – 440

Area = ½|Σx y – Σy x|  
    = ½| (14b – 112) – (54b – 440) |  
    = ½| –40b + 328 |  
    = 20b – 164.  (Here b > 14 ⇒ the absolute-value gives the positive form.)

Step 7.  Area multiple of 16  
Require 20b – 164 ≡ 0  (mod 16).  
Factor: 20b – 164 = 4(5b – 41).  
Thus 5b – 41 ≡ 0  (mod 4)  ⇒  b ≡ 1  (mod 4).

Step 8.  Collect all conditions on b  
1.  b is a positive integer with b < 2026.  
2.  b > 14 (so C stays above AE).  
3.  b ≡ 1 (mod 4).

Step 9.  Count the admissible integers  
The smallest b satisfying both b > 14 and b ≡ 1 (mod 4) is 17.  
The largest b < 2026 with b ≡ 1 (mod 4) is 2025.

They form an arithmetic progression 17, 21, 25, … , 2025 with common difference 4.

Number of terms = (2025 – 17)/4 + 1 = 2008/4 + 1 = 502 + 1 = 503.

Hence there are 503 possible integer lengths for AB.

\boxed{503}

Step 1.  Place the pentagon on the coordinate plane.

Put A (0,0) and E (20,0) so that AE = 20 lies on the x–axis.  
Because ∠A and ∠E are right angles and B,C,D are on the same (upper) side of AE:

• AB is vertical, so let B (0,k) with k=AB>0.  
• DE is also vertical, so let D (20,s) with s>0.

Step 2.  Fix the edge BC.

At B we need ∠B = 45°.  If BC were directed up–right, ∠D would turn out to be larger than AB (contradicting DE<AB).  
Instead take BC to go down–right making a 45° angle with BA:

BC = 14√2·(1,−1)/√2 = (14,−14).

Hence     C = B + (14,−14) = (14 , k−14).

(For k>14 this keeps C above AE.)

Step 3.  Use ∠D = 45° to find s.

Vectors at D:

D→C = C−D = (14−20 , k−14−s) = (−6 , k−14−s)  
D→E = (0 , −s).

The cosine of the 45° angle between them is

(D→C·D→E)/(|D→C||D→E|)= (s−k+14)/√(6²+(s−k+14)²) = √2/2.

BC = (14, 14)  (because \(|BC| = 14\sqrt2\)),

The requirement that B and C be under the x–axis forces \(b>14\).

Let D = (20, −d) with \(d>0\) (so D too is below \(AE\)).

At D the interior angle is \(45^\circ\); therefore the vector \(D\!\to\!C\) makes \(45^\circ\) with the upward vertical.  As C lies to the left of D, this vector must be “up–left’’:

D→C  =  (−u, u) with u>0  and  |u| = |Δy|.

From the x–coordinates:

−14  = 20 − u ⇒ u = 34.

From the y–coordinates:

14 − b = −d + u ⇒ d = b − 20.

\(DE = d = b-20 < b = AB\) (since \(b>20\)), so the given inequality holds.

C→B = (−14,−14), C→D = (34,6).

\(20b - 480 \equiv 4b \pmod{16}\).

Let

g = max{d_i} , s = g + 1     (so s is the least base larger than every digit).

The function is  

f(n) = Σ d_i s^{\,i}.

We need f(n) = n, i.e.  

Σ d_i 10^{\,i} = Σ d_i s^{\,i}.        (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. One–digit numbers  

If n = d (1 ≤ d ≤ 9) then (1) is simply d = d, always true.

Count : 9 numbers.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Two–digit numbers  

n = 10a + b (a ≠ 0).

Equation (1):

10a + b = a s + b  ⇒ (10 – s) a = 0.

Since a ≠ 0, we must have s = 10  ⇒  g = 9.

Thus at least one digit of the two–digit number is 9.

Count of two–digit numbers with at least one 9:

• Tens digit 9 : 90–99 → 10 numbers.  
• Tens digit 1–8 and units 9 : 8 numbers.

Total : 10 + 8 = 18.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Three–digit numbers  

n = 100a + 10b + c (a ≠ 0).

Equation (1):

100a + 10b + c = a s² + b s + c  
 ⇒ 100a + 10b = a s² + b s  
 ⇒ a(100 – s²) = b(s – 10).      (2)

For s < 10 we have 100 – s² > 0 and s – 10 < 0, so the two sides of (2)
have opposite signs (unless both zero, which is impossible because a ≠ 0).
Hence (2) cannot hold when s < 10.

Therefore we must again have s = 10 ⇒ g = 9, i.e. at least one digit 9.

Count of three–digit numbers containing a 9:

Total three–digit numbers: 900.  
Numbers with no 9: first digit 1–8 (8 choices), each of the other digits 0–8 (9 choices each) ⇒ 8·9·9 = 648.  
Thus numbers with at least one 9: 900 – 648 = 252.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Total count

One–digit:  9  
Two–digit: 18  
Three–digit: 252  

Total = 9 + 18 + 252 = 279.

Let n be written in base–10 as  
n = d_k d_{k-1}\dots d_1 d_0  (each d_i is a digit, d_k ≠ 0).

Let  

M = max{d_k , d_{k-1}, … , d_0},  
B = M + 1   (the least base larger than the greatest digit).

Define  

f(n) = d_k B^{k} + d_{k-1} B^{k-1} + … + d_1 B + d_0.

We want the numbers n < 1000 for which f(n) = n.

------------------------------------------------
1. One–digit numbers (1 ≤ n ≤ 9)

For a single digit d, B = d + 1.  
Since both “values” of the numeral are just d, f(n) = n automatically.

Count: 9 numbers.

------------------------------------------------
2. Two–digit numbers  
n = 10a + b with a (≠0) the tens digit and b the units digit.

Equation:
10a + b = aB + b  ⇒  a(10 – B) = 0.

Because a ≠ 0, we must have B = 10.  
Thus M = B – 1 = 9, i.e. at least one digit is 9.

How many 2–digit numbers contain a 9?
• Tens digit 9: 10 numbers   (90–99)  
• Units digit 9 but tens digit 1–8: 8 numbers (19, 29, …, 89)

Total: 18 numbers.

------------------------------------------------
3. Three–digit numbers  
n = 100a + 10b + c with a ≠ 0.

Equation:
100a + 10b + c = aB² + bB + c  
⇒ a(100 – B²) + b(10 – B) = 0  
⇒ a(100 – B²) = b(B – 10).

For 2 ≤ B ≤ 9, the left‐hand side is positive while the right‐hand side is non-positive, forcing b = 0 and then B = 10 — impossible when B ≤ 9.  
Hence B must be 10.

So again M = 9; at least one digit is 9.

Count of 3–digit numbers (100–999) containing a 9:  
Total 900 minus those with no 9.  
Without a 9: 8 choices for hundreds digit (1–8) and 9 choices each for tens and units: 8·9·9 = 648.  
With a 9: 900 – 648 = 252 numbers.

Write the (decimal) digits of n:

n = d_k … d_1 d_0 (without leading zeros).

Let  

m = max{d_i} and b = m + 1 (the least integer exceeding the largest digit).

Define  

f(n) = Σ d_i b^i.  

We need f(n) = n = Σ d_i 10^i with n < 1000 (at most three digits).

1. One–digit numbers  
For n = d (1 ≤ d ≤ 9)

 f(n) = d in any base b > d, so f(n)=n always holds.  
Hence the 9 one–digit numbers 1–9 all qualify.

2. Two–digit numbers  
Let the digits be a (tens) and c (units), a ≥ 1.  
Equation: 10a + c = a b + c  ⇒ a(10 – b) = 0.  
Since a ≠ 0, we need b = 10.  
Thus m = 9, i.e. at least one digit is 9.  
Therefore a two–digit number works iff it contains a 9.

3. Three–digit numbers  
Digits a (hundreds), b (tens), c (units) with a ≥ 1.  
Equation: 100a + 10b + c = a (m+1)^2 + b (m+1) + c  
 ⇔ a(100 – (m+1)^2) + b(10 – (m+1)) = 0.

For m ≤ 8 we have 100 – (m+1)^2 > 0 and 10 – (m+1) > 0, so the left side is positive
(because a, b ≥ 0), a contradiction.  
Hence m must be 9 → b = 10, which again means at least one digit 9 is present.  
Thus a three–digit number works iff it contains a 9.

Summary  
• All numbers that contain the digit 9 (of any length ≤3) work.  
• In addition, the one–digit numbers 1–8 work.

Count numbers < 1000 containing a 9.  
Treat 000–999 as 3–digit strings; digits may be 0–9 except 9 is forbidden:

Without any 9: 9 choices for each position → 9^3 = 729 strings, one of which is 000.  
Hence numbers 1–999 with at least one 9:

999 – 728 = 271.

Finally add the eight extra numbers 1–8 (they have no 9):

Total = 271 + 8 = 279.

b = k + 1  (the least integer greater than the greatest digit).  

When the string a₂a₁a₀ is read in base b we obtain  

f(n) = a₂ b² + a₁ b + a₀ .

We need f(n) = n:

a₂·100 + a₁·10 + a₀ = a₂ b² + a₁ b + a₀

Because n < 1000, k ≤ 9 → b ≤ 10.

• 1 through 8 (8 numbers)  
• the 271 numbers below 1000 that contain at least one digit 9

Let  

r = number of red marbles (r ≥ 7)  
b = number of blue marbles (b ≥ 7)  
n = r + b.

When 7 marbles are drawn without replacement,  

P(exactly k reds) = C(r, k)·C(b, 7–k) / C(n, 7).

Given  
P(4 reds) = P(5 reds):

C(r,4)·C(b,3) = C(r,5)·C(b,2).

1. Expand the combinations:

[r(r−1)(r−2)(r−3)/24] · [b(b−1)(b−2)/6]  
= [r(r−1)(r−2)(r−3)(r−4)/120] · [b(b−1)/2].

2. Cancel common factors and simplify:

( b−2 ) · 240 / [ 144 · ( r−4 ) ] = 1  
⇒ 5(b − 2) = 3(r − 4)  
⇒ 3r = 5b + 2  
⇒ r = (5b + 2)/3.

3. For r to be integral, 5b + 2 ≡ 0 (mod 3) ⇒ b ≡ 2 (mod 3).

Write b = 3k + 2 with integer k ≥ 0.  
Then

r = (5(3k + 2) + 2)/3 = 5k + 4.

4. Colour-count constraints:

r ≥ 7  ⇒ 5k + 4 ≥ 7  ⇒ k ≥ 1.  
b ≥ 7  ⇒ 3k + 2 ≥ 7  ⇒ k ≥ 2.

Hence k = 2, 3, 4, … .

5. Total number of marbles:

n = r + b = (5k + 4) + (3k + 2) = 8k + 6.

The five smallest k (2 through 6) give

k = 2  →  n = 8·2 + 6 = 22  
k = 3  →  n = 30  
k = 4  →  n = 38  
k = 5  →  n = 46  
k = 6  →  n = 54.

6. Sum of these five least n’s:

22 + 30 + 38 + 46 + 54 = 190.

Let  

r = number of red marbles  
b = number of blue marbles (so r + b = n)

We are told r ≥ 7 and b ≥ 7.

Probability of drawing exactly i red marbles in 7 draws without replacement is  

P(i red) = C(r,i) · C(b,7−i) / C(n,7).

Set P(4 red) = P(5 red):

C(r,4)·C(b,3) = C(r,5)·C(b,2).

Using the identity C(m,k)/C(m,k+1) = (k+1)/(m−k),  

C(r,4)/C(r,5) = 5/(r−4),  
C(b,2)/C(b,3) = 3/(b−2).

Therefore  
5/(r−4) = 3/(b−2)  ⇒  5(b−2) = 3(r−4)  
⇒ 5b − 10 = 3r − 12  
⇒ 3r − 5b = 2.

Solve the linear Diophantine equation 3r − 5b = 2.

A particular solution is r ≡ 4 (mod 5), so write r = 5k + 4.  
Substitute:

3(5k + 4) − 5b = 2  
15k + 12 − 5b = 2  
5b = 15k + 10  
b = 3k + 2.

Hence all solutions are  
r = 5k + 4, b = 3k + 2, k ∈ ℤ.

Apply the minimum-count conditions:

r ≥ 7 ⇒ 5k + 4 ≥ 7 ⇒ k ≥ 1,  
b ≥ 7 ⇒ 3k + 2 ≥ 7 ⇒ k ≥ 2.

Thus k ≥ 2.

For each k ≥ 2, the total number of marbles is  
n = r + b = (5k + 4) + (3k + 2) = 8k + 6.

The five smallest values arise from k = 2,3,4,5,6:

k = 2 ⇒ n = 22  
k = 3 ⇒ n = 30  
k = 4 ⇒ n = 38  
k = 5 ⇒ n = 46  
k = 6 ⇒ n = 54.

Sum of these five n’s:  
22 + 30 + 38 + 46 + 54 = 190.

k = 5: R = 29, B = 17 ⇒ n = 46

22 + 30 + 38 + 46 + 54 = 190.

r = number of red marbles  
b = number of blue marbles  

with r + b = n, r ≥ 7, b ≥ 7.

Drawing 7 marbles without replacement, the hypergeometric probability of getting exactly k reds is  

P(k) = C(r,k)·C(b,7-k) / C(n,7).

Given P(4) = P(5):

C(r,4)·C(b,3) = C(r,5)·C(b,2).

Rewrite the combinations:

C(r,5) = C(r,4)·(r–4)/5, C(b,3) = C(b,2)·(b–2)/3.

Substitute and cancel the common factors C(r,4)·C(b,2):

(b – 2)/3 = (r – 4)/5  
⇒ 5(b – 2) = 3(r – 4)  
⇒ 3r – 5b = 2.

Solve the linear Diophantine equation 3r – 5b = 2.

Since 3r ≡ 2 (mod 5) and the inverse of 3 mod 5 is 2, we have  

r ≡ 4 (mod 5) ⇒ r = 5k + 4.

Insert into the original equation:

3(5k + 4) – 5b = 2  
⇒ 15k + 12 – 5b = 2  
⇒ 5b = 15k + 10  
⇒ b = 3k + 2.

Thus the general solution is  

r = 5k + 4, b = 3k + 2 (k ∈ ℤ).

Color counts must each be at least 7:

r ≥ 7 ⇒ 5k + 4 ≥ 7 ⇒ k ≥ 1,  
b ≥ 7 ⇒ 3k + 2 ≥ 7 ⇒ k ≥ 2.

Hence k ≥ 2.

The total number of marbles is  

n = r + b = (5k + 4) + (3k + 2) = 8k + 6.

Compute the five smallest values (k = 2,3,4,5,6):

k = 2: n = 8·2 + 6 = 22  
k = 3: n = 30  
k = 4: n = 38  
k = 5: n = 46  
k = 6: n = 54

Their sum is  
22 + 30 + 38 + 46 + 54 = 190.

\boxed{190}

Let  
C(4, 39) be the centre of the circle and  
P(x, y) be a point of tangency on the parabola  

  2y = x² – 8x + 12  ⟹  y = f(x) = (x² – 8x + 12)/2.

For tangency we need two conditions:

1.  P lies on both the circle and the parabola:  
  (x – 4)² + (y – 39)² = r²,  y = f(x).

2.  The radius CP is perpendicular to the tangent of the parabola at P.  
   The slope of the tangent to the parabola is  
  f '(x) = (2x – 8)/2 = x – 4,  
   so the normal slope is –1/(x – 4).

Hence the slope of CP must equal this normal slope:

  (y – 39)/(x – 4) = –1/(x – 4),  x ≠ 4.

Cancelling (x – 4) gives  
  y – 39 = –1  ⟹  y = 38.

Plugging y = 38 into the parabola:

  38 = (x² – 8x + 12)/2  
  ⇒ x² – 8x – 64 = 0  
  ⇒ x = 4 ± 4√5  (both ≠ 4).

Thus the two tangency points are  
P₁(4 + 4√5, 38) and P₂(4 – 4√5, 38).

Their common distance to C is

  r² = (x – 4)² + (38 – 39)²  
    = (4√5)² + (–1)² = 80 + 1 = 81,  
  r = 9.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––

Special case x = 4  
For x = 4 the tangent to the parabola is horizontal (slope 0), so the normal is vertical, matching the line through C.  
The point on the parabola is  

  P₃(4, –2).

Distance to C:  
  r = |39 – (–2)| = 41.

To confirm tangency, set  

  g(x) = (x – 4)² + (f(x) – 39)² – 41².

A quick derivative check gives  

  g(x) = 0 and g '(x) = 0 at x = 4,

so the intersection there is indeed a double root—hence a tangency.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––

Therefore the only radii for which the circle is tangent to the parabola are

  r = 9 and r = 41,

and their sum is

  9 + 41 = 50.

Let C = (4,39) be the center of the circle and let   
P be the parabola 2y = x² – 8x + 12  ⇒  y = (x² – 8x + 12)/2.

1.  Express the squared distance from C to an arbitrary point (x,y) on P:

   y − 39 = (x² − 8x + 12)/2 − 39  
              = (x² − 8x − 66)/2.

   D(x) = (x − 4)² + (y − 39)²  
        = (x − 4)² + [(x² − 8x − 66)/2]².

2.  Simplify with a = x − 4:

   x = a + 4  
   x² − 8x − 66 = (a + 4)² − 8(a + 4) − 66  
                  = a² − 82.

   So D(a) = a² + [(a² − 82)²]/4.

3.  Find the critical points of D(a):

   D′(a) = 2a + ½ (a² − 82)(2a)  
          = 2a + a(a² − 82) = a(a² − 80).

   Set D′(a)=0: a = 0, a = ±√80 = ±4√5.

4.  Evaluate D at the critical points.

   a = ±4√5 (a² = 80):  
   D = 80 + [(80 − 82)²]/4 = 80 + 4/4 = 80 + 1 = 81.

   a = 0:  
   D = 0 + [(0 − 82)²]/4 = 82²/4 = 1681 = 41².

   Second derivative D″(a) = 3a² − 80 gives  
   • D″(±4√5) = 160 > 0 ⇒ minima (closest points).  
   • D″(0)     = −80 < 0 ⇒ local maximum.

5.  Tangency occurs when the circle’s radius squared equals D at a critical point, i.e.  
   r² = 81  or  r² = 1681  ⇒  r = 9  or  r = 41.

These are the only radii for which the circle is tangent to the parabola.

Sum of all such real radii: 9 + 41 = 50.

(x – 4)² + [(x² – 8x – 66)/2]² = r².

Define  
g(x) = (x – 4)² + ¼(x² – 8x – 66)².  
The circle and parabola intersect where g(x) = r².

For tangency they must meet with multiplicity 2, i.e. g(x) = r² and g′(x) = 0 for some real x.

Compute g′(x).

Let h(x) = x² – 8x – 66.  
Then g(x) = (x – 4)² + ¼h(x)².

g′(x) = 2(x – 4) + ½h(x)·h′(x)  
       = 2(x – 4) + ½h(x)(2x – 8)  
       = 2(x – 4)[1 + ½h(x)].

Hence g′(x) = 0 when

1. x – 4 = 0  ⇒ x = 4, or  
2. 1 + ½h(x) = 0  ⇒ h(x) = –2  
  ⇒ x² – 8x – 66 = –2  
  ⇒ x² – 8x – 64 = 0  
  ⇒ x = 4 ± 4√5.

Thus the critical x-values are  
x₁ = 4, x₂ = 4 + 4√5, x₃ = 4 – 4√5.

a) x = 4: h = –82  
 g(4) = (4 – 4)² + ¼(–82)² = 0 + ¼·6724 = 1681  
 ⇒ r² = 1681 ⇒ r = 41.

b) x = 4 ± 4√5: h = –2, (x – 4)² = (4√5)² = 80  
 g = 80 + ¼(–2)² = 80 + 1 = 81  
 ⇒ r² = 81 ⇒ r = 9.

Sum of all such real radii: 9 + 41 = 50.

  (x – 4)² + (y – 39)² = r². (1)

  2y = x² – 8x + 12  ⇔  y = (x² – 8x + 12)/2. (2)

 A = x² – 8x – 66  (because (x² – 8x + 12)/2 – 39 = A/2).

Then

 R(x) = (x – 4)² + (A/2)² = (x – 4)² + A²/4.

The circle is tangent to the parabola exactly when the equation R(x) = r² has a repeated root; i.e. R′(x) = 0 at the same x.

Compute R′(x) conveniently by clearing denominators:  
Let S(x) = 4R(x) = 4(x – 4)² + A².

A = x² – 8x – 66 ⇒ A′ = 2x – 8.

S′(x) = 8(x – 4) + 2A(2x – 8)  
  = 4(x – 4)[A + 2].

Hence critical points occur when

 x = 4  or  A + 2 = 0.

1.  x = 4  
 A = 16 – 32 – 66 = –82.  
 R(4) = (4 – 4)² + (–82/2)² = 0 + 41² = 1681.  
 r = √1681 = 41.

2.  A + 2 = 0 ⇒ x² – 8x – 64 = 0  
  x = 4 ± 4√5.  
 For either root, A = –2 and (x – 4)² = (±4√5)² = 80.  
 R(x) = 80 + (–2)²/4 = 80 + 1 = 81.  
 r = √81 = 9.

 r = 9 and r = 41.

Their sum is

 9 + 41 = 50.

 (x – 4)² + (y – 39)².

3.  Substitute y:

4.  Let t = (x – 4)² (t ≥ 0).

     = t + ¼t² – 41t + 1681  
     = ¼(t² – 160t + 6724).

 So r² = g(t).

 g(t) = r² and g′(x) = 0  
 has a common x.  Since g(x) = g(t) with t = (x – 4)²,

 g′(x) = g′(t)·dt/dx = g′(t)·2(x – 4).

 Thus g′(x) = 0 when  
 a) x = 4  (t = 0) or b) g′(t) = 0.

6.  Case a)  x = 4, t = 0:  
 g(0) = ¼·6724 = 1681 = 41² ⇒ r = 41.

7.  Case b)  g′(t) = 0:  
 g(t) = ¼(t² – 160t + 6724)  
 ⇒ g′(t) = ¼(2t – 160) = ½t – 40.  
 ½t – 40 = 0 ⇒ t = 80.

 g(80) = ¼(80² – 160·80 + 6724) = ¼·324 = 81 = 9² ⇒ r = 9.

Let  

1. Parabola :   2y = x² − 8x + 12 ⇒ y = ½(x² − 8x + 12) = ½x² − 4x + 6.  
2. Circle (centre (4, 39), radius r) : (x − 4)² + (y − 39)² = r².

-------------------------------------------------
1.  Express the squared distance from the centre to a general point of the parabola
-------------------------------------------------

Let y(x) = ½x² − 4x + 6.

Distance-squared from (4, 39) to (x, y(x)):

D(x) = (x − 4)² + [y(x) − 39]²  
   = (x − 4)² + (½x² − 4x + 6 − 39)²  
   = (x − 4)² + (½x² − 4x − 33)².

Call B(x) = ½x² − 4x − 33.  
Then D(x) = (x − 4)² + B(x)².     (★)

-------------------------------------------------
2.  Locate the points where the distance is extremal
-------------------------------------------------

Tangency occurs when the distance from the centre to the parabola is an extremum
(the radius is perpendicular to the parabola there).  
Differentiate (★):

D′(x) = 2(x − 4) + 2B(x)·B′(x)  
where B′(x) = x − 4.

Hence D′(x) = 2(x − 4)[1 + B(x)].

Set D′(x) = 0:

(i) x − 4 = 0  ⇒ x = 4.

(ii) 1 + B(x) = 0 ⇒ B(x) = −1  
   ½x² − 4x − 33 = −1  
   ½x² − 4x − 32 = 0  
   x² − 8x − 64 = 0  
   x = 4 ± 4√5.

Thus the three critical abscissae are

x₁ = 4, x₂ = 4 + 4√5, x₃ = 4 − 4√5.

-------------------------------------------------
3.  Compute the corresponding squared distances
-------------------------------------------------

•  At x = 4:  
 A = (x − 4)² = 0, B = −41  
 D(4) = 0 + (−41)² = 1681 ⇒ r = √1681 = 41.

•  At x = 4 ± 4√5:  
 x − 4 = ±4√5 ⇒ A = (4√5)² = 80, B = −1  
 D = 80 + (−1)² = 81 ⇒ r = √81 = 9.

-------------------------------------------------
4.  Tangency verification
-------------------------------------------------

For each critical point:

Slope of parabola m_p = dy/dx = x − 4.  
Slope of radius  m_r = (y − 39)/(x − 4).

At x = 4 ± 4√5: m_p = ±4√5, m_r = −1/(±4√5);  
the circle’s tangent is the negative reciprocal of m_r, i.e. ±4√5 = m_p.

At x = 4: m_p = 0, radius is vertical, circle’s tangent is horizontal (slope 0).

Thus the curve and circle share the same tangent at these points, confirming tangency.

-------------------------------------------------
5.  Collect all admissible radii
-------------------------------------------------

The only radii obtained are

r = 9 and r = 41.

Both satisfy the requirement “there is at least one point of tangency”.

-------------------------------------------------
6.  Sum of the radii
-------------------------------------------------

9 + 41 = 50.
