Let  

A : “the roll is 1 or 2”  (Alice gets a coin)  
B : “the roll is 3 or 4”  (Bob gets a coin)  
C : “the roll is 5 or 6”  (Carol gets a coin)

Each event has probability  

P(A)=P(B)=P(C)=1/3.

We roll until the first time C occurs.  
The sequence that we see before this first C consists only of A’s and B’s.
We need the probability that, in this pre-C sequence, Alice and Bob have each
received at least two coins.

1.  Probability of a particular pre-C sequence  

If the sequence contains a A’s and b B’s (a+b=s) and is followed by the first C,
its probability is  

P = (1/3)^a (1/3)^b (1/3) = (1/3)^{a+b+1}.

2.  Counting the favourable sequences  

For fixed a≥2 and b≥2 the number of such sequences is C_{a+b}^{a}.  
Hence the desired probability is  

P = Σ_{a=2}^{∞} Σ_{b=2}^{∞} C_{a+b}^{a} (1/3)^{a+b+1}.

3.  Re-index with s = a+b (s ≥ 4):

P = (1/3) Σ_{s=4}^{∞} (1/3)^{s} Σ_{a=2}^{s-2} C_{s}^{a}.

But  

Σ_{a=2}^{s-2} C_{s}^{a} = 2^{s} − (C_{s}^{0}+C_{s}^{1}+C_{s}^{s-1}+C_{s}^{s})
                       = 2^{s} − (1 + s + s + 1)
                       = 2^{s} − 2s − 2.

Therefore  

P = (1/3) Σ_{s=4}^{∞} (1/3)^{s} (2^{s} − 2s − 2).

4.  Evaluate the three series separately.

a) Σ_{s=4}^{∞} (2/3)^{s} = (2/3)^{4}/(1 − 2/3) = 16/27.

b) Σ_{s=4}^{∞} s (1/3)^{s}  
   The general sum Σ_{s=0}^{∞} s x^{s} = x/(1−x)^2.  
   For x = 1/3 it is 3/4.  
   Subtract the first four terms (s = 0,1,2,3):
   3/4 − (1/3 + 2/9 + 1/9) = 3/4 − 2/3 = 1/12.

c) Σ_{s=4}^{∞} (1/3)^{s} = (1/3)^{4}/(1 − 1/3) = 1/54.

Combine:

Σ_{s=4}^{∞} (1/3)^{s} (2^{s} − 2s − 2)
   = 16/27 − 2·(1/12) − 2·(1/54)
   = 16/27 − 1/6 − 1/27
   = 32/54 − 9/54 − 2/54
   = 21/54
   = 7/18.

5.  Multiply by the outer factor (1/3):

P = (1/3) · (7/18) = 7/54.

Thus the probability that Alice and Bob each obtain at least two coins before Carol gets any coin is 7/54 = m/n with m = 7, n = 54.

Finally,

100m + n = 100·7 + 54 = 700 + 54 = 754.

\boxed{754}

Let  

A = outcome 1 or 2  (probability 1/3)  
B = outcome 3 or 4  (probability 1/3)  
C = outcome 5 or 6  (probability 1/3)

We roll until the first time a C appears.  
All rolls before that C are only A’s and B’s.  
We need at least two A’s and at least two B’s before that first C.

1.  Let k be the number of A/B rolls before the first C.  
    Probability the first C occurs after exactly k A/B rolls:  
    (2/3)^k · (1/3).

2.  For a fixed k, the sequence of the first k rolls is a string of A’s and B’s, each with probability 1/2.  
    Let P_k be the chance that this string contains at least two A’s and at least two B’s.

    For k ≥ 4,
        P_k = 1 – 2·P(A’s ≤ 1)
             = 1 – 2[(k choose 0)+(k choose 1)](1/2)^k
             = 1 – 2(1+k)(1/2)^k.

3.  Total desired probability

    P = Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (2/3)^k (1/3) [1 – 2(1+k)(1/2)^k]  
      = (1/3) Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (2/3)^k – (1/3) Σ_{k=4}^{∞} 2(1+k)(1/3)^k.

    a) Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (2/3)^k  
       = (2/3)^4 / (1 – 2/3)  
       = 3·(2/3)^4  
       = 16/27.

    b) Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (1+k)(1/3)^k.  
       Total Σ_{k=0}^{∞} (1+k)x^k = 1/(1–x)^2.  
       With x = 1/3, this is 9/4.  
       Subtract k = 0,1,2,3 terms:  
       1 + 2/3 + 1/3 + 4/27 = 58/27.  
       Hence Σ_{k=4}^{∞}(1+k)(1/3)^k = 9/4 – 58/27 = 11/108.

       Therefore Σ_{k=4}^{∞} 2(1+k)(1/3)^k = 2·11/108 = 11/54.

    c) P = (1/3)(16/27 – 11/54)  
          = (1/3)(32/54 – 11/54)  
          = (1/3)(21/54)  
          = 7/54.

Thus the probability is 7/54 = m/n with m = 7, n = 54, and

100m + n = 100·7 + 54 = 754.

\boxed{754}

Let 

A = outcome 1 or 2 (Alice gets a coin)  
B = outcome 3 or 4 (Bob gets a coin)  
C = outcome 5 or 6 (Carol gets a coin)  

Each roll gives A, B, C with probabilities 1/3, 1/3, 1/3 respectively.

Stop the experiment the first time C appears.  
Let L be the number of rolls before that first C.  
Then  P(L = k) = (2/3)^k (1/3), k = 0,1,2,…

Conditioned on “not C”, each roll is equally likely to be A or B, so given L = k the k pre-C rolls are independent A/B with probability 1/2 each.

We want the probability that, before the first C, both Alice and Bob have obtained at least two coins.  
That is, with those k rolls we need at least 2 A’s and at least 2 B’s.  
For k < 4 this is impossible; for k ≥ 4:

P(“at least 2 A and 2 B” | L = k)  
= 1 − 2·P(#A ≤ 1)  (the events “#A ≤ 1” and “#B ≤ 1” cannot both happen when k ≥ 3)  
= 1 − 2[(C(k,0)+C(k,1)) (1/2)^k]  
= 1 − 2(1 + k)/2^k.

Define f(k) = 1 − 2(1 + k)/2^k for k ≥ 4, and f(k) = 0 for k ≤ 3.

Overall probability

P = Σ_{k=0}^{∞} P(L = k) · f(k)  
  = (1/3) Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (2/3)^k [1 − 2(1 + k)/2^k].

Rewrite (2/3)^k = 2^k/3^k :

P = (1/3) Σ_{k=4}^{∞} [2^k/3^k − 2(1 + k)/3^k]  
  = (1/3)[ Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (2/3)^k − 2 Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (1 + k)/3^k ].

1. Geometric part: Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (2/3)^k  
   = (2/3)^4 / (1 − 2/3) = 16/27.

2. Arithmetic-geometric part: Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (1 + k)/3^k.  
   Using Σ_{k=0}^{∞} (1 + k)x^k = 1/(1 − x)^2, at x = 1/3:

   Total Σ_{k=0}^{∞} (1 + k)/3^k = 1/(1 − 1/3)^2 = 9/4.  
   Subtract first four terms k = 0..3:

   (1) + 2/3 + 1/3 + 4/27 = 58/27.

   Hence Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (1 + k)/3^k = 9/4 − 58/27 = 11/108.

Therefore

P = (1/3)[ 16/27 − 2·(11/108) ]  
  = (1/3)[ 16/27 − 11/54 ]  
  = (1/3)(7/18)  
  = 7/54.

The reduced fraction is m/n = 7/54.

Compute 100m + n:

100·7 + 54 = 700 + 54 = 754.

\boxed{754}

Let  
p(i , j) = probability of ultimately succeeding (both Alice and Bob get ≥2 coins before Carol gets any) when Alice currently has i coins and Bob has j coins, with i , j capped at 2 ( “2” means “2 or more”).

At every roll:
• Alice’s coin (faces 1–2) occurs with probability 1/3.  
• Bob’s coin (faces 3–4) occurs with probability 1/3.  
• Carol’s coin (faces 5–6) occurs with probability 1/3 and ends the game in failure unless the goal has already been met.

Thus, for any non-terminal state (i , j) with i<2 or j<2,

 p(i , j) = ⅓ p(i+1 , j) + ⅓ p(i , j+1) + ⅓·0. (1)

If i ≥ 2 and j ≥ 2, the goal is already met, so

 p(2 , 2) = 1. (2)

Because counts above 2 don’t matter, the distinct states are

(0,0), (1,0)=(0,1), (1,1), (2,0)=(0,2), (2,1)=(1,2), (2,2).

Let  
A = p(0,0)  
B = p(1,0) = p(0,1)  
C = p(1,1)  
D = p(2,0) = p(0,2)  
E = p(2,1) = p(1,2)  
S = p(2,2) = 1.

Write the equations given by (1) and symmetry:

1. A = ⅓ B + ⅓ B = ⅔ B.  
2. B = ⅓ D + ⅓ C.  
3. C = ⅓ E + ⅓ E = ⅔ E.  
4. For state (2,0): D = ⅓ D + ⅓ E  ⇒ 2D = E.  
5. For state (2,1): E = ⅓ E + ⅓ S = ⅓ E + ⅓ ⇒ 2E = 1  ⇒ E = ½.

Now back-substitute:

E = ½.  
D = E/2 = ¼.  
C = (⅔)E = 1/3.  
B = ⅓(D + C) = ⅓(¼ + 1/3) = ⅓(7/12) = 7/36.  
A = (⅔)B = (⅔)(7/36) = 7/54.

We started in state (0,0), so the desired probability is p(0,0) = 7/54.

m = 7, n = 54, 100m + n = 700 + 54 = 754.

Let the rolls preceding the first 5 or 6 be counted; the process stops as soon as a 5 or 6 (Carol’s first coin) appears.

1.  The number of rolls before the first 5 or 6 is geometric with parameter 1/3.  
   Thus P(L = ℓ) = (2/3)^ℓ · (1/3),  ℓ = 0,1,2,…

2.  Given that no 5 or 6 has appeared yet, each roll is equally likely to be  
   A-type (1 or 2) or B-type (3 or 4), each with probability 1/2.

3.  For a fixed length ℓ, the probability that both A and B have at least two coins is  
   Pℓ = ∑_{k=2}^{ℓ-2} C(ℓ,k) (1/2)^ℓ  
      = 2^ℓ − [1 + ℓ + ℓ + 1] (all possibilities minus k = 0,1,ℓ-1,ℓ)  
      = 2^ℓ − 2 − 2ℓ.

4.  The required probability is  

   P = Σ_{ℓ=4}^{∞} (2/3)^ℓ (1/3) [2^ℓ − 2 − 2ℓ]  
     = Σ_{ℓ=4}^{∞} (1/3)^{ℓ+1} [2^ℓ − 2 − 2ℓ].

   Separate the sums:

   S₁ = Σ_{ℓ=4}^{∞} (1/3)^{ℓ+1} 2^ℓ  
       = (1/3) Σ_{ℓ=4}^{∞} (2/3)^ℓ  
       = (1/3) · (2/3)^4 / (1 − 2/3) = 16/81.

   S₂ = Σ_{ℓ=4}^{∞} (1/3)^{ℓ+1} = 1/162.

   S₃ = Σ_{ℓ=4}^{∞} ℓ (1/3)^{ℓ+1}.  
   Using Σ_{ℓ=0}^{∞} ℓ r^{ℓ+1} = r²/(1−r)² with r = 1/3, this sum is 1/36.

   Hence  
   P = S₁ − 2S₂ − 2S₃  
     = 16/81 − 1/81 − 1/18  
     = 7/54.

5.  With m = 7 and n = 54, 100m + n = 700 + 54 = 754.

\boxed{754}

Let the rolls continue until the first time a 5 or 6 appears.  
That roll ends the game; before it occurs only the numbers 1–4 can appear.

1.  Probability model for the length of the pre-terminating string  
   • Each roll is a 5 or 6 with probability 2/6 = 1/3.  
   • Therefore the number N of rolls that occur before the first 5 or 6 is geometric with  
     P(N = k) = (2/3)^{k}·(1/3), k = 0,1,2,…

2.  Distribution of the rolls 1–4 within that string  
   Conditional on being in the set {1,2,3,4}, each roll is equally likely to be  
   1 or 2 (Alice) or 3 or 4 (Bob).  
   Hence for the k rolls preceding the first 5/6,
     A ~ Binomial(k, ½), B = k – A.

3.  Desired event  
   We want A ≥ 2 and B ≥ 2.  
   For fixed k ≥ 4,
     P(A ≥ 2 and B ≥ 2 | N = k) = 1 – P(A ≤ 1) – P(B ≤ 1)
                                = 1 – 2[(1/2)^{k} + k(1/2)^{k}]
                                = 1 – 2(1 + k)·(1/2)^{k}.

4.  Combine over all possible k ≥ 4  
   P = Σ_{k=4}^{∞} P(N = k) · P(A ≥ 2 , B ≥ 2 | N = k)

   = Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (2/3)^{k}(1/3) [1 – 2(1 + k)(1/2)^{k}]

   = (1/3) S₁  –  (2/3) S₂,  where  
     S₁ = Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (2/3)^{k},  
     S₂ = Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (2/3)^{k}(1 + k)(1/2)^{k} = Σ_{k=4}^{∞} (1/3)^{k}(1 + k).

   Compute S₁:  
     S₁ = (2/3)^{4} / (1 – 2/3) = (16/81)/(1/3) = 16/27.

   Compute S₂:  
     Let r = 1/3.  
     Σ_{k=4}^{∞} r^{k} = r^{4}/(1 – r) = 1/54.  
     Σ_{k=4}^{∞} k r^{k} = [r/(1 – r)^{2}] – Σ_{k=1}^{3} k r^{k}  
                         = 3/4 – (1/3 + 2/9 + 1/9)  
                         = 3/4 – 2/3 = 1/12.  
     Hence S₂ = 1/54 + 1/12 = 11/108.

   Now
     P = (1/3)·S₁ – 2·S₂·(1/3)  
       = (1/3)(16/27) – 2(11/108)(1/3)  
       = 16/81 – 11/162  
       = 32/162 – 11/162  
       = 21/162  
       = 7/54.

So the required probability is 7/54.  
With m = 7 and n = 54, we have 100m + n = 700 + 54 = 754.

\boxed{754}

Let A, B, C denote respectively the events “Alice gets a coin”, “Bob gets a coin”, “Carol gets a coin”.
Each roll of the die gives A, B, C with equal probability 1/3.

Stop the experiment at the first time Carol (C) appears.
Let N be the number of rolls before this first C.  
Then N has the geometric distribution  
P(N = n) = (2/3)^n (1/3), n = 0,1,2,…

Conditioned on N = n, the n rolls consist only of A’s and B’s, each with probability 1/2.  
We need both Alice and Bob to have at least two coins before the first C, i.e.  
(number of A’s ≥ 2) and (number of B’s ≥ 2) in those n rolls.

1.  Probability that the requirement is met for a fixed n (n ≥ 3)

Failure occurs if A’s ≤ 1 or B’s ≤ 1.  
For one side (say A’s) the probability is

P(A ≤ 1) = P(A = 0) + P(A = 1) = (1/2)^n + n(1/2)^n = (1+n)/2^n.

For n ≥ 3 the events “A ≤ 1” and “B ≤ 1” cannot both occur, so

f(n) = 1 − 2·P(A ≤ 1) = 1 − 2(1+n)/2^n
     = (2^n − 2 − 2n)/2^n, n ≥ 3.

For n = 0,1,2,3 we have f(n)=0, so the first useful value is n = 4.

2.  Overall desired probability

P = Σ_{n=4}^{∞} (1/3)(2/3)^n f(n)
  = (1/3) Σ_{n=4}^{∞} (2/3)^n (2^n − 2 − 2n)/2^n
  = (1/3) Σ_{n=4}^{∞} (1/3^n)(2^n − 2 − 2n)
  = (1/3)(S₁ − S₂ − S₃),

where
S₁ = Σ_{n=4}^{∞} (2/3)^n,
S₂ = Σ_{n=4}^{∞} 2/3^n,
S₃ = Σ_{n=4}^{∞} 2n/3^n.

Compute the sums:

S₁ = (2/3)^4 / (1 − 2/3) = 16/27,
S₂ = 2·[ (1/3)^4 / (1 − 1/3) ] = 1/27,
For S₃ let T = Σ_{n=0}^{∞} n/3^n = x/(1−x)^2 at x = 1/3 ⇒ T = 3/4.
Subtract the first three terms (n = 1,2,3): 1/3 + 2/9 + 1/9 = 2/3,
so Σ_{n=4}^{∞} n/3^n = 3/4 − 2/3 = 1/12,
hence S₃ = 2·(1/12) = 1/6.

Therefore
S₁ − S₂ − S₃ = 16/27 − 1/27 − 1/6 = 5/9 − 1/6 = 7/18,

and
P = (1/3)(7/18) = 7/54.

Thus the required probability is 7/54 = m/n with m = 7, n = 54 (coprime).

Compute 100m + n:

100·7 + 54 = 700 + 54 = 754.

\boxed{754}

Let  

f(a,b) = probability that Alice and Bob will each reach 2 coins before the first coin is given to Carol  
given that Alice already has a coins and Bob has b coins (where a,b ∈ {0,1,2} and 2 means “at least 2”).

If Carol gets a coin next, the game stops and the attempt fails.  
If Alice gets the next coin, a increases by 1 (capped at 2).  
If Bob gets the next coin, b increases by 1 (capped at 2).

Because each roll gives a coin to Alice, Bob or Carol with equal probability 1/3, for every non-terminal state (a,b)

  f(a,b) = (1/3)·0 + (1/3)·f(min(a+1,2), b) + (1/3)·f(a, min(b+1,2)).

The success state is (2,2) with f(2,2)=1.

We solve the system, working backward from (2,2).

1. State (2,1):
   f(2,1)= (1/3)·f(2,1) + (1/3)·1 ⇒ (2/3)f(2,1)=1/3 ⇒ f(2,1)=1/2.  
   By symmetry, f(1,2)=1/2.

2. State (2,0):
   f(2,0)= (1/3)·f(2,0) + (1/3)·f(2,1)  
   ⇒ (2/3)f(2,0)= (1/3)·(1/2)=1/6 ⇒ f(2,0)=1/4.  
   Thus f(0,2)=1/4.

3. State (1,1):
   f(1,1)= (1/3)·f(2,1) + (1/3)·f(1,2)  
   = (1/3)·(1/2)+(1/3)·(1/2)=1/3.

4. State (1,0):
   f(1,0)= (1/3)·f(2,0) + (1/3)·f(1,1)  
   = (1/3)·(1/4)+(1/3)·(1/3)=1/12+1/9=7/36.  
   Hence f(0,1)=7/36.

5. Starting state (0,0):
   f(0,0)= (1/3)·f(1,0)+ (1/3)·f(0,1)  
   = (1/3)·(7/36)+(1/3)·(7/36)=2·(7/108)=7/54.

Therefore the probability that Alice and Bob each receive at least two coins before Carol receives any is 7/54 = m/n with m=7, n=54.

Compute 100m+n: 100·7+54 = 700+54 = 754.

Let  

AB = BC = s , AC = b, and I = incenter of ΔABC.  
Put P₁ = perimeter(ΔABC) = 2s + b,  
  P₂ = perimeter(ΔAIC) = AI + CI + AC = 2AI + b.  

Given P₁ : P₂ = 125 : 6 ⇒ (2s + b)/(2AI + b) = 125/6.     (1)

------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Express AI in terms of s and b
------------------------------------------------------------
For an angle-bisector segment from a vertex to the incenter one has  

AI = r / sin(A/2),

where r is the inradius.

In ΔABC (with a = s, b = AC = b, c = s)

cos A = (s² + b² – s²)/(2sb) = b/(2s).

Hence  
sin(A/2) = √[(1 – cos A)/2] = √[(2s – b)/(4s)] = √(2s – b)/(2√s).

The inradius is  

r = Δ / (semiperimeter)  
  = ( (b/4)√(4s² – b²) ) / ( (2s + b)/2 )  
  = b√(4s² – b²) / [2(2s + b)].

Since 4s² – b² = (2s – b)(2s + b),

r = b√(2s – b)√(2s + b) / [2(2s + b)].

Therefore  

AI = r / sin(A/2)  
    = b√s / √(2s + b).      (2)

Because ΔAIC must have integer sides, AI must be an integer.

------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Use the ratio of perimeters
------------------------------------------------------------
From (1):

6(2s + b) = 125(2AI + b)
⇒ 12s – 119b = 250AI.                                     (3)

Insert (2) into (3):

12s – 119b = 250· b√s / √(2s + b).

Square and simplify; writing x = s / b gives

(12x – 119)²(2x + 1) = 62 500 x.                        (4)

Equation (4) is a cubic in x:

288x³ − 5 568x² − 37 034x + 14 161 = 0.

By rational–root testing one finds the only positive root larger than 119/12 is

x = 49/2 = 24.5.

Hence every solution must satisfy

s : b = 49 : 2.                                          (5)

Set b = 2k (k ∈ ℕ).  
Then s = 49k.

------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Make AI an integer
------------------------------------------------------------
From (2) with b = 2k, s = 49k:

AI² = (b² s)/(2s + b)
     = (4k²·49k)/(98k + 2k)
     = 196k³ / (100k)
     = 49k² / 25,

so AI = 7k/5.

For AI to be integral we need 5 | k ⇒ k = 5m, m ∈ ℕ.

The smallest such k is k = 5, giving

b = 2k = 10, s = 49k = 245, AI = 7.

------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Verification
------------------------------------------------------------
ΔABC: sides 245, 245, 10 ⇒ perimeter 2·245 + 10 = 500.  
ΔAIC: sides 7, 7, 10 ⇒ perimeter 7 + 7 + 10 = 24.

500 : 24 = 125 : 6, as required, and all sides are integers.

No smaller s is possible because (5) fixes the ratio s : b and AI is integral only when k is a multiple of 5.

Therefore the minimal value of AB is

\boxed{245}.

Write the sum in a more convenient form.

1 ⁄ (10ⁿ − 1) = 1⁄10ⁿ · 1⁄(1 − 10⁻ⁿ)  
                 = 10⁻ⁿ (1 + 10⁻ⁿ + 10⁻²ⁿ + ⋯)  
                 = ∑_{k=1}^{∞} 10^{-nk}

Hence  

S = ∑_{n=1}^{∞} 1/(10ⁿ − 1) = ∑_{n=1}^{∞} ∑_{k=1}^{∞} 10^{-nk}  
  = ∑_{m=1}^{∞} d(m)·10^{-m},

where d(m) is the number of positive divisors of m (because each divisor n of m gives the pair (n,k) with k = m/n).

Multiply by 10¹⁰⁰:

10¹⁰⁰ S = ∑_{m=1}^{∞} d(m) · 10^{100-m}  
        = ∑_{m=1}^{100} d(m)·10^{100-m}  +  ∑_{m=101}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100-m}  
        = I + F.

The integer part
I = ∑_{m=1}^{100} d(m)·10^{100-m}
is an integer, while the fractional part
F = ∑_{m=101}^{∞} d(m)·10^{100-m}
satisfies 0 < F < 0.3 (computing the first few terms gives 0.282884… and the remaining terms contribute less than 0.000001).  
Thus ⎣10¹⁰⁰ S⎦ = I.

To find I mod 1000 we need only the terms that survive mod 1000, i.e. those multiplied by 10^0, 10¹, 10²:

I ≡ d(100)·10⁰ + d(99)·10¹ + d(98)·10² (mod 1000).

Compute the divisor counts:
• 100 = 2²·5²  ⇒ d(100) = (2+1)(2+1) = 9  
•  99 = 3²·11   ⇒ d(99)  = (2+1)(1+1) = 6  
•  98 = 2·7²    ⇒ d(98)  = (1+1)(2+1) = 6

Therefore  
I ≡ 9 + 6·10 + 6·100 = 9 + 60 + 600 = 669  (mod 1000).

Since the fractional part F is less than 1, no carry occurs, and

⎣10¹⁰⁰ S⎦ ≡ 669  (mod 1000).

\boxed{669}

Write every term of the series with a geometric-series expansion.

1/(10^n-1)=10^{-n}/(1-10^{-n})=\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}10^{-nm}

Hence  

S=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{10^{n}-1}
  =\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}10^{-nm}
  =\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}d(k)\,10^{-k},

where d(k) is the number of positive divisors of k (because the pair (n,m) with nm=k contributes once).

Multiply by 10^{100}:

10^{100}S=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}d(k)\,10^{100-k}
         =\Bigl(\sum_{k=1}^{100}d(k)\,10^{100-k}\Bigr)
          +\Bigl(\sum_{k=101}^{\infty}d(k)\,10^{100-k}\Bigr).

The second parenthesis is less than  

∑_{j=1}^{\infty}d(100+j)10^{-j}
<∑_{j=1}^{\infty}(100+j)^{1/2}10^{-j}<1,

so it is the fractional part. Thus  

⌊10^{100}S⌋ = ∑_{k=1}^{100}d(k)10^{100-k}.

To find this integer modulo 1000, only the terms with 100-k<3 (i.e. k=98,99,100) matter, because 10^{100-k} is a multiple of 1000 when k≤97.

Compute the needed divisor counts:

98 = 2·7^2  ⇒ d(98) = (1+1)(2+1) = 6  
99 = 3^2·11 ⇒ d(99) = (2+1)(1+1) = 6  
100 = 2^2·5^2 ⇒ d(100)= (2+1)(2+1) = 9  

Now

d(98)·10^{2} + d(99)·10^{1} + d(100)·10^{0}
= 6·100 + 6·10 + 9
= 600 + 60 + 9
= 669.

Therefore  

⌊10^{100}S⌋ ≡ 669 (mod 1000).

Therefore B < 0.28 + 0.03 = 0.31 < 1, so floor(B)=0 and B contributes no carry.

floor(10¹⁰⁰ S) ≡ A + floor(B) ≡ 669 + 0 ≡ 669  (mod 1000).

Let  

AB = 200 , AC = 225 , BC = a , BD = p , CD = q ,

and let  

AE = x , AF = y .

1.  Angle–bisector theorem  
   Since AD bisects ∠BAC,

  BD / DC = AB / AC = 200 / 225 = 8 / 9.

   Write BD = 8k , CD = 9k  (k > 0).  
   Hence  BC = BD + CD = 8k + 9k = 17k.  (1)

2.  Power of a point – point B  
   The line BC is tangent to ω at D, so BD is a tangent segment.  
   For point B,

  BD² = BA · BE  
  (8k)² = 200 · (200 – x).

   Thus
  64k² = 200(200 – x) ⇒ 200 – x = 64k² / 200 = 8k² / 25  
  x = 200 – 8k² / 25.  (2)

3.  Power of a point – point C  
   Similarly, for point C,

  CD² = CA · CF  
  (9k)² = 225 · (225 – y).

   Thus
  81k² = 225(225 – y) ⇒ 225 – y = 81k² / 225 = 9k² / 25  
  y = 225 – 9k² / 25.  (3)

4.  Integrality conditions  
   x = AE, y = AF, BD, CD are given to be positive integers, so the right–hand
   sides of (2) and (3) must be integers.  
   Because gcd(8, 25) = gcd(9, 25) = 1, this forces 25 | k², i.e. k = 5m with m ∈ ℕ.

   Substituting k = 5m gives  

  BD = 8k = 40m, CD = 9k = 45m, BC = 17k = 85m,  

  AE = 200 – 8m², AF = 225 – 9m².  (4)

5.  Positivity of AE and AF  
   AE > 0  ⇒  200 – 8m² > 0  ⇒  m² < 25.  
   AF > 0  ⇒  225 – 9m² > 0  ⇒  m² < 25.

   Hence m ≤ 4.  The possible values are m = 1, 2, 3, 4, giving

  BC = 85m ∈ {85, 170, 255, 340}.

6.  Triangle inequality  
   For m = 4, BC = 340 and

  AB + AC = 425 > 340, AB + BC = 540 > 225, AC + BC = 565 > 200,

   so a triangle with sides 200, 225, 340 exists.  
   Since BC grows with m, m = 4 yields the greatest possible BC.

Therefore the maximum possible length of BC is

\boxed{340}.

Let the angle-bisector of ∠BAC meet BC at D.  
Because AD is an angle bisector,

 BD / CD = AB / AC = 200 / 225 = 8 / 9.

Hence set BD = 8k, CD = 9k ⇒ BC = 17k, k > 0.  (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Using the circle tangent at D  
The circle ω through A is tangent to BC at D, so for any point on BC the power with respect to ω equals the square of the tangent segment.  
For B (whose tangent point is D) and the secant BA meeting ω again at E we have

 BD² = BA · BE.     (2)

Similarly, for C and the secant CA meeting ω again at F,

 CD² = CA · CF.     (3)

Put BE = AB – AE = 200 – AE and CF = AC – AF = 225 – AF.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Solving for AE  
From (2) with BD = 8k:

 (8k)² = 200(200 – AE)
 64k² = 200(200 – AE)

Divide by 8: 25(200 – AE) = 8k²  
Rewrite AE = 8m (because the left side must be divisible by 8). Then

 25(200 – 8m) = 8k²  
 5000 – 200m = 8k²  
Divide by 8: 625 – 25m = k²  
 k² = 25(25 – m) ⇒ k = 5√(25 – m).

Thus 25 – m must be a perfect square.  Let 25 – m = s², where s = 1,2,3,4 (s = 0 would give AE = 200, not allowed). Then

 m = 25 – s², AE = 8m = 8(25 – s²), k = 5s.  (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Solving for AF  
Using (3) with CD = 9k:

 (9k)² = 225(225 – AF)
 81k² = 225(225 – AF)

Divide by 9: 9k² = 25(225 – AF)  
Divide by 25 and substitute k² = 25s²:

 225 – AF = (9/25)k² = 9s²
 AF = 225 – 9s².  (5)

For s = 1,2,3,4 we obtain positive integers AE, AF:

s = 1:  AE = 192, AF = 216, k = 5
s = 2:  AE = 168, AF = 189, k = 10
s = 3:  AE = 128, AF = 144, k = 15
s = 4:  AE =  72, AF =  81, k = 20

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Computing BC  
From (1) and k = 5s:

 BC = 17k = 17·5s = 85s.

Hence
s = 1 ⇒ BC =  85  
s = 2 ⇒ BC = 170  
s = 3 ⇒ BC = 255  
s = 4 ⇒ BC = 340

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Verifying the largest possibility  
For s = 4 we have integers

 BD = 8k = 8·20 = 160, CD = 9k = 180,
 AE = 72, AF = 81,

all satisfying the required relations, so this case is achievable.  
No larger s is allowed (s = 5 would give AE = AF = 0).

Therefore the greatest possible length of BC is

\boxed{340}.

By the Angle-Bisector Theorem  

  BD : DC = AB : AC = 200 : 225 = 8 : 9.

Hence we can write  
BD = 8t , DC = 9t , BC = BD + DC = 17t  (t is a positive integer).  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Using the tangent–secant (power of a point) relations  
for the circle ω through A and tangent to BC at D:

 At B  BD² = BA · BE  ⇒  BE = BD² / BA = (8t)² / 200 = 8t² / 25  
 At C  CD² = CA · CF  ⇒  CF = CD² / CA = (9t)² / 225 = 9t² / 25.

Because A, E, B are collinear and A lies between B and E,

 AE = AB – BE = 200 – 8t² / 25 = (5000 – 8t²)/25.

Similarly,

 AF = AC – CF = 225 – 9t² / 25 = (5625 – 9t²)/25.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Integer and positivity conditions  

1. AE and AF must be positive integers.  
 Since both numerators must be divisible by 25,

  5000 – 8t² ≡ 0 (mod 25) and 5625 – 9t² ≡ 0 (mod 25)  
 ⇔ t² ≡ 0 (mod 25) ⇔ t ≡ 0 (mod 5).

2. Positivity: 5000 – 8t² > 0 and 5625 – 9t² > 0  
 ⇒ t² < 625 ⇒ t ≤ 24.

Thus the admissible values of t are 5, 10, 15, 20.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Compute BC for each admissible t  

t = 5 → BC = 17·5 = 85  
t = 10 → BC = 17·10 = 170  
t = 15 → BC = 17·15 = 255  
t = 20 → BC = 17·20 = 340

Each of these gives

 AE = {192, 168, 128, 72} , AF = {216, 189, 144, 81} ,

all positive integers, and the triangle inequality is satisfied
(BC < 200 + 225 = 425).

The largest possible BC occurs when t = 20.

Step 1.  Set up notation  
Let  
r₁ = α² , r₂ = β² , r₃ = γ²                        (the three roots of the given cubic)  
s₁ = √r₁ , s₂ = √r₂ , s₃ = √r₃               (choose one square–root for every rᵢ).

For every triple (ε₁,ε₂,ε₃) with εᵢ = ±1 we obtain a possible value of  
α+β+γ = ε₁s₁ + ε₂s₂ + ε₃s₃ .                 (1)

Altogether there are 8 expressions (1).  
We are told that exactly seven different values occur, i.e. two of the 8 sums coincide and no other repetitions appear.

Step 2.  When do two different sign–patterns give the same sum?  
Assume (ε₁,ε₂,ε₃) ≠ (ε₁',ε₂',ε₃'), but
ε₁s₁ + ε₂s₂ + ε₃s₃ = ε₁'s₁ + ε₂'s₂ + ε₃'s₃ .    (2)

Subtracting, (ε₁-ε₁')s₁ + (ε₂-ε₂')s₂ + (ε₃-ε₃')s₃ = 0,  
where every coefficient εᵢ-εᵢ' equals 0 or ±2.

•  If the two patterns differ in exactly one coordinate, (2) forces a square–root to be 0 – that would make at least 4 sums coincide, contradicting “exactly seven”.

•  If they differ in two coordinates, (2) gives sᵢ = ±sⱼ, which again causes at least two distinct pairs of equalities, so fewer than 7 distinct sums appear.

•  Therefore the only admissible way to have a single coincidence is that the two patterns differ in all three coordinates, i.e. (ε₁,ε₂,ε₃) = (1,1,1) and (–1,–1,–1).  
Equation (2) with those patterns reduces to  

s₁ + s₂ + s₃ = 0.                                      (3)

When (3) holds, the sums coming from (+++) and (---) are both 0, while the other six sums are pairwise distinct provided the sᵢ are non–zero and no two of them are ±-equal.  
Thus condition (3) is both necessary and sufficient for having exactly seven distinct values.

Hence we must (and shall) impose  
s₁ + s₂ + s₃ = 0.                                      (4)

Step 3.  Express the cubic’s coefficients through the sᵢ  
Write the elementary symmetric sums in s₁,s₂,s₃:

σ₁ = s₁ + s₂ + s₃ = 0                       (by (4))  
σ₂ = s₁s₂ + s₂s₃ + s₃s₁  
σ₃ = s₁s₂s₃ .

Because rᵢ = sᵢ², the symmetric sums of the roots r₁,r₂,r₃ are

r₁ + r₂ + r₃ = s₁² + s₂² + s₃²
              = σ₁² – 2σ₂ = –2σ₂,                       (5)

r₁r₂ + r₂r₃ + r₃r₁ = (s₁s₂)² + (s₂s₃)² + (s₃s₁)²
                    = σ₂²,                                (6)

r₁r₂r₃ = (s₁s₂s₃)² = σ₃² .                               (7)

Step 4.  Match with the given polynomial  
The cubic in the statement is

x³ – (n/6)x² + (n – 11)x – 400.

Therefore

r₁ + r₂ + r₃ = n/6,                                    (8)
r₁r₂ + r₂r₃ + r₃r₁ = n – 11,                           (9)
r₁r₂r₃ = 400.                                         (10)

Using (5)–(7) and (8)–(10):

–2σ₂ = n/6        ⇒        n = –12σ₂,                (11)
σ₂²  = n – 11,                                        (12)
σ₃²  = 400             ⇒      σ₃ = ±20.               (13)

Step 5.  Determine σ₂ and n  
Insert (11) into (12):

σ₂² = (–n/12)² = n²/144  = n – 11  
⟹  n² – 144n + 1584 = 0  
⟹  (n – 12)(n – 132) = 0.

Hence n = 12  or  n = 132.                          (14)

Step 6.  Admissibility of the two values  
For each candidate we must make sure that suitable s₁,s₂,s₃ fulfilling (4), (11) and (13) exist and that they are all distinct and non-zero (so that no extra equalities among the 8 sums appear).

A triple with σ₁ = 0, σ₂ given by (11) and σ₃ = 20, say, is a root set of the cubic
z³ + σ₂z + σ₃ = 0.

Its discriminant is Δ = –4σ₂³ – 27σ₃².

•  If n = 132, then σ₂ = –11.  
Δ = –4(–11)³ – 27·20² = –4(–1331) – 10800 = –5476 ≠ 0.

•  If n = 12, then σ₂ = –1.  
Δ = –4(–1)³ – 27·20² = –4(–1) – 10800 = –10796 ≠ 0.

Non-zero discriminant means the cubic has three distinct (complex) roots, all non-zero; moreover σ₁ = 0 rules out any two of them being negatives of each other.  
Therefore both n = 12 and n = 132 indeed give exactly seven different values of α+β+γ.

Step 7.  Choose the greatest integer  
Among the admissible integers, the greatest one is 132.

\boxed{132}

Let  

P(x)=x^{3}-\frac{n}{6}x^{2}+(n-11)x-400.

Let its roots be  

α^{2}, β^{2}, γ^{2}\;(α,β,γ\in\mathbb C).

For every choice of signs (ε1 ,ε2 ,ε3 )∈{±1}^{3} define  

S(ε1 ,ε2 ,ε3 ) = ε1 α + ε2 β + ε3 γ.

There are 8 such sign–patterns, so at most 8 distinct values of α+β+γ can occur.  
The problem says that exactly 7 different values appear; hence two patterns
give the same sum and the other six give different sums.

1.  When can two different patterns give the same sum?  
 If S(e)=S(f) with e≠f, then (e1 -f1)α+(e2 -f2)β+(e3 -f3)γ=0,  
 where every coefficient is 0 or ±2.
 Thus a non–trivial relation

  a1 α + a2 β + a3 γ = 0   (a1 ,a2 ,a3 ∈{0,±1})

 must hold.  
 If only one coordinate is non–zero, or exactly two are non–zero,
 then α=0, β=0, or α=±β, etc., and several further coincidences follow,
 giving ≤6 distinct sums, not 7.

 Therefore exactly one relation of the form

  α + β + γ = 0                        (1)

 may hold; then only the two patterns (+++) and (---) yield the same
sum (0), while the remaining six give different values, so we get 7
distinct sums.

2.  Impose (1).  Set

 s1 = α+β+γ = 0,  
 s2 = αβ+βγ+γα,  
 s3 = αβγ.

 Because the roots of P are α^{2},β^{2},γ^{2}, Vieta gives

 α^{2}+β^{2}+γ^{2}= n/6        (2)  
 α^{2}β^{2}+β^{2}γ^{2}+γ^{2}α^{2}= n-11 (3)  
 (αβγ)^{2}=400 ⇒ |s3|=20.             (4)

 From (1):

 0 = (α+β+γ)^{2}=α^{2}+β^{2}+γ^{2}+2s2
  ⇒ s2 = -\frac{1}{2}(α^{2}+β^{2}+γ^{2}) = -\frac{n}{12}.     (5)

 Also, because α+β+γ=0,

 α^{2}β^{2}+β^{2}γ^{2}+γ^{2}α^{2} = (αβ+βγ+γα)^{2} = s2^{2}.    (6)

 Substituting (5) into (6) and comparing with (3):

 n-11 = s2^{2} = \left(-\frac{n}{12}\right)^{2}=\frac{n^{2}}{144}.

 Hence

 n^{2}-144n+1584=0.

 Δ =144^{2}-4·1584 =20736-6336 =14400 =120^{2},

 so

 n = (144 ±120)/2 = 12  or  132.            (7)

3.  Verify that for these two values there are indeed exactly 7
different sums.

 •  Because |s3|=20, none of α,β,γ is 0.  
 •  If, say, α=β then γ=-2α and (4) gives (αβγ)= -2α^{3}=±20,
  which is impossible for either n=12 or n=132; similarly α=±β
  cannot occur.  
 Thus α,β,γ are pairwise unequal and not negatives of one another, so
 the only duplicate sums are produced by (+++) and (---).
 Consequently the eight patterns give exactly seven distinct values.

Both n=12 and n=132 satisfy the requirement; the greatest such integer
is therefore

\boxed{132}

Let the three (possibly complex) roots of  

f(x)=x^{3}-\frac{n}{6}x^{2}+(n-11)x-400  

be  

r_{1}=\alpha ^2,\; r_{2}=\beta ^2,\; r_{3}= \gamma ^2.

For every choice of the square-roots  
α=±√{r_1}, β=±√{r_2}, γ=±√{r_3} we obtain a value

S=α+β+γ = ε_{1}\sqrt{r_{1}}+ε_{2}\sqrt{r_{2}}+ε_{3}\sqrt{r_{3}},\qquad ε_{i}\in\{1,-1\}.      (1)

There are 8 sign–triples, hence at most 8 different sums S.
Because the problem states that exactly 7 different values occur, two different
sign–triples must give the same sum.
Subtracting the corresponding expressions in (1) shows that  
there must be a non–trivial relation of the form

±√{r_1} ± √{r_2} ± √{r_3}=0,                         (2)

in which every coefficient is non–zero (if only one or two signs differed we would get a single square-root equal to 0 or two equal roots, giving more coincidences than allowed).
Re-labelling the roots we may write

√{r_3}=√{r_1}+√{r_2}.                                 (3)

------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Express the elementary symmetric sums through two numbers  
    u=√{r_1}, v=√{r_2}.

Put  
u=√{r_1},\; v=√{r_2},\; w=u+v=√{r_3},\;
p=uv.

Then                                          
r_{1}=u^{2},\; r_{2}=v^{2},\; r_{3}=w^{2},\;
p=uv,\qquad pw=\pm 20\;( \because r_1r_2r_3=400).      (4)

The three elementary symmetric sums are

A=r_1+r_2+r_3 = 2(u^{2}+v^{2}+uv)=2(w^{2}-p),
B=r_1r_2+r_2r_3+r_3r_1 =(w^{2}-p)^{2},
C=r_1r_2r_3 = (pw)^{2}=400.                            (5)

------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Equate these with the coefficients of f(x).

Because f(x)=x^{3}-\frac{n}{6}x^{2}+(n-11)x-400,

A=\frac{n}{6},\; B=n-11,\; C=400.                      (6)

Using (5) and (6)

w^{2}-p=\frac{n}{12},\qquad B=(w^{2}-p)^{2}=\left(\frac{n}{12}\right)^{2}. 

Hence

n-11=\left(\frac{n}{12}\right)^{2}
\Longrightarrow n^{2}-144n+1584=0
\Longrightarrow n=12\quad\text{or}\quad n=132.         (7)

------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Show that each of these n really occurs and yields exactly 7 sums.

•  n=132  

   Equation (4) with the +-sign is w^{3}-11w-20=0,
   which has w=4 as a root.  
   Then p=w^{2}-\frac{n}{12}=16-11=5,\; pw=20.  
   The quadratic t^{2}-wt+p=0 gives u=2+i,\; v=2-i.

   The eight sums (1) are
   {±(2+i)±(2-i)±4}.  They take the seven distinct values  
   8,\;4+2i,\;4-2i,\;-4+2i,\;-4-2i,\;-8,\;0  
   – the value 0 occurring twice (when the signs are (1,1,-1) and (-1,-1,1)).  
   Thus exactly seven different values occur.

•  n=12  

   A completely parallel calculation (using w^{3}-w-20=0) also
   supplies suitable u and v and again produces seven distinct sums.
   Hence both 12 and 132 work.

------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Conclusion

Both integers 12 and 132 satisfy the required property, and the problem asks for the greatest such integer.

\boxed{132}

Let  

 p(x)=x^{3}-\dfrac n6\,x^{2}+(n-11)x-400  

and let its three roots be  

 α^{2}, β^{2}, γ^{2}\qquad(α,β,γ\in\mathbb C).
For every root r (≠0) there are exactly two square–roots ±√r, so from the eight possible triples (α,β,γ) we can obtain at most eight different values of  

 Σ(ε)=ε_{1}α+ε_{2}β+ε_{3}γ  with ε_{i}\in\{+1,-1\}.  

The problem says that exactly seven different values occur, i.e. two of the eight sums coincide.  
If Σ(ε)=Σ(ε') with ε≠ε' then

 (ε_{1}-ε'_{1})α+(ε_{2}-ε'_{2})β+(ε_{3}-ε'_{3})γ=0 .   

Each difference ε_{i}-ε'_{i} equals 0 or ±2, so dividing by 2 gives a relation

 k_{1}α+k_{2}β+k_{3}γ=0 with k_{i}\in\{-1,0,1\}, not all 0. (★)

If any k_{i}=0 in (★), two of the three numbers are opposite and their squares are equal, forcing multiple coincidences among the eight sums – we would get fewer than seven different values.  
Hence all k_{i} are non–zero and (up to an overall sign) the only possibility is

 α+β+γ=0.                                                         (1)

With (1) exactly the two choices (+α,+β,+γ) and (–α,–β,–γ) give the same sum 0, while the other six choices give distinct, non–zero sums, so the total number of different values is exactly seven.

Therefore condition (1) is necessary and sufficient.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––

Express the symmetric sums of α,β,γ in terms of n.

Let  

 s_{1}=α+β+γ=0,  
 s_{2}=αβ+βγ+γα,  
 s_{3}=αβγ .

Because α^{2},β^{2},γ^{2} are the roots of p(x),

1.  α^{2}+β^{2}+γ^{2}=σ_{1}=n/6,  
2.  α^{2}β^{2}+β^{2}γ^{2}+γ^{2}α^{2}=σ_{2}=n-11,  
3.  α^{2}β^{2}γ^{2}=σ_{3}=400.

Using s_{1}=0:

α^{2}+β^{2}+γ^{2}=(α+β+γ)^{2}-2s_{2}=-2s_{2}.  
Hence  s_{2}= -\dfrac{1}{2}\bigl(α^{2}+β^{2}+γ^{2}\bigr)= -\dfrac{n}{12}.          (2)

Further,

α^{2}β^{2}+β^{2}γ^{2}+γ^{2}α^{2}=(αβ+βγ+γα)^{2}-2αβγ(α+β+γ)=s_{2}^{2}.        (3)

Equating (3) with σ_{2} gives

 n-11 = s_{2}^{2}= \Bigl(-\dfrac{n}{12}\Bigr)^{2}= \dfrac{n^{2}}{144}.

Multiply by 144:

 n^{2}-144n+1584=0.

Solve:

 Δ =144^{2}-4·1584=14400=120^{2},

 n=\dfrac{144\pm120}{2}=12\quad\text{or}\quad132.               (4)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––

Existence of α,β,γ for n=12 and n=132  
For each value of n, set s_{2} from (2) and let s_{3}=±20 (because αβγ=s_{3} and σ_{3}=s_{3}^{2}=400).  
One readily produces concrete triples satisfying (1), s_{2}, s_{3}:

•  n=132: α=-2+i,\;β=-2-i,\;γ=4   ⇒ α+β+γ=0, αβ+βγ+γα=-11, αβγ=20.  
 Among the eight sign–choices the sums are  
 0, 4-2i, 4+2i, -8, 8, -4-2i, -4+2i; exactly 7 different values.

•  n=12:  a similar construction (take γ=4, α,β roots of t^{2}+t+1=0) also gives
 exactly seven different sums.

Hence both n=12 and n=132 satisfy the requirement; the problem asks for the
greatest such integer.

Final answer:

\boxed{132}

Let  

 P(x)=x^{3}-\dfrac{n}{6}x^{2}+(n-11)x-400

and let its roots be  

 α^{2}, β^{2}, γ^{2}.  

For every choice of signs ε1,ε2,ε3∈{±1} we can obtain

 α+β+γ = ε1√{α^{2}} + ε2√{β^{2}} + ε3√{γ^{2}} .

Hence at most 2^{3}=8 different values of α+β+γ are possible.  
The problem states that exactly seven of these eight values are distinct, so
there is a single repetition.  
The only way two different sign-choices give the same sum is when the sum is
0, because 0 = −0.  Thus one of the eight combinations must be 0, i.e. for
a suitable labelling of the roots

 √{α^{2}} + √{β^{2}} − √{γ^{2}} = 0 ⇒ √{γ^{2}} = √{α^{2}} + √{β^{2}} .

Write a = √{α^{2}}, b = √{β^{2}}.  Then

 α^{2}=a^{2}, β^{2}=b^{2}, γ^{2}=(a+b)^{2}.                         (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Express the symmetric sums in terms of a and b
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let s = a+b and p = ab.  From (1)

 R1 = a^{2}, R2 = b^{2}, R3 = s^{2}.

Sum of roots:
 R1+R2+R3 = a^{2}+b^{2}+s^{2}
      = 2(a^{2}+b^{2}+ab) = 2(s^{2}-p).

Pairwise sum of roots:
 R1R2 + R2R3 + R3R1 = a^{2}b^{2} + b^{2}s^{2} + s^{2}a^{2}
           = p^{2} + s^{2}(a^{2}+b^{2})
           = p^{2} + s^{2}(s^{2}-2p)
           = (s^{2}-p)^{2}.

Product of roots:
 R1R2R3 = a^{2}b^{2}s^{2} = p^{2}s^{2} = [ps]^{2}.             (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Apply Viète’s formulas
--------------------------------------------------------------------
From P(x):

 R1+R2+R3 = n/6,                   (3)  
 R1R2+R2R3+R3R1 = n-11,            (4)  
 R1R2R3 = 400.                     (5)

Insert the expressions just obtained.

(5) and (2): [ps]^{2} = 400 ⇒ ps = ±20.  
Choose ps = 20 (changing both a and b to −a,−b leaves the squares
unchanged, so the sign is irrelevant).

(3): 2(s^{2}-p) = n/6 ⇒ n = 12(s^{2}-p).                     (6)

(4): (s^{2}-p)^{2} = n-11.                                     (7)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Eliminate s and p
--------------------------------------------------------------------
From (6): s^{2}-p = n/12.  
Insert into (7):

 (n/12)^{2} = n - 11  
 ⇒ n^{2} - 144n + 1584 = 0  
 ⇒ (n-132)(n-12) = 0.

Hence n = 12 or n = 132.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Verify that both values are attainable
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For n = 132:  
 s^{2}-p = 132/12 = 11, ps = 20.  
 Solve p = 20/s ⇒ s^{3}-11s-20=0 ⇒ s = 4, p = 5.  
 Then a,b are the roots of t^{2}-4t+5 = 0, namely 2±i, so all three roots
a^{2}, b^{2}, (a+b)^{2} are distinct and non-zero; the eight sign–choices
produce the seven distinct sums described above.

For n = 12:  
 s^{2}-p = 1, ps = 20 ⇒ s^{3}-s-20=0.  
 This cubic has a real root s≈2.84, giving p=20/s≈7.04 and complex
conjugate a,b.  Again the conditions are satisfied and exactly seven
different values of α+β+γ arise.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Both n = 12 and n = 132 meet the requirement, and the problem asks for the
greatest such integer.

\boxed{132}

Let  

r₁ = α² , r₂ = β² , r₃ = γ²  

be the three roots of the cubic  

x³ − (n/6)x² + (n−11)x − 400 = 0.  (1)

1.  How can two different sign–choices give the same sum α+β+γ ?  
 For any fixed square–roots a = √r₁ , b = √r₂ , c = √r₃ the eight possible
 values of α+β+γ are  

  S(ε₁,ε₂,ε₃) = ε₁a + ε₂b + ε₃c  (εᵢ = ±1).

 If S(ε₁,ε₂,ε₃) = S(ε₁',ε₂',ε₃') with (ε₁,ε₂,ε₃) ≠ (ε₁',ε₂',ε₃'),
 then, after cancelling a factor 2, a non–empty signed subsum of a,b,c
 must vanish:

  ±a ± b ± c = 0.  (2)

 • If a = ±b (or b = ±c, c = ±a) then two different letters have the same
   square, hence two roots of (1) coincide – a repeated root.  
 • If a = 0 the cubic would possess the root 0.  
 • Otherwise the only possibility left is

  a + b + c = 0.  (3)

 Condition (3) gives exactly one coincidence among the eight sums,
 hence exactly seven distinct values.

 Conclusion: to have seven (and not fewer) distinct values we must have
 a + b + c = 0 while the three squares r₁,r₂,r₃ are pairwise different
 and non–zero.

2.  Translate (3) into symmetric relations.  
 Put p = ab + bc + ca and q = abc.  
 Because a + b + c = 0,

  r₁ + r₂ + r₃ = a² + b² + c²
         = (a + b + c)² − 2(ab + bc + ca)
         = −2p,                             (4)

  r₁r₂ + r₂r₃ + r₃r₁ = a²b² + b²c² + c²a²
             = (ab + bc + ca)² − 2abc(a + b + c)
             = p²,                          (5)

  r₁r₂r₃ = (abc)² = q².           (6)

3.  Match (4)–(6) with the coefficients of (1).  
 For the monic cubic (1)

  r₁ + r₂ + r₃ = n/6,                      
  r₁r₂ + r₂r₃ + r₃r₁ = n − 11,            
  r₁r₂r₃ = 400.                           

 So (4)–(6) give

  −2p = n/6  ⇒ p = −n/12,                    (7)  
  p² = n − 11.                                (8)

 Insert (7) in (8):

  (−n/12)² = n − 11  
  n²/144 = n − 11  
  n² − 144n + 1584 = 0  
  (n − 12)(n − 132) = 0.

 Hence n = 12 or n = 132.

 From (6) we also have q² = 400, therefore q = ±20; both signs are
 possible, so neither value of n is ruled out by the constant term.

4.  Do these two n really give seven different sums?

 a)  n = 132  
  The cubic is x³ − 22x² + 121x − 400 = (x−16)(x² − 6x + 25).  
  Its roots are 16, 3+4i, 3−4i.  
  Choose square–roots a = 4, b = 2+i, c = 2−i.  Then a = b + c, so
  a − b − c = 0, giving one (and only one) repetition:

  S(1,−1,−1) = S(−1,1,1) = 0,

  while the other six sign–choices give 4 ± 2i, −4 ± 2i, 8, −8 – seven
  different sums in all.

 b)  n = 12  
  The cubic x³ − 2x² + x − 400 has no repeated root (its derivative and
  it share none), so r₁,r₂,r₃ are distinct and non–zero.  
  Because (7)–(8) are satisfied, we still have a + b + c = 0; since no
  two squares coincide, there is no relation of the form a = ±b.  Hence
  again exactly one equality among the eight sums and therefore exactly
  seven distinct values.

5.  Required value.  
 Both n = 12 and n = 132 work, and no other integer can, so the greatest
 one is

\boxed{132}

Let the roots of  

x³ − (n/6)x² + (n−11)x − 400 = 0          (1)

be α² , β² , γ².  
Write  

A = √(α²) , B = √(β²) , C = √(γ²)

so that A² , B² , C² are the roots of (1).  
For every choice of signs ε₁,ε₂,ε₃ ∈ {±1}

S = ε₁A + ε₂B + ε₃C = α + β + γ

is a possible value of the sum of the square roots.  
There are at most 2³ = 8 such sums; we are told that exactly 7 of them are distinct,  
so two different sign–choices must give the same value.  
That happens iff

ε₁A + ε₂B + ε₃C = ε'₁A + ε'₂B + ε'₃C

with (ε₁,ε₂,ε₃) ≠ (ε'₁,ε'₂,ε'₃), i.e. if A,B,C satisfy one linear relation with coefficients ±1.  
Without loss of generality we may choose the signs so that  

A + B + C = 0.                                   (2)

(If another linear relation held as well, some of A,B,C would be 0 or two of them equal, giving fewer than 7 sums; we shall make sure that does not occur.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Express the coefficients of (1) by A,B,C
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because A² , B² , C² are the roots of (1),

A² + B² + C²        = n/6,                         (3)
A²B² + B²C² + C²A²  = n − 11,                     (4)
A²B²C²              = 400.                         (5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Use the condition A + B + C = 0
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put  

σ₁ = A + B + C = 0,  
σ₂ = AB + BC + CA,  
σ₃ = ABC.

Then

A² + B² + C² = σ₁² − 2σ₂ = −2σ₂,      so (3) gives  −2σ₂ = n/6 ⇒ σ₂ = −n/12.  (6)

Moreover

(AB + BC + CA)² = A²B² + B²C² + C²A² + 2ABC(A+B+C)
                 = A²B² + B²C² + C²A²        (because σ₁ = 0).

Hence, from (4) and (6),

n − 11 = (σ₂)² = (−n/12)² = n²/144.                      (7)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Find the possible n
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Multiply (7) by 144:

n² − 144n + 1584 = 0  
⇒ (n − 132)(n − 12) = 0  
⇒ n = 132   or   n = 12.                               (8)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Check that each candidate really yields seven distinct sums
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(i)  n = 132  

σ₂ = −132/12 = −11, σ₃² = 400 ⇒ σ₃ = ±20.

Take σ₃ = −20.  
Choose A = 2 + i, B = 2 − i, C = −4.  
Then A + B + C = 0, AB + BC + CA = −11, ABC = −20, and

A² + B² + C² = 22, A²B² + B²C² + C²A² = 121,

so the cubic (1) becomes x³ − 22x² + 121x − 400, i.e. n = 132.  
Here A,B,C are non-zero and pairwise distinct, so the eight sign combinations give exactly the seven distinct values

0, ±2A, ±2B, ±2(A+B).

(ii)  n = 12  

σ₂ = −1, σ₃² = 400 ⇒ σ₃ = ±20.  
Solving s = A + B = −C with

s³ − s − 20 = 0

gives s ≈ 2.835 (real).  
Let ab = p = s² − 1 ≈ 7.04 and take A,B the two roots of t² − st + p = 0.  
Then A,B,C satisfy (2)–(5) with n = 12, and again A,B,C are all non-zero and unequal, giving exactly seven distinct sums.

Thus both n = 12 and n = 132 work, and the greater is required.

\boxed{132}

Step 1.  Set the notation  
Let  
a, b, c  be the three roots of  
x³ – (n/6)x² + (n – 11)x – 400 = 0 ;   (1)  
and choose square–roots  

α² = a , β² = b , γ² = c.

Put  

r₁ = α , r₂ = β , r₃ = γ ,

so   a = r₁² ,  b = r₂² ,  c = r₃².  

For any choice of the three signs ±r₁, ±r₂, ±r₃ we obtain a value of  
α+β+γ = ±r₁ ± r₂ ± r₃.  
There are 2³ = 8 sign–choices, hence at most 8 different sums.  
We are told that exactly 7 different values occur; therefore two different sign–choices give the same sum and every other pair gives different sums.

Step 2.  When do two sign–choices coincide?  
If  

s₁r₁ + s₂r₂ + s₃r₃ = t₁r₁ + t₂r₂ + t₃r₃   (sᵢ , tᵢ ∈ {±1})

then  

(s₁–t₁)r₁ + (s₂–t₂)r₂ + (s₃–t₃)r₃ = 0,

with each coefficient in {0,±2}.  Dividing by 2 shows that some combination  
ε₁r₁ + ε₂r₂ + ε₃r₃ = 0  (εᵢ ∈ {0,±1}, not all 0)  (2)  
must hold.

•  If one εᵢ were 0, (2) would say two of the r’s are equal up to sign, which would make at least two different pairs of sign–choices coincide, giving ≤6 different sums.  
•  Therefore all three εᵢ are non–zero and, after multiplying a suitable sign, we may assume  

r₁ + r₂ + r₃ = 0.   (3)

Conversely, (3) yields exactly two sign–choices (+,+,+) and (−,−,−) whose sums are 0, while the remaining six sums are all distinct; altogether 7 different values.  
Hence (3) is the necessary and sufficient condition for the required “exactly seven”.

Step 3.  Express the symmetric functions of a, b, c in terms of r₁,r₂,r₃  
With p = r₁+r₂+r₃, q = r₁r₂+r₂r₃+r₃r₁, r = r₁r₂r₃ we have p = 0 by (3) and

a+b+c = r₁²+r₂²+r₃² = p² – 2q = –2q,  
ab+bc+ca = (r₁r₂)²+(r₂r₃)²+(r₃r₁)² = q²,  
abc = (r₁r₂r₃)² = r².   (4)

Step 4.  Match (4) with the coefficients in (1)  
Comparing (4) with the coefficients of (1) gives the system  

−2q = n/6,   q² = n – 11,   r² = 400.   (5)

From the first two equations of (5):

n = −12q,   so  q² = n – 11 = (−12q) – 11 = −12q − 11,

hence  

q² + 12q + 11 = 0  
⇒ (q + 1)(q + 11) = 0  
⇒ q = –1 or q = –11.   (6)

Using n = −12q,

•  If q = −1 ⇒ n = 12.  
•  If q = −11 ⇒ n = 132.

Step 5.  Feasibility of r  
The last equation of (5) is r² = 400, so r = ±20.  
For each pair (q,r) found above the cubic  

t³ + q t − r = 0   (roots r₁,r₂,r₃, since p = 0)

has non–zero discriminant  

Δ = −4q³ − 27r²,

which equals  
Δ = −4(−1)³ − 27(±20)² = 4 − 10800 = −10796 (for q = –1),  
Δ = −4(−11)³ − 27(±20)² = 5324 − 10800 = −5476 (for q = –11).

Because Δ ≠ 0, the three roots r₁,r₂,r₃ are distinct and non-zero, so no extra coincidences of sums arise.  Thus each value of n obtained above indeed gives exactly seven different possible values of α+β+γ.

Step 6.  Choose the greatest integer n  
Both n = 12 and n = 132 work, and the problem asks for the greatest such integer.

\boxed{132}

Label the vertices of the tetrahedron \(A,B,C,D\) so that  

1. \(\triangle ABC\) and \(\triangle ABD\) are the two \((5\sqrt{10},5\sqrt{10},18)\) faces,  
2. \(\triangle ACD\) and \(\triangle BCD\) are the two \((5\sqrt{10},5\sqrt{10},10)\) faces.

That forces the six edge–lengths to be  

\[
AB=18,\; CD=10,\;
AC=AD=BC=BD=5\sqrt{10}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A convenient coordinate model

Put  

\[
A=(0,0,0),\qquad B=(18,0,0).
\]

Because \(AC=BC\) and \(AD=BD\), the points \(C\) and \(D\) lie in the plane \(x=9\).
Choose them so that \(|C|=|D|=5\sqrt{10}\;(=13)\) and \(CD=10\):

\[
C=(9,5,12),\qquad D=(9,-5,12).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The volume

\[
V=\frac16\bigl|(B-A)\cdot\bigl((C-A)\times(D-A)\bigr)\bigr|
      =\frac16(18)\cdot|y_Cz_D-z_Cy_D|
      =3\cdot120=360.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The inradius \(r\)

Face areas:

\[
[\triangle ACD]=[\triangle BCD]=75,\qquad 
[\triangle ABC]=[\triangle ABD]=117.
\]

Total surface area \(A_T=2(75)+2(117)=384\).

For a tangential tetrahedron \(V=\dfrac13\,r\,A_T\); hence

\[
r=\frac{3V}{A_T}=\frac{3\cdot360}{384}=\frac{45}{16}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The circumradius \(R\)

Because of the symmetry \(C,D\) share the same \(z\)-coordinate and their \(y\)-coordinates are negatives, the circum-center \(S\) is on the line \(x=9,\;y=0\); let \(S=(9,0,h)\).

Imposing \(|SA|=|SC|\):

\[
81+h^{2}=y_C^{2}+(h-z_C)^{2}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
h=\frac{44}{z_C}=\frac{44}{12}=\frac{11}{3}.
\]

Thus  

\[
R=|SA|=\sqrt{81+\Bigl(\dfrac{11}{3}\Bigr)^{2}}
     =\sqrt{\frac{850}{9}}=\frac{5\sqrt{34}}{3}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  The centers \(R\) (incenter) and \(S\) (circumcenter)

Barycentric coordinates of the incenter are proportional to the areas of the opposite faces:

\[
I=\frac{75A+75B+117C+117D}{384}
  =\Bigl(9,\,0,\,\frac{117}{16}\Bigr).
\]

Hence  

\[
S=(9,0,\tfrac{11}{3}),\qquad
R=(9,0,\tfrac{117}{16}),
\]
so they lie on the same vertical line.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  The required distance

\[
RS=\Bigl|\tfrac{11}{3}-\tfrac{117}{16}\Bigr|
    =\Bigl|\frac{176-351}{48}\Bigr|
    =\frac{175}{48}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7.  Final answer

\(RS=\dfrac{175}{48}\) with relatively prime \(m=175,\;n=48\), so  

\[
m+n = 175+48 = 223.
\]

\boxed{223}

Step 1. Label the vertices so that  
AB = 10, CD = 18, AC = AD = BC = BD = 5√10.  
Faces ABC and ABD are the two (5√10, 5√10, 10) triangles, and faces ACD and BCD are the two (5√10, 5√10, 18) triangles.

Step 2. Choose coordinates.  
Put AB on the x–axis with its midpoint at the origin:

A(−5, 0, 0), B(5, 0, 0).

Because AC = BC and AD = BD, both C and D lie on the plane x = 0 and 15 units from the origin:

C(0, 15, 0).

Let D = (0, 15 cos φ, 15 sin φ).  
Since CD = 18,

18² = 2·15²(1−cos φ) ⇒ cos φ = 7⁄25, sin φ = 24⁄25,

so

D(0, 15·7/25, 15·24/25) = (0, 21⁄5, 72⁄5).

Step 3. The circumcenter S.  
Write S = (0, y_s, z_s).  
Equality of SA and SC gives 25 + y_s² + z_s² = (y_s−15)² + z_s² ⟹ y_s = 20⁄3.

Equality of SA and SD gives

25 + y_s² + z_s² = (y_s − 21⁄5)² + (z_s − 72⁄5)²  
⟹ 42y_s + 144z_s = 1000 ⟹ z_s = 5.

Thus S(0, 20⁄3, 5) and the circum-radius is

R = √(25 + (20⁄3)² + 5²) = √(850) ⁄ 3 = 5√34 ⁄ 3.

Step 4. The incenter R.  
Write R = (0, y_r, z_r) with r = z_r (distance to face ABC, whose plane is z = 0).

Face ABD has equation 7z − 24y = 0.  
Requiring the perpendicular distance to this face to equal r gives

|7z_r − 24y_r| / 25 = z_r ⟹ either y_r = −¾z_r or y_r = 4⁄3 z_r.

Only the positive choice keeps R inside the tetrahedron, so y_r = 4⁄3 z_r.

Face ACD has equation 12x − 4y − 3z + 60 = 0, whose normal length is 13.  
Equality of its distance to r gives

|60 − 4y_r − 3z_r| / 13 = z_r.  
Substituting y_r = 4⁄3 z_r yields

60 − 25⁄3 z_r = 13 z_r ⟹ z_r = 45 ⁄ 16, y_r = 15 ⁄ 4.

Hence R(0, 15⁄4, 45⁄16) and the in-radius is r = 45 ⁄ 16.

Step 5. Distance RS.  
Δy = 20⁄3 − 15⁄4 = 35⁄12, Δz = 5 − 45⁄16 = 35⁄16.

RS² = (35⁄12)² + (35⁄16)²  
   = 35² (1⁄144 + 1⁄256)  
   = 1225 · 25 ⁄ 2304  
   = (49 · 625) ⁄ 2304  
   = (7·25 ⁄ 48)².

Therefore RS = 175 ⁄ 48.

Step 6. Answer.  
RS = m⁄n with m = 175, n = 48 (coprime).  
m + n = 175 + 48 = 223.

Let the tetrahedron’s vertices be labelled so that

• AC = AD = BC = BD = 5√10.  

Place the tetrahedron in 3-space as follows:

A = (−5, 0, 0)  B = (5, 0, 0)  
C = (0, 9, 12)  D = (0, −9, 12)

Quick checks  
AC² = 25 + 9² + 12² = 250 = (5√10)², and similarly for the other four equal edges.  
CD = 2·9 = 18.  
Thus the coordinates are valid.

Because AB and CD are opposite edges and the other four edges are equal, the tetrahedron is an isosceles (disphenoid) tetrahedron; all its symmetry planes force both the circumcenter S and the incenter R onto the line x = y = 0 (the z-axis).

----------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The circumcenter S

Let S = (0, 0, z_s).  Equating distances to A and to C:

SA² = 25 + z_s²  
SC² = 9² + (12 − z_s)²

Hence  
25 + z_s² = 81 + 144 − 24z_s + z_s²  
24z_s = 200 ⇒ z_s = 25/3.

Circumradius  
R_c² = 25 + (25/3)² = 25 + 625/9 = 850/9,  
R_c = 5√34 / 3 (value not needed later).

----------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The incenter R

Faces opposite the vertices:

• Opposite A: △BCD, with sides 5√10, 5√10, 18  
• Opposite B: △ACD, same as above  
• Opposite C: △ABD, with sides 5√10, 5√10, 10  
• Opposite D: △ABC, same as above  

Areas of the two kinds of isosceles triangles:

For sides a = 5√10, base c:

Area = c/4 · √(4a² − c²).

Small face (base 10):  
s = 10/4 · √(1000 − 100) = 2.5 · 30 = 75.

Large face (base 18):  
b = 18/4 · √(1000 − 324) = 4.5 · 26 = 117.

Let R = (0, 0, z_r).  Using barycentric coordinates weighted by opposite face areas,

z_r = (0·b + 0·b + 12·s + 12·s)/(2b + 2s)
     = 24s / (2b + 2s)
     = 12s / (b + s)
     = 12·75 / (117 + 75)
     = 900 / 192
     = 75/16.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The distance RS

RS = |z_s − z_r|  
    = 25/3 − 75/16  
    = (400 − 225)/48  
    = 175/48.

m + n = 175 + 48 = 223.

Step 1.  Label the tetrahedron

ABC and ABD have sides 5√10, 5√10, 10;  
ACD and BCD have sides 5√10, 5√10, 18.

Because AC = BC and AD = BD, both C and D lie in the plane x = 0.  
Let C and D have coordinates (0, y, z).

Since AC = 5√10,  
25 + y² + z² = 250  ⇒ y² + z² = 225   (1)

Since CD = 18, the distance between C and D is 18.  Choosing C first at (0, 15, 0) (so it satisfies (1)), the chord formula gives the central angle θ by  

2·15·sin(θ/2) = 18  ⇒ sin(θ/2)=0.6.  

Hence cos θ = 0.28, sin θ = 0.96 and we take  

D(0, 15·0.28, 15·0.96) = (0, 4.2, 14.4) = (0, 21/5, 72/5).

Thus  

C(0, 15, 0),  D(0, 21/5, 72/5).

By symmetry S lies in the plane x = 0, so let S = (0, y, z).

Equal distances SA = SC give  
25 + y² + z² = (y − 15)² + z² ⇒ y = 20/3.

Equal distances SA = SD give  
25 + (20/3)² + z² = (20/3 − 21/5)² + (z − 72/5)²,

which yields z = 5.

Hence  
S(0, 20/3, 5) and the circum-radius is  

R = SA = √(25 + (20/3)² + 5²) = √(850)/3 = (5√34)/3.

For a tetrahedron, barycentric weights at the vertices are the areas of the opposite faces.

Areas of the faces  
Type-A face (5√10, 5√10, 10): height = √(250 − 25) = 15, area = ½·10·15 = 75.  
Type-B face (5√10, 5√10, 18): height = √(250 − 81) = 13, area = ½·18·13 = 117.

So

Face opposite A (BCD) area 117  
Face opposite B (ACD) area 117  
Face opposite C (ABD) area 75  
Face opposite D (ABC) area 75.

R = (117A + 117B + 75C + 75D)/384
