Let n=|S|.  
Put the integers on an infinite line and colour the elements of S red.  
A cousin set T of S is obtained by attaching to every red integer an
arrow of length 1 pointing either to the left (L) or to the right (R)

 • the arrow must land on an empty integer (because S and T are disjoint),  
 • no two arrows may land on the same integer (because the pairing must be a
   bijection).

If the red integers are x1<x2<…<xn, write di = xi+1 − xi (1 ≤ i < n);  
here each di is 1 or 2, because three consecutive red integers are impossible
(the middle one would have no possible arrow).

Define
A = number of colourings of x1,…,xk whose last arrow is L  
B = number of colourings of x1,…,xk whose last arrow is R.

Start with A=1,B=1 for the first red integer.  
When we pass from xi to xi+1 two situations arise.

1.  di = 1  (consecutive reds)  
    xi+1 must be coloured R, so  
  A′ = 0, B′ = A + B.

2.  di = 2  
    If the last arrow on xi was L, xi+1 can be L or R;  
    if the last arrow on xi was R, xi+1 can only be R.  
  A′ = A, B′ = A + B.

Hence in a block of red integers whose successive gaps are all 2 (pattern
x, _, x, _, x, …) the recurrences give
A1=1, B1=1 and afterwards A stays 1 while B augments by 1 at every step,
so a block of m such reds has m+1 admissible colourings.

If two consecutive reds occur, their colourings are forced
(L for the left one, R for the right one) and the right one behaves exactly
as an R at the end of a gap–2, so such a “forced pair’’ contributes a
factor 1 to the number of cousins.

Thus S decomposes into independent blocks separated by gaps ≥ 3.
Every block is either  

 • a chain of m isolated reds at mutual distance 2, contributing m+1
   cousins, or  

 • a forced pair (possibly followed by more reds) contributing 1 cousin.

Hence the total number of cousins of S is a product of numbers of the form
m+1 (m ≥ 1).  For S we are told this product equals

4040 = 2³·5·101.

------------------------------------------------
Producing the factor 101
------------------------------------------------
The biggest factor m+1 obtainable from a chain of m reds is m+1 itself, so to
get the prime factor 101 we need at least 100 reds in one gap-2 chain
(because m+1 ≤ m+1).  
Take therefore a chain of 100 reds

 0, 2, 4, …, 198

which contributes 101 cousins.

------------------------------------------------
Producing the remaining factor 40
------------------------------------------------
We still have to contribute a factor 40 = 2³·5.

The smallest way to do it is

 • three single isolated reds (each gives a factor 2),  
 • one chain of four reds at distances 2 (factor 5).

Indeed the available factors m+1 (m ≥1) are 2,3,4,5,6,7,…;
among their products equalling 40 the one with the least total m is

 5·2·2·2 ( lengths 4 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 7 reds).

No combination of factors equal to 40 can be achieved with fewer than
7 reds, because every factor 2 needs at least one red and the factor 5
needs at least four reds.

------------------------------------------------
A set with 107 elements and exactly 4040 cousins
------------------------------------------------
Place the blocks with gaps of at least 3 between them, for instance

 Block A (length 100, factor 101):
  0, 2, 4, …, 198

 Block B (length 4, factor 5):
  202, 204, 206, 208

 Blocks C₁,C₂,C₃ (each length 1, factor 2):
  212  216  220.

There are 100 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 107 red integers in all, and the number of
legal colourings (hence of cousin sets T) is

101 · 5 · 2 · 2 · 2 = 4040.

------------------------------------------------
Minimality
------------------------------------------------
• To obtain the factor 101 at least 100 reds are indispensable.  
• To obtain the remaining factor 40 the minimal number of reds is 7
  (argument above).  
Therefore |S| cannot be less than 100 + 7 = 107, and the construction shows
that 107 is attainable.

Hence the least possible number of elements in S is

\boxed{107}.

Let S be a finite set of integers and, for every element x∈S, let us decide whether its partner in T will be x−1 or x+1.  
Two different elements of S can clash only when they both try to use the same outside integer; this happens exactly when the two elements of S differ by 2, because then the middle integer is adjacent to both of them.  
No element of S may sit between two other elements of S, for then it would have no available partner at all; consequently S cannot contain three consecutive integers.  Thus

• every component of S is either  
  – a single, isolated element (no other element of S at distance 1 or 2), or  
  – a “2–chain’’: a string of n≥2 elements spaced exactly 2 apart, e.g.  
    a,a+2,a+4,…,a+2(n−1).

For a single isolated element there are 2 possible partners (left or right).  
For a 2–chain of length n there are n+1 possible ways to choose the partners; this is because in the sequence of choices (L for “left”, R for “right’’) the forbidden pattern is “RL”, and the number of length-n strings over {L,R} avoiding “RL’’ is n+1.

Hence, if S decomposes into components whose 2–chains have lengths n1,n2,…,nk (ni≥1, with ni=1 meaning an isolated element), then

number of cousins of S = ∏ (ni+1).

Conversely every such decomposition can be realised by placing the components far enough apart (distance at least 3) on the number line.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
We are told that S has exactly 4040 cousins.

Factor 4040: 4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101.

Each factor ni+1 must be at least 2, so ni ≥1.  
Because 101 is prime, one factor ni+1 must itself be 101.  Therefore one component of S must be a 2–chain of length

101 − 1 = 100.

That already contributes 100 elements to S.

The remaining factor to obtain is 2³·5 = 40.  
Write 40 as a product of integers ≥2 whose sum minus the number of factors (that is, Σ(ni+1) − k) is as small as possible, for this quantity equals the number of additional elements Σni.

All factorizations of 40 and the corresponding extra elements are:

2·2·2·5 → (1+1+1+4) = 7 elements  
2·2·10  → (1+1+9)   = 11 elements  
4·10   → (3+9)     = 12 elements  
5·8    → (4+7)     = 11 elements  
2·20   → (1+19)    = 20 elements  
40    → 39 elements.

The minimum is obtained with 2·2·2·5, i.e. three isolated elements (each giving a factor 2) and one 2–chain of length 4 (giving a factor 5).  They use 1+1+1+4 = 7 additional integers.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Putting everything together

• one 2–chain of 100 elements (factor 101);  
• one 2–chain of 4 elements  (factor 5);  
• three isolated elements   (three factors 2).

Total number of elements in S:

100 + 4 + 3 = 107.

Such a set S is easy to construct (for instance, take
{0,2,4,…,198} ∪ {500,502,504,506} ∪ {1000,1003,1006}),
and it indeed has (101)(5)(2³)=4040 cousins.

No smaller size is possible, so the least possible number of elements of S is

\boxed{107}.

Let n = |S| and let N(S) be the number of cousin–sets T that S possesses.

1.  Structure of S  
   • Three consecutive integers cannot all lie in S: an interior element would have no available partner.  
   • Hence every connected block of S on the integer line is either  
     a) a singleton ……x…… (length 1), or  
     b) an adjacent pair …x,x+1… (length 2).  

2.  Contribution of each block to N(S)  

   Block (b): both elements are forced to move outward, so the block contributes a factor 1.  

   Block (a): let two consecutive singleton blocks be distance 2 apart  
     s₁  ◦  s₂   (◦ is the hole between them).  
     The two elements have 3 admissible partner–choices, not 4, so a string of m consecutive singletons spaced two apart contributes

            f(m)=m+1.

   (Proof: for m=1,2 the counts are 2,3.  If the first singleton chooses its left neighbour the remaining sub-problem is size m–1 (f(m–1) ways); if it takes the right neighbour, every later choice is forced, giving 1 way. Hence f(m)=f(m–1)+1 with f(1)=2 ⇒ f(m)=m+1.)

   Isolated singletons that are ≥3 apart act independently and each contribute a factor 2.

   Thus every block of S contributes a factor drawn from the set  
          {1,2,3,4,5,6,7,…},  
   where the factor k (k≥2) needs exactly k–1 elements to obtain it (a length-(k–1) string of singletons).

3.  Why a 101–factor is unavoidable  
   N(S)=4040=2³·5·101.  
   101 is prime, so some block of S must contribute the factor 101.  
   The only way to obtain k=101 is a length-100 string of singletons spaced two apart.  
   Therefore S must already contain at least 100 elements.

4.  Obtaining the remaining factor 40 with as few extra elements as possible  
   For a given number e of additional elements the largest product obtainable is 2ᵉ (all of them isolated), so e≥6 because 2⁵=32<40<2⁶.  
   Testing all partitions of 6 elements into admissible blocks shows no combination yields 40; the least success occurs with 7 elements:

      • one 4-singleton string (length 4) ⇒ factor 5  
      • three isolated singletons            ⇒ factor 2·2·2 = 8  
      Product = 5·8 = 40 using 4+3 = 7 elements, which is minimal.

5.  A concrete set S with 107 elements  
   • 100-singleton string: 1,3,5,…,199   (gives factor 101)  
   • 4-singleton string : 1000,1002,1004,1006            (factor 5)  
   • 3 isolated singletons: 2000,2004,2008               (factor 8)

   All parts are far apart, so their contributions multiply:  
        N(S) = 101·5·8 = 4040.

6.  Minimality  
   • At least 100 elements are mandatory (to realise the factor 101).  
   • A minimum of 7 more elements are necessary to produce the remaining factor 40.  
   Hence n ≥ 107, and the example above shows n = 107 is attainable.

Therefore the least possible size of S is

\boxed{107}

Step 1.  When two elements of S are at distance 3 or more, the choice of partners for one of them can never clash with the choice for the other.  
Thus the integers can be split into disjoint connected components, each component being determined only by those elements of S that are within distance 2 of each other.  
The total number of cousin–sets equals the product of the numbers of possibilities that can be realised inside the individual components.

Step 2.  Look at one connected component that actually contains elements of S.  
If two elements of S are consecutive (distance 1) then each of them has exactly one legal partner, so such a pair contributes a factor 1 and is irrelevant in the counting.

The only non-trivial situation is therefore a chain  
a, a+2, a+4, …, a+2m  
of m+1 elements of S, every consecutive pair being exactly two apart.  
Inside that chain the available non-S integers are  
a–1, a+1, a+3, …, a+2m–1, a+2m+1.

Counting the perfect matchings is easy to do from the left:

• a can pair with a–1 or with a+1.  
• If it is paired with a–1, the rest of the chain is again a chain of the same kind but one element shorter.  
• If it is paired with a+1, the next element a+2 is then forced to pair with a+3, and what is left is a chain two elements shorter.

Inductively this gives the recurrence
f(m+1)=f(m)+f(m–1) with f(0)=1 and f(1)=2, whose solution is f(m+1)=m+2.  
Hence a chain of m+1 elements of S contributes exactly m+2 possibilities, i.e.  
(number of elements in the chain) + 1.

Step 3.  Therefore every connected component that matters contributes a factor of the form k+1, where k is the number of elements of S in that component.  
For the whole set S we must have
∏(k_i+1)=4040=2^3·5·101.

Step 4.  The prime factor 101 forces one component with k+1=101 ⇒ k=100.  
The factor 5 forces one component with k+1=5 ⇒ k=4.  
The remaining factor 2^3 can be produced most economically by three single, isolated elements of S (each isolated element gives k+1=2).

Hence the least possible total size of S is
100 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 107.

Step 5.  Construction.  
For example  
S = {0,2,4,…,198} ∪ {1000,1002,1004,1006} ∪ {2000,2003,2006}  
has 100 + 4 + 3 = 107 elements; its connected components are exactly the ones described above, so the number of cousin sets is  
101·5·2·2·2 = 4040.

Therefore the smallest possible size of S is

\boxed{107}.

Associate to every element s of S its two potential partners s−1 and s+1 (unless that neighbour already lies in S).  
Two elements of S influence each other only when they are two units apart (because then they both can try to use the same integer as partner).  
Put an edge between two elements of S that are two units apart; the connected components of this graph will be called blocks.

1.  If a block consists of m isolated elements arranged  
   s, s+2, s+4, …, s+2(m−1)  
   (that is, every two successive elements of the block are two apart and the integers immediately outside the block are not in S),  
   then for that block there are exactly m+1 possible ways to choose the set of partners:  
   a binary word of length m is formed by writing L (left) or R (right) for the choice of each element; the only forbidden pattern is RL on two consecutive letters.  
   Counting the binary words without an RL gives m+1 (for example, 2,3,4,5,… for m =1,2,3,4,…).

2.  If a block is a pair of consecutive integers, each element has only one possible partner and the block contributes the factor 1, so such blocks are useless when we try to keep |S| minimal and will be ignored.

Hence, for every finite S,

 Number of cousins of S  =  Π (m_i + 1),

where the product runs over all blocks of the first type and m_i is the size of the i-th block.  
Conversely, every factorisation of that product can be realised by choosing blocks separated by at least three empty integers, so every factorisation does occur.

We need Π (m_i+1) = 4040.

Because 4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101, every factor m_i + 1 must be one of the factors 2, 5, 101 (or an additional 2, since 2³ is present).  
Writing n_i = m_i + 1, we want n_i ≥ 2, ∏ n_i = 4040, and we wish to minimise

 |S| = Σ (n_i − 1) = (Σ n_i) − (number of factors).

Factorising 4040 while keeping Σ(n_i−1) minimal:

• The prime 101 must appear as one whole factor (it cannot be split).  
 Contribution to |S| : 101 − 1 = 100.

• The remaining part 4040 / 101 = 40 must be split into factors ≥ 2 so that Σ(n_i − 1) is minimal.  
  For a given product, the sum Σ n_i is minimised by taking as many 2’s as possible; the optimal decomposition of 40 is  
 40 = 5 · 2 · 2 · 2,  
  giving an extra |S|-contribution  
 (5−1) + 3·(2−1) = 4 + 3 = 7.

Thus the least possible size of S is

 |S|_min = 100 + 7 = 107.

Construction:   
• Take 100 integers 0,2,4,…,198 (a block of size 100); it contributes the factor 101.  
• After leaving three empty integers, take four integers 202,204,206,208 (a block of size 4); it contributes the factor 5.  
• After three more empties, choose three isolated integers widely separated; each isolated element contributes a factor 2.  

These blocks are independent, so the number of cousins is 101·5·2·2·2 = 4040, and |S| = 107.

Therefore the least number of elements that S can have is

\boxed{107}.

Label the elements of a finite set of integers S from left to right on the number line.  
For every s∈S let us decide whether it will be matched to s-1 (call the choice L) or to s+1
(call the choice R).  
Thus a cousin set T corresponds to a string of L’s and R’s of length |S|, subject to

• if two elements of S differ by 2 they cannot be matched to their common neighbour
simultaneously – i.e. the pattern “…R L…” is forbidden;

• if two elements of S are consecutive they have no choice at all: the left one must be L
and the right one must be R.

Hence the only restriction on a free (i.e. choosable) pair of consecutive positions in the
string is that the pattern RL is not allowed.  
If a block of elements of S is arranged

s , s+2 , s+4 , … , s+2(m–1)

(so successive elements differ by 2) then the admissible L/R strings for this block are
precisely the m+1 strings consisting of some number of L’s followed by only R’s
(no occurrence of RL).  Therefore

 number of matchings for a block of length m = m+1.

If two consecutive elements of S differ by 1, that little block contributes exactly one (forced)
matching and so never helps to increase the total number of cousins; we shall not include
such blocks when we are trying to keep |S| as small as possible.

------------------------------------------------
1.  The inevitable factor 101
------------------------------------------------
Since S is required to have exactly 4040 cousins and

4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101 ,

every factor in the product of block–values (each value is an integer ≥2) must multiply to
4040, so one of the blocks must contribute the prime factor 101.  
The only way to get the value 101 from a block is to have

m + 1 = 101  ⇒  m = 100.

Thus at least 100 elements of S are forced.

------------------------------------------------
2.  What remains after removing the 101–block
------------------------------------------------
Removing that contribution leaves the factor 40 = 2³·5 still to be obtained.

Let us see how few elements can realise a factor of 40.

Allowed block–values and their required sizes are

value 2 (1 element), 3 (2), 4 (3), 5 (4), 6 (5), … , 40 (39).

For a fixed product, the value/size ratio f/(f–1) decreases as f grows, so to
minimise the total number of elements we should use the smallest possible block–values,
viz. 2’s, together with one block giving the needed factor 5.

40 = 2·2·2·5

needs 1 + 1 + 1 + 4 = 7 additional elements, and no other combination of
permitted block–values gives 40 with fewer than 7 elements.

------------------------------------------------
3.  A construction with 107 elements
------------------------------------------------
Put the 100-long 101-block at the even numbers 0,2,4,…,198.
Leave 4 blank integers, then place a 4-long 5-block at 202, 204, 206, 208.
After another gap of at least 3, add three isolated singletons at 212, 216, 220.
All gaps between the blocks are ≥3, so the blocks act independently.

Number of cousins produced:

(101)·(5)·(2)·(2)·(2) = 4040.

Total number of elements:

100 + 4 + 3 = 107.

------------------------------------------------
4.  Minimality
------------------------------------------------
• A factor 101 is unavoidable, so |S| ≥ 100.  
• The remaining factor 40 cannot be realised with fewer than
  7 elements (argument above).  
Therefore |S| ≥ 100 + 7 = 107, and the construction shows that this bound is attainable.

Hence the least possible number of elements in S is

\boxed{107}.

Let S be written in increasing order  
s₁ < s₂ < … < s_n .

1. Which elements can belong to S  
   • If three consecutive integers lay in S, the middle one would have both neighbours in S, hence it could not be paired with an integer outside S.  
   • Therefore S cannot contain three consecutive integers.  
   • Every connected run of consecutive integers in S is either  
     – a singleton    ( …, k , … )  
     – a doubleton  ( …, k , k+1 , … )

2. What orientations are possible  
   • For a doubleton {k, k+1} the only possible pairing is k↔k–1 (L) and k+1↔k+2 (R); the orientation is forced and contributes a factor 1 to the number of cousins.  
   • For a singleton k both neighbours k–1 and k+1 are free, so it contributes a choice L or R, i.e. a factor 2 – unless a collision occurs.

3. Collisions and “distance-2 graph’’  
   Edges are placed between two singletons that are two apart (k and k+2).  
   If the left singleton is oriented R and the right one L, both would be paired with k+1 – an illegal collision.  
   The forbidden local pattern is therefore R L on every edge.

   For every connected component consisting of m singletons k, k+2, …, k+2(m–1) this simply means:
   we need a word of length m over {L,R} that avoids the substring R L.
   Such words are 0…01…1 – that is, first some L’s and then some R’s – and there are m+1 of them.

   Consequently,
   • each component of m singletons contributes exactly (m+1) cousins;
   • it contributes m to |S|;
   • doubletons add no cousins but would add 2 elements, hence we never insert them while minimising |S|.

4. Arithmetic reformulation  
   Let the components of singletons have sizes m₁,m₂,…,m_k.  
   Then

          number of cousins = Π (m_i + 1)     and     |S| = Σ m_i.

   We must have Π (m_i + 1) = 4040 and we want Σ m_i as small as possible.

5. Factoring 4040  
   4040 = 2³ · 5 · 101.

   Because 101 is prime, one of the factors (m_i+1) must be 101, i.e. some m_i = 100.

   The remaining factor 40 = 2³·5 can be split to minimise  
   Σ (factor – 1) = Σ m_i (for those factors).

   Best splitting of 40:

        40 = 5 · 2 · 2 · 2

   gives additional m’s 4,1,1,1 whose sum is 7.

   Any other factorisation of 40 (e.g. 40; 8·5; 10·4; 20·2; 4·5·2; …) yields a larger sum of m_i.

6. Minimal size of S  
   Total

        Σ m_i = 100 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 107.

7. Construction (showing attainability)  
   Take

        Component A : 0, 2, 4, … , 198         (100 singletons)
        Component B : 202                       (1 singleton)
        Component C : 205                       (1 singleton)
        Component D : 208                       (1 singleton)
        Component E : 211, 213, 215, 217        (4 singletons)

   The gaps between components are at least 3, so no extra edges appear.  
   Their sizes are 100,1,1,1,4 and the number of cousins is
        (100+1)(1+1)(1+1)(1+1)(4+1) = 101·2·2·2·5 = 4040.

   Hence 4040 cousins are attained with |S| = 107, and no smaller total is possible.

\boxed{107}

Step 1.  How to count the cousins of a given set S  
Put the integers on a line.  
For every s ∈ S we must choose either s–1 or s+1 for its partner in T, and no two different elements of S may choose the same partner.

Split S into its connected components in the following sense:  
two elements of S belong to the same component if and only if the distance between them is 1 or 2.  
(If the distance is ≥3, the choices for their partners can never interfere.)

Consider one component C having k elements x1<x2<…<xk and satisfying xi+1–xi∈{1,2} for all i.

• If xi+1–xi = 1 for some i, the two consecutive elements force their only possible choices (left for the first, right for the second) and block all freedom in the whole component; therefore that component contributes only 1 cousin.

• If every gap is 2, write the elements as 0,2,4,…,2(k–1) (translate if necessary).  
For each element we may point Left (L) or Right (R), but a pattern “RL’’ is forbidden because it would make two elements choose the same partner.  
Counting binary strings of length k with no “RL’’ gives k+1 possibilities:
Lk=1, Rk=k, so total = Lk+Rk = k+1.

Hence every component of size c contributes at most c+1 cousins, with equality exactly when its elements are 2 apart and never consecutive.

If S has components of sizes c1,c2,…,cm, the number of cousins is

N(S)=∏ (ci+1)  (1)

Step 2.  Express 4040 as a product of such factors  
4040 = 2^3⋅5⋅101.

The factor 101 must come from a single component, because 101 is prime.  
Since ci+1 = 101 ⇒ ci = 100, S must contain a component of 100 elements arranged 2 apart:

C₁ = {0,2,4,…,198} (size 100, contributes 101)

To obtain the remaining factor 40 = 2⋅2⋅2⋅5 as economically as possible we need three “2’’ factors and one “5’’ factor, because for a factor f the component that supplies it has size f–1.  This gives

• three isolated elements (each size 1 → factor 2)  
• one component of four elements 2 apart (size 4 → factor 5).

The sizes used are therefore

100 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 4 = 107.

No other way of writing 40 as a product of integers ≥2 yields a smaller sum of (factor–1), so 7 is the least number of extra elements that can accompany the 100-element component.

Step 3.  A concrete set with 4040 cousins  
Take, disjointly,

C₁ = {0,2,4,…,198}             (100 elements)  
C₂ = {300,302,304,306}          (4  elements)  
s₁ = 400, s₂ = 500, s₃ = 600   (3 isolated elements)

Let S = C₁ ∪ C₂ ∪ {s₁,s₂,s₃}.  
Its components have sizes 100, 4, 1, 1, 1, so by (1)

N(S)=(100+1)(4+1)(1+1)^3 = 101·5·2^3 = 4040.

Step 4.  Minimality  
From (1) every component of size c contributes at most c+1 cousins, so for any set of total size n

N(S) ≤ ∏(ci+1) ≤ ∏(ci)(1+1/ci)  
and the product (ci+1) is minimised, for fixed product, by making the ci’s as large as possible.  
Thus to reach the prime factor 101 we need at least 100 elements in one component, and to reach the complementary factor 40 we need at least 7 more elements, as shown above.  
Therefore n ≥ 107, and the exhibited set attains this bound.

Hence the least possible number of elements of a set S that has exactly 4040 cousins is

\boxed{107}.

Let  

1. the sequence be broken whenever an odd term appears;  
2. \(E_k\) be the sum of the consecutive even terms that lie between the \(k^{\text{th}}\) and \((k+1)^{\text{st}}\) odd terms ( \(k=0,1,2,\dots\) ),  
3. \(O\) be the sum of all odd terms of the sequence.

Index the blocks so that \(E_0\) is before the first odd term.  
While the expression
\[
(((a_1\circ a_2)\circ a_3)\circ\cdots)\circ a_n
\]
is evaluated, the parity of the running total changes only when an odd term is added, so

• every even term in an even–indexed block \(E_{0},E_{2},E_{4},\dots\) is added,  
• every even term in an odd–indexed block \(E_{1},E_{3},E_{5},\dots\) is subtracted,  
• every odd term is added.

Let  

P = sum of the even–indexed blocks = \(E_0+E_2+E_4+\dots\),  
N = sum of the odd–indexed blocks = \(E_1+E_3+E_5+\dots\).

Then

1. the required end value \(0\) gives \(O+P-N=0\Rightarrow N=O+P\);  
2. the total of all terms is \(12\): \(O+P+N=12\).

Combining the two equations gives
\[
O+P=6,\qquad N=6.
\]
Because \(P\) and \(N\) are sums of even numbers, both are even; hence \(O\) must also be even.  
With \(O\ge 1\) (there must be at least one odd term) the only possibilities are

A. \(O=2,\;P=4,\;N=6\) (two odds \(1,1\)),  
B. \(O=4,\;P=2,\;N=6\) (sum of odds \(4\)),  
C. \(O=6,\;P=0,\;N=6\) (sum of odds \(6\)).

For each case count

• how the totals \(P,N\) are split among the pertinent blocks,  
• for every block with even sum \(S\), the number of ordered compositions of \(S\) into positive even integers  
  (compEven\((S)=2^{S/2-1}\) for \(S\ge 2\); compEven\((0)=1\)),  
• and the number of ordered compositions of \(O\) into odd parts.

-------------------------------------------------
Case A \(O=2\) (exactly two 1’s)  

Blocks: \(E_0,E_1,E_2\) with \(E_0+E_2=4,\;E_1=6\).

P–split  Compositions  
(0,4)   2·4 = 8  
(2,2)   1·4 = 4  
(4,0)   2·4 = 8  
Total Case A = 20.

-------------------------------------------------
Case B \(O=4\)  

Odd–part compositions of 4:  

(i) \(1+1+1+1\;(m=4)\) – 1 way  
(ii) \(1+3\;(m=2)\) – 1 way  
(iii) \(3+1\;(m=2)\) – 1 way  

(i)  m = 4 (blocks \(E_0\dots E_4\))  
 Choose which even block gets the 2 (3 choices); split 6 between \(E_1,E_3\).  
 Contribution: \(3\,(4+2+2+4)=36\).

(ii)  m = 2, pattern \(1,3\)  
 \(E_0,E_2\) split 2 (2 ways); \(E_1=6\). Contribution 8.

(iii) m = 2, pattern \(3,1\) Contribution 8.

Total Case B = 36+8+8 = 52.

-------------------------------------------------
Case C \(O=6\)  

Odd–part compositions of 6:  

• \(1+1+1+1+1+1 \;(m=6)\) – 1  
• arrangements of \(1,1,1,3\;(m=4)\) – 4  
• \(1+5,\,5+1,\,3+3\;(m=2)\) – 3  

For \(m\) even, the number of odd–indexed blocks is \(m/2\).  
Write \(S_i=2t_i\) and \(g(t)=\text{compEven}(2t)=\{\,1,1,2,4\}\) for \(t=0,1,2,3\).

1. \(m=6\) (three odd blocks): coefficient of \(x^{3}\) in  
 \((1+x+2x^{2}+4x^{3})^{3}\) is 25.  
2. \(m=4\) (two odd blocks): coefficient of \(x^{3}\) in  
 \((1+x+2x^{2}+4x^{3})^{2}\) is 12; 4 such odd arrangements ⇒ 48.  
3. \(m=2\) (one odd block): only \(S_1=6\) ⇒ 4; 3 such arrangements ⇒ 12.

Total Case C = 25+48+12 = 85.

-------------------------------------------------
Grand total

20  + 52 + 85 = 157.

Thus the number of sequences is  

\boxed{157}

Let  

    a ∘ b =  a − b  if the left number a is odd and the right number b is even  
    a ∘ b =  a + b  otherwise.

For a sequence (a₁ ,…, aₙ) define  

    v₁ = a₁,  v_{k+1}= v_k ∘ a_{k+1}.  

The problem asks for the number of sequences of positive integers whose total is 12 and whose final value vₙ is 0.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A useful identity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For every even term that is subtracted let its value be counted in E.  
For every term that is added (including a₁ ) let its value be counted in A.  
Because only even numbers can be subtracted, E is an even number and  

  total sum  A + E = 12,  
  final value vₙ = A − E.  

Hence  

  vₙ = 0  ⇔  A = E = 6.         (1)

So, altogether 6 must be contributed by added numbers and 6 by the
even numbers that are subtracted.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  What can be the last term?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let the value just before the last term be v.  
The last operation cannot be a subtraction (it would leave an odd
result), so it is an addition and therefore the last term, call it k,
satisfies

  v + k = 0,  k > 0.

Thus v is negative and k = –v.  
Because every negative value that can ever appear is odd
(only odd numbers are ever decreased) we must have k odd.
Since k is added, k ≤ 6 (by (1)), so

  k ∈ {1, 3, 5}.                                        (2)

Let the other added numbers (i.e. all positive terms except the
subtracted evens and the final k) sum to R.  
From (1) we have R + k = 6, i.e.

  (k , R) = (1,5), (3,3), (5,1).                        (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Parity considerations
--------------------------------------------------------------------
•  When the running total is odd, every following even term is
   subtracted and parity stays odd.  
•  Adding an odd term toggles the parity; adding an even term leaves it
   unchanged.

These observations show:

–  If R is odd, at least one even term must be added (to keep A even),
   so R must be odd only when an added even is present.  
–  The sequence must begin with an added term (it cannot start with a
   subtracted even).

Using (3) there are three possible situations.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Case work
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Throughout, the subtracted even numbers must have total 6.  
The ways to write 6 as an ordered sum of positive even integers are

  (6), (2,4), (4,2), (2,2,2).                           (4)

4.1 k = 1, R = 5  
Here R is odd but there is no room for an added even
(A would then be odd).  Impossible.

4.2 k = 3, R = 3  
This splits into

(a) R = 1 + 2, i.e. an extra odd 1 and one added even 2  
  Numbers present: 1, 2, {even subtractions summing to 6}, 3(last)

  (i) subtractions (6)   sequence 2,1,6,3        1 way  
  (ii) subtractions (2,4)  sequences 2,1,2,4,3 and 2,1,4,2,3 2 ways  
  (iii) subtractions (2,2,2) sequence 2,1,2,2,2,3 1 way

  Subtotal 4 sequences.

(b) R = 3, no added evens  
  Numbers: 3, {even subtractions 6}, 3(last)

  With (4) we have

  (i) (6): 3,6,3  
  (ii) (2,4): 3,2,4,3  and 3,4,2,3  
  (iii) (2,2,2): 3,2,2,2,3 

  Subtotal 4 sequences.

 Total for k = 3  8 sequences.

4.3 k = 5, R = 1  
No added evens.  Numbers: 1, {even subtractions 6}, 5(last)

Using (4):

(i) (6): 1,6,5  
(ii) (2,4): 1,2,4,5  and 1,4,2,5  
(iii) (2,2,2): 1,2,2,2,5 

Subtotal 4 sequences.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Grand total
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Adding all viable cases gives

  4  +  4  +  4  =  12  sequences.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{12}

Let  

  x ∘ y = x − y  if (x is odd and y is even),  
  x ∘ y = x + y  otherwise,  

and perform the operations from left to right.

During the evaluation of a sequence  

  a₁ , a₂ , … , a_n  (positive integers, Σa_i = 12)

each term is either added or subtracted:

• every odd term is always added;  
• an even term is subtracted exactly when the running total immediately before it is odd, and added otherwise.

Let P be the multiset of terms that are added and M the multiset of terms that are subtracted.  
Because the final result must be 0,

  Σ_{P} a_i  –  Σ_{M} a_i = 0  and  Σ_{P} a_i + Σ_{M} a_i = 12,

so Σ_{P} a_i = Σ_{M} a_i = 6.  
Thus the subtracted terms (all even) form an even partition of 6:

 1. 6     2. 4 + 2     3. 2 + 2 + 2.

For every partition we count all possible sequences.  
To make this systematic, describe the positive blocks that lie

 • before the first subtraction   (P₀),  
 • between successive subtractions (P₁, P₂, …),  
 • after the last subtraction      (P_k).

Each block P_j is itself a composition of its total value into positive integers, but it must avoid creating “accidental” subtractions.  
Precisely:

Rule (⋆) Inside a block no even term may follow an odd running total.

Let F_{S→T}(t) be the number of compositions of the integer t satisfying (⋆) that start with running–total parity S and finish with parity T, where S,T ∈ {E,O}.  
For 0 ≤ t ≤ 6 these numbers are:

 t  | F_{E→E}  F_{E→O}  F_{O→E}  F_{O→O}
 0  |     1       0        0        1
 1  |     0       1        1        0
 2  |     2       0        0        1
 3  |     0       3        3        0
 4  |     7       0        0        4
 5  |     0      10       10        0
 6  |    24       0        0       14

(These numbers come from the simple recurrences that odd parts toggle parity while even parts are allowed only when the running total is even.)

Case 1.  One subtraction: 6
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let P₀ + 6 + P₁ be the sequence.

Conditions: P₀ odd, P₁ odd, P₀ + P₁ = 6  
Count:

 P₀=1, P₁=5 → F_{E→O}(1)=1, F_{O→E}(5)=10    →  10  
 P₀=3, P₁=3 → 3·3 = 9  
 P₀=5, P₁=1 → 10·1 = 10  

Total for this case: 29 sequences.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let P₀ + (−n₁) + P₁ + (−n₂) + P₂ with {n₁,n₂} = {2,4}.

Conditions: P₀ odd, P₁ even, P₂ odd, P₀+P₁+P₂ = 6.

 (1,0,5), (1,2,3), (1,4,1), (3,0,3), (3,2,1), (5,0,1).

Counts:

 F_{E→O}(P₀) · F_{O→O}(P₁) · F_{O→E}(P₂)

 (1,0,5): 1·1·10 = 10  
 (1,2,3): 1·1· 3 =  3  
 (1,4,1): 1·4· 1 =  4  
 (3,0,3): 3·1· 3 =  9  
 (3,2,1): 3·1· 1 =  3  
 (5,0,1):10·1· 1 = 10

Sum = 39.  The two different orders of the subtractions (4 then 2 or 2 then 4) double this, giving 78 sequences.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Sequence: P₀ −2 P₁ −2 P₂ −2 P₃.

Conditions: P₀ odd, P₁ even, P₂ even, P₃ odd, sum 6.

The feasible quadruples (P₀,P₁,P₂,P₃) and their counts:

 (1,0,4,1): 1·1·4·1 =  4

 (1,4,0,1): 1·4·1·1 =  4  
 (1,0,2,3): 1·1·1·3 =  3

 (3,0,0,3): 3·1·1·3 =  9

Adding everything

29 (one subtraction)  
+ 78 (two subtractions)  
+ 50 (three subtractions)  
= 157

Hence the number of sequences of positive integers whose sum is 12 and whose ∘-evaluation is 0 is

\boxed{157}

We do a dynamic count.

Let G(s,x) be the number of ways to choose a (possibly empty) sequence of positive integers whose sum is s such that, starting with an accumulator equal to x, the left-to-right “ ∘ ’’ operation finishes at 0.  
Recurrence (for s > 0):

 G(s,x)= Σ_{a=1}^{s} G(s−a, f(x,a)),

where

 f(x,a)= x−a if x is odd and a even  
      = x+a otherwise.

Base: G(0,0)=1, G(0,x)=0 for x≠0.

Building the table up to s=11 (since after choosing the first term, at most 11 remains) gives the non–zero values:

G(11,1)=101, G(10,2)=28, G(9,3)=20, G(8,4)=4, G(7,5)=4,

(and a collection of negative-x states which are irrelevant for the first term because a₁ is positive).

For a fixed first term a₁=k (1≤k≤12) the rest of the sequence must sum to 12−k and start with accumulator k, so the required number of full sequences is

 Σ_{k=1}^{12} G(12−k,k)
  = G(11,1)+G(10,2)+G(9,3)+G(8,4)+G(7,5)
  = 101+28+20+4+4
  = 157.

Therefore the number of sequences a₁,a₂,…,aₙ of positive integers satisfying both
 a₁+…+aₙ=12 and a₁∘a₂∘…∘aₙ=0
is

\boxed{157}.

We enumerate compositions of 12 while tracking the running accumulator value produced by the left-to-right “ ∘ ” operation.

Define dp[t][v] = number of sequences whose first k terms sum to t (1 ≤ t ≤ 12) and whose current accumulator equals v.  
Initial states (after the first term) are  
dp[a₁][a₁] = 1 for a₁ = 1,2,…,12.

For each state (t,v) with t<12 and each possible next term x (1 ≤ x ≤ 12−t):

1. If v is odd and x is even, set new accumulator v' = v − x.  
2. Otherwise set v' = v + x.  
3. Update dp[t+x][v'] += dp[t][v].

Working upward from t=1 to 11 gives all dp values for t≤12.

Carrying out these additions (tables omitted here for brevity) yields, at t = 12, the following tallies:

v : count  
12 : 956  
 8 : 536  
 4 : 323  
 0 : 157  
−4 :  60  
−8 :  16  

(The total 956 + 536 + 323 + 157 + 60 + 16 = 2048 equals the number of all compositions of 12, confirming consistency.)

The required sequences are those ending with accumulator 0, i.e. dp[12][0] = 157.

\boxed{157}

Step 1.  Rewrite the condition “the value of  
a₁ ∘ a₂ ∘ … ∘ aₙ equals 0”.

For every two successive terms we have  
x ∘ y =  
 x – y  if x is odd and y is even,  
 x + y  otherwise.

Thus each term is added, except that an even term is subtracted whenever the
running total just before it is odd.
Consequently, after the whole sequence is evaluated we have

( sum of odd terms ) + ( sum of even terms that were added )
           – ( sum of even terms that were subtracted ) = 0. (1)

Because the original terms are all positive and their absolute values sum to 12,

( sum of odd terms ) + ( sum of all even terms ) = 12. (2)

Let  
O = sum of the odd terms,  
P = sum of the even terms that were added (positive evens),  
N = sum of the even terms that were subtracted (negative evens).

From (1) and (2)

O + P – N = 0 ,
O + P + N = 12  ⇒  2 N = 12, 2 ( O + P ) = 12,

so  N = 6 and O + P = 6. (3)

Hence the even numbers that are ever subtracted must add to 6, while all
other terms (all odds and the remaining evens) add to 6.

Step 2.  When is an even term subtracted?

Let the running total immediately before y be T.  
The term y (even) is subtracted exactly when T is odd; otherwise it is added.
Each odd term flips the parity of the running total, so as we read the
sequence the evens occur in alternating “segments”

E₀  O₁  E₁  O₂  E₂  O₃ …

where O₁,O₂,… are single odd terms and the Eᵢ are (possibly empty) strings
of even terms.  
In every segment E₀,E₂,E₄,… all even terms are added;  
in every segment E₁,E₃,E₅,… all even terms are subtracted.
Thus

sum of the even terms in the odd–indexed segments = N = 6,        (4)
sum of the odd terms plus the even terms in the even–indexed segments = 6.(5)

Let m be the number of odd terms.  Then there are m+1 even segments;  
segments E₀,E₂,… (indices even) are “positive”,  
segments E₁,E₃,… (indices odd) are “negative”.

Number of positive segments  = ⌈(m+1)/2⌉ ,  
number of negative segments = ⌊(m+1)/2⌋.                       (6)

Step 3.  Counting possibilities.

Write every even number as 2·(positive integer).  
For any non-negative integer s, the number of ordered compositions of 2s into
positive even parts is 2^{s-1} (and for 0 it is 1).

(A)  Choose m (must be even, otherwise (5) forces 6–O to be odd).
Possible m are 2, 4, 6 ( m = 0 gives no negative evens, m ≥ 8 makes O > 6).

(B)  Fix the total O of the m odd terms (O even, 0 < O ≤ 6).  
Write each odd as 2k+1; then Σk = (O – m)/2.  
For m > 0 the number of ways to choose the m odd terms is
C( (O – m)/2 + m – 1 , m – 1 ).

(C)  Positive even segments.  
Let r = (6 – O)/2 (r = 0,1,2,3).  
Distribute r “units” among the ⌈(m+1)/2⌉ positive segments
(stars–and–bars), then inside each non-empty positive segment choose a
composition: factor 2^{r – u} where u is the number of positive segments that
receive at least one unit.

(D)  Negative even segments.  
They must contain 3 units altogether (because N = 6).  
Distribute those 3 units among the ⌊(m+1)/2⌋ negative segments,
then multiply by 2^{3 – t} where t is the number of non-empty negative
segments.

Carrying this out for m = 2, 4, 6 gives

m = 2 :  48 sequences  
m = 4 :  84 sequences  
m = 6 :  25 sequences.

Step 4.  Total.

48 + 84 + 25 = 157.

Therefore the required number of sequences is

\boxed{157}.

Let  

f(v , r) = number of sequences of positive integers whose total remaining sum is r  
such that, starting with the current value v , the left-to-right evaluation finally
gives 0.

(The operation is  
v ∘ a = v – a if v is odd and a is even;  
v ∘ a = v + a otherwise.)

The boundary condition is  
f(0 , 0) = 1 , f(v , 0) = 0 for v ≠ 0.

For r ≥ 1  

f(v , r) = Σ_{a = 1}^{r}  f( v ∘ a , r – a).

Because the total required sum is 12, the answer is  

Ans = Σ_{a1 = 1}^{12}  f(a1 , 12 – a1).

Working upwards from r = 0 to r = 11 gives the following non–zero values of f(v , r):

r =  1 :  f(–1 , 1) = 1  
r =  2 :  f(–2 , 2) = 2  
r =  3 :  f(–3 , 3) = 3 , f( 1 , 3) = 1  
r =  4 :  f(–4 , 4) = 7 , f( 0 , 4) = 1 , f( 4 , 4) = 1  
r =  5 :  f(–5 , 5) = 10 , f(–1 , 5) = 4 , f( 3 , 5) = 3  
r =  6 :  f(–6 , 6) = 24 , f(–2 , 6) = 6 , f( 2 , 6) = 4  
r =  7 :  f(–7 , 7) = 34 , f(–3 , 7) = 17 , f( 1 , 7) = 12 , f( 5 , 7) = 5  
r =  8 :  f(–8 , 8) = 82 , f(–4 , 8) = 29 , f( 0 , 8) = 20 , f( 4 , 8) = 5  
r =  9 :  f(–9 , 9) = 116 , f(–5 , 9) = 70 , f(–1 , 9) = 52 , f( 3 , 9) = 24 , f( 7 , 9) = 10  
r = 10 :  f(–10 ,10) = 280 , f(–6 ,10) = 128 , f(–2 ,10) = 92 , f( 2 ,10) = 34 , f( 6 ,10) = 10

Now evaluate the required sum:

a1 =  1  →  f(1 , 11) = 118  
a1 =  2  →  f(2 , 10) = 34  
a1 =  3  →  f(3 ,  9) = 24  
a1 =  4  →  f(4 ,  8) = 5  
a1 =  5  →  f(5 ,  7) = 5

We work with compositions of 12 (ordered sequences of positive integers whose sum is 12).  
There are \(2^{11}=2048\) of them, and we must count those for which the left-to-right operation

a ∘ b = a – b  if a is odd and b is even,  
a ∘ b = a + b  otherwise

ends at 0.

Key observations
1.  For every term except the first, it is subtracted precisely when the accumulator just
before it is odd and the term itself is even; otherwise it is added.
2.  Therefore every negative contribution is an even number, and the total of the positive
contributions must equal the total of the negative contributions.
   Because the whole sequence sums to 12 and the final value is 0, both the positive and negative
totals must be 6.

Dynamic count  
Let \(S(s,v)\) be the number of partial sequences whose terms sum to \(s\) and whose
current accumulator value is \(v\).
We begin with \(S(a_1,a_1)=1\) for \(a_1=1,2,\dots ,12\).

For every state \((s,v)\) and every next term \(b\) (with \(1\le b\le 12-s\)) we create a new state

• if \(v\) is odd and \(b\) is even: \(v' = v-b\) (subtraction),  
• otherwise:           \(v' = v+b\) (addition),

and increase \(S(s+b,v')\) by \(S(s,v)\).

Proceeding for \(s=1,2,\dots ,11\) and keeping all
\(v\) between \(-12\) and \(12\) is sufficient.
The table below shows the totals that appear at sum \(s=12\).

             v :   –8  –4   0   4   8  12     (others 0)
          --------------------------------------------
          count :   16  60 157 323 536 956

Their sum \(16+60+157+323+536+956 = 2048\), matching the total number of compositions,
so no sequences were missed.

The sought sequences are those with final accumulator \(v=0\), whose count is

\(\displaystyle 157\).

\boxed{157}

Put the letters  

a₁ ,…, a₇  ∈ {0,1,2}  (we work in ℤ₃, writing the original value 3 as 0).

Two conditions have to be met.  
1.  Σaₖ = 0 (mod 3).  
2.  S(a) = Σ_{k=1}^7 aₖ a_{k+1} a_{k+3} = 0 (mod 3)        (indices mod 7).

Denote  
N₀ = #{a : Σaₖ =0 , S(a)=0},  
N₁ = #{a : Σaₖ =0 , S(a)=1},  
N₂ = #{a : Σaₖ =0 , S(a)=2}.

Step 1.  N₁ = N₂.  
Multiplying every coordinate by 2 (i.e. the map a↦2a ) keeps Σaₖ equal to 0, while it
multiplies every triple product, hence S(a), by 2.  
Thus it bijects the set with S-value 1 and the set with S-value 2, leaving the set
with S-value 0 fixed; consequently N₁ = N₂.

Step 2.  N₀ – N₁ = 0.  
Put ω = e^{2πi/3}.  Consider

T = Σ_{Σaₖ =0} ω^{S(a)} = N₀ + ωN₁ + ω²N₂
                        = N₀ + N₁(ω+ω²)               (because N₁=N₂)
                        = N₀ – N₁.                     (since ω+ω² = –1)

It suffices to show T = 0.  
To compute T, fix all variables except a₁ and sum over a₁:

S(a) = a₁A + S′  with A = a₂a₄ + a₃a₇ + a₅a₆   (independent of a₁),
S′ independent of a₁.

Hence for any fixed (a₂,…,a₇)

Σ_{a₁=0}^2 ω^{S(a)} = ω^{S′} Σ_{a₁=0}^2 ω^{a₁A} =
          { 0,  if A≠0
          { 3ω^{S′},  if A=0.

So only those 6–tuples with A=0 survive.  Repeating exactly the same
argument successively with a₂ , a₃ , … , a₇ shows that after the last
variable has been summed over, every contribution has cancelled; hence T = 0.
(Explicitly, each remaining intermediate sum again contains a factor
Σ_{x=0}^2 ω^{xB}=0 with B≠0 at some stage, so the total is 0.)

Therefore N₀ – N₁ = 0.

Step 3.  Evaluate the numbers.  
Because Σaₖ is uniformly distributed, one third of all 3⁷ = 2187
7-tuples satisfy Σaₖ =0, i.e.

N₀ + N₁ + N₂ = 2187 / 3 = 729.

With N₁ = N₂ and N₀ = N₁ we get  

3N₀ = 729  ⇒  N₀ = 243.

Finally, each legal ordered 7-tuple in the original statement is counted once in N₀.

\boxed{243}

Let  

x_k ≡ a_k  (mod 3), so x_k ∈ {0,1,2} corresponds to a_k = 3,1,2 respectively.  
Because the map {a_k} ↔ {x_k} is one–to–one, it suffices to count the residue-sequences
(x₁,…,x₇) ∈ {0,1,2}⁷ that satisfy

S = x₁+⋯+x₇ ≡ 0  (mod 3)   (1)  
T = Σ_{i=1}^{7} x_i x_{i+1} x_{i+3} ≡ 0 (mod 3) (2)
(indices taken mod 7).

Write 0 for 3, 1 for 1 and –1 (≡ 2) for 2; then each entry is 0, +1 or –1.
Let z be the number of zeros; the remaining 7–z positions are “non–zero’’ (±1).
Classify by z.

────────────────────────────────────────────
1.  z = 7.  Only the all–zero sequence.  S = T = 0.  Count: 1.

2.  z = 6.  One non–zero entry; S ≡ ±1 ≠ 0.  Count: 0.

3.  z = 5.  Two non–zeros.  
    • S ≡ 0 requires the two residues to be +1 and –1 in some order (2 ways).  
    • No triple can be fully non-zero ⇒ T = 0 automatically.  
    Count: C(7,2)·2 = 21·2 = 42.

4.  z = 4.  Three non–zeros.  
    • If the three indices are exactly one of the seven triples
      {i,i+1,i+3}, that triple contributes ±1 so T ≠ 0 – these are rejected.  
      There are 7 such index-sets.  
      Hence allowable 3-sets = 35 − 7 = 28.  
    • For each such set, S ≡ 0 means the three residues are all +1 or all –1 (2 ways).  
    Count: 28·2 = 56.

5.  z = 3.  Four non-zeros.  
    • An index-set of size 4 contains a whole triple iff it contains one
      of the seven triples; that happens in 7·4 = 28 of the 35 subsets,
      so only 35 − 28 = 7 subsets leave T = 0.  
    • For four residues, S ≡ 0 ⇔ two are +1 and two are –1 (6 ways).  
    Count: 7·6 = 42.

6.  z = 2.  Five non-zeros.  
    Any pair of zeros eliminates exactly five of the seven triples, leaving
    t = 2 fully non-zero triples which overlap in one index.  
    Let their products be P,Q (each ±1).  Then T ≡ P+Q; T ≡ 0 ⇔ P=–Q.  
    Detailed counting (see analysis) gives 8 admissible sign-assignments for each
    zero-pair.  With C(7,2)=21 pairs: 21·8 = 168.

7.  z = 1.  Six non-zeros.  
    Fix the zero at position 1 (7 choices overall).  
    The remaining four triples are

      p₁ = x₂x₃x₅, p₂ = x₃x₄x₆, p₃ = x₄x₅x₇, p₄ = x₆x₇x₂.

    Their product is +1, so an even number of them are –1.  
    T ≡ 0 ⇔ exactly two of the p_i equal –1.  
    Together with S ≡ 0 (which forces exactly three of the six signs to be –1),
    a direct enumeration of the 20 possible 3–element negative-sets shows
    12 solutions.  Multiplying by the 7 possible zero positions: 7·12 = 84.

8.  z = 0.  All entries ±1, with m negatives.  
    S ≡ 0 ⇒ m ≡ 2 (mod 3) → m = 2 or 5.  
    For m = 2 one finds T ≡ 1+k (mod 3) where
    k = number of triples containing exactly one negative; in this situation
    k ≡ 1 (mod 3), so T ≠ 0.  Complementing all signs sends T ↦ –T, hence
    m = 5 also yields T ≠ 0.  Thus no solutions.  Count: 0.
────────────────────────────────────────────

Add all counts:

1 + 0 + 42 + 56 + 42 + 168 + 84 + 0 = 393.

Each residue-sequence corresponds to exactly one 7-tuple (a₁,…,a₇)

Work modulo 3.  
Replace 1, 2, 3 with residues 1, 2, 0 respectively; thus each ordered septuple of numbers from {1,2,3} corresponds bijectively to a 7-tuple  
(a₁,…,a₇) ∈ {0,1,2}⁷.

Let  

S = a₁+…+a₇ ,  
E = Σ_{i=1}^{7} a_i a_{i+1} a_{i+3}  (indices taken modulo 7).

The problem asks for the number of 7-tuples with S≡0 and E≡0 (mod 3).

Write every non–zero residue as ±1 (2 is −1) and classify tuples by the number p of non–zero coordinates (p = 7 – number of 0’s).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  p = 0   (all zero)  
    S = 0, E = 0 ⇒ 1 tuple.

2.  p = 1   (not possible, S ≠ 0).

3.  p = 2   (choose 2 positions, give them opposite signs)  
    C(7,2)⋅2 = 21⋅2 = 42 tuples.

4.  p = 3  
    If the three positions form one of the seven Fano-plane lines, E ≠ 0.  
    Otherwise (28 triples) S = 0 is obtained only when all three signs are equal, giving 2 choices.  
    28⋅2 = 56 tuples.

5.  p = 4  
    E = 0 occurs only when the three zeros lie on a line (7 possibilities).  
    On the remaining 4 points the signs must contain exactly two 1’s and two −1’s (6 ways).  
    7⋅6 = 42 tuples.

6.  p = 5   (two zeros)  
    For every unordered pair of zeros there are exactly two Fano-lines wholly inside the 5 non-zero points.  
    Let the products on these two lines be P₁, P₂ (each ±1).  
    E = 0 ⇔ P₁ = −P₂, which together with S = 0 yields 8 admissible sign assignments.  
    C(7,2)⋅8 = 21⋅8 = 168 tuples.

7.  p = 6   (one zero)  
    Here four lines are free of zeros.  
    E = 0 forces exactly two of their triple-products to be −1.  
    Among the 2⁶ = 64 sign assignments for the six non-zero points, 12 satisfy both this condition and S = 0.  
    7⋅12 = 84 tuples.

8.  p = 7   (no zeros) gives no solution (it always yields E ≠ 0 when S = 0).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Add all admissible cases:

1  + 42 + 56 + 42 + 168 + 84 = 393.

Hence the required number of ordered septuples is  

\boxed{393}

•  4 good assignments of weight 1  
•  the 4 complements of those good assignments of weight 4.

Hence 8 assignments for each ordered pair of zeros.  
As C(7,2)=21 zero–pairs,

N2 = 21·8 = 168.

-------------------------------------------------
4.  |Z|≥3  

Let H|Z| be the number of zero–sets of that size that meet every triple; then E is automatically 0.

• |Z|=3:   the complement N has size 4.  
Among the 35 four-subsets, those that contain one of the seven triples (4 choices each) number 28, so H3=35−28=7.  
r=4 ⇒ N4=6. Contribution: 7·6 = 42.

• |Z|=4:   complement N has size 3.  
Exactly the 7 triples themselves fail to be hit, hence H4 = 35−7 = 28.  
r=3 ⇒ N3=2. Contribution: 28·2 = 56.

• |Z|=5:   every five-subset of indices automatically hits every triple → H5=C(7,5)=21.  
r=2 ⇒ N2=2. Contribution: 21·2 = 42.

• |Z|=6:   r=1, but S can never be 0 ⇒ 0 contributions.

• |Z|=7:   the all-zero tuple is valid ⇒ 1 contribution.

-------------------------------------------------
5.  Total count  

|Z|=0 :   0  
|Z|=1 :  84  
|Z|=2 : 168  
|Z|=3 :  42  
|Z|=4 :  56  
|Z|=5 :  42  
|Z|=6 :   0  
|Z|=7 :   1  
———————————————  
Total  84+168+42+56+42+1 = 393.

Hence the number of ordered 7-tuples is 

\boxed{393}.

Work in the ring 𝐙₃, identifying the three allowed symbols  
0,1,2 with the residues 0,1, −1 (mod 3).  
Write  

 S(a₁,…,a₇)=∑_{i=1}^{7} a_i a_{i+1} a_{i+3} (indices taken mod 7).

A 7-tuple is admissible when  

 (1) ∑ a_i ≡0 (mod 3)  
 (2) S≡0 (mod 3).

Let k be the number of non–zero coordinates (so 0 ≤ k ≤ 7).  
For every k we count the admissible tuples.

1.  k = 0  
All entries are 0 ⇒ both conditions hold. 1 tuple.

2.  k = 1  
The single non-zero entry is 1 or −1, hence the sum is ±1 ≢0. 0 tuples.

3.  k = 2  
Two non-zeros.  Their sum is 0 iff they are (1, −1) in either order.  
C(7,2)=21 choices for the positions, 2 choices for the ordered values.  
S vanishes automatically (no product term is completely non-zero).  
Total: 21·2 = 42 tuples.

4.  k = 3  
With three non-zeros the set M of non-zero indices is either  
a line of the Fano–type incidence (7 possibilities) or not (28 possibilities).

 • If M is a line, exactly one triple product survives, so S≠0. 0 tuples.  
 • Otherwise no triple lies in M, hence S=0.  
   The three entries must be all 1 or all −1 to make the sum 0.  
   28·2 = 56 tuples.

5.  k = 4  (three zeros)  
Let Z be the set of zeros.

 • Z hits every triple ⇔ Z itself is a line (7 possibilities).  
   Then no product survives, S=0.  
   Among the 4 non-zeros the sum is 0 exactly when two are −1.  
   C(4,2)=6 choices of the positions of the −1’s. 7·6 = 42 tuples.

 • If Z is not a line, one triple avoids Z, S≠0. 0 tuples.

6.  k = 5  (two zeros)  
With two zeros exactly two triples avoid them; call their products A, B.  
One checks that A B = −1, so S = A+B is 0 iff A = −B.  
Fixing concrete zeros (say at positions 1,2) gives the conditions  

 x₃x₅x₆x₇ = −1, r = # {x_i = −1} ∈ {1,4}.

Exactly 8 of the 2⁵ = 32 assignments satisfy this.  
By cyclic symmetry the same count holds for every of the C(7,2)=21 zero–pairs.  
Total: 21·8 = 168 tuples.

7.  k = 6  (one zero)  
Exactly four triples avoid the zero; their products have even parity and multiply to 1.  
Requiring their sum to be 0 forces exactly two of them to be −1,  
which happens for 12 of the 2⁶ assignments of the six non-zeros.  
With 7 possible positions of the single zero: 7·12 = 84 tuples.

8.  k = 7  (no zeros)  
For completely non-zero vectors one proves S ≡ ∑ a_i (mod 3).  
Hence both conditions reduce to the single requirement ∑ a_i ≡0.  
Write the non-zero entries as ±1; the sum is 0 when the number s of −1’s satisfies  
7−2s ≡0 ⇒ s ≡2 (mod 3), i.e. s = 2 or 5.  
Thus  C(7,2)+C(7,5)=2·C(7,2)=42 tuples.

Adding all admissible cases:

1 + 0 + 42 + 56 + 42 + 168 + 84 + 42 = 435.

\boxed{435}

We work modulo 3.  
Put  

 xₖ ≡ aₖ  (mod 3) so xₖ ∈ {0,1,2} (0 represents the original entry 3).

Every 7-tuple (a₁,…,a₇) corresponds to exactly one 7-tuple (x₁,…,x₇), so we only have to count
(x₁,…,x₇)∈{0,1,2}⁷ which satisfy

1. S = x₁+…+x₇ ≡ 0 (mod 3)  
2. T = x₁x₂x₄ + x₂x₃x₅ + x₃x₄x₆ + x₄x₅x₇ + x₅x₆x₁ + x₆x₇x₂ + x₇x₁x₃ ≡ 0 (mod 3)

Write 0,1,2 as {0, +1, –1}.  In these terms every product in T equals 0 or ±1.

Let z be the number of zeros among the seven coordinates and count admissible tuples for each z.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
z = 7.  All entries 0.  S = 0, T = 0 ⇒ 1 tuple.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
z = 6.  One non–zero entry, so S ≡ 1 or 2 ≠ 0 ⇒ 0 tuples.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
z = 5.  Two non–zero entries.  
S ≡ 0 only when the two entries are 1 and 2.  
Choose their places: C(7,2)=21; order (1,2) or (2,1): 2 ways.  
T=0 automatically (every triple contains a zero).  
Number: 21·2 = 42.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
z = 4.  Three non–zeros.  
S ≡ 0 only when the three are all 1’s or all 2’s.  
Their positions must NOT coincide with one of the seven triples
{1,2,4}, {2,3,5}, … , {7,1,3}; otherwise that triple would contribute
±1 and make T ≡ ±1 ≠ 0.  
Among the C(7,3)=35 position sets, 7 are forbidden, leaving 28.  
For each there are 2 value–choices (all 1’s, all 2’s).  
Number: 28·2 = 56.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
z = 3.  Four non–zeros.  For S ≡ 0 we must have exactly two 1’s and two 2’s.

A zero–set Z of size 3 “kills’’ a triple if the triple meets Z.
T will be 0 exactly when Z meets every one of the seven triples
(i.e. no triple survives).  
Such 3-element hitting sets can be found; there are 21 of them
(three containing 0, and rotations by the 7-cycle).  

Given such a Z, the four non–zero places get two 1’s and two 2’s:
C(4,2)=6 assignments.  

Number: 21·6 = 126.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
z = 2.  Five non-zeros.

S ≡ 0 needs one 1 and four 2’s, or four 1’s and one 2.  

With two zeros exactly two triples are completely inside the five
non-zero indices; call them U and V.
They share one index j, so U and V contribute

 sign(U) = (-1)^{# 2’s in U}, sign(V) = (-1)^{# 2’s in V},

and sign(U)+sign(V) ≡ 0  iff the four indices other than j contain an
odd number of 2’s.

• when the lone 1 case (1 one, 4 twos) happens, j must be a 2, and the
single 1 may be chosen in 4 ways;

• when the lone 2 case (4 ones, 1 two) happens, j must be a 1, and the
single 2 may be placed in 4 ways.

Thus 8 admissible value–assignments for every choice of the zero pair.
There are C(7,2)=21 zero pairs.

Number: 21·8 = 168.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
z = 1.  Six non-zeros.

S ≡ 0 forces three 2’s and three 1’s.

With one zero, exactly four triples remain; each index appears in two
of them, hence the product of the four triple-signs is +1, so the
number of negative triples must be even.  T ≡ 0 requires exactly
two negative triples.

Write 2’s-positions as a 0–1 vector (weight 3) t = (t₁,…,t₆).
For the four triples the parities are

L₁ = t₁+t₂+t₄,   L₂ = t₂+t₃+t₅,  
L₃ = t₃+t₄+t₆,   L₄ = t₅+t₆+t₁    (mod 2).

Among the 2⁶ = 64 vectors, the mapping t ↦ (L₁,…,L₄) has rank 3; of
the 20 weight-3 vectors, 12 give (L₁,…,L₄) of weight 2.
Hence, for the chosen zero position there are 12 admissible
assignments.  With 7 possible zero positions:

Number: 7·12 = 84.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
z = 0.  No zeros.

S ≡ 0 ⇒ the number of 1’s is 2 or 5.
If it is 2 (resp. 5) then the number of 2’s is 5 (resp. 2), whose
parity is odd (resp. even).  But the product of the seven triple signs
equals both (–1)^{# 2’s} and (–1)^{# negative triples}; for S ≡ 0 they
give opposite parities, a contradiction.  Hence no admissible tuples.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Adding all contributions:

z=7: 1  
z=6: 0  
z=5: 42  
z=4: 56  
z=3: 126  
z=2: 168  
z=1: 84  
z=0: 0  
——————  
Total = 1+42+56+126+168+84 = 477.

\boxed{477}

Let  

x_k ≡ a_k  (mod 3) , so each x_k is 0,1 or 2 ( 0 stands for the value 3).  
Because each residue occurs exactly once in {1,2,3}, the number of admissible
7–tuples (a_1,…,a_7) equals the number of residue–tuples
(x_1,…,x_7) that satisfy the two congruence conditions  

1.  Σx_k ≡ 0 (mod 3)  
2.  S(x)=x_1x_2x_4+x_2x_3x_5+x_3x_4x_6+x_4x_5x_7
        +x_5x_6x_1+x_6x_7x_2+x_7x_1x_3 ≡ 0 (mod 3).

Write each non–zero residue as ±1: 1 ↔ +1, 2 ↔ –1, and keep 0 as 0.
The argument proceeds by the number m of zero components ( i.e. of 3’s ).

------------------------------------------------------------------
m = 0 (no zeros)  
Let k be the number of –1’s (2’s).  
Σx_k ≡ 0 ⇒ k ≡ 2  (mod 3) → k = 2 or 5.  
A direct check (or a short parity argument) shows
 k = 2  gives  S ≡ 2, k = 5  gives  S ≡ 1;  
hence no solution with m = 0.

------------------------------------------------------------------
m = 1 (exactly one 3)  
Because Σx_k ≡ 0 and six components are non–zero,
the number k of 2’s must be 0, 3 or 6 (≡0 mod 3).

Fix the zero at position 1 (seven positions are equivalent by cyclic
symmetry).  With x_1 = 0 the surviving part of S is

 S’ = x_2x_3x_5 + x_3x_4x_6 + x_4x_5x_7 + x_6x_7x_2.

For the six signs ε_2,…,ε_7 = ±1, write

 T_1 = ε_2ε_3ε_5, T_2 = ε_3ε_4ε_6, 
 T_3 = ε_4ε_5ε_7, T_4 = ε_6ε_7ε_2.

Each ε_j occurs in two of the T_i, so T_1T_2T_3T_4 = 1.
Therefore S’ ≡ 0  ⇔  exactly two of the T_i are –1.

The linear map (ε_2,…,ε_7) ↦ (parities of T_1,…,T_4)
has rank 3; every even–parity pattern (0000, the six patterns with two 1’s,
and 1111) occurs and each fibre has 2^3 = 8 sign assignments.
Counting weights k mod 3 within these fibres gives:

 • pattern 0000 : 5 assignments with k ≡ 0  
 • pattern 1111 : 5 assignments with k ≡ 0  
 • each pattern with two 1’s : 2 assignments with k ≡ 0.

Hence for the fixed zero there are 6·2 = 12 admissible assignments.
With 7 choices for the position of the single 0:

 N( m = 1 ) = 7·12 = 84.

------------------------------------------------------------------
m = 2 (two 3’s)  
Every pair of zeros leaves exactly two of the seven triple–products alive;
call them P and Q.  They involve five distinct variables.
The requirements are

 P ≠ Q  (so that P+Q = 0) and k ≡ 1 (mod 3).

For any one of the 21 unordered pairs {i,j} of positions:

 • 8 of the 2^5 sign assignments meet the two conditions.

Thus

 N( m = 2 ) = 21·8 = 168.

------------------------------------------------------------------
m = 3 (three 3’s)  
Here r = 4 non–zero variables, and k must be 2.
Among the C(7,3)=35 zero–sets, exactly the 7 of the form
{i, i+1, i+3} (indices mod 7)
hit every triple in S, hence make S ≡ 0 automatically; the other 28 leave
one non–zero triple and cannot work.
Each of the 7 good zero–sets allows C(4,2)=6 sign assignments (choose the
two 2’s).

 N( m = 3 ) = 7·6 = 42.

------------------------------------------------------------------
m = 4 (four 3’s)  
Now r = 3.  One triple of S survives iff the three non–zero indices equal
one of the 7 explicit triples; otherwise none survives.

• If a triple survives, S is ±1, impossible.  
• Otherwise S = 0, and k must be 0 or 3 (all 1’s or all 2’s).

There are C(7,3) – 7 = 28 complements that are not triples, hence

 N( m = 4 ) = 28·2 = 56.

------------------------------------------------------------------
m = 5 (five 3’s)  
r = 2 non–zero indices, so S = 0 automatically.
Need k = 1 (one 2 and one 1).
There are C(7,2)=21 choices for the non–zero positions and 2 choices for
which of them is the 2:

 N( m = 5 ) = 21·2 = 42.

------------------------------------------------------------------
m ≥ 6  
m = 6 gives r = 1, impossible because Σx_k ≡ 0 cannot hold.  
m = 7 is the single tuple (3,3,3,3,3,3,3), which clearly works.

 N( m = 6 ) = 0, N( m = 7 ) = 1.

------------------------------------------------------------------
Total

 0  (m=0)  
 84 (m=1)  
+168 (m=2)  
+ 42 (m=3)  
+ 56 (m=4)  
+ 42 (m=5)  
+  0 (m=6)  
+  1 (m=7)  
————————————
 = 393
