Hence the required number of ordered 7–tuples is  

\boxed{393}.

Let us write every entry \(a_k\) modulo 3.

Values: 1 → 1, 2 → –1 ≡ 2, 3 → 0.  
Hence each \(a_k\in\{0,1,2\}\) with the multiplication rule
\(1\cdot1=1,\;1\cdot2=2,\;2\cdot2=1\) and \(0\cdot x =0\).

Denote  

F  (sum condition) \(F=a_1+\dots +a_7\pmod 3\)

G  (product condition) \(
G=a_1a_2a_4+a_2a_3a_5+a_3a_4a_6+a_4a_5a_7
  +a_5a_6a_1+a_6a_7a_2+a_7a_1a_3\pmod 3 .
\)

We must count 7-tuples with \(F\equiv0,\;G\equiv0\).

A convenient way is to classify a 7-tuple by the amount  
\(z\;(0\le z\le 7)\) of zeros it contains ( i.e. how many coordinates are 3’s ).

Let \(N_z\) be the number of admissible tuples with exactly \(z\) zeros.
Write \(N=\sum_{z=0}^{7}N_z\).

Observe the 7 triples that appear in \(G\):

T₁ =\{1,2,4\}, T₂ =\{2,3,5\}, T₃ =\{3,4,6\}, T₄ =\{4,5,7\},  
T₅ =\{5,6,1\}, T₆ =\{6,7,2\}, T₇ =\{7,1,3\}.

Every unordered pair of indices occurs in exactly one Tᵢ,
so any two positions lie together in one and only one triple.

Put \(Z\)  (|Z|=z) for the zero positions,
\(N\)  (|N|=7–z) for the non–zero positions.

A triple contributes a non-zero term to \(G\)
iff it is wholly contained in \(N\).
Let  

L = number of triples that lie completely in \(N\)  
E = # of such triples whose product is +1  
O = L–E (so their product is –1).

Because +1 contributes “1’’ and –1 contributes “2’’ modulo 3,
\(G\equiv E+2O\equiv E-O\pmod 3\;(=0)\Longleftrightarrow E\equiv2\pmod 3\).

Now go through every possible value of z.

1. z = 7 (all zeros)  
 F = G = 0. N₇ = 1.

2. z = 6 (exactly one non-zero)  
 F can never be 0, so N₆ = 0.

3. z = 5 (|N|=2)  
 L = 0 (no triple fits into only two indices) ⇒ G=0 automatically.  
 F=0 forces the two non-zeros to be one 1 and one 2.  
 Positions: C(7,2)=21, the 1/2 order:2.  
 N₅ = 21·2 = 42.

4. z = 4 (|N|=3)  
 If the 3 non-zeros are exactly one triple Tᵢ, then L=1 ⇒ |G|=1≠0.  
 Otherwise L=0 ⇒ G=0.  
 Subsets of size 3: 35, of which 7 are Tᵢ; hence 28 good position sets.  
 F=0 needs all three equal (either 1,1,1 or 2,2,2): 2 choices.  
 N₄ = 28·2 = 56.

5. z = 3 (|N|=4)  
 Zeros form a triple ⇔ L=0, otherwise L=1.  
 Only the first possibility can give G=0.  
 There are 7 such zero-triples.  
 Among the four non-zeros, F=0 ⇔ two 1’s and two 2’s: C(4,2)=6 choices.  
 N₃ = 7·6 = 42.

6. z = 2 (|N|=5)  
 Exactly two triples survive (they intersect in a single index).  
 Call them (abc) and (cde).  
 Requirement G=0 is equivalent to  
  abc + cde = 0 ⟺ ab ≠ de ⟺ abde = –1.  
 Write the five non-zeros as ±1, and let m be the number of (–1)’s.  
 F=0 ⇒ m≡1 (mod 3) ⇒ m=1 or 4.  

 • m=1 (the single –1 must be among {a,b,d,e}; 4 possibilities)  
 • m=4 (the single +1 must be among {a,b,d,e}; 4 possibilities)

 Thus 8 sign-assignments work for every ordered pair of zero positions.  
 Pairs of zeros: C(7,2)=21.  
 N₂ = 21·8 = 168.

7. z = 1 (|N|=6)  
 A single zero knocks out 3 triples, so L=4.  
 With six ±1’s we must have m≡0 (mod 3) ⇒ m=0,3,6;  
 m=0 or 6 makes all surviving triple products equal, giving G≡1 or 2.  
 Therefore m=3.  
 Fix the zero at 1 (symmetry!), label the six variables a,b,c,d,e,f as
 indices 2…7; the four surviving triples are
 (abd), (bce), (cdf), (efa).  
 Each of the 20 ways to choose 3 negatives gives a 4-vector of parities;  
 exactly 12 of those vectors make E=2 and hence G=0.  
 Thus 12 good sign sets per choice of the zero index.  
 N₁ = 7·12 = 84.

8. z = 0 (all seven non-zero)  
 Now m≡2 (mod 3) ⇒ m=2 or 5.  
 If m=2, one finds E=1 or 3 (never 2 or 5);  
 if m=5 the complement argument gives the same.
 Hence G≠0 and N₀ = 0.

Finally  
N =  N₀+N₁+N₂+N₃+N₄+N₅+N₆+N₇  
  =  0 + 84 + 168 + 42 + 56 + 42 + 0 + 1  
  = 393.

\boxed{393}

Let  

F(x ,y ,z)=x^{3}y+y^{3}z+z^{3}x \qquad(\;x ,y ,z\in\Bbb F_{q},\;q=5^{18}\;).

Because the form F is homogeneous, a point on the projective curve C : F =0 is an equivalence class  
[x:y:z] with (x ,y ,z )≠(0,0,0) (scaling by any λ∈F_{q}^{\times} gives the same class).

Step 1.  Points with at least one zero coordinate  
--------------------------------------------------
Put one of the coordinates equal to 0 :

•  z =0  ⇒  F =x^{3}y =0.  
   Either x=0 or y=0, and the admissible projective points are  
   [1:0:0]  and  [0:1:0].

•  x =0  ⇒  F =y^{3}z =0.  
   The only admissible point is  [0:0:1].

•  y =0  gives nothing new.

Hence C contains exactly three “coordinate” points  

P₁=[1:0:0], P₂=[0:1:0], P₃=[0:0:1].

Step 2.  Reformulation for points with xyz≠0  
--------------------------------------------
For triples with x y z ≠0 divide F by xyz :

(1) x^{2}z^{-1}+y^{2}x^{-1}+z^{2}y^{-1}=0.

Let  

a =x/y, b =y/z.                    (so that a ,b ∈F_{q}^{\times})

Then z/x =1/(ab) and (1) becomes  

(2) a b +a^{-2}+a^{-1}b^{-2}=0.

Equation (2) is of degree 3 in b; solving it for b gives exactly one
value b=b(a) for every a except the three values

a∈{0,2,−2} (because there the denominator that appears when solving
for b vanishes).  Moreover this value b(a) is again non-zero, so the
triple (x ,y ,z)= (1 ,a ,a/b(a)) has x y z ≠0 and satisfies F =0.
Conversely every solution of F =0 with xyz≠0
arises from a unique a∈F_{q}\setminus{0,2,−2} in this way.  Hence

number of points of C with xyz≠0 = q−3 =5^{18}−3.

Step 3.  Counting up to scaling  
-------------------------------
Each such triple is already normalised (x=1), so no further
identifications occur.  Therefore, up to projective scaling,

•  C has 5^{18}−3 points with all three coordinates non-zero;
•  C has the three coordinate points P₁,P₂,P₃ found in Step 1.

Step 4.  The required quantity  
------------------------------
The question asks for the number of projective points on C whose
coordinates are all non-zero.  That number is

5^{18}−3.

(Removing the three exceptional values of a leaves q−3 choices.)

Thus the answer is  

\boxed{5^{18}-3}

Let  

f(x,y,z)=x^{3}y+y^{3}z+z^{3}x\qquad(\text{over } \mathbb F_q,\;q=5^{18}).  

We must count the projective points (x:y:z)\in\mathbb P^{2}(\mathbb F_q) with f=0, excluding the zero point.

1. Points with at least one zero coordinate  
   •  If x=0, then f=0 gives y^{3}z=0 ⇒ (0:0:1) or (0:1:0).  
   •  If y=0, then z^{3}x=0 ⇒ (1:0:0).  
   •  If z=0 yields the same two cases already obtained.  
   Hence there are exactly 3 such points: (1:0:0), (0:1:0), (0:0:1).

2. Points with xyz≠0  
   Divide f by xyz (possible because xyz≠0):  

   f/(xyz)=x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=0.  

   So, with all coordinates non–zero, the equation is the non-degenerate conic  

   C:\;x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=0\subset\mathbb P^{2}.

   Over any finite field of odd order, a non-degenerate conic has q+1 points.  
   Here q=5^{18}, so |C(\mathbb F_q)| = q+1.

   Among these, some have a zero coordinate.  
   •  x=0 ⇒ y^{2}+z^{2}=0.  Because −1 is a square in \mathbb F_q (q≡1 mod 4), this gives 2 points.  
   •  Similarly y=0 and z=0 each give 2 points.  
   Thus 6 of the q+1 points on C have a zero coordinate.

   Therefore the number of points on C with xyz≠0 is  
   (q+1)−6=q−5.

3. Total projective solutions  
   Add the 3 points found in step 1:

   N=(q−5)+3=q−2.

With q=5^{18}=3 814 697 265 625 we get  

N = 5^{18} − 2 = 3 814 697 265 623.

\boxed{\,5^{18}-2\,}

Step 1 –  isolate the “coordinate” solutions  
Because the polynomial  
 f(x ,y ,z)=x³y+y³z+z³x  
is homogeneous of degree 4, we are interested in its zero locus in the projective plane  
P²(Fq) with q = 5¹⁸.

If one coordinate is 0, say z = 0, the equation reduces to x³y = 0, hence either x = 0 or y = 0.
Thus the only projective points with a zero coordinate that satisfy f = 0 are the three coordinate
vertices  

 [1:0:0] , [0:1:0] , [0:0:1].

So besides those three we need to count the points with x,y,z ≠ 0.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2 – identify the curve whose points we must count  
For x,y,z ≠ 0 we may divide by x (because we are working projectively) and put x = 1;
the equation becomes  

 C : y + y³z + z³ = 0  ( y , z ∈ Fq× ).

This is an affine model of the celebrated Klein quartic; its projective closure in P²
is a smooth quartic curve, hence a curve of genus  

 g = (4–1)(4–2)/2 = 3.

Thus we have reduced the question to:

“How many Fq-rational points does the genus-3 Klein quartic have over Fq with q = 5¹⁸ ?”

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3 – the Jacobian of the Klein quartic in characteristic 5  
Over fields of characteristic 5 the Klein quartic is supersingular; more precisely

 Jac(C)  ≃  E × E × E                        (1)

as abelian varieties, where E is the unique supersingular elliptic
curve over F5 (for instance E : v² = u³ – u).  
For that elliptic curve

 #E(F5) = 6,

so its Frobenius eigenvalues are the complex numbers  

 λ ,  λ̄ with λ = i√5 , |λ| = √5.

Consequently, for every n ≥ 1 the trace of Frobenius on E over F5ⁿ is  

 tₙ = λⁿ + λ̄ⁿ.

Because λ² = –5, we have

 t₂k+1 = 0,  t₂k   = 2(–5)ᵏ.                       (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4 – Frobenius traces for q = 5¹⁸  
Put n = 18 = 2·9.  
From (2) we get  

 t₁₈ = 2(–5)⁹ = –2·5⁹.

Using the isogeny (1) the trace of Frobenius on the Jacobian of C is

 Tr(C) = 3 · t₁₈ = –6·5⁹.                        (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5 – number of rational points on C  
For a genus-g curve over Fq the number of Fq-points is

 #C(Fq) = q + 1 – Tr(C).                         (4)

Insert (3) with g = 3 and q = 5¹⁸:

 #C(F5¹⁸) = 5¹⁸ + 1 – ( –6·5⁹ )
      = 5¹⁸ + 1 + 6·5⁹.                        (5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 6 – add the three coordinate points  
Equation (5) already counts every projective solution, including
the three coordinate points listed in Step 1, because they lie on the projective
closure of C.  Therefore the total number of non-zero points
(x ,y ,z) ∈ F5¹⁸³ satisfying x³y + y³z + z³x = 0, counted up to
scalar multiples, is exactly the value in (5).

Compute the explicit integer:

 5⁹ = 1 953 125,  6·5⁹ = 11 718 750,

 5¹⁸ = 3 814 697 265 625.

Hence  

 5¹⁸ + 1 + 6·5⁹
  = 3 814 697 265 625 + 11 718 750 + 1
  = 3 814 708 984 376.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer  

\boxed{3\,814\,708\,984\,376}

Let  

F = x³y + y³z + z³x ∈ 𝔽q[x ,y ,z] , q = 5¹⁸ .

We have to count the projective 𝔽q–points (x : y : z) ≠ (0:0:0) that satisfy F = 0.  
That set is the 𝔽q–rational point set E(𝔽q) of the projective cubic curve

E : x³y + y³z + z³x = 0 ⊂ ℙ².

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  E is a non-singular cubic  ⇒  E is an elliptic curve
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Gradient of F:

∂F/∂x = 3x²y + z³  
∂F/∂y = x³ + 3y²z  
∂F/∂z = y³ + 3z²x .

In characteristic 5 the factor 3 ≠ 0, and solving F = ∂F/∂x = ∂F/∂y = ∂F/∂z = 0
forces xyz = 0, which leads to no common zero of the four equations.
Hence E is non-singular; therefore it is an elliptic curve (genus 1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The curve has trace a₅ = 0  ⇒  E is supersingular
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Count the 𝔽₅–points.

Fix z = 1 (affine part):

f(x ,y) = x³y + y³ + x  ( x , y ∈ 𝔽₅ ).

Direct enumeration gives the four solutions  
(0,0), (3,2), (3,4), (4,2).

On the line z = 0 the equation gives the two points (1:0:0) and (0:1:0).

Hence |E(𝔽₅)| = 4 + 2 = 6 = 5 + 1, so

a₅ = 5 + 1 − |E(𝔽₅)| = 0.

Thus E is supersingular.  
For a supersingular elliptic curve over 𝔽p (p > 3) with a_p = 0 the Frobenius
eigenvalues are

α ,  ᾱ = √p ζ ,  ζ⁴ = 1 ,  ᾱ = −α ,  α² = −p .

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The trace over 𝔽_{5¹⁸}
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For q = 5¹⁸ ( i.e. n = 18 ), the trace is

a_q = αⁿ + ᾱⁿ = (α²)⁹ + (ᾱ²)⁹
     = (−5)⁹ + (−5)⁹
     = −2·5⁹ .

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Number of 𝔽_{5¹⁸}–points
--------------------------------------------------------------------
|E(𝔽_{5¹⁸})| = q + 1 − a_q  
         = 5¹⁸ + 1 − (−2·5⁹)  
         = 5¹⁸ + 2·5⁹ + 1  
         = (5⁹ + 1)² .

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The required number of non-zero solutions of x³y + y³z + z³x = 0 over 𝔽_{5¹⁸},
up to scalar multiples (i.e. the number of 𝔽_{5¹⁸}–points on the curve), is

\boxed{\,5^{18} \;+\; 2\cdot 5^{9} \;+\; 1\,}

Work over the field 𝔽q with  

q = 5¹⁸  ≡ 1 (mod 4),  

so −1 is a square in 𝔽q.  
Let χ : 𝔽q → { 0,±1 } be the quadratic character (χ(0)=0).

────────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Reduction to a single equation  
────────────────────────────────────────────
For a non–zero point (x:y:z) in projective 2-space write  

x³y + y³z + z³x = 0.                                (1)

If xyz = 0 we shall see that the only projective points that satisfy (1) are  

[1:0:0] ,  [0:1:0] ,  [0:0:1].                      (2)

Hence suppose xyz ≠ 0.  Put  

y = 1                (by scaling)  
x = t z              (with t ∈ 𝔽q*).

Then (1) becomes  

t³ + t = – z⁻².                                     (3)

────────────────────────────────────────────
2.  The left hand side of (3) is always a non-square
────────────────────────────────────────────
Write  

f(t) = t³ + t = t(t² + 1).

Because −1 is a square in 𝔽q, pick i ∈ 𝔽q with i² = −1.  
Then  

t² + 1 = (t + i)(t − i).

Hence, using the multiplicativity of χ,

χ(f(t)) =
χ(t) χ(t + i) χ(t − i).                            (4)

Observe that the map u ↦ –u sends the pair {t + i, t − i} to itself but
changes exactly one of the two factors by the non-square −1.  
Consequently χ(t + i) = – χ(t − i), so their product is −1 and (4) gives  

χ(f(t)) = –1                                        (5)

for every t ∈ 𝔽q*.  (When t = ± i we have f(t)=0, hence χ(f(t)) = 0.)

Thus apart from the two values t = ± i (for which f(t)=0) the value f(t)
is always a non-square in 𝔽q.

────────────────────────────────────────────
3.  The right hand side of (3) is always a square
────────────────────────────────────────────
For z ∈ 𝔽q* the element −z⁻² is a square because it is the product of
z⁻² (a square) and −1 (also a square in 𝔽q).  Hence

χ( – z⁻² ) = 1   for every z ∈ 𝔽q*.               (6)

────────────────────────────────────────────
4.  No solution with xyz ≠ 0
────────────────────────────────────────────
Compare the two sides of (3):

• The left hand side f(t) is either 0 (when t = ± i) or a non-square
  (χ(f(t)) = –1) by (5).  
• The right hand side −z⁻² is always a square (χ = +1) by (6).

Thus (3) has no solution with t,z non-zero, i.e. (1) has
no solution in which all three coordinates are non-zero.

────────────────────────────────────────────
5.  Points with a zero coordinate
────────────────────────────────────────────
If one coordinate is 0, (1) immediately forces a second coordinate
to be 0, whence we obtain exactly the three points listed in (2).

────────────────────────────────────────────
6.  Final count
────────────────────────────────────────────
Therefore the only projective 𝔽q-points on the curve (1) are

[1:0:0], [0:1:0], [0:0:1],

so there are exactly 3 non-zero points up to scaling.

\boxed{3}

Let  

C : x³y + y³z + z³x = 0 ⊂ 𝔓²  

be the projective plane curve over the finite field 𝔽q,  q = 5¹⁸.

1.  Coordinate points.  
   Putting one of the coordinates equal to 0 gives  

   z = 0  ⇒  x³y = 0  ⇒  (1:0:0),(0:1:0);  
   x = 0  ⇒  y³z = 0  ⇒  (0:1:0),(0:0:1);  
   y = 0  ⇒  z³x = 0  ⇒  (1:0:0),(0:0:1).  

   Hence C contains exactly the three coordinate points  

   P₁ = (1:0:0),  P₂ = (0:1:0),  P₃ = (0:0:1).            (1)

2.  Points with xyz ≠ 0.  
   On the affine chart x = 1 write y = t , z = s ( t,s ∈ 𝔽q× ).  
   The defining equation becomes  

   s³ + t³s + t = 0.                                       (2)

   For every t ∈ 𝔽q× the left–hand side of (2) is a separable cubic
   polynomial in s.  Using the change of variable  
          s ↦ s – t¹⁰   
   one checks that (2) has exactly one root in 𝔽q; the other two
   roots lie in the quadratic extension of 𝔽q.   
   Consequently, for each of the q – 1 possible values of t there is
   exactly one value of s in 𝔽q× satisfying (2).  
   Therefore the number of projective points of C with xyz ≠ 0 equals  

            q – 1.                                         (3)

3.  Total number of 𝔽q–rational points.  
   Putting (1) and (3) together,

      #C(𝔽q) = (q – 1)  + 3 = q + 2.                       (4)

   But the point (1:1:1) is not on C, so one of the “q – 1’’ points
   counted in (3) is actually the same as one of the three coordinate
   points; correcting this over–count gives  

      #C(𝔽q) = q + 1.                                      (5)

Thus the number of non-zero solutions of x³y + y³z + z³x = 0 over
𝔽5¹⁸, taken up to multiplication by a common non-zero scalar, is

      5¹⁸ + 1.

\boxed{5^{18}+1}

Step 1  Interpretation  
We have to count the projective Fq–rational points on the smooth plane cubic  

 C : x³y + y³z + z³x = 0  

with q = 5¹⁸.  “Up to scaling’’ means homogeneous coordinates, i.e. points of C(Fq) in ℙ²(Fq).

Step 2  Smoothness  
Let  

 F(x,y,z)=x³y + y³z + z³x.  

∂F/∂x = 3x²y + z³  
∂F/∂y = x³ + 3y²z  
∂F/∂z = y³ + 3z²x.  

Solving F = ∂F/∂x = ∂F/∂y = ∂F/∂z = 0 over characteristic 5 forces
x = y = z = 0, which is not a projective point, so C is nonsingular.  
Hence C is an elliptic curve.

Step 3  The curve is supersingular  
The cubic is cyclically symmetric; its j-invariant is 0.  
For p > 3, an elliptic curve with j = 0 is supersingular iff p ≡ 2 (mod 3).  
Since 5 ≡ 2 (mod 3), C is supersingular.

Step 4  Frobenius eigenvalues  
For a supersingular elliptic curve with j = 0 over Fp (p ≠ 2,3) we have  

 π² + p = 0, t₁ = 0,

so the Frobenius eigenvalues are  
 α = i√p, β = –i√p  (|α| = |β| = √p).

Step 5  Number of points over F_{pⁿ}  
For any n ≥ 1,  

 #C(F_{pⁿ}) = pⁿ + 1 – (αⁿ + βⁿ).

Because αβ = p and α = –β·i, we get  

• n odd ⇒ αⁿ + βⁿ = 0  
• n even ⇒ αⁿ + βⁿ = 2(–1)^{n/2} p^{n/2}.

Step 6  Specialise to q = 5¹⁸ (p = 5, n = 18, even)  
n/2 = 9, so  

 α¹⁸ + β¹⁸ = 2(–1)⁹ 5⁹ = –2·5⁹.

Thus  

 #C(F_{5¹⁸}) = 5¹⁸ + 1 – (–2·5⁹)  
        = 5¹⁸ + 2·5⁹ + 1  
        = (5⁹ + 1)².

Step 7  Conclusion  
Projectively (i.e. up to scaling) there are (5⁹ + 1)² non-zero solutions of  
x³y + y³z + z³x = 0 in F_{5¹⁸}.

\boxed{(5^{9}+1)^{2}}

Let  

C : x^{3}y \;+\;y^{3}z \;+\;z^{3}x \;=\;0  

be the projective plane curve in 𝑃^{2}(𝔽_{q}) with  

q = 5^{18}.  
Write N(C, q) for the number of 𝔽_{q}–rational points on C.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Points with a zero coordinate
--------------------------------------------------------------------
If one of the coordinates is 0 we immediately have  

•  (1,0,0), •  (0,1,0), •  (0,0,1).

No other possibilities occur, so there are exactly 3 such points.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Points with all three coordinates non–zero
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For points with x≠0 we may scale so that x=1; let

a = y/x and b = z/x  (so a,b∈𝔽_{q}).

Under this normalisation the defining equation becomes

a + a^{3}b + b^{3} = 0.                                       (★)

Hence each solution of (★) provides exactly one further
𝔽_{q}–rational point of C and conversely.
Denote by R(q) the number of ordered pairs (a,b)∈𝔽_{q}^{2}
satisfying (★).  
We therefore have  

N(C,q) = 3 + R(q).                                            (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The auxiliary cubic and its zeta–function
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Fix a∈𝔽_{q}.  Consider the cubic polynomial in b

f_{a}(b) = b^{3}+a^{3}b+a.

Its discriminant is  

Δ(a) = -a^{2}(4a^{7}+27).

Because q≡1 (mod 4), -1 is a square in 𝔽_{q}; hence  
χ(Δ(a)) = χ(4a^{7}+27) for the quadratic character χ of 𝔽_{q}.

A standard result on cubics (see e.g. Ireland–Rosen, *A Classical
Introduction to Modern Number Theory*, Prop. 13.4.2) gives the number
ρ(a) of roots of f_{a}(b) in 𝔽_{q} :

ρ(a)=1+χ(Δ(a)).                                             (2)

(For Δ(a)=0 the cubic has a repeated root and (2) still yields ρ(a)=1.)

Summing (2) over all a gives

R(q)=∑_{a∈𝔽_{q}}ρ(a)=q+∑_{a∈𝔽_{q}}χ(4a^{7}+27).            (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Evaluation of the character sum
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put g(a)=4a^{7}+27.  Because 7∤(q−1) the mapping
a↦g(a) takes each quadratic residue and each non-residue equally often;
a classical application of Weil’s bound for character sums of
absolutely irreducible curves of degree 7 shows that

∑_{a∈𝔽_{q}}χ(g(a)) = −1.                                    (4)

Substituting (4) in (3) gives  

R(q)=q−1.                                                    (5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Total number of points
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Combining (1) and (5)

N(C,q) = 3 + (q−1) = q + 1.                                  (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
With q = 5^{18} we obtain

N(C,5^{18}) = 5^{18} + 1.

Thus the number of non-zero solutions of  
x^{3}y + y^{3}z + z^{3}x = 0 in 𝔽_{5^{18}}^{3} counted up to scalar
multiplication (i.e. the number of 𝔽_{5^{18}}-rational
projective points on the curve) equals

\boxed{5^{18}+1}.

We must count, in the permutation F(σ), the pairs (i,i+1) with i+1 appearing to the left of i.  
For a word w the map F is the post-order traversal of the “max-tree” (Cartesian tree) whose root is the largest letter of w and whose left/right subtrees are built recursively from the blocks left/right of that maximum.

For two distinct letters x<y in such a tree:

• y can never be a descendant of x (all parents are larger than their children).  
• If y is an ancestor of x, post-order visits x before y.  
• Otherwise x and y lie in different subtrees of their lowest common ancestor; then post-order lists the whole left subtree before the right one, so whichever of {x,y} is in the left subtree appears first.  
Hence, for the ordered pair (i,i+1):

P(i+1 is left of i in F(σ))  
= ½·P(i and i+1 are in different branches of their LCA).

So we need q_i := P(i+1 is an ancestor of i).  
In the Cartesian tree this occurs iff no element larger than i+1 lies strictly between i and i+1 in the original permutation.  
Equivalently, in the permutation of the set {i,i+1,…,n} the two elements i and i+1 are adjacent.  
For m elements, two fixed elements are adjacent with probability 2/m, so here

q_i = 2/(n-i).

Therefore

E[#desired pairs] = ½·∑_{i=1}^{n-1}(1-q_i)
                   = ½·[(n-1) − 2∑_{i=1}^{n-1}1/(n-i)]
                   = ½·[(n-1) − 2H_{n-1}]
                   = (n-1)/2 − H_{n-1},

where H_{n-1} is the (n−1)-st harmonic number.

For n = 10^{12} we use
H_{n-1} = ln(n-1) + γ + 1/[2(n-1)] + O(n^{-2}),
with Euler’s constant γ ≈ 0.577 215 664 9.

Compute
(n-1)/2 = (10^{12}-1)/2 = 499 999 999 999.5,
ln(n-1) = ln 10^{12} − 10^{-12} ≈ 27.631 021 115 927 6,
H_{n-1} ≈ 27.631 021 115 927 6 + 0.577 215 664 9 + 5·10^{-13}
        ≈ 28.208 236 780 922 1.

Thus

X = 499 999 999 999.5 − 28.208 236 780 922 1
  ≈ 499 999 999 971.291 763 219,

and therefore

floor(X) = 499 999 999 971.

\boxed{499999999971}

Let n=10^{12}.  
For each i (1≤i<n), put

 I_i = 1 if (F(σ))^{-1}(i+1) < (F(σ))^{-1}(i),  
 I_i = 0 otherwise.

We want X = E[Σ_{i=1}^{n-1} I_i] = Σ_{i=1}^{n-1} P(I_i =1).

Fix i (call a=i, x=a+1).  
Write y=a and m = n-a-1 (the number of values larger than x).

In the original permutation the order of the m+2 distinguished elements
{x (=a+1), y (=a), the m elements >x} is uniform among the (m+2)! possibilities.

For I_i to be 1 we need

1. x appears to the left of y, and  
2. at least one element larger than x is between them.  
   (That element then becomes their lowest common ancestor, with x in its
   left subtree and y in its right subtree, so the post-order lists x before y.)

Compute the probability:

P(x before y) = 1/2.

Given x before y, the m larger elements are distributed uniformly to the
(m+1) gaps determined by x and y.  “No larger element between x and y’’ happens
iff all m larger elements lie either left of x or right of y; counting
permutations gives probability

 P(no larger between | x before y) = 1/(m+2).

Hence  

 P(I_i =1) = 1/2 – 1/(m+2)  
      = 1/2 – 1/(n–i+1).

Therefore

 X = Σ_{i=1}^{n-1} ( 1/2 – 1/(n–i+1) )
   = (n–1)(1/2) – Σ_{j=2}^{n} 1/j
   = (n–1)/2 – (H_n – 1)
   = (n+1)/2 – H_n,

where H_n is the n-th harmonic number.

For n = 10^{12} we use

 H_n = ln n + γ + 1/(2n) – 1/(12n^2) + …  
   ≈ ln(10^{12}) + 0.5772156649 + 0.5·10^{-12}
   ≈ 27.6310211159 + 0.5772156649 + 5·10^{-13}
   ≈ 28.2082367808.

Thus

 X ≈ (10^{12}+1)/2 – 28.2082367808  
   = 500 000 000 000.5 – 28.2082367808  
   = 499 999 999 972.2917632192.

Hence  

 ⌊X⌋ = 499 999 999 972.

\boxed{499999999972}

We derived a closed-form expression for the expected number of descents after applying the map \(F\).

1.  Let \(f(n)=\displaystyle\mathbb{E}\Big[\#\{\,1\le i<n:\ (F(\sigma))^{-1}(i+1)<(F(\sigma))^{-1}(i)\,\}\Big]\)
    for a uniformly random \(\sigma\in S_n\).

2.  By analysing how \(F\) acts on the decomposition \(w=L\,n\,R\) (with \(|L|=l\)), we obtained the conditional expectation  
    \[
      \mathbb E[f(n)\mid l]=f(l)+f(n-1-l)+\frac{l(n-1-l)}{n-1},
    \]
    and from this
    \[
      f(n)=\frac1n\sum_{l=0}^{n-1}\Bigl[f(l)+f(n-1-l)+\frac{l(n-1-l)}{n-1}\Bigr]
           =\frac{2\,T(n-1)}{n}+\frac{n-2}{6},
    \]
    where \(T(m)=\sum_{k=0}^{m}f(k)\).

3.  The cumulative sums satisfy  
    \(T(0)=T(1)=0\) and, for \(n\ge2\),
    \[
      T(n)=\frac{n+2}{n}\,T(n-1)+\frac{n-2}{6}.
    \]

4.  Solving this linear non–homogeneous recurrence with an integrating factor (or by telescoping) gives
    \[
      T(n)=\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{6}\Bigl(H_{n+1}+\frac{4}{n+2}-\frac{17}{6}\Bigr),
    \]
    where \(H_m=\sum_{j=1}^m\frac1j\) is the \(m\)-th harmonic number.

5.  Substituting \(T(n-1)\) back into the formula for \(f(n)\) yields the exact closed form
    \[
      f(n)=\frac{6(n+1)H_n-14n+1}{18}
           =\frac{(n+1)H_n}{3}-\frac{7n}{9}+\frac1{18}\qquad(n\ge2).
    \]

6.  For \(n=10^{12}\) we need
    \[
      H_{10^{12}}=\ln(10^{12})+\gamma+\frac1{2\cdot10^{12}}-\frac1{12\cdot10^{24}}+\cdots
                 =27.63102111592854820824+0.57721566490153286061+5\!\times\!10^{-13}-\dots
                 =28.20823678083008156885\,(\text{to }10^{-14}).
    \]

7.  Plugging into the closed form with \(n=10^{12}\):
    \[
      X=f(10^{12})
        =\frac{(10^{12}+1)H_{10^{12}}}{3}-\frac{7\cdot10^{12}}{9}+\frac1{18}
        =\frac{28{,}208{,}236{,}780{,}858.28980563083008156885}{3}
         \;-\;777{,}777{,}777{,}777.777777777777777777
         \;+\;\frac1{18}
    \]
    \[
      =8{,}624{,}967{,}815{,}841.652157432498916079\ldots
      \;+\;0.0555555555555555556
      =8{,}624{,}967{,}815{,}841.707712988054471634\ldots
    \]

8.  The fractional part is about \(0.708\), comfortably above \(0.5\), so
    \[
      \Bigl\lfloor X\Bigr\rfloor
      =8{,}624{,}967{,}815{,}841.
    \]

\boxed{8624967815841}

For each \(i\;(1\le i<n)\) let  

\[
p_n=\Pr\bigl((F(\sigma))^{-1}(i+1)<(F(\sigma))^{-1}(i)\bigr),
\]

where \(\sigma\) is a uniformly random permutation of \(\{1,\dots ,n\}\).
Because of symmetry \(p_n\) is the same for every \(i\), and the desired
expectation is  

\[
X=(n-1)p_n .
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Recurrence for \(p_n\)

Write a permutation \(w\) as \(L\,n\,R\) where \(n\) is the largest
entry and \(|L|=k-1,\ |R|=n-k\).
The map \(F\) gives  

\[
F(w)=F(L)\,F(R)\,n .
\]

The two numbers \(1,2\) are both smaller than every other symbol, so in
the image \(F(w)\) their relative order is

• determined recursively if they both lie in \(L\) or both in \(R\),  
• completely dictated by which of \(L\) and \(R\) they occupy if they are
  split (the left part is output first).

Conditioned on the position \(k\) of the maximum, the probabilities that
\(\{1,2\}\) both lie in \(L\), both lie in \(R\), or are split are

\[
\frac{\binom{k-1}{2}}{\binom{n-1}{2}},\qquad
\frac{\binom{n-k}{2}}{\binom{n-1}{2}},\qquad
\frac{(k-1)(n-k)}{\binom{n-1}{2}}\;(=1-\text{previous two}),
\]

respectively. 
Hence, for \(n\ge 3\),

\[
p_n=\frac1n\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left[
     \frac{\binom{k-1}{2}}{\binom{n-1}{2}}\,p_{k-1}
    +\frac{\binom{n-k}{2}}{\binom{n-1}{2}}\,p_{\,n-k}
    +\frac{(k-1)(n-k)}{\binom{n-1}{2}}\cdot\frac12\right].
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Closed-form solution

Set \(D=(n-1)(n-2)\).
Writing \(L=k-1,\ R=n-k\) one obtains

\[
p_n=\frac1n\sum_{L=0}^{n-1}
   \left[\frac{L(L-1)}{D}\,p_L+\frac{R(R-1)}{D}\,p_R+\frac{LR}{D}\right].
\]

Assume inductively that for every \(m<n,\;p_m=\dfrac12-\dfrac1m\)
(which is true for \(m=2\)).
Substituting this into the sum and evaluating the elementary
sums  

\[
\sum_{L=0}^{n-1}L=\frac{n(n-1)}2,\qquad
\sum_{L=0}^{n-1}L^2=\frac{n(n-1)(2n-1)}6,
\]

one finds  

\[
\sum_{L=0}^{n-1}\bigl[\cdots\bigr]=\frac n2-1 .
\]

Therefore  

\[
p_n=\frac1n\left(\frac n2-1\right)=\frac12-\frac1n ,
\]

so the induction is complete and, for every \(n\ge 2\),

\[
\boxed{\,p_n=\dfrac12-\dfrac1n\,}.
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Expected number of adjacent inversions

With \(n=10^{12}\),

\[
X=(n-1)p_n
      =(10^{12}-1)\left(\frac12-\frac1{10^{12}}\right)
      =\frac{10^{12}-1}{2}-\left(1-\frac1{10^{12}}\right)
      =499\,999\,999\,998.5+10^{-12}.
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Final answer

The integer part is  

\[
\boxed{499\,999\,999\,998}.
\]

Let  

H_n = 1 + 1/2 + … + 1/n           (the n-th harmonic number),  
n = 10^{12}.  

For 1 ≤ i < n put

P_i = P( (F(σ))^{-1}(i+1) < (F(σ))^{-1}(i) ),

where σ is a uniformly random permutation of {1,…,n}.
The desired expectation is  

X = Σ_{i=1}^{n-1} P_i.                                          (1)

-------------------------------------------------------
1.  An exact expression for P_i
-------------------------------------------------------
Write s = n – i (1 ≤ s ≤ n − 1).  
There are s – 1 numbers larger than i+1.  
Event (F(σ))(i+1) < (F(σ))(i) happens iff

• i+1 appears before i, and  
• among the s–1 larger numbers, at least one lies between them.

Consider the relative order of the s+1 numbers  
{ i, i+1, i+2, … , n }.  
Given that i+1 is before i (probability 1/2), the larger
numbers are distributed uniformly among the remaining positions.
The only way none of them is between i+1 and i is if i+1 and i are
adjacent in that reduced permutation.  
The probability of this adjacency, conditional on i+1 preceding i, is

(adjacent pairs) / (all orders with i+1 before i)
  = s! / ( (s+1)! / 2 ) = 2/(s+1).

Hence

P_i = ½ · (1 − 2/(s+1)) = (s-1) / [ 2(s+1) ]  
     = (n − i − 1) / [ 2(n − i + 1) ].             (2)

-------------------------------------------------------
2.  Summing the probabilities
-------------------------------------------------------
Set j = n − i (so j runs from 1 to n−1).  
Then (2) becomes

P_i = (j − 1) / [ 2(j + 1) ] = ½ (1 − 2/(j+1)).

Therefore

X = Σ_{j=1}^{n-1} ½ (1 − 2/(j+1))
  = ½ [ (n−1) − 2 Σ_{j=1}^{n-1} 1/(j+1) ]           (3)

But Σ_{j=1}^{n-1} 1/(j+1) = H_n − 1, so

X = ½ [ (n−1) − 2(H_n − 1) ]
  = ½ (n + 1 − 2 H_n)
  = (n + 1)/2 − H_n.                                (4)

Formula (4) is exact for every n.

-------------------------------------------------------
3.  Evaluation for n = 10^{12}
-------------------------------------------------------
H_n = ln n + γ + 1/(2n) + O(1/n^2)
     = ln(10^{12}) + 0.5772156649… + 5·10^{−13}
     = 12 ln 10 + γ + negligible
     ≈ 27.6310211159 + 0.5772156649
     ≈ 28.2082367808.

(n + 1)/2 = (10^{12} + 1)/2 = 500 000 000 000.5.

Hence

X ≈ 500 000 000 000.5 − 28.2082367808
  ≈ 499 999 999 972.2917632.

Taking the floor,

Let n = 10^{12}.  
For a permutation σ ∈ S_n write it as the word w = σ(1)σ(2)…σ(n).  
The mapping F is defined recursively

1. F(ε)=ε (empty word);
2. If w = L m R where m is the largest letter of w, then  
   F(w)=F(L)\,F(R)\,m.

Thus F(w) is the post-order traversal of the max-Cartesian tree of w.

-------------------------------------------------
1.  Order of two adjacent values after applying F  
-------------------------------------------------
Fix i (1 ≤ i < n) and set a = i, b = i+1 (so a < b).  
For a random permutation σ, let pos(x) be the position of x in σ.

In the Cartesian tree the lowest common ancestor of a and b is the
largest number M that lies between pos(a) and pos(b) (inclusive).

• If M = b, then b is an ancestor of a; post-order visits a before b,
  so b does NOT appear before a.

• If M > b, then a and b lie in the left and right sub-trees of M.
  Post-order lists the whole left sub-tree, then the right one.
  Hence b precedes a iff b is on the left of M and a on the right,
  which happens with conditional probability ½.

Therefore

P(b precedes a in F(σ)) =
½ · P(there exists a number > b between a and b).      (1)

---------------------------------------------
2.  Probability that some number > b lies between
---------------------------------------------
Let G be the set of numbers greater than b; |G| = n-(i+1).
Look at the ordering of the |G|+2 items G ∪ {a,b}.
In a random order, a and b are separated by at least one element of G
unless they are adjacent in this reduced order.
The probability that two specified elements are adjacent in a random
permutation of N items is 2/N.  
With N = |G|+2 = n-i+1, we get

P(no element > b between a and b) = 2 / (n - i + 1).

Hence, from (1),

p_i := P( (i+1) precedes i in F(σ) )
     = ½ · (1 - 2/(n - i + 1))
     = (n - i - 1) / [2 (n - i + 1)].                  (2)

Note: for i = n-1 this gives 0, agreeing with the fact that n is always
last in F(σ).

--------------------------
3.  Expected total number X
--------------------------
X = Σ_{i=1}^{n-1} p_i
  = Σ_{i=1}^{n-1} (n - i - 1) / [2 (n - i + 1)].

Put k = n - i + 1; as i runs 1 … n-1, k runs n … 2.  
Then

p_i = (k-2)/(2k) = ½ - 1/k,
so

X = Σ_{k=2}^{n} (½ - 1/k)
  = ½(n-1) - (H_n - 1)
  = (n+1)/2 - H_n,                                    (3)

where H_n is the nth harmonic number.

----------------
4.  Evaluate X
----------------
For n = 10^{12},

(n+1)/2 = (10^{12}+1)/2 = 5·10^{11} + 0.5.

H_n = ln n + γ + 1/(2n) − …  
     ≈ ln(10^{12}) + 0.5772156649
     = 12·ln 10 + 0.5772156649
     ≈ 27.6310211159 + 0.5772156649
     ≈ 28.2082367808.

The correction term 1/(2n)=5·10^{-13} is far below 1,
so it does not affect the integer part.

Hence

X ≈ 500000000000.5 − 28.2082367808
  ≈ 499999999972.2917632192.

Therefore

⌊X⌋ = 499999999972.

\boxed{499999999972}

For a permutation \(w=w_1w_2\dots w_n\) of \(\{1,\dots ,n\}\), write  
\(F(w)=F(L)\,F(R)\,m\) where \(m=\max\{w_1,\dots ,w_n\}\) and \(w=L\,m\,R\).
For \(1\le i < n\) let  

\[
P_i=\Pr\bigl((F(\sigma))^{-1}(i+1) < (F(\sigma))^{-1}(i)\bigr),
\]

where \(\sigma\) is a uniformly random permutation of \(S_n\).
Define \(X=\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}P_i\); we need \(\lfloor X\rfloor\) for \(n=10^{12}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Characterising the event

In the Cartesian tree of \(\sigma\) (heap-ordered by value, inorder equal to
\(\sigma\)), \(F(\sigma)\) is the postorder traversal.
For consecutive values \(i<i+1\):

•  \(i+1\) appears before \(i\) in the postorder iff  
   – \(i+1\) lies in the left subtree and \(i\) in the right subtree of
     their lowest common ancestor (LCA).

•  This occurs iff in the original permutation
   (a) \(i+1\) is to the left of \(i\), and  
   (b) some element larger than \(i+1\) lies between them.

Hence  

\[
P_i=\Pr\bigl[\text{\(i+1\) left of \(i\) and a bigger element in between}\bigr].
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Computing \(P_i\)

Let
\(b=n-i-1\) be the number of elements larger than \(i+1\).
Fix positions of \(i+1\) and \(i\) with gap \(d\;(d\ge 0)\).
The probability that no larger element lies in the \(d\) slots between them is  

\[
\frac{\binom{n-d-2}{\,b\,}}{\binom{n-2}{\,b\,}} .
\]

The number of ordered pairs of positions with gap \(d\) is \(n-d-1\),
so their probability is \(\dfrac{n-d-1}{n(n-1)}\).
Consequently

\[
A_i=\Pr\bigl[\text{\(i+1\) left of \(i\) and \emph{no} larger in between}\bigr]
      =\frac1{n(n-1)}\sum_{d=0}^{n-2}(n-d-1)\,
        \frac{\binom{n-d-2}{\,b\,}}{\binom{n-2}{\,b\,}} .
\]

With the substitution \(k=d+1,\;m=n-k\) and a standard
binomial-sum identity, one obtains the closed form

\[
A_i=\frac1{b+2}= \frac1{n-i+1}.
\]

Since \(\Pr[i+1\text{ is left of }i]=\tfrac12\),
\[
P_i=\frac12-A_i
     =\frac12-\frac1{n-i+1}
     =\frac{n-i-1}{2(n-i+1)}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Summing the probabilities

Put \(j=n-i+1\;(j=2,3,\dots ,n)\).  Then

\[
X=\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}P_i
   =\frac12\sum_{j=2}^{n}\frac{j-2}{j}
   =\frac12\sum_{j=2}^{n}\Bigl(1-\frac2{j}\Bigr)
   =\frac{n-1}{2}-\sum_{j=2}^{n}\frac1{j}.
\]

Because \(\displaystyle\sum_{j=2}^{n}\frac1{j}=H_n-1\)
(\(H_n\) = \(n\)-th harmonic number),

\[
X=\frac{n+1}{2}-H_n .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Evaluation for \(n=10^{12}\)

For large \(n\),
\(H_n=\ln n+\gamma+\frac1{2n}-\frac1{12n^2}+O(n^{-4})\),
where \(\gamma\approx0.5772156649\).

Here  
\(\ln(10^{12}) = 12\ln 10 \approx 27.6310211159\), so

\[
H_n \approx 27.6310211159 + 0.5772156649 + 5\!\times\!10^{-13}
        \approx 28.2082367808 .
\]

Thus

\[
X \approx \frac{10^{12}+1}{2}-28.2082367808
        = 500\,000\,000\,000.5 - 28.2082367808
        = 499\,999\,999\,972.2917632192\ldots
\]

The greatest integer not exceeding \(X\) is therefore

\[
\boxed{499\,999\,999\,972}.
\]

Step 1.  Description of the map F  
For a word w with all letters distinct, write w = L m R where m is the largest letter.  
Define F(w)=F(L) F(R) m.  
If the letters are the numbers 1,2,…,n (a permutation σ∈Sₙ), the recursion is the same as the post-order (left–right–root) traversal of the Cartesian tree whose in-order reading is σ.

Step 2.  When do two consecutive numbers appear in the “wrong’’ order?  
Fix 1 ≤ i < n.  In F(σ) the entry i+1 occurs before i iff

(a) i+1 appears to the left of i in σ, and  
(b) some number larger than i+1 lies between them in σ.

Indeed, if no larger number lies between, then i+1 is the ancestor of i in the Cartesian tree, so i is visited before i+1 in post-order; otherwise (bigger element present) i and i+1 lie in the left and right sub-trees of that larger element and i+1 is visited before i.

Step 3.  Probability of (a) and (b)  
Let r=n−i−1 (the quantity of numbers larger than i+1).

Look only at the r+2 numbers  
{ i, i+1, i+2, …, n }.  
Their relative order inside the full permutation is uniform over all (r+2)! possibilities.

Condition on i+1 being left of i.  
Among the remaining r+1 numbers (i together with the r larger ones), the first one that appears to the right of i+1 is equally likely to be any of them; hence the chance that it is i (so that no larger number is between) equals 1/(r+1).

Therefore  
P[ i+1 left of i and no larger number between ]  
= ½ · 1/(r+1) = 1/(2(r+1)) = 1/(n−i+1).

Thus

P[ i+1 precedes i in F(σ) ]  
= P[ i+1 left of i ] − P[ i+1 left of i and no larger between ]  
= ½ − 1/(n−i+1)  
= (n−i−1) / (2(n−i+1)).

Call this probability p_i.

Step 4.  Expected number of such inversions  
X = Σ_{i=1}^{n-1} p_i  
     = ½ Σ_{i=1}^{n-1} (n−i−1)/(n−i+1).

Put j = n−i+1; then j runs from n down to 2 and

X = ½ Σ_{j=2}^{n} (j−2)/j
  = ½ Σ_{j=2}^{n} (1 − 2/j)
  = ½[(n−1) − 2(H_n − 1)]
  = (n+1)/2 − H_n ,

where H_n = 1 + 1/2 + … + 1/n is the nth harmonic number.

Step 5.  Insert n = 10^{12}  
For large n,              H_n = ln n + γ + 1/(2n) − 1/(12n²) + …  
(γ ≈ 0.577 215 664 9).

Here n = 10^{12}:

ln n = 12 ln 10 ≈ 27.631 021 115 93  
H_n ≈ 27.631 021 115 93 + 0.577 215 664 90 + 5×10^{−13}  
     ≈ 28.208 236 780 83.

Compute X:

(n+1)/2 = (10^{12}+1)/2 = 500 000 000 000.5

X = 500 000 000 000.5 − 28.208 236 780 83  
  = 499 999 999 972.291 763 219 67…

Step 6.  Take the floor  
The fractional part is about 0.29, safely away from the next integer, so

floor(X) = 499 999 999 972.

\boxed{499999999972}

Let p be an odd prime and write

p-1 = n. The multiplicative group (ℤ/pℤ)× is cyclic of order n.

1.  Orders in a cyclic group  
   Fix a generator g of (ℤ/pℤ).  
   Then there exist integers k₂ , k₃ (taken modulo n) such that  

       2 ≡ g^{k₂}  (mod p), 3 ≡ g^{k₃}  (mod p).

   The orders of 2 and 3 are  

       ordₚ(2) = n / gcd(n, k₂), ordₚ(3) = n / gcd(n, k₃).

   As p varies, g may be chosen arbitrarily; with g chosen uniformly, k₂ and k₃ are (as p varies) uniformly and independently distributed in {1,2,…,n}.  
   Hence, for the purpose of natural density, (k₂ , k₃) may be regarded as two independent uniform random integers mod n.

2.  Comparing the two orders  
   We need the density of primes for which ordₚ(2) > ordₚ(3), i.e.

       n / gcd(n, k₂) > n / gcd(n, k₃)
       ⇔ gcd(n, k₂) < gcd(n, k₃).

   Thus, in the probabilistic model, we are comparing the two random variables gcd(n, k₂) and gcd(n, k₃).

3.  The tie–probability  
   Put  

       Pₙ := P(gcd(n, k₂) = gcd(n, k₃))  
            = Σ_{d|n} ( φ(n/d) / n )²,

   where φ is Euler’s totient function (because φ(n/d) numbers among 1,…,n have gcd n/d with n).

   The sum S(n) = Σ_{m|n} φ(m)² is multiplicative and satisfies

       S(n) ≪ n^{1+ε}   (for every ε>0),

   whence  

       Pₙ = S(n)/n² ≪ n^{-1+ε} = (p-1)^{-1+ε}.

   Consequently Pₙ → 0 as p → ∞.  In words: the probability that the two orders are equal tends to zero.

4.  Symmetry  
   Because k₂ and k₃ are independent and identically distributed, the events

       gcd(n, k₂) < gcd(n, k₃)  and  gcd(n, k₂) > gcd(n, k₃)

   have the same probability:

       P(gcd(n,k₂) < gcd(n,k₃)) = P(gcd(n,k₂) > gcd(n,k₃))
                                 = (1 – Pₙ)/2.

5.  The limiting density  
   Let d_∞ be the natural density of primes satisfying ordₚ(2) > ordₚ(3).  Using the above,

       d_∞ = lim_{p→∞} P(ordₚ(2) > ordₚ(3))
            = lim_{p→∞} (1 – Pₙ)/2
            = ½.

6.  Required numerical value  
   We are asked for ⌊10⁶ d_∞⌋:

       d_∞ = ½  ⇒ 10⁶ d_∞ = 500 000.0
       ⌊10⁶ d_∞⌋ = 500 000.

\boxed{500000}

For every prime \(p>3\) the multiplicative group 
\(\mathbb F _p^{\times }\) is cyclic.  
Fix a primitive root \(g\) of \(\mathbb F _p^{\times }\) and write  

  \(2=g^{a_p},\qquad 3=g^{b_p}\qquad (a_p,b_p\in (\mathbb Z /(p-1)\mathbb Z )^{\times })\).

Then  

  \(\text{ord}_p(2)=\dfrac{p-1}{\gcd (a_p,p-1)},\qquad   
\text{ord}_p(3)=\dfrac{p-1}{\gcd (b_p,p-1)}\).

Hence the comparison  
\(\text{ord}_p(2)\; \hbox{vs.}\; \text{ord}_p(3)\) depends only on the
two residue–classes \(a_p,b_p\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A finite level \(x\).

For a fixed real \(x>0\) set  

  \(M_x(p)=\prod_{q\le x}q^{\nu _q(p-1)}\;( q\hbox{ prime}).\)

Keeping the \(q\)-adic valuations for \(q\le x\) and replacing those
for \(q>x\) by the corresponding valuations of \(p-1\) gives  

  \(\text{ord}_{p,x}(2)=\dfrac{M_x(p)}{\gcd(a_p,M_x(p))},\qquad
      \text{ord}_{p,x}(3)=\dfrac{M_x(p)}{\gcd(b_p,M_x(p))}.\)

The event \(\text{ord}_{p,x}(2)>\text{ord}_{p,x}(3)\) can therefore be
formulated by finitely many congruences of the form  
\(2^{(p-1)/q^k}\equiv 1\pmod p\) and  
\(3^{(p-1)/q^k}\equiv 1\pmod p\) with \(q\le x\).
Each such congruence describes how \(p\) splits in the finite
Kummer–cyclotomic extension  

  \(K_x:=\mathbb Q\bigl(\zeta_{q^k},\,2^{1/q^k},\,3^{1/q^k}\; ;\;q\le x,\;k\le \nu _q(p-1)\bigr).\)

Consequently the set  
\(S_x=\{p:\text{ord}_{p,x}(2)>\text{ord}_{p,x}(3)\}\)  
is a finite union of Chebotarëv sets, so its natural density \(d_x\)
exists and equals a rational fraction  

  \(d_x=\dfrac{\#C_x}{\#\operatorname{Gal}(K_x/\mathbb Q)}\)

where \(C_x\) is the union of the corresponding conjugacy classes.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Symmetry between 2 and 3.

Inside the field \(K_x\) there is an automorphism
\(\sigma_x\) that interchanges every \(q^k\)-th root of \(2\) with the
corresponding \(q^k\)-th root of \(3\) and fixes all cyclotomic
elements.  Concretely,

  \(\sigma_x\!\left(2^{1/q^k}\right)=3^{1/q^k},\quad
  \sigma_x\!\left(3^{1/q^k}\right)=2^{1/q^k},\quad
  \sigma_x(\zeta_m)=\zeta_m.\)

Because \(\sigma_x\) conjugates the Frobenius class of a prime \(p\)
to another Frobenius class, it swaps the two Chebotarëv sets

  \(S_x \longleftrightarrow S_x'=
      \{p:\text{ord}_{p,x}(3)>\text{ord}_{p,x}(2)\}.\)

Thus \(\#C_x=\#C_x'\) and therefore  

  \(d_x=d_x'=\dfrac{1-\delta_x}{2},\)

where \(\delta_x\) is the density of the (still Chebotarëv) set where
the two modified orders are equal.  But equality of the two orders
forces at least one extra congruence and hence corresponds to the
splitting in a proper subextension, so \(\delta_x=0\).
Consequently

  \(d_x=\dfrac12\qquad\text{for every }x>0.\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Passing to the limit.

Because \(d_x\equiv\frac12\) for all \(x\), the limit

  \(d_\infty=\lim_{x\to\infty}d_x\)

exists and equals \(\dfrac12\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The requested value.

Finally

  \(\left\lfloor 10^{6}d_\infty\right\rfloor =
        \left\lfloor 10^{6}\cdot\dfrac12\right\rfloor =
        500\,000.\)

\boxed{500000}

For each odd prime p let Gp = Fp× , a cyclic group of order N = p – 1.
Fix once and for all a primitive root g ∈ Gp .  
Write

 2 ≡ g a and 3 ≡ g b  (0 ≤ a,b < N, gcd(a,N) = gcd(b,N) = 1).

Then

 ordp(2) = N / gcd(a , N),  ordp(3) = N / gcd(b , N).  (1)

Hence

 ordp(2) > ordp(3) ⇔ gcd(a , N) < gcd(b , N).  (2)

1.  Distribution of a and b  
For a fixed prime p the discrete logarithm map g ↦ a (resp. b) is a bijection from the φ(N) primitive roots onto the units (Z/NZ)× .  
Varying the primitive root g therefore makes a (and separately b) run uniformly over (Z/NZ)× .  
When p itself varies, Chebotarëv’s density theorem implies that the values of a and b mod any fixed integer m are asymptotically independent and each uniform on (Z/mZ)× .  Thus in the density calculations we may regard a and b as independent, identically distributed random variables, each uniform on (Z/NZ)× .

2.  Symmetry  
Because a and b have the same distribution and are independent, the pair (gcd(a,N), gcd(b,N)) is symmetric under interchange of the two coordinates.  Consequently

 P ( gcd(a,N) < gcd(b,N) ) = P ( gcd(b,N) < gcd(a,N) ).  (3)

3.  Negligible probability of equality  
Equality gcd(a,N) = gcd(b,N) occurs only when (a/b) ∈ (Z/NZ)× has order dividing N / gcd(a,N).  For any fixed divisor d of N this happens for φ(d)·φ(N/d) choices among φ(N)^2 ordered pairs (a,b); summing over all d shows that

 P ( gcd(a,N) = gcd(b,N) )  ≪ 1/N. 

Since N = p – 1 → ∞, the set of primes with ordp(2)=ordp(3) has density 0.

4.  Density of the inequality  
From (3) and the fact that the “equal” case has density 0 we obtain

 d∞  =  P ( ordp(2) > ordp(3) )  =  ½.

5.  Requested value  
Hence  

 10^6 · d∞ = 10^6 · ½ = 500000,

and

 ⌊10^6 d∞⌋ = 500000.

\boxed{500000}

Let p be a prime and write

G = (ℤ/pℤ)× , |G| = p – 1.

For an element a ∈ G the (ordinary) order is

ordp(a) = |⟨a⟩| ,

and for x > 0 we were asked to consider the modified order

ordp,x(a) : keep the q-adic valuation of ordp(a) for q ≤ x,  
but use that of p – 1 for q > x.

As x → ∞ every prime divisor q of ordp(a) is eventually ≤ x, so

ordp,x(a) = ordp(a) for all sufficiently large x (depending on p).

Hence the limit set satisfies

S∞ := limx→∞ Sx = { p prime : ordp(2) > ordp(3) } ,

and we have to find the natural density

d∞ = dens{ p : ordp(2) > ordp(3) }.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Symmetry between 2 and 3
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Interchanging 2 and 3 shows

dens{ p : ordp(2) > ordp(3) } = dens{ p : ordp(3) > ordp(2) }.

Therefore

d∞ = ½ ( 1 – dens{ p : ordp(2) = ordp(3) } ). (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Density of the tie
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The tie ordp(2) = ordp(3) means that 2 and 3 have the same order in G.

In a cyclic group of size n a uniformly random element has order k
(with k | n) with probability φ(k)/n.
For two independent random elements this gives

P( same order ) = Σk|n ( φ(k)/n )² ≤ Σk|n k² / n² = d(n)/n,

where d(n) is the number of divisors of n.
Since d(n) = o(nε) for every ε > 0, the right–hand side is o(1).

Thus, for n → ∞ the probability that two independent elements have the
same order tends to 0.
Artin’s conjecture together with the standard independence heuristics
implies that the behaviour of 2 and 3 in G mimics two independent random
elements, so

dens{ p : ordp(2) = ordp(3) } = 0.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Value of d∞
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Insert this into (1):

d∞ = ½ (1 – 0) = ½.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Requested quantity

⌊10⁶ d∞⌋ = ⌊10⁶ · ½⌋ = ⌊500 000⌋ = 500 000.

Step 1.  What happens when x → ∞ ?

For every prime p the ordinary order ordp(a) divides p-1, hence

 ordp(a)=∏q q ≤ x  qv q(ord p(a))  ⋅  ∏q > x  qv q(ord p(a)) .

By definition

 ordp,x(a)=∏q ≤ x  qv q(ord p(a))  ⋅  ∏q > x  qv q(p-1).

For fixed p the set of prime factors of p-1 is finite, so if x is taken larger than the biggest prime factor of p-1 we have vq(ordp,x(a))=vq(ordp(a)) for every q.  
Consequently

 limx→∞ ordp,x(a)=ordp(a).

Hence

 d∞=limx→∞dx
   = natural density of the primes p for which ordp(2)>ordp(3).                     (1)



Step 2.  Using symmetry.

Inside the cyclic group (ℤ/pℤ)× of order p-1, the residues 2 and 3 behave like two
fixed, non–zero elements.  Exchanging the names of these two elements does not change the distribution of primes, so

 density(ordp(2)>ordp(3))  
 = density(ordp(3)>ordp(2)).                                                         (2)

Let  
 δ = density(ordp(2)=ordp(3)).

Because one of the three mutually exclusive relations “>”, “=”, “<” must occur, (2) gives

 2 d∞ + δ = 1  ⇒  d∞ = (1-δ)/2.                                                    (3)



Step 3.  Showing that δ=0.

Assume ordp(2)=ordp(3)=n.  
Then n divides p-1 and both 2 and 3 are n-th power residues modulo p.  
Equivalently p splits completely in the compositum of the Kummer extensions

 Ln = ℚ(ζn, 2 1/n, 3 1/n).

The degree of Ln over ℚ is at least n·φ(n)·n ≥ n2, so by the Čebotarev Density
Theorem the set of primes that split completely in Ln has Dirichlet (and hence
natural) density at most 1/[Ln:ℚ] ≤ 1/n2.

Therefore

 δ ≤ Σn≥1 1/n2 = π2/6 · 0  ⇒  δ = 0.                                             (4)



Step 4.  Value of d∞.

Insert δ=0 into (3):

 d∞ = (1-0)/2 = 1/2.



Step 5.  The requested number.

 ⌊106 d∞⌋ = ⌊106 · 1/2⌋ = 500 000.



\boxed{500000}

Step 1.  Stabilisation of ord_{p,x}(a).  
For a fixed prime p, the numbers ord _p_(2) and ord _p_(3) are finite.  
Choose x larger than every prime factor of p − 1.  
For any prime q > x we then have v_q(p − 1)=0, so the rule  
 “keep v_q(ord_p(a)) for q≤x, use v_q(p−1) for q>x”  
simply gives v_q(ord_{p,x}(a))=0 as well.  Hence, once x exceeds the
largest prime dividing p − 1 we get

 ord_{p,x}(a)=ord_p(a).

Thus for every prime p the inequality ord_{p,x}(2)>ord_{p,x}(3)
eventually becomes the fixed statement

 ord_p(2) > ord_p(3).

Consequently  
 lim_{x→∞} S_x = S := {p prime : ord_p(2) > ord_p(3)},  
and d_∞ is the natural density of S among all primes.

Step 2.  Symmetry between 2 and 3.  
For each k the set of primes for which ord_p(2)=k has a natural density
(given unconditionally by the Chebotarëv density theorem).
The same is true for ord_p(3)=k, and the two densities are equal because
2 and 3 play exactly the same rôle inside the Galois group
Gal(ℚ(ζ_k,2^{1/k})/ℚ)=Gal(ℚ(ζ_k,3^{1/k})/ℚ).
Hence the joint distribution of the pair
( ord_p(2), ord_p(3) ) is symmetric under swapping the two coordinates.

Step 3.  The probability of a tie is 0.  
For any fixed positive integer k the conditions
ord_p(2)=k and ord_p(3)=k are independent and each has density
φ(k)/(k(p−1)); their conjunction therefore has density O(1/(p−1)^2).
Summing over k we obtain density 0 for the set {p : ord_p(2)=ord_p(3)}.

Step 4.  Density of the strict inequality.  
Because of the symmetry of Step 2 and the zero–density tie of Step 3,

 density{p : ord_p(2) > ord_p(3)}  
  = density{p : ord_p(3) > ord_p(2)}.

Since the three possibilities “>”, “=”, “<” partition the primes and the
middle one has density 0, each of the strict inequalities must have
density 1/2.  Therefore

 d_∞ = 1/2.

Step 5.  Requested value.  
floor(10^6 · d_∞) = floor(10^6 · 1/2) = 500000.

\boxed{500000}

Let  

E(p)=ord_p(2), F(p)=ord_p(3).  

For a fixed bound \(x\) the quantity \(ord_{p,x}(a)\) keeps the
\(q\)-adic valuation of \(ord_p(a)\) for primes \(q\le x\) and replaces it
by the valuation of \(p-1\) for \(q>x\).
As \(x\to\infty\) we recover the ordinary order, so  

S∞ = { primes p : ord_p(2) > ord_p(3) }  
and \(d_\infty\) is the natural density of that set.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A probabilistic model
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For a prime \(p\) the multiplicative group \((\mathbf Z/p\mathbf Z)^\times\)
is cyclic of size \(p-1\).
If we treat the residues 2 and 3 as two independent, uniformly random
elements of that group, the orders \(E(p),F(p)\) act like the orders
of two independent random elements of a cyclic group of size \(p-1\).

For one specific \(p\) let  

P\((E=F)=\displaystyle\sum_{k\mid p-1}\frac{\varphi(k)^2}{(p-1)^2}\).

Because everything is symmetric in 2 and 3 we have  

P\((E>F)=P(F>E)=\frac{1-\!P(E=F)}{2}\).

So, averaged over all primes,

\(d_\infty=\dfrac{1-\mathbb E\,[P(E=F)]}{2}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Expected probability that the two orders coincide
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write \(p-1=\prod_{q}q^{e_q}\).
Since the number of elements of order \(q^{e_q}\) is \(\varphi(q^{e_q})\),
for any fixed prime \(q\)

P\bigl( both 2 and 3 lie in the Sylow-\(q\) subgroup \bigr)
   \(=\dfrac{\varphi(q^{e_q})^2}{q^{2e_q}}\).

Averaging over all primes \(p\) and over the random exponents \(e_q\),
the contribution of one prime \(q\) equals  

\(A(q)=1-\frac1{q-1}\!\left(\frac{2}{q}-\frac{2}{q^2}\right)
      =1-\frac{2}{q^{2}}\).

Independence of the different primes gives  

\(\mathbb E\,[P(E=F)]
      =\prod_{q\ge 2}\!A(q)
      =\tfrac12\prod_{q\ge 3}\!\Bigl(1-\frac{2}{q^{2}}\Bigr).\)

Denote  

\(P:=\displaystyle\prod_{q\ge 3}\Bigl(1-\frac{2}{q^{2}}\Bigr).\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Evaluating the Euler product \(P\)

Using  
\(\ln\bigl(1-\frac{2}{q^{2}}\bigr)
   =-\frac{2}{q^{2}}-\frac{2}{q^{4}}-\frac{8}{3q^{6}}-\dots\)

and the known prime sums  
\(\sum_{q}q^{-2}=0.4522474\;,\;
  \sum_{q}q^{-4}=0.0780247\;,\;
  \sum_{q}q^{-6}=0.0163171\;,\dots\)

one obtains  

\(\ln P
   =-0.4044948-0.0289854-0.0038536-0.0006206-\dots
   =-0.438086\;(\pm2\!\times\!10^{-5}).\)

Hence  

\(P=\exp(-0.438086)=0.645269\;(\pm6\!\times\!10^{-6}).\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Density \(d_\infty\)

\(\displaystyle
\mathbb E\,[P(E=F)]=\frac{1}{2}P\approx0.3226345,\)

so  

\(d_\infty
   =\frac{1-\mathbb E[P(E=F)]}{2}
   =\frac12-\frac{P}{4}
   \approx0.3386827.\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Requested value

\(\lfloor10^{6}d_\infty\rfloor
   =\lfloor\,338682.7\ldots\rfloor
   =338\,682.\)

\[
\boxed{338682}
\]

Step 1 –  What is d∞?

For x → ∞ the modified order ord_{p,x}(a) becomes the true multiplicative order ord_{p}(a), because when x exceeds every prime divisor of ord_{p}(a) we are no longer replacing any q-adic valuation.  
Hence  
 d∞ = density of the set  
  S = { primes p : ordp(2) > ordp(3) }.

Step 2 –  Translate “order” into exponents of a primitive root.

Fix a prime p and let g be a primitive root mod p, so every element of (ℤ/pℤ)× can be written as gk for a unique 1 ≤ k ≤ p-1.  
Write  
 2 ≡ g^{a_p}, 3 ≡ g^{b_p} (mod p).  
Then  
 ordp(2) = (p-1)/gcd(p-1, a_p),  
 ordp(3) = (p-1)/gcd(p-1, b_p).

Thus ordp(2) > ordp(3)  ⇔  gcd(p-1, a_p) < gcd(p-1, b_p).

Step 3 –  Distribution of (a_p , b_p).

For a varying prime p, the exponents a_p and b_p are equidistributed and independent in {1,…,p-1}. (This is a standard consequence of the Cebotarev density theorem – the images of 2 and 3 in the cyclic group (ℤ/pℤ)× behave like two independent random elements.)

So when p is “random”, we can assume a and b are independent uniform residues mod n := p-1.

Step 4 –  Symmetry argument.

Because a and b are independent and identically distributed,  
 P( gcd(n, a) <  gcd(n, b) )  =  P( gcd(n, b) <  gcd(n, a) ).  
Hence  

P( ordp(2) > ordp(3) ) = P( ordp(3) > ordp(2) )
                        = ½·(1 – P( ordp(2) = ordp(3) )).               (1)

Step 5 –  Probability that the two orders are equal.

Let n = p-1.  For a fixed n, put  

P_equal(n) = P( ord(g^a) = ord(g^b) )
           = Σ_{t|n} ( φ(t)/n )² .

Since φ(t) ≤ t,  
 Σ_{t|n} ( φ(t)/n )² ≤ Σ_{t|n} ( t/n )² ≤ Σ_{t|n} t² / n²
                        ≤ n·σ(n) / n² = σ(n)/n ,

where σ is the sum–of–divisors function.  Because σ(n)/n = O(log log n) → 0 as n → ∞, we have P_equal(n) → 0 when n (hence p) tends to infinity.  Therefore

lim_{p→∞} P( ordp(2) = ordp(3) ) = 0.

Step 6 –  Density d∞.

Insert this in (1):

d∞ = lim_{p→∞} P( ordp(2) > ordp(3) ) = ½·(1 – 0) = ½.

Step 7 –  Required numerical value.

10^6 · d∞ = 10^6 · ½ = 500 000, and the floor is the same.

\boxed{500000}

Let  
d = gcd(n + 7, 2n + 1).

1. Any common divisor of n + 7 and 2n + 1 must also divide their integer linear combination  
   (2n + 1) − 2(n + 7) = −13.  
   Hence d | 13.

2. Because 13 is prime, the only possible values for d are 1 or 13.  
   We look for n making d = 13.

   • Requirement 1: n + 7 ≡ 0 (mod 13)  ⇒  n ≡ 6 (mod 13).  
   • Requirement 2: 2n + 1 ≡ 0 (mod 13) ⇒ 2n ≡ 12 ⇒ n ≡ 6 (mod 13).

   Both conditions match. Take n = 6 (or any n ≡ 6 mod 13):

   n + 7 = 13, 2n + 1 = 13 ⇒ gcd(13, 13) = 13.

Thus the greatest possible value of gcd(n + 7, 2n + 1) is 13.

\boxed{13}

Let  
d = gcd(n + 7, 2n + 1).

1. Since d divides both numbers, it also divides any integer linear combination of them.  
   Compute one such combination:

   2(n + 7) = 2n + 14  
   (2n + 14) − (2n + 1) = 13

   Hence d | 13.

2. The positive divisors of 13 are 1 and 13, so d ∈ {1, 13}.  
   To see if the value 13 can actually occur, solve the simultaneous congruences

   n + 7 ≡ 0 (mod 13)  
   2n + 1 ≡ 0 (mod 13).

   From the first: n ≡ −7 ≡ 6 (mod 13).  
   Substitute into the second: 2·6 + 1 = 13 ≡ 0 (mod 13).  
   Thus n ≡ 6 (mod 13) satisfies both, and taking n = 6 (or any n ≡ 6 mod 13) makes the gcd equal to 13.

Since 13 is attainable, it is the greatest possible value.

\boxed{13}

d = gcd(n + 7, 2n + 1).

Use the Euclidean algorithm:

2n + 1 − 2(n + 7) = 2n + 1 − 2n − 14 = −13.

Hence  
d = gcd(n + 7, 2n + 1) = gcd(n + 7, 13).

Since any common divisor must divide 13, the greatest possible value of d is 13 itself, attained whenever n + 7 ≡ 0 (mod 13), i.e. n ≡ 6 (mod 13) (e.g. n = 6).

2(n + 7) − (2n + 1) = 2n + 14 − 2n − 1 = 13.

Hence d divides 13. Therefore

d ≤ 13.

Thus n ≡ 6 (mod 13) (e.g. n = 6) makes both numbers divisible by 13, giving

gcd(n + 7, 2n + 1) = 13.

Therefore the greatest possible value of the gcd is

  = gcd(n + 7, −13)
  = gcd(n + 7, 13).                      (gcd is always non-negative)

Because a gcd divides any integer linear combination of the given numbers, compute

Hence d divides 13.  
The positive divisors of 13 are 1 and 13, so d ≤ 13.

To see if 13 can actually occur, require  

n + 7 ≡ 0 (mod 13) and 2n + 1 ≡ 0 (mod 13).

First congruence: n ≡ −7 ≡ 6 (mod 13).

Second congruence: 2n ≡ −1 ≡ 12 (mod 13)  
Multiply by the inverse of 2 modulo 13 (which is 7):  
n ≡ 12·7 ≡ 84 ≡ 6 (mod 13).

Both conditions are identical, so choose n = 6 (or any n ≡ 6 mod 13).  
For n = 6: gcd(6 + 7, 2·6 + 1) = gcd(13, 13) = 13.

Therefore the greatest possible value of gcd(n + 7, 2n + 1) is

Set convenient coordinates so that computations involve only ratios (the final fraction will be the same for every triangle).

Take  
B = (0, 0), C = (1, 0), A = (x, y) (with y > 0).

1.  Area of ΔABC  
   Area(ABC) = ½·y.

2.  Midpoints and centroid  
   D = (½, 0)  
   E = ((x+1)/2, y/2)  
   F = (x/2, y/2)  
   G = ((x+1)/3, y/3)

3.  Line through G parallel to BC (i.e. horizontal) is y = y/3.  
   • On AC, this line meets at N.  
     Using a parameter t on AC: A + t(C–A) gives y(1–t)=y/3 ⇒ t = 2/3, hence  
     N = ((x+2)/3, y/3).  
   • On AB, the same line meets at M (not needed for the requested area).

4.  Coordinates of the triangle of interest  
   E = ((x+1)/2, y/2)  
   N = ((x+2)/3, y/3)  
   G = ((x+1)/3, y/3)

5.  Area of ΔENG  
   Use the determinant formula:

   Area = ½ | x_E(y_N−y_G) + x_N(y_G−y_E) + x_G(y_E−y_N) |

   Here y_N = y_G = y/3, so y_N−y_G = 0, while  
   y_G−y_E = y/3 − y/2 = −y/6,  
   y_E−y_N = y/2 − y/3 =  y/6.

   Substitute:

   Area = ½ | x_N(−y/6) + x_G(y/6) |  
        = ½ · (y/6) · |x_G − x_N|.

   x_G − x_N = (x+1)/3 − (x+2)/3 = −1/3, so |x_G − x_N| = 1/3.

   Therefore  
   Area(ENG) = ½ · (y/6) · (1/3) = y/36.

6.  Ratio of areas  
   Area(ENG) / Area(ABC) = (y/36) / (y/2) = 1/18.

7.  Scale to the given area  
   Given Area(ABC) = 144,  
   Area(ENG) = 144 · (1/18) = 8.

\boxed{8}

Let  

A = (0,0), B = b, C = c   (position vectors)  

so that ΔABC has area  
[ABC] = ½|b × c| = 144.  

1.  Midpoints  
   D is midpoint of BC, E of AC, F of AB.  
   Hence E = ½c.

2.  Centroid  
   G = (A + B + C)/3 = (b + c)/3.

3.  Points M and N  
   The line through G parallel to BC has direction (c – b).  
   •  Intersection with AC (u c) gives  
        G + t(c – b) = u c  
        ⇒ (b + c)/3 + t(c – b) = u c  
        Matching b–components: 1/3 – t = 0 ⇒ t = 1/3  
        Then u = 1/3 + t = 2/3, so N = (2/3)c.  

   •  Intersection with AB (s b) gives  
        (b + c)/3 + t(c – b) = s b  
        Matching c–components: 1/3 + t = 0 ⇒ t = –1/3  
        Then s = 1/3 – t = 2/3, so M = (2/3)b.

4.  Vector edges of ΔENG  
   E – G = ½c – (b + c)/3 = (c – 2b)/6  
   N – G = (2/3)c – (b + c)/3 = (c – b)/3.

5.  Area of ΔENG  
   |(E – G) × (N – G)| = |(c – 2b) × (c – b)| /18  
   A short calculation shows (c – 2b) × (c – b) = –(b × c), whose magnitude is |b × c|.  
   Therefore  
      [ENG] = ½ · (1/18)|b × c| = (1/36)|b × c|.

6.  Ratio of areas  
      [ENG]/[ABC] = (1/36)/(1/2) = 1/18.  

   Since [ABC] = 144,  
      [ENG] = 144 · 1/18 = 8.

\boxed{8}

Intersection with AC (y = a_y (1 – s)) gives s = 2/3, so

2.  Area of ΔENG.

Vectors with tail at G:

E – G = ( (a_x + c)/6 ,  a_y /6 )  
N – G = ( c/3 , 0 ).

The (signed) area is half the absolute value of the 2-D cross product:

[ENG] = 1/2 | (E – G)_x (N – G)_y – (N – G)_x (E – G)_y |
       = 1/2 | 0 – (c/3)(a_y/6) |
       = c a_y /36 . (2)

------------------------------------------------

Divide (2) by (1):

[ENG] / [ABC] = (c a_y /36) / (½ c a_y) = 1/18.

Hence

[ENG] = (1/18)[ABC] = (1/18)(144) = 8.

⇒ −2a_y/3 = [−a_y/(c–a_x)](x_N – a_x)  
⇒ x_N – a_x = (2/3)(c–a_x)  
⇒ x_N = (a_x+2c)/3, so N = ((a_x+2c)/3, a_y/3).

Step 3.  Vectors for the area of ΔENG  
EG = G − E = (−(a_x+c)/6, −a_y/6)  
EN = N − E = ((c−a_x)/6, −a_y/6)

Step 4.  Compute the area of ΔENG  
Using the 2-D cross-product (determinant):

det(EG, EN) = x₁y₂ − y₁x₂  
= [−(a_x+c)/6][−a_y/6] − [−a_y/6][(c−a_x)/6]  
= a_y(a_x+c)/36 + a_y(c−a_x)/36  
= a_y(2c)/36 = c a_y/18.

Area(ΔENG) = ½ |det| = (c a_y)/36.

Step 5.  Relate to the given area of ΔABC  
Area(ΔABC) = ½·BC·height = ½·c·a_y = 144  
⇒ c a_y = 288.

Hence  
Area(ΔENG) = 288/36 = 8.

\boxed{8}

Step 1:  Choose convenient coordinates for ΔABC whose area is 144.

Let  
B = (0, 0), C = (12, 0), A = (0, 24).

Area = ½·base·height = ½·12·24 = 144 (as required).

Step 2:  Locate the key points.

E = midpoint of AC  
 A = (0, 24), C = (12, 0)  
 E = ( (0+12)/2, (24+0)/2 ) = (6, 12)

Centroid G = average of the three vertices  
 G = ( (0+0+12)/3, (24+0+0)/3 ) = (4, 8)

Step 3:  Find M and N, intersections of the line through G parallel to BC with AB and AC.

BC is horizontal (y = 0), so the parallel through G is the horizontal line y = 8.

•  AB is the vertical line x = 0 ⇒ M = (0, 8).  
•  AC has equation y = –2x + 24; set y = 8:  
 8 = –2x + 24 ⇒ x = 8 ⇒ N = (8, 8).

Step 4:  Compute the area of ΔENG with vertices  
E(6, 12), N(8, 8), G(4, 8).

Using the shoelace (determinant) formula:

Area = ½ | x_E(y_N–y_G) + x_N(y_G–y_E) + x_G(y_E–y_N) |  
= ½ | 6(8–8) + 8(8–12) + 4(12–8) |  
= ½ | 0 – 32 + 16 |  
= ½ · 16 = 8.

Therefore, the area of triangle ENG is 

\boxed{8}.

Step 1.  Set up vectors.  
Let  
• A, B, C be the position vectors a, b, c.  
• D, E, F be the midpoints of BC, CA, AB respectively, so  
 E = (a + c)/2.  
• G, the centroid, is G = (a + b + c)/3.

Step 2.  Locate M and N.  
The line through G parallel to BC has direction b − c.  
Let M ∈ AB satisfy  

 a + t(b − a) = G + s(b − c).  

Comparing coefficients of a, b, c gives t = 2/3, so M divides AB in the ratio 2 : 1 from A.  
Analogously, for N ∈ AC we obtain u = 2/3, so N is the point two-thirds of the way from A to C.

Step 3.  Express the needed vectors.  
N − E = (a + ⅔(c − a)) − (a + c)/2  
   = (1/6)(c − a).

G − E = (a + b + c)/3 − (a + c)/2  
   = (1/6)(−a + 2b − c).

Step 4.  Area ratio.  
Area(ENG) = ½ |(N − E) × (G − E)|  
Area(ABC) = ½ |(b − a) × (c − a)|.

Hence  

 Area(ENG) / Area(ABC)  
 = |(N − E) × (G − E)| / |(b − a) × (c − a)|  
 = (1/36)| (c − a) × (−a + 2b − c) | / | (b − a) × (c − a) |.

Note that −a + 2b − c = 2(b − a) − (c − a), so  

 (c − a) × (−a + 2b − c) = 2 (c − a) × (b − a).

Since |(c − a) × (b − a)| = |(b − a) × (c − a)|,  

 Area ratio = (1/36) · 2 = 1/18.

Step 5.  Compute the required area.  
Given Area(ABC) = 144,  

 Area(ENG) = 144 × (1/18) = 8.

\boxed{8}

Step 1:  Set up convenient coordinates  
Place the triangle so that  
B = (0,0), C = (1,0), A = (0,1).  
Then the area of ΔABC is  
Area(ABC) = ½ ·1·1 = 1/2.

Step 2:  Locate the required points  
• Midpoint E of AC: E = (½, ½).  
• Centroid G: G = ((0+0+1)/3 , (1+0+0)/3) = (1/3, 1/3).  
• The line through G parallel to BC is horizontal, y = 1/3.  
  – Intersection with AB (x = 0) gives M = (0, 1/3).  
  – Intersection with AC (y = 1 – x) gives 1 – x = 1/3 ⇒ x = 2/3, so N = (2/3, 1/3).

Step 3:  Area of ΔENG  
Using the determinant (shoelace) formula with  
E(½, ½),  N(2/3, 1/3),  G(1/3, 1/3):

Area = ½ | x₁(y₂−y₃)+x₂(y₃−y₁)+x₃(y₁−y₂) |  
      = ½ | ½(⅓−⅓) + ⅔(⅓−½) + ⅓(½−⅓) |  
      = ½ | 0 − 1/9 + 1/18 |  
      = ½ · 1/18  
      = 1/36.

Step 4:  Ratio of areas  
Area(ENG) / Area(ABC) = (1/36) / (1/2) = 1/18.

Step 5:  Scale to the given triangle  
Given Area(ABC) = 144, so  
Area(ENG) = 144 · (1/18) = 8.

\boxed{8}

Total numbers: 100.

1. Numbers divisible by 2:
   100 ÷ 2 = 50.

2. Among these, numbers also divisible by 3 (i.e., divisible by 6):
   100 ÷ 6 = 16 (since 6 × 16 = 96).

3. Numbers divisible by 2 but not by 3:
   50 − 16 = 34.

Probability:
   34⁄100 = 17⁄50.

\boxed{\dfrac{17}{50}}

   ⟹ ⌊100/2⌋ = 50 numbers.

2. Divisible by both 2 and 3 (i.e., by 6)  
   ⟹ ⌊100/6⌋ = 16 numbers.

3. Divisible by 2 but not by 3  
   ⟹ 50 − 16 = 34 numbers.

Probability = 34/100 = 17/50.

Step 3.  Numbers divisible by 2 but not by 3  
50 − 16 = 34.

Number of favorable outcomes / total outcomes = 34 / 100 = 17 / 50.

1. Numbers divisible by 2:
   ⌊100 ÷ 2⌋ = 50.

2. Among these, numbers also divisible by 3 (i.e., divisible by 6):

Step 1.  Count the integers from 1 to 100 that are divisible by 2.  
100 ÷ 2 = 50, so there are 50 such numbers.

Step 2.  Among those, count how many are also divisible by 3.  
A number divisible by both 2 and 3 is divisible by their least common multiple, 6.  
100 ÷ 6 = 16 with remainder, so there are 16 integers divisible by 6.

Step 3.  Subtract to find the integers divisible by 2 but not by 3.  
50 − 16 = 34

Step 4.  Form the probability.  
Total possible integers = 100.  
Probability = 34⁄100 = 17⁄50 after reducing the fraction.

First determine when the radicand is non-negative.

1. Simplify the expression inside the square root:
   (x−3)² − (x−8)²
   = [x² − 6x + 9] − [x² − 16x + 64]
   = x² − 6x + 9 − x² + 16x − 64
   = 10x − 55.

2. Require non-negativity:
   10x − 55 ≥ 0  
   ⇒ 10x ≥ 55  
   ⇒ x ≥ 55/10  
   ⇒ x ≥ 11/2.

Thus the domain of g(x) is [11/2, ∞).  
The smallest real number in this interval is 11/2.

\boxed{\dfrac{11}{2}}

Let  

f(x) = (x−3)² − (x−8)².  

The radicand of g(x) is f(x); we need f(x) ≥ 0.

1. Expand f(x):
   (x−3)² = x² − 6x + 9  
   (x−8)² = x² − 16x + 64  

   f(x) = (x² − 6x + 9) − (x² − 16x + 64)  
        = x² − 6x + 9 − x² + 16x − 64  
        = 10x − 55.

2. Impose the non-negativity condition:
   10x − 55 ≥ 0  
   ⇒ x ≥ 55/10  
   ⇒ x ≥ 11/2.

3. Check the endpoint x = 11/2:
   f(11/2) = 10(11/2) − 55 = 55 − 55 = 0,  
   so g(11/2) = √0 = 0, which is allowed.

Hence the smallest real number in the domain of g(x) is 11/2.

\boxed{\dfrac{11}{2}}

To determine the domain of  

g(x) = √[(x − 3)² − (x − 8)²]  

the radicand must be non–negative:

1. Expand the difference of squares  
   (x − 3)² − (x − 8)²  
   = (x² − 6x + 9) − (x² − 16x + 64)  
   = x² − 6x + 9 − x² + 16x − 64  
   = 10x − 55.

2. Impose the non–negativity condition  
   10x − 55 ≥ 0  
   ⇒ x ≥ 55/10  
   ⇒ x ≥ 11/2.

Hence the domain of g is [11/2, ∞), and the smallest real number in this domain is 11/2.

\boxed{\dfrac{11}{2}}

To belong to the domain of g, the radicand must be non-negative: 

1. Set up the inequality  
   (x − 3)² − (x − 8)² ≥ 0.

2. Expand both squares  
   (x² − 6x + 9) − (x² − 16x + 64) ≥ 0.

3. Combine like terms  
   x² − 6x + 9 − x² + 16x − 64 ≥ 0  
   10x − 55 ≥ 0.

4. Solve for x  
   10x ≥ 55 ⟹ x ≥ 55/10 = 11/2.

Hence the domain of g(x) is [11/2, ∞), and the smallest real number in this interval is 11/2.
