Let  

A = {a1 , a2 , … , a1000}, 1 ≤ a1 < a2 < … < a1000 ≤ 2017,

and assume that  

(*) for every pair of indices 1 ≤ i , j ≤ 1000  
   if i + j ∈ A, then ai + aj ∈ A.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  Every element of A does not exceed 1000
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Assume on the contrary that A contains a number bigger than 1000.  
Let  

L = max { x ∈ A | x ≤ 2000 }.  (1≤L≤2000 and L>1000)

Because 2 ≤ i + j ≤ 2000 for 1 ≤ i , j ≤ 1000, the number L can be written
as a sum of two indices:

L = (L – 1000) + 1000,  with 1 ≤ L – 1000 ≤ 1000.                (1)

Insert i = L – 1000 and j = 1000 in (*); since L ∈ A, we must have

ai + a1000 ∈ A.                                                (2)

Now, ai ≥ i = L – 1000 and a1000 ≥ L (because L is not larger than the
greatest element a1000), so

ai + a1000 ≥ (L – 1000) + L = 2L – 1000.                      (3)

Because L>1000, the right–hand side of (3) exceeds L, hence

ai + a1000 > a1000.                                           (4)

Two mutually exclusive possibilities arise.

•  If ai + a1000 ≤ 2017, then (2) says that a strictly larger
   number than a1000 also belongs to A, contradicting the fact that
   a1000 is the largest element of A.

•  If ai + a1000 > 2017, then (2) demands that a number greater than
   2017 lie in A, impossible because every element of A is ≤2017.

In both cases we obtain a contradiction; therefore

A contains no number larger than 1000.                         (5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  A contains every integer from 1 to 1000
--------------------------------------------------------------------
By (5) the set A is a subset of {1,2,…,1000}.  
But |A| = 1000, so A must coincide with the whole set:

A = {1,2,…,1000}.                                             (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Verification
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For this set we have ai = i.  
If i + j ∈ A, then i + j ≤ 1000, and

ai + aj = i + j ∈ A,

so condition (*) is fulfilled.  Hence (6) indeed satisfies the
requirement.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  Counting the admissible sets
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because every admissible set must equal {1,2,…,1000}, there is
exactly one such set.

\boxed{1}

Step 1.  Observation about index–value relations  
For any pair of indices 1 ≤ i, j ≤ 1000 we have i + j ≤ 2000.  
Thus, if a number x > 2000 belongs to A it can never equal i + j, so condition  
“if i + j ∈ A then a_i + a_j ∈ A” is never triggered by such an x.

Step 2.  Structure forced below 2001  
Let r be the largest integer for which {1,2,…,r} ⊆ A.  
If some m ≤ r were missing, choose i and j with i+j=m (possible because m≥2): then
i+j∉A and the condition would never speak about m.  
Hence A may freely omit numbers bigger than r.

Step 3.  Why omissions must form a suffix  
Assume a number s with r+1 ≤ s ≤ 1000 is kept while some smaller t (r+1≤t<s) is omitted.  
Take indices i=t and j=1.  
Here i+j=s ∈ A, but  
a_i+a_j = a_t + a_1 = (value of position t) + 1
           = s + 1      (because the order shifts by the omission)
           = t + 1,  
which is not in A (t+1≤s yet t+1 was also omitted).  
Thus the condition fails.  
Therefore, once the first integer bigger than r is omitted, every larger integer up to 1000 must also be omitted; i.e. the missing numbers form the suffix {r+1,r+2,…,1000}.

Step 4.  Admissible form of A  
Consequently every admissible set is of the form  
A = {1,2,…,r} ∪ T  
where  
•  r ranges from 983 to 1000 (because at most 17 numbers can be added later),  
•  T is any subset of the 17 integers 2001,…,2017 having size 1000–r.

Step 5.  Verification of the condition  
Take any indices i, j.

(a)  If i+j ≤ r then i+j ∈ A and i, j ≤ r, so a_i=i and a_j=j.  Hence  
a_i+a_j = i+j ∈ A, satisfying the requirement.

(b)  If i+j > r then i+j ∉ A (because all numbers between r+1 and 2000 are absent), so the requirement is not activated.

Thus every set described in Step 4 satisfies the given condition.

Step 6.  Counting the sets  
Let k = 1000 – r (the number of omitted integers ≤1000 and therefore the size of T).  
k can be any integer from 0 to 17.  
For each k we may choose any k-element subset of the 17 numbers 2001,…,2017, giving  
C(17, k) possibilities.  Summing over k,

∑_{k=0}^{17} C(17,k) = 2^{17} = 131072.

Therefore the number of sets A that satisfy the condition is

\boxed{131072}.

Write the elements of A in increasing order  
A = {a1 < a2 < … < a1000 }.

1.  Numbers that can occur in the hypothesis  
For the indices 1 ≤ i , j ≤ 1000 the sum i + j is always contained in the set  

S = { 2 , 3 , … , 2000 }.                                   (1)

Thus an element of A can take part in the condition of the statement only
if it belongs to S.
The 18 integers

T = { 1 , 2001 , 2002 , … , 2017 }                         (2)

are never of the form i + j (1 ≤ i , j ≤ 1000); they will be called
free numbers – their presence or absence in A never
influences the given condition.

2.  What the condition forces inside S  
Fix an integer m ∈ S and suppose that all numbers 1 , 2 , … , m − 1 are in A
whereas  m ∉ A.
Because m ≤ 2000, write m = p + q with 1 ≤ p , q ≤ 1000.
By construction p + q (= m) is not in A, so the hypothesis of the
statement is not invoked for the pair (p , q).  
Now enlarge m step by step:

m + 1 = p + (q + 1) ,   …   , 2000 = 1000 + 1000              (3)

Sooner or later we meet a number of the form (3) that already lies in A
(the set A contains 1000 different members of S, while only at most
m − 1 of them are forbidden so far).  
Take the first such number  
s = u + v  with  1 ≤ u , v ≤ 1000 and s ∈ A, s > m .          (4)

Because u , v ≤ s − 1 we have u , v < s and therefore all
1 , 2 , … , u , v belong to A.  Consequently

a_u = u   ,   a_v = v .                                       (5)

Applying the given property to the indices (u , v) we must have

a_u + a_v = u + v = s ∈ A ,                                   (6)

which is already true.  But then replace u by u + 1 in (4);
by the minimal choice of s the new sum u + 1 + v exceeds s, hence it is
> 2000, contradicting a_{u+1} ≤ 2017.  
Therefore the assumption m ∉ A is impossible, and we conclude

All numbers 2 , 3 , … , 1000 are contained in A.              (7)

3.  The structure of A ⊂ [1 , 2017]  
Because of (7) there are at most 1000 − 999 = 1 further member of A
not yet identified in S; every other element of A must lie in the free
set T.  Let

t = 0 , 1 , … , 17                                            (8)

be the quantity of indices that we decide to “move” from the
right end of the block  {1 , 2 , … , 1000} to T.
Precisely, for a fixed t we keep

1 , 2 , … , 1000 − t                                          (9)

inside A and replace the t discarded numbers
1000 − t + 1 , … , 1000 by any t different elements chosen from
{ 2001 , 2002 , … , 2017 }.  
Because every i appearing in (9) satisfies a_i = i, any sum
i + j with i , j ≤ 1000 − t again lies in the left block (9),
so the requirement i + j ∈ A ⇒ a_i + a_j ∈ A is automatically fulfilled.
Whenever at least one of the indices is > 1000 − t the sum i + j is
≥ 1000 − t + 1 and therefore not contained in (9); but the
numbers ≥ 2001 that replaced the discarded members are not of the form
i + j, hence the hypothesis of the statement is never activated.
Consequently every set constructed in this way satisfies the demanded
property, and there are no others.

4.  Counting the admissible sets  
For a fixed t we have to choose t numbers from the 17 free
integers 2001 , … , 2017, giving C(17 , t) possibilities.
Summing over t = 0 , 1 , … , 17 we obtain

∑_{t = 0}^{17} C(17 , t) = 2^{17}.                           (10)

Hence the total number of sets A that satisfy the condition equals

\boxed{\,2^{17}\,}.

Put the elements of the required set in increasing order  

A = { a1 , a2 , … , a1000 } ,             a1 < a2 < … < a1000 ≤ 2017.

1.  Numbers larger than 2000 are harmless  
For indices 1 ≤ i , j ≤ 1000 we always have i + j ≤ 2000.  
Hence an element of A that is larger than 2000 can never be of the
form i + j, so the condition of the problem is never invoked for such
an element.  
Consequently every subset of {2001 , 2002 , … , 2017} can be put into
A without causing any difficulty.

There are 17 such numbers, so at most 17 of the 1000 elements of A can
be placed “at the top’’ without interacting with the condition.

2.  What must happen below 2001  
Let m be the amount of room that is left after the (possible) insertion
of these big numbers; i.e. let

m = 1000 – r    with    r = |A ∩ {2001 , … , 2017}|    (0 ≤ r ≤ 17).

So exactly m elements of A lie in the interval [1 , 2000].  
We prove that these m elements have to be the complete initial segment

                       1 , 2 , … , m.                      (★)

Proof.  
Assume to the contrary that some integer s ≤ 2000 is missing from A
while a larger integer t ≤ 2000 does belong to A.  
Write s = p + q with 1 ≤ p , q ≤ 1000 (this is always possible because
2 ≤ s ≤ 2000).  
Then both p and q are smaller than s and therefore (by choice of s)
belong to A; in particular ap = p and aq = q.  
Because t ∈ A, choose indices u , v ≤ 1000 with u + v = t ; the required
property gives

            au + av = ap + aq = p + q = s ∈ A,

contradicting the choice of s.  Thus no integer ≤ 2000 can be skipped
and (★) follows.

From (★) we also get ai = i for every i ≤ m.

3.  A complete description of all admissible sets  
Choose freely a subset R of the 17 “harmless’’ numbers 2001 … 2017;
put those |R| = r numbers at the end of the sequence (they will then be
am = m+1 , am+1 , … , a1000 with m = 1000 – r).  
Precede them with the unavoidable block 1 , 2 , … , m.  
One easily checks (using ai = i for i ≤ m and i + j > m whenever at least
one of i , j exceeds m) that the required condition is fulfilled.

Conversely, Section 2 shows that every set satisfying the condition
must arise in exactly this manner.  Therefore

number of admissible sets  =  number of subsets of the 17 big numbers
                            =  2^17.

\boxed{2^{17}}

Denote  

A = {a1 < a2 < … < a1000 } ⊂ {1 , 2 , … , 2017}  

and write  

g(k)=a_k – k , 1 ≤ k ≤ 1000.                                     (1)

Because the ai are strictly increasing, g(k+1) – g(k)= (a_{k+1}-a_k)-1 ≥0;  
hence the sequence (g(k)) is non–decreasing.                           (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The first element must be 1
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Assume a1 = 1+d with d ≥ 1.  
Because a1 ≤ 2017 and d ≤1017, put  

i = 1000,   j = a1 – 1000  (so 1 ≤ j ≤18 and i , j ≤ 1000).  
Then i + j = a1 ∈ A, so the given condition forces  

a_i + a_j ∈ A.                                                   (3)

By (1) and (2),

a_i ≥ i + d = 1000 + d,
a_j ≥ j + d ≥ 1 + d = a1.

Consequently  

a_i + a_j ≥ (1000 + d) + (1 + d) = 1001 + 2d.                  (4)

But  d ≥ 1 ⇒ 1001 + 2d ≥ 1003, and for every d ≥ 1

1001+2d > 2017 ,                                               (5)

so (4) lies outside {1,…,2017}.  
That contradicts (3).  
Hence d = 0 and

a1 = 1.                                                         (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Induction:  ak = k   for every k
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Suppose ak = k for all k ≤ m (with m < 1000) and assume a_{m+1} ≥ m+2.  
Put i = 1 and j = m+1.  Then

i + j = m+2 .                                                     (7)

Because m+1 ≥ 1,  m+2 ≤ 1000+1 =1001 ≤ 2017; hence (7) is a possible
member of A.  In fact,  by the induction hypothesis the numbers
1,2,…,m+1 are in A, so m+2 ∈ A as well.  Therefore the condition
has to be applied to the pair (i , j) = (1 , m+1):

a_1 + a_{m+1}  =  1 + a_{m+1} ≥ 1 + (m+2) = m+3.                 (8)

Thus m+3 must belong to A.  But among the first m+2 indices every value
up to m+2 is already occupied by the equalities ak = k (k ≤ m+1);
there is no room left for the new element m+3, contradiction.
Hence our assumption was impossible and we must have

a_{m+1} = m+1.                                                  (9)

With a1 =1 from (6) and (9) the induction gives

ak = k  for all  k =1,2,…,1000.                                (10)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The set A is unique
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Relation (10) says that the 1000 elements of A are exactly the first
1000 positive integers.  Conversely, for

A0 = {1,2,…,1000}

the required property is obvious because for any
i , j ≤1000 the equalities ai = i , aj = j give

i + j ∈ A0   ⇒   ai + aj = i + j ∈ A0.

Therefore A0 fulfils the condition and, by (10), it is the only set
that does.

Number of admissible sets
--------------------------------------------------------------------
There is exactly one set A that satisfies the given condition.

\boxed{1}

Call the required set  

A = {a1 < a2 < … < a1000} ⊂ {1,2,…,2017}

and write  

f(k) = ak  (1 ≤ k ≤ 1000).

The hypothesis is  

(*)   for every pair (i , j) with 1 ≤ i , j ≤ 1000  
      if i + j ∈ A then   f(i)+f(j) ∈ A.

Step 1.  1 is in A and a1 = 1.  

Assume a1 ≥ 2.  
If a1 = 2 then (1,1) gives 2 ∈ A so by (*) we would need 4 ∈ A, but A has only one element smaller than 4, contradiction.  
If a1 ≥ 3 then 2 ∉ A.  Take any i with 2 ≤ i ≤ 1000.  
Because i + (1000-i+2) = 1002 ∈ [2,2000] whilst 1002 > 2017-1000, one sees (after checking the two sub-cases i≤1000 and i>1000) that (*) cannot be satisfied.  
Hence a1 = 1.

Step 2.  The map g(k)=ak−k is non–negative and non-decreasing.  

Indeed ak ≥ k (strict increase of the sequence a1<a2<⋯) so g(k) ≥ 0.  
Because ak+1−ak ≥ 1 we have   
g(k+1)−g(k) = (ak+1−ak) − 1 ≥ 0,  
so g is non-decreasing.

Step 3.  If g(p) > 0 for some p (take the smallest such p) a contradiction is obtained.

Let g(p)=d ≥ 1, so ap = p+d.  
Because p is the first index with a surplus, a1=1, a2=2, … , ap-1 = p-1.

(a)  Inductively one shows ak = k+d for every k ≥ p :  
Suppose ak = k+d for some k ≥ p.  
The pair (1,k) satisfies 1+k = ak = k+d ∈ A, so by (*)  
a1 + ak = 1 + (k+d) = k+d+1 ∈ A.  
Since the sequence a1<a2<⋯ is strictly increasing, this new element must be ak+1, i.e. ak+1 = (k+1)+d.  

(b)  Consequently  
a1000 = 1000 + d  (≥ 1001).

(b) now forces 1001 ∈ A, because the set  
{1,2,…,p-1, p+d , p+d+1 , … , 1000+d}  
contains every integer from 1 up to 1000+d except p; in particular it contains 1001.

(c)  Finally use the pair (1,1000):  
1 + 1000 = 1001 ∈ A, so (*) gives  
a1 + a1000 = 1 + (1000+d) = 1001 + d ∈ A.  
But 1001+d > a1000, contradicting the fact that a1000 is the largest element of A.  

Therefore d cannot be positive; hence g(k)=0 for every k and

ak = k   for all k = 1,…,1000.

Step 4.  Verification.  

The set  
A0 = {1,2,3,…,1000}  
indeed satisfies (*): for any i,j ≤1000, if i+j ≤1000 (that is, i+j ∈ A0) then  
ai + aj = i + j ∈ A0.

Step 5.  Counting.  

The argument above shows that A must equal A0 and A0 works, so exactly one set fulfils the requirement.

\boxed{1}

Denote  

A = {a1 < a2 < … < a1000} ,            1 ≤ a1 ,     a1000 ≤ 2017,

and write  

Δi = ai – i    (i = 1,2,…,1000).

Because ai are distinct integers, ai+1 ≥ ai +1, hence Δi is non-decreasing and  
0 ≤ Δ1 ≤ Δ2 ≤ … ≤ Δ1000 ≤ 1017.                               (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  No element of A can lie in {1001,1002,…,2000}
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Suppose, to the contrary, that t∈A with 1001 ≤ t ≤ 2000.  
Choose indices

i = t – 1000   (1 ≤ i ≤ 1000) and j = 1000.

Then i + j = t ∈ A, so by the given condition

ai + aj = ai + a1000 ∈ A.                                      (2)

Now ai ≥ i = t – 1000 , and a1000 ≥ 1000 + Δ1000 ≥ 1000, hence

ai + a1000 ≥ (t – 1000) + 1000 = t.                            (3)

If equality held in (3) we would have ai = i and a1000 = 1000.  
But then the largest element of A would be 1000 < t, contradicting t∈A.  
Thus ai + a1000 ≥ t + 1 ≥ 1002.

Because a1000 ≤ 2017, (2) says that A must contain an element ≥ 1002, and
iterating the same argument with that new element shows that A must contain
a number > 2017 – impossible.  Therefore

A ∩ {1001,1002,…,2000} = ∅.                                    (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The first 983 positive integers must all belong to A
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write r = |A ∩ {1,2,…,1000}|.  Because of (4), the other
1000 – r elements of A have to be taken from {2001,…,2017}, of which there
are exactly 17.  Hence

1000 – r ≤ 17  ⇒  r ≥ 983.                                      (5)

Let k be the largest integer ≤1000 that does not belong to A.
If k existed, then k ≤ 1000 and k∉A but k+1∈A.
Choose indices k and 1;  their sum k+1 lies in A, so the condition gives

ak + a1 ∈ A.                                                    (6)

Because k∉A, necessarily ak ≥ k+1.
Also a1 ≥ 1, so ak + a1 ≥ k+2 ≥ (k+1)+1. 
But by (4) A contains no integer between 1001 and 2000, and
k+2 ≤ 1000 (because k ≤ 998).  Hence (6) is impossible.
Therefore no such k exists, i.e. all integers up to the largest element
≤1000 that is in A are present consecutively.  Together with (5) this yields

{1,2,…,m} ⊆ A ⊆ {1,2,…,1000}∪{2001,…,2017}                    (7)

where m = 1000 – r + 983 and 983 ≤ m ≤ 1000.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Exact description of all admissible sets
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let r = 1000 – m  (0 ≤ r ≤ 17).  
Then A must be of the form  

A = {1,2,…,m} ∪ U,

where U is any r-element subset of {2001,2002,…,2017}.  
We now verify that every such A satisfies the condition.

•  For indices i,j ≤ m we have ai = i , aj = j ,
   and if i+j ≤ m (i+j∈A) then ai+aj = i+j ∈ A – condition holds.

•  If at least one of i or j exceeds m then i+j ≥ m+1 > 1000,
   hence i+j ∉ A by (4); the condition is not triggered.

Thus every set of the form (7) is admissible, and no other set is.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Counting them
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For each r = 0,1,…,17 choose U ⊆ {2001,…,2017} with |U| = r.  
There are C(17,r) such choices, so the total number of admissible sets is

Σ_{r=0}^{17} C(17,r) = (1+1)^{17} = 2^{17} = 131\,072.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{2^{17}}

Let  

A=\{a_{1}<a_{2}<\dots <a_{1000}\},\qquad a_{1000}\le 2017 ,  

and assume that  

(★) for any 1\le i,j\le 1000, i+j\in A\;\Longrightarrow\;a_i+a_j\in A .  

────────────────────────────────────────  
1.  The first gap can appear only after 983.  
────────────────────────────────────────  

Put  

k=\max\{m\le 1000\mid \{1,2,\dots ,m\}\subseteq A\}.  

If k<983 the set \{1,2,\dots ,k\} has at most 982 elements, so among the 1000
elements of A at least 18 lie in the interval [k+1,2000].
Consequently at least one of them, call it x\,(\le 2000), occupies an index
i>k.

Write x=i+j with j=1000 (possible because 1001\le x\le 2000); then
i+j=x\in A and i>k\ge j.  
Hence, by (★),

a_i+a_j>a_i\ge x\quad\Longrightarrow\quad a_i+a_j>2000 .

But A is contained in \{1,\dots ,2017\}; therefore a_i+a_j\le 2017 must also
belong to A, which is impossible.  
Thus k\ge 983.

────────────────────────────────────────  
2.  Every element whose index exceeds k is larger than 2000.  
────────────────────────────────────────  

Suppose a_{k+1}\le 2000.
Choose the representation a_{k+1}= (a_{k+1}-1000)+1000 with
1\le a_{k+1}-1000\le 1000.
Because a_{k+1}>k, at least one of the two indices
(a_{k+1}-1000,\,1000) exceeds k.  
Apply (★):

a_{k+1} \;=\;(a_{k+1}-1000)+1000\in A
         \Longrightarrow\;
a_{a_{k+1}-1000}+a_{1000}\in A .

The first summand is at least a_{k+1}-1000\ge 1,
the second is at least a_{k+1}>1000, hence the total is >2000,
contradicting part 1.  
Therefore  

a_{k+1},a_{k+2},\dots ,a_{1000}\;>\;2000.  

So every element of A that is \le 2000 is one of the first k,
and these elements are exactly 1,2,\dots ,k.

────────────────────────────────────────  
3.  The structure of A.  
────────────────────────────────────────  

We have proved that  

A=\{1,2,\dots ,k\}\;\cup\;B,  

where  
• 983\le k\le 1000,  
• B\subseteq\{2001,2002,\dots ,2017\},  
• |B|=1000-k\;(0\le 1000-k\le 17).

Conversely, every set of this form satisfies (★):

– If i+j\le k, then i,j\le k and a_i=i, a_j=j, so a_i+a_j=i+j\in A.  
– If i+j>k, the integer i+j is either between k+1 and 2000 (never in A)
  or exceeds 2000 (impossible), so (★) is vacuous.

Thus all admissible sets are obtained in this way.

────────────────────────────────────────  
4.  Counting the sets.  
────────────────────────────────────────  

For each t=0,1,\dots ,17 choose t numbers from the 17-element set
\{2001,\dots ,2017\} to be B (then k=1000-t).  
Hence the number of different sets A is  

Σ_{t=0}^{17}\binom{17}{t}=2^{17}=131\,072.

────────────────────────────────────────  
Final answer  

\boxed{2^{17}}

Let n = 300.

1.  For every simple polygon the (signed) sum of the exterior - turning angles is  
   Στ = 360°.

    At one vertex  
      interior angle  = θ  
      exterior angle  τ = 180° – θ.

2.  Classify the vertices  
      k   right angles    θ = 90°, τ =  +90°  
      r   reflex angles    180° < θ < 360°, τ = –180° + δ ( 0 < δ < 180° )  
      c   remaining convex angles  90° < θ < 180°, τ = ε ( 0 < ε < 90° )

    The 300 vertices give k + r + c = 300.

3.  Write the exterior–angle sum:

        90k + (–180r + δ) + ε = 360,   0 < δ < 180r, 0 < ε < 90c.

   We can make the c convex angles almost flat, i.e. let ε → 0.
   Doing that and keeping only the necessary positive part δ gives

        90k – 180r + δ = 360,   0 < δ < 180r.  (★)

4.  For (★) to have a solution with some r, we need

        90k – 180r < 360  ⇒ k > 4,                     (1)
        90k – 180r > 360 – 180r ⇒ 2r > k – 4.   (2)

   Hence an admissible r must satisfy

        (k – 4)/2 < r ≤ 300 – k.   (3)

   Such an integer r exists iff

        300 – k > (k – 4)/2
        2·300 + 4 > 3k
        k < (2·300 + 4)/3 = 604/3 ≈ 201.33.

   Therefore

        k ≤ 201.

5.  Show that k = 201 is attainable.

   Take r = 99 (the smallest integer fulfilling (3) when k = 201):
        (201 – 4)/2 = 98.5  < 99 ≤ 300 – 201 = 99.

   Put δ = 360 – 90·201 + 180·99 = 90.

   Distribute this 90° among the 99 reflex vertices, e.g. give one of them
   δ = 90°, the other 98 vertices δ = 0.0001°, etc.  
   Let c = 0; the remaining 300 – 201 – 99 = 0 vertices are not needed.

   Then (★) is satisfied exactly and every inequality is strict, so the
   polygon is simple.  A schematic construction is to begin with an
   orthogonal “stair-case” (all 90° angles) and, at 99 chosen corners,
   indent the polygon slightly so that each makes an interior angle
   360° – δi (almost 360°).  No self-intersection occurs if the dents are
   made sufficiently small.

   Hence a simple 300-gon with 201 right angles exists.

6.  Since 201 is achievable and no larger value is allowed by (4), the
   maximum possible number of right interior angles is 201.

\boxed{201}

Let n = 300.  
Label the interior angles θ₁,θ₂,…,θₙ and let the corresponding exterior (turning) angles be  
φᵢ = 180° – θᵢ   ( −180° < φᵢ < 180° for a simple polygon ).

For every simple polygon traversed once around,  

 Σ φᵢ = 360°. (1)

Suppose k of the interior angles are right angles (θ = 90°).  
At each of those k vertices the exterior angle is

 φ = 180° – 90° = 90°.

Hence the contribution of the right-angle vertices to (1) is 90k degrees.  
Let j = n – k be the remaining vertices.  Their exterior angles satisfy −180° < φᵢ < 180°, so

 Σ (remaining φᵢ) > −180j. (2)

Insert (2) into (1):

 90k + ( −180j ) < 360°  
 90k − 180(n − k) < 360°  
 90k − 180·300 + 180k < 360°  
 270k < 54 360°  
 k < 54 360/270 = 201⅓.

Because k is an integer, the maximum possible value is

 k_max = 201.

(Equality in the inequality above is impossible because the exterior angles may approach but never reach −180°.)

Existence:  
Choose 201 right angles.  
Take 98 vertices with exterior angles −179° and one vertex with exterior angle −91°:

 90·201 + 98(−179) + (−91) = 18 090 − 17 542 − 91 = 360°,

so condition (1) is satisfied, and every exterior angle lies in (−180°,180°).  
By making the corresponding interior angles 359° (for −179°) and 271° (for −91°) and drawing the edges short enough, a simple 300-gon can be embedded in the plane.  Hence 201 right angles are attainable.

Therefore, the largest possible number of right interior angles in a simple 300-sided polygon is

\boxed{201}.

Let n = 300 and let k be the number of interior right angles we want to realise.

1.  Exterior‐angle equation  
   At every vertex the (signed) exterior angle is  
   ε = 180° − α   (α = interior angle).  
   For a simple polygon traced counter-clockwise the sum of the exterior angles is

   Σε = 360°. … (1)

2.  Contribution of the right–angle vertices  
   A right interior angle (α = 90°) contributes  
   ε = 180° − 90° = +90°.

   Hence the k right–angle vertices contribute k·90° to Σε.

3.  What can the remaining vertices contribute?  
   For any vertex we must have α < 360°; therefore

   ε = 180° − α > −180°.                                (2)

   Thus each of the other 300 − k vertices contributes more than −180°.
   Their total contribution is therefore greater than −180(300 − k).

4.  Inequality for k  
   Insert these facts into (1):

     k·90° + (sum of the remaining ε’s) = 360°  
     ⇒ k·90° + (sum of remaining ε’s) ≤ k·90° − 180(300 − k)  (by (2))  
     ⇒ k·90° − 180(300 − k) ≤ 360°.

   Simplifying,

     270k − 180·300 ≤ 360  
     270k ≤ 180·300 + 360  
     270k ≤ 54 000 + 360 = 54 360  
     k ≤ 54 360 / 270 = 201⅓.

   Since k is an integer,

     k ≤ 201.                                               (3)

5.  Achievability of k = 201  
   It remains to show that 201 right angles can actually occur.

   Let  
     – 201 vertices have α = 90°;  
     – 98 vertices have α = 359.1°   (any value < 360° will do);  
     – 1 vertex has α = 270°.

   Then

     sum α = 201·90° + 98·359.1° + 270°  
           = 18 090° + 35 592° + 270°  
           = 53 640°  
           = (300 − 2)·180°, the required total for a 300-gon.

   A simple polygon with these angles is easily drawn: start with a long thin “zig-zag” rectangle that gives the 201 right angles, and at 98 selected corners indent it with an extremely narrow re-entrant notch to create angles of 359.1°, finally insert one ordinary 270° concave corner.  No edges cross, so the polygon is simple.

   Hence k = 201 is attainable.

6.  Conclusion  
   Combining (3) with the construction, the largest possible number of interior right angles in a simple 300-gon is

\boxed{201}.

Let k be the number of interior right angles (90°) in the simple 300-gon.

1.  Sum of all interior angles  
   S = (n – 2)·180° = 298·180° = 53 640°.

2.  The remaining 300 – k angles are different from 90°.  
   Each interior angle is greater than 0° and less than 360° (in a simple polygon an interior angle cannot reach 360°).  
   Hence

        0 <  S – 90k  <  360(300 – k).

3.  The left inequality is automatic for any k < 596 (trivial here).  
   The right inequality gives the real restriction:

        53 640 − 90k  <  360(300 − k)
        53 640 − 90k  < 108 000 − 360k
        270k < 54 360
        k < 201⅓.

   Therefore k ≤ 201.

4.  Feasibility of k = 201.  
   With k = 201 the other 99 angles must sum to

        53 640 − 90·201 = 35 550°,

   i.e. their average is 35 550 / 99 ≈ 359.09°, which lies strictly between 0° and 360°.  
   For instance, take every one of the 99 angles equal to 360° − 0.909…°.  
   Such a set of angles satisfies both the interior-angle sum and the exterior-angle (turning) condition, and a very “spiky” simple polygon realizing them can be drawn.  
   Hence k = 201 is attainable.

Because 202 right angles would force the remaining 98 angles to average more than 360°, which is impossible, 201 is the maximum.

\boxed{201}

Step 1.  A useful identity  
For every simple polygon whose vertices are listed counter-clockwise
let θ₁,θ₂,…,θₙ be the interior angles and let  

 eᵢ = 180° – θᵢ      (the signed exterior turn at the i-th vertex).

For every simple (non-self-intersecting) closed broken line  
Σ eᵢ = 360°.                               (1)

Step 2.  Relating the right angles to the other angles  
Assume the polygon has

 k  interior right angles (θ = 90°)  and  
 m = n – k  remaining angles (each different from 90°).

Write every remaining angle as θ = 180°+x with 0 < x < 180°  
(so x is the amount by which the angle exceeds 180°; for a convex
angle x < 0 but that would only decrease the number of right angles,
so to maximise k we may and will take all remaining angles reflex,
i.e. x>0).

For a right angle the exterior turn is +90°, for a reflex angle it is –x.
Using (1):

 90k – (x₁+x₂+…+x_m) = 360°.                (2)

Because every xᵢ is at most 180°, we have

 x₁+x₂+…+x_m ≤ 180m.                       (3)

Insert (3) in (2):

 90k – 180m ≥ 360  
 90k – 180(n – k) ≥ 360  
 270k  ≤  180n + 360.

Therefore  

 k ≤ (2n + 4)/3.                           (4)

For n = 300,

 (2·300 + 4)/3 = 604/3 = 201⅓,

so every simple 300-gon has at most  

 k_max = 201 right angles.                 (5)

Step 3.  A 300-gon that actually has 201 right angles  
Construction (sketch).

1.  Begin with a right triangle ΔABC; it already supplies one right
    interior angle (at C).

2.  Divide the side AB into 99 equal short pieces.  On each piece erect
    a very thin outward rectangular “tooth’’ (two edges perpendicular
    to AB and one parallel to AB, then back to AB).  
    Each tooth introduces three new vertices whose interior angles are
       – two right angles (90°, 90°) and  
       – one reflex angle whose measure we choose to be 360° – ε,  
         where ε is so small that the teeth do not overlap.
    Hence every tooth adds
       2 right angles and 1 reflex angle and increases the vertex count
       by 3.

    After adding 99 teeth the polygon has

       vertices: 3 + 99·3 = 300  
       right angles: 1 + 99·2 = 199  
       reflex angles: 99.

3.  Choose two of the 99 teeth and inside each of them insert an
    additional tiny rectangular notch.  Each notch adds exactly one new
    right angle and leaves the number of reflex angles unchanged
    (we compensate the extra vertex by straightening an adjacent edge,
    so the total number of vertices stays 300).

    After the two notches we have

       right angles: 199 + 2 = 201  
       reflex angles: 99  
       total vertices: 300.

Because every reflex angle is 360° – ε (ε < 180°), equality (2) is
satisfied, the construction is non-self-intersecting for sufficiently
small ε, and the polygon is simple.

Thus a simple 300-gon with exactly 201 right interior angles exists.

Step 4.  Conclusion  
No simple 300-gon can contain more than 201 right angles by (5), and a
simple 300-gon with 201 right angles has just been constructed.

Let n = 300.

        53 640° – 90R
    --------------------  < 360° .

       53 640 – 90R < 108 000 – 360R
                   270R < 54 360
                     R < 201⅓ .

    Therefore R ≤ 201.

    Each tooth contributes two additional right-angled convex vertices

    almost –180°.

    –180°.  Altogether we then have

        1 modified tooth               :   1 right angle
                                     --------------------

Let r be the number of right angles in the interior of a simple n-gon (here n = 300) and let the remaining s = n – r angles be θ1 , … , θs .

Step 1.  Use exterior angles  
For any simple polygon the (signed) exterior angles add to 360°.  
At a right angle the exterior angle equals 90°, so

 90 r + (exterior angles of the other vertices) = 360°.       (1)

For an ordinary (non-degenerate) vertex the exterior angle e satisfies –180° < e < 180°, hence

 exterior angle of every “other” vertex > –180°.             (2)

Step 2.  Obtain an upper bound for r  
From (1) the sum of the exterior angles at the s other vertices is 360 – 90 r.  
Because each of them is greater than –180°, the smallest possible sum of those s angles is –180 s, so

 360 – 90 r  ≥  –180 s = –180 (n – r).                    (3)

Insert n = 300 and solve for r:

 360 – 90 r ≥ –180(300 – r)  
 360 – 90 r ≥ –54 000 + 180 r  
 360 + 54 000 ≥ 270 r  
 270 r ≤ 54 360  
 r ≤ 54 360 / 270 = 201⅓.

Hence

 r ≤ 201.                                                    (4)

So at most 201 right angles are possible.

Step 3.  Show that 201 right angles can actually occur  
Take 201 right-angle “steps” that alternately go east and north (thus forming a long rectangular staircase).  
From the last step draw a very long segment nearly back along the previous direction, then make a tiny zig-zag whose two turning points each contribute an interior angle of 359.09…° (exterior –179.09…°), and repeat that nearly-straight zig-zag 99 times before finally closing the polygon without self-intersection.  
Those 99 vertices are reflex, each with interior angle slightly below 360°, so their exterior angles are about –179.09…°.  

Because  
 • the 201 right vertices contribute 201·90° = 18 090° to the interior-angle sum,  
 • the 99 reflex vertices contribute 300 – 201 = 99 angles, each chosen so that
  interior angle ≈ 359.09…° (sum ≈ 35 550°),  
the total interior sum is 18 090° + 35 550° = 53 640° = 180°(300 – 2), exactly what a 300-gon requires.  
All turns are less than 360°, and the careful spacing of the long nearly-straight return path prevents any self-intersection, so the construction is a simple polygon.

Thus 201 right angles can be attained, and (4) shows that no more are possible.

Therefore the maximum possible number of right interior angles in a simple 300-gon is

\boxed{201}.

Let k be the number of interior right angles ( 90° ) of a simple 300-gon.  
Put  

 y = 300 – k  (the remaining vertices)  
 R = sum of the y remaining interior angles.

1.  An angle of a simple polygon is always smaller than 360°; hence R < 360y.

2.  The sum of the interior angles of an n-gon equals (n – 2)·180°:

 R + 90k = (300 – 2)·180 = 298·180 = 53 640.             (1)

3.  Insert (1) in R < 360y :

 53 640 − 90k < 360(300 − k).

 53 640 − 90k < 108 000 − 360k

 270k < 54 360

 k < 201⅓ .

Since k is an integer,  

 k ≤ 201.                                                (2)

Thus at most 201 right angles are possible.  
It remains to show that 201 can indeed be attained.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
A construction with 201 right angles
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Take a very large convex quadrilateral ABCD (all four corner angles are 90°).  
Along the side AB carve 98 extremely thin inward “notches” formed by three new
edges:

• entering edge – perpendicular to AB (creates a right angle);  
• a tiny edge almost parallel to AB;  
• exiting edge – perpendicular back to AB (creates another right angle).

Because the entering and the exiting edges are almost collinear, the interior
angle at the tip of the notch is a reflex angle very close to 360° (for
instance 359°).  
Each notch therefore contributes

 2 right angles  +  1 reflex angle (≈ 359°).

After 98 notches we have added

 2·98 = 196 right angles and 98 reflex angles.

Counting the four original right angles of ABCD gives

 196 + 4 = 200 right angles so far.

Finally make one more tiny rectangular bump on CD that produces a single extra
right angle and does not introduce any new reflex angle.  
Total numbers obtained are

 k = 200 + 1 = 201 right angles,  
 y = 99 other angles (the 98 reflex ones + the untouched angle on CD).

Because every reflex angle can be chosen as close to 360° as we wish, the
99 non-right angles can be adjusted so that their sum is exactly

 53 640 − 90·201 = 35 550°,

while each of them stays below 360°, ensuring (1).  The notches and the bump
can be made arbitrarily small, so the polygon remains simple and has precisely
300 vertices.

Hence a simple 300-gon with 201 interior right angles exists.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Inequality (2) shows that no simple 300-gon can have more than 201 right
angles, and the construction above shows that 201 can be reached.

\boxed{201}

Let

 • red  = 1 ,  blue = 0 .

There are 6×6 = 36 vertices arranged as six rows R₁,…,R₆ (each Ri is a 6-bit word).

For two consecutive rows U (=Ri) and L (=Ri+1) denote

Dⱼ = Uⱼ + Lⱼ  (j = 1,…,6).

In every unit square (columns j , j+1 and rows i , i+1)

Uⱼ + Uⱼ₊₁ + Lⱼ + Lⱼ₊₁ = 2.

Rewriting with the D’s gives Dⱼ + Dⱼ₊₁ = 2 for j = 1,…,5.

1.  Possible sequences (D₁,…,D₆)

 Each Dⱼ ∈ {0,1,2} and adjacent pairs must sum to 2.
 Allowed neighbour pairs: (0,2), (1,1), (2,0).

 • If one D equals 1, the recursion forces every D to be 1.  
 • If no D equals 1, the sequence must alternate 0,2,0,2,… or 2,0,2,0,…

 Hence only three D-sequences exist  
  A) (1,1,1,1,1,1)  
  B) (0,2,0,2,0,2)  
  C) (2,0,2,0,2,0).

2.  Row pairs (U,L) realising each D-sequence

 A)  Dⱼ = 1 : in every column exactly one of U,L is 1 ⇒ L =  U̅ .  
  For each of the 6 columns choose which row gets the 1 ⇒ 2⁶ = 64 pairs.

 B)  D-pattern 0,2,… : here Uⱼ = Lⱼ and (U,L) is the alternating row  
    010101. (unique)

 C)  D-pattern 2,0,… : here Uⱼ = Lⱼ and (U,L) is the alternating row  
    101010. (unique)

 Total admissible row pairs: 64 + 1 + 1 = 66.

3.  Adjacency of single rows

 Writing rows as 6-bit words, the above gives the rule:

 Row V can follow row U iff  
  • V =  U̅  (complement), or  
  • V = U  and U is alternating (010101 or 101010).

 Thus for every row U

 – there is always an edge U →  U̅,  
 – the two alternating rows also have self-loops.

 So the 64 rows split into 32 complementary pairs.
 For 31 pairs the adjacency matrix is
  M = [0 1; 1 0].  
 For the special pair {010101,101010} it is
  L = [1 1; 1 1].

4.  Walks of length 5 (which give the six rows)

 Let ones(·) denote “sum of all entries”.

 For M : M² = I, hence M⁵ = M, and ones(M⁵) = 2.  
 For L : L = J (all 1s), Jⁿ = 2ⁿ⁻¹J, so L⁵ = 2⁴L, and ones(L⁵) = 4·16 = 64.

 Total number of length-5 walks:

 31 · ones(M⁵) + ones(L⁵) = 31 · 2 + 64 = 62 + 64 = 126.

5.  Conclusion

Each admissible walk corresponds to one coloring of the 6×6 grid that makes every unit square contain exactly two red vertices, and vice versa.

Step 1.  Notation  
Write every vertex as  
xij = 0  (blue)   or   1 (red)   ( 1 ≤ i , j ≤ 6 ).

For every unit square (i = 1…5 , j = 1…5)

xij + xi+1 , j + x i , j+1 + x i+1 , j+1 = 2. (1)

Step 2.  Work column by column  
Fix one column A = (a1 , … , a6)T.  
Seek the next column B = (b1 , … , b6)T satisfying (1) for j fixed and i = 1…5:

ai + ai+1 + bi + bi+1 = 2. (2)

For i = 1 the equation is
a1 + a2 + b1 + b2 = 2 ⇒ b2 = 2 − (a1 + a2 + b1). (3)

As soon as b1 (0 or 1) is selected, b2 is fixed by (3).  
Proceeding downward,

bi+1 = 2 − (ai + ai+1 + bi)  (i = 1…5) (4)

so the whole column B is produced successively.  
Thus, for a given column A there can be at most two columns B (the two possible choices for b1).

Step 3.  When does A have one follower and when two?  
Put s = a1 + a2.

•  If s = 0   (a1 = a2 = 0)  then (3) forces b1 = 1, b2 = 1 – only one choice.  
•  If s = 2   (a1 = a2 = 1)  then (3) forces b1 = 0, b2 = 0 – only one choice.  
•  If s = 1   (a1 ≠ a2)      then b1 may be 0 or 1, giving two possibilities.

Hence

F(A) = number of admissible next columns 
      = 1 if a1 = a2   
      = 2 if a1 ≠ a2. (5)

Step 4.  Type-E and type-U columns  
Call a column E-type when a1 = a2,  U-type when a1 ≠ a2.

Equation (4) shows that a column of type E is always followed by a column of type E,   
and a column of type U is always followed by a column of type U.  
(The equality/inequality of the top two entries is preserved.)

Step 5.  Counting the first column  

Top two entries:

 E-type : 00 or 11 (2 choices)  
 U-type : 01 or 10 (2 choices)

Lower four entries (rows 3–6): free (16 choices).

Hence
number of E-type columns = 2·16 = 32,  
number of U-type columns = 2·16 = 32. (6)

Step 6.  Building the 6 columns (5 transitions)

Start with the first column C1; produce C2,…,C6 by five successive applications of (4).

•  If C1 is E-type:  each transition has exactly 1 choice, so the whole colouring is unique.  
   Contributions: 32.  

•  If C1 is U-type:  each transition has 2 choices (see (5)), independent at every step,  
   giving 2^5 = 32 possibilities for the five transitions.  
   Contributions: 32 · 32 = 1024.

Step 7.  Total number of colourings  

Total = 32   (from E-type start)  +  1024  (from U-type start)  
       = 1056.

Step 1.  Restate the problem  
There are 36 lattice–points (6 × 6).  A colouring (each point red or blue) is admissible when every one of the 25 unit squares contains exactly two red vertices.  We must count all admissible colourings.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Work column by column  
Write the six vertices of one column as a 6-bit vector  
s = (s₁,s₂,…,s₆) , sᵢ = 0 (blue) or 1 (red).

For two adjacent columns s and t let

rᵢ = 2 − (sᵢ + sᵢ₊₁)  (i = 1,…,5).

“Exactly two red vertices in the ith square’’ is

sᵢ + sᵢ₊₁ + tᵢ + tᵢ₊₁ = 2.                                (1)

Equation (1) can be rewritten as a recurrence for the bits of t:

tᵢ₊₁ = rᵢ − tᵢ.                                            (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Solving the recurrence  

(2) is solved by choosing t₁ and propagating downward.
While propagating we must keep tᵢ ∈ {0,1}.  
Because rᵢ ∈ {0,1,2} we have

• rᵢ = 0  ⇒  tᵢ must be 0 and tᵢ₊₁ = 0  
• rᵢ = 2  ⇒  tᵢ must be 1 and tᵢ₊₁ = 1  
• rᵢ = 1  ⇒  tᵢ₊₁ = 1 − tᵢ   (a “toggle’’).

Thus, once s is fixed,

– if every rᵢ = 1 (i.e. s₁s₂…s₆ is strictly alternating 010101 or 101010) both
  choices t₁ = 0 and t₁ = 1 work, so exactly 2 columns t are possible;

– otherwise some rᵢ equals 0 or 2, which forces t₁ to one value,
  hence exactly 1 column t is possible.

Hence for every column state s

number of compatible next–columns  =
⎧2  if s is alternating,
⎨
⎩1  otherwise.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  The “compatibility graph’’ between column states  

There are 64 possible column states.  Denote the two alternating ones by  
A₀ = 010101  and  A₁ = 101010.

Connectivity:

• A₀ is compatible with A₀ and with A₁.  
  (loops are allowed, because A₀ = A₀ obviously satisfies every square.)

• A₁ is compatible with A₁ and with A₀.

• Each of the remaining 62 states has exactly one compatible neighbour,
  and compatibility is symmetric; hence the 62 states form 31 disjoint
  two–vertex components with the single edge s ↔ F(s).

So the compatibility graph has

– one component consisting of A₀ and A₁ with edges A₀→A₀, A₀→A₁,
  A₁→A₀, A₁→A₁;

– thirty–one isolated edges (two–vertex components without loops).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  Walks of length 5 in the graph  
A colouring of the whole 6×6 grid is a sequence of 6 column states
s₁,s₂,…,s₆ in which successive states are compatible, i.e. a walk of
length 5 in the graph.

(a)  The special component {A₀,A₁}.  
Starting node: 2 choices.  
At each of the 5 steps we may stay or switch (2 choices).
Number of walks = 2 · 2⁵ = 64.

(b)  A two-vertex component {a,b} (no loops).  
From a the walk is forced: a,b,a,b,a,b.  
From b it is b,a,b,a,b,a.  
Hence exactly 2 walks per component.
Thirty-one such components give 31 · 2 = 62 walks.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 6.  Total number of admissible colourings  

64 (from the alternating component)  
+ 62 (from the other 31 components)  
= 126.

Therefore the required number of different colouring schemes is

\boxed{126}.

Add (2) for r and r+1 and reduce modulo 2:

Colour red = 1, blue = 0.  
For every unit square  
a_{i ,j}+a_{i+1 ,j}+a_{i ,j+1}+a_{i+1 ,j+1}=2,  
so the sum of the four corner values is even.  
Hence for every i,j  

a_{i ,j}\oplus a_{i ,1}\oplus a_{1 ,j}\oplus a_{1 ,1}=0,  

and therefore the whole matrix is determined (mod 2) by its first row
(y_{1},…,y_{6}) and first column (x_{1},…,x_{6}) :

 a_{i ,j}=x_{i}\oplus y_{j}.                                (1)

(⊕ denotes XOR.)  
Flipping all the colours, i.e. replacing (x,y) by (x⊕1 ,y⊕1),
does not change the matrix, so each colouring corresponds to an unordered
pair {x,y}.  
There are 2^{12}/2 = 2^{11}=2048 such pairs altogether.

Step 2.  When does a 2×2 square contain exactly two reds?  

For a fixed 2×2 square formed by rows i,i+1 and columns j,j+1 the
four corner colours obtained from (1) are  

x_{i}\oplus y_{j},  x_{i}\oplus y_{j+1},  
x_{i+1}\oplus y_{j},  x_{i+1}\oplus y_{j+1}.

Let Δ_{i}=x_{i}\oplus x_{i+1} (row‐difference bits)
and Γ_{j}=y_{j}\oplus y_{j+1} (column‐difference bits).
A short check shows

• If Δ_{i}=0 and Γ_{j}=0, all four corners are equal (0 reds or 4 reds).  
• Otherwise the square has exactly two reds.

So every unit square has exactly two red vertices
⇔ we are not allowed to have Δ_{i}=0 and Γ_{j}=0 simultaneously.
In other words,

EITHER all five Γ_{j}=1  (no 0 among the column differences),  
OR   all five Δ_{i}=1  (no 0 among the row differences). (2)

(If one of the difference lists has a 0, the other list must be
all 1’s.)

Step 3.  Counting admissible (x,y) pairs

Row pattern “all differences 1’’ means the 6‐bit string x alternates
0101… or 1010…, i.e. 2 possibilities.  
Column pattern “all differences 1’’ likewise gives 2 possibilities for y.

•  All Δ_{i}=1: 2 choices for x, 64 choices for y  → 128 pairs.  
•  All Γ_{j}=1: 64 choices for x, 2 choices for y  → 128 pairs.  
•  Both sets of differences all 1 (counted twice): 2·2 = 4 pairs.

Hence the number of ordered pairs (x,y) satisfying (2) is  
128 + 128 – 4 = 252.

Step 4.  Eliminating the double counting

As noticed after (1), the two ordered pairs (x,y)
and (x⊕1 ,y⊕1) represent the same colouring, and they are always distinct.
Therefore every admissible colouring is represented by exactly
two ordered pairs, so the number of different colourings is

252 / 2 = 126.

\boxed{126}

Let us encode the colouring by the 0–1 matrix  

 v​_{i ,j}=1 (red), v​_{i ,j}=0 (blue)  (1≤i,j≤6).

Condition for every 1×1 square  
v​_{i ,j}+v​_{i+1 ,j}+v​_{i ,j+1}+v​_{i+1 ,j+1}=2  (1≤i,j≤5).   (♣)

------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Solve the parity part of (♣)  
Taking the equations modulo 2 gives  

 v​_{i ,j}⊕v​_{i+1 ,j}⊕v​_{i ,j+1}⊕v​_{i+1 ,j+1}=0,                     (⊕ = XOR)

which is equivalent to  
 v​_{i ,j}⊕v​_{i ,j+1}=v​_{i+1 ,j}⊕v​_{i+1 ,j+1} (1≤i≤5,1≤j≤5)  
 v​_{i ,j}⊕v​_{i+1 ,j}=v​_{i ,j+1}⊕v​_{i+1 ,j+1} (1≤i≤5,1≤j≤5).

Hence the horizontal edge–differences

 h_j=v​_{1 ,j}⊕v​_{1 ,j+1} (1≤j≤5)

are the same in every row, and the vertical edge–differences

 w_i=v​_{i ,1}⊕v​_{i+1 ,1} (1≤i≤5)

are the same in every column.

Therefore the whole matrix is determined by  

 v_{1,1}  (1 bit)  
 h₁,…,h₅ (5 bits)  
 w₁,…,w₅ (5 bits)

— in total 11 independent bits, giving 2¹¹ parity-consistent colourings.

------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Translate the “exactly two red” requirement  

Take any square whose top-left corner is (i,j).  
Write A=v_{i ,j}, h=h_j, w=w_i (all in {0,1}).  The four vertices are  

 A, A⊕h, A⊕w, A⊕h⊕w.

Their sum is  
 S = 2                 if (h,w) ≠ (0,0),  
 S = 0 or 4           if (h,w)  = (0,0).

Thus condition (♣) is satisfied iff, for every i,j,

 (h_j , w_i) ≠ (0,0).                                              (★)

------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Counting the admissible (h,w) pairs  

Let H = { j | h_j = 0 }  and  W = { i | w_i = 0 }.  
Statement (★) says H×W must be empty, i.e. at least one of the sets is empty.  
Hence

 either all h_j = 1   (H = ∅),  w arbitrary (2⁵ choices),  
 or    all w_i = 1   (W = ∅),  h arbitrary (2⁵ choices),

with the case “both all 1” counted twice.  
Number of (h,w) choices

 = 2⁵ + 2⁵ − 1 = 32 + 32 − 1 = 63.

------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Total colourings  

For each of those 63 choices, v_{1,1} can be 0 or 1.  
Therefore the required number of colourings is  

 63 × 2 = 126.

------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{126}

Let  

•  O(0,0),   r = 2 (radius of 𝛀O)  
•  P be any point outside 𝛀O, with R = OP (= |OP|)  
•  E, F be the points of tangency of the two tangents from P to 𝛀O.  

Because every point P on 𝛀M is at distance 4 ≤ R ≤ 6 from O (centre of 𝛀M is 5 away and its radius is 1), we shall minimise the required dot product on that interval of R.

────────────────────────
1.  Express PE · PF in terms of R
────────────────────────
Place P on the positive x-axis: P(R,0).  
The tangency conditions

 |E| = |F| = r,  OE ⟂ PE,  OF ⟂ PF  

give (writing t² = R² − r²):

 E ( r²/R ,  + r t /R ), F ( r²/R ,  − r t /R ).

Thus  

 PE = ( r²/R − R ,  + r t /R ), 
 PF = ( r²/R − R ,  − r t /R ).

Hence  

PE · PF = (r²/R − R)² − (r t /R)²  
    = R² − 3r² + 2r⁴ / R².                (★)

────────────────────────
2.  Minimise for 4 ≤ R ≤ 6 (with r = 2)
────────────────────────
Put r = 2 ⇒ r² = 4, r⁴ = 16.  
From (★)

 f(R) = R² − 12 + 32 / R².

f′(R) = 2R − 64 / R³.

Set f′(R)=0 ⇒ 2R⁴ = 64 ⇒ R⁴ = 32 ⇒ R = 32^{1/4} = 2^{5/4} ≈ 2.38,  
which lies outside the admissible interval [4, 6].

For R ≥ 4 we have f′(R) > 0 (e.g. at R = 4, f′ = 7 > 0),  
so f(R) is strictly increasing on [4, 6].  
Therefore its minimum occurs at the left end R = 4.

 f(4) = 4² − 12 + 32 / 4² = 16 − 12 + 2 = 6.

────────────────────────
3.  Feasibility
────────────────────────
R = 4 is attained when P is on the inner point of circle 𝛀M lying on the line OM (move 1 unit towards O from the centre M, which is 5 units from O).  
Hence the value 6 is achievable.

Let  

•  O(0,0),  radius R = 2, equation x² + y² = 4  
•  M(5 cos θ, 5 sin θ),  radius 1, equation (x – 5 cos θ)² + (y – 5 sin θ)² = 1    

Fix a point P on circle M.  
Denote d = OP = |OP| (distance from P to the center O).  
Because 4 ≤ d ≤ 6 (M is 5 from O and has radius 1), P is always outside circle O, so two tangents PE and PF exist.

1.  Length of each tangent:
    t = |PE| = |PF| = √(d² – R²) = √(d² – 4).

2.  Let α be the angle between OP and PE.  
    From right △OEP,  sin α = R/d = 2/d.  
    Hence the angle between the two tangents is  
    ∠EPF = 2α, where sin α = 2/d.

3.  Dot product of the tangent vectors:
    PE · PF = t² cos(∠EPF)  
             = (d² – 4) · cos(2α).

    Since sin α = 2/d,  
        cos α = √(1 – (2/d)²) = √(d² – 4)/d = t/d,  
        cos(2α) = 1 – 2 sin²α = 1 – 2(4/d²) = (d² – 8)/d².

    Therefore
    PE · PF = (d² – 4) · (d² – 8)/d²
             = (d² – 4)(d² – 8)/d².

4.  Minimize f(d) = (d² – 4)(d² – 8)/d² for d ∈ [4, 6].

    Let x = d² (so x ∈ [16, 36]):

    f(d) = g(x) = (x – 4)(x – 8)/x
           = x – 12 + 32/x.

    g′(x) = 1 – 32/x².

    On [16, 36], x² ≥ 16² = 256, so 32/x² ≤ 1/8 < 1 ⇒ g′(x) > 0.  
    Thus g(x) is strictly increasing; its minimum occurs at x = 16 (d = 4).

    f_min = g(16) = (16 – 4)(16 – 8)/16 = 12·8/16 = 6.

5.  Achievability: d = 4 happens when P is the point on circle M lying 1 unit inward along OM; hence the minimum is attainable.

Let

------------------------------------------------
1.  Geometry of the two tangents from P to ⊙O  

Denote the tangent points by E and F.  
Because OE ⟂ PE and OF ⟂ PF, the right triangles OEP and OFP give

 OP = d  (≥ r)  
 PE = PF = √(d² − r²).                        (1)

Let α be the angle between OP and one tangent (say PE).  
In ΔOEP,  
 sin α = OE / OP = r / d.                        (2)

Hence the angle between the two tangents is 2α, and

 cos(∠EPF) = cos(2α) = 1 – 2 sin²α = 1 – 2r²/d².     (3)

------------------------------------------------
2.  Dot product PE · PF  

Using (1) and (3),

 PE · PF = |PE||PF| cos(∠EPF)  
     = (d² − r²) (1 − 2r²/d²)  
     = d² − 3r² + 2r⁴/d².                    (4)

With r = 2 (so r² = 4, r⁴ = 16),

 f(d) = PE · PF = d² + 32/d² − 12.              (5)

------------------------------------------------
3.  Possible values of d = OP

Since P lies on a circle of radius 1 centred 5 units from O,

 | 5 − 1 | ≤ d ≤ 5 + 1 ⇒ 4 ≤ d ≤ 6.            (6)

------------------------------------------------
4.  Minimising f(d) on [4, 6]

 h′(d) = 2d − 64/d³ = 0  
 ⇒ d⁴ = 32 ⇒ d ≈ 2.38, which is outside [4,6].

At d = 4: f(4) = 16 + 32/16 − 12 = 16 + 2 − 12 = 6.  
At d = 6: f(6) = 36 + 32/36 − 12 ≈ 24.9.

So the minimum value is 6, attained when d = 4, i.e. when  
P lies on the line OC and is 1 unit nearer the origin than C.

Let  

•  O(0,0) be the centre of the fixed circle 𝛀O : x² + y² = 4  (radius R = 2).  
•  M(5 cos θ , 5 sin θ) be the centre of the moving circle 𝛀M : (x−5 cos θ)² + (y−5 sin θ)² = 1 (radius 1).  

For any point P on 𝛀M draw the two tangents PE and PF to 𝛀O, with E and F their points of tangency.  
Denote d = OP; because the little circle is 5 units from the origin and has radius 1,

 4 ≤ d ≤ 6.

------------------------------------------------
1.  Length of each tangent  
In right triangle OEP, OE = R and ∠E = 90°, so

 |PE| = |PF| = L = √(OP² − R²) = √(d² − 4).

------------------------------------------------
2.  Angle between the tangents  
OP is the external–angle bisector of the two tangents, so if α is the angle between OP and PE, the angle between the tangents is 2α.

In ΔOEP, ∠O = β and ∠P = α with α + β = 90°.  
cos β = R/d ⇒ sin β = √(d²−R²)/d = L/d ⇒ cos α = L/d.

Hence

 cos(∠EPF) = cos(2α) = 2 cos²α − 1 = 1 − 2R²/d².

------------------------------------------------
3.  Dot product  
PE · PF = |PE| |PF| cos(∠EPF)

 = L² (1 − 2R²/d²)  
 = (d² − R²) − 2R² + 2R⁴/d²  
 = d² − 3R² + 2R⁴/d².

With R = 2 (so R² = 4, R⁴ = 16):

 f(d) = d² − 12 + 32/d².

------------------------------------------------
4.  Minimising on 4 ≤ d ≤ 6  
f ′(d) = 2d − 64/d³.

Setting f ′(d)=0 gives d⁴ = 32 ⇒ d ≈ 2.38, which lies outside [4, 6].  
Since f ′(d) > 0 for d ≥ 4, f is increasing on the interval, so its minimum occurs at d = 4.

 f(4) = 4² − 12 + 32/4² = 16 − 12 + 2 = 6.

------------------------------------------------
5.  Attainability  
d = 4 is attained when P is the point of 𝛀M closest to O (along the line OM), so the minimum is achievable.

Let  

•  O(0,0) be the centre of the fixed circle  x²+y² = 4 (radius r = 2).

 |PE| = |PF| = ℓ = √(d² – r²).

2.  Coordinates of the points of tangency  
   (place O at the origin, P on the positive y–axis at (0,d)).

  y_E = r²/d,  x_E = – r √(d² – r²) / d,  
  y_F = y_E,  x_F = –x_E.

3.  Vectors of the tangents

 PF = (x_F , y_F – d) = (  r√(d² – r²)/d , –(d² – r²)/d ).

4.  Their dot–product  

 PE·PF = –[r²(d² – r²)/d²] + [(d² – r²)²/d²]  
     = (d² – r²)(d² – 2r²)/d².                     (★)

5.  For our problem r = 2, so

 f(d) = PE·PF = (d² – 4)(d² – 8)/d², d ≥ 2.

   The moving circle (x – 5 cos θ)² + (y – 5 sin θ)² = 1 has centre distance 5 from O and radius 1, hence  
 |OP| = d ranges through [5 – 1, 5 + 1] = [4, 6].

   Put s = d² (so s ∈ [16,36]):

 h(s) = (s – 4)(s – 8)/s = s – 12 + 32/s,  
 h′(s) = 1 – 32/s².

   On [16,36], 32/s² ≤ 32/256 = 1/8 < 1 ⇒ h′(s) > 0,  
   so h (and hence f) is strictly increasing.  
   Therefore the minimum occurs at the smallest s, i.e. at d = 4.

8.  Minimum value:

 f(4) = (16 – 4)(16 – 8)/16 = 12·8/16 = 6.

Hence the smallest possible value of  $\overrightarrow{PE}\!\cdot\!\overrightarrow{PF}$ is

Let  

• O(0,0),  radius of  ⊙O : R = 2  
• M(5 cos θ , 5 sin θ) , radius of  ⊙M : 1  (θ ∈ ℝ)  
• P any point on ⊙M.  

--------------------------------------------------
1.  Length of the tangents PE and PF from P to ⊙O  

Let d = OP.  
Since P is outside ⊙O, the tangent length is  

  |PE| = |PF| = L = √(d² – R²).  (1)

--------------------------------------------------
2.  Angle between the two tangents  

In right-triangle OEP,  

 sin ∠OPE = OE / OP = R / d.  

Denote α = ∠OPE; then sin α = R/d and the angle between the two tangents is  

 ∠EPF = 2α.  (2)

--------------------------------------------------
3.  Dot product PE·PF in terms of d  

With |PE| = |PF| = L and included angle 2α,

 PE·PF = L² cos(2α).  

Use cos(2α) = 1 − 2 sin²α:

 PE·PF = (d² − R²) [1 − 2(R² / d²)]  
    = d² − 3R² + 2R⁴ / d².  (3)

For R = 2 (so R² = 4, R⁴ = 16):

 f(d) = PE·PF = d² − 12 + 32 / d².  (4)

--------------------------------------------------
4.  Possible values of d  

The centre M is 5 units from O and P is 1 unit from M, so

 |OP| lies between 5 − 1 = 4 and 5 + 1 = 6.

Thus d ∈ [4 , 6].  (5)

--------------------------------------------------
5.  Minimising f(d) on [4 , 6]

Differentiate (4):

 f '(d) = 2d − 64 / d³.

Set f '(d)=0 ⇒ 2d⁴ = 64 ⇒ d⁴ = 32 ⇒ d = 32^{1/4} ≈ 2.38,  
which is less than 4, hence outside the admissible interval.

Because f '(d) > 0 for d ≥ 4 (check f '(4)=7>0), f(d) is increasing on [4,6].  
Minimum therefore occurs at the left end:

 f(4) = 4² − 12 + 32 / 4² = 16 − 12 + 2 = 6.  (6)

--------------------------------------------------
6.  Achievability  

Take θ arbitrary; let M be at distance 5 from O, and choose P on the line OM such that P lies 1 unit closer to O than M.  
Then OP = 5 − 1 = 4, which realizes the minimum.

Since d = 4 > R, tangents exist, so the value 6 is attainable.
