\boxed{6}

Let  

  P₁ ,P₂ ,…,P₁₀₀   be the 100 given points,  
  l₁ ,l₂ ,…,l_n      the n given lines, a_k =|l_k∩{P_i}|.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A necessary lower bound for n
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For every point let d_i be the number of the given lines passing through it
(d_i = 0,1,2,…).  
The total number of incidences equals

                ∑_{i=1}^{100} d_i = 250.                             (1)

If a point is on d_i≥2 lines, every unordered pair of those lines meets
at that point.  Hence the number s of pairs of lines that are forced to
pass through one of the 100 points is  

                s = ∑_{i=1}^{100} C(d_i,2).                          (2)

Two different lines have at most one common point, so  

                s ≤ C(n,2)=n(n–1)/2.                                 (3)

We want the smallest n for which (1)–(3) can be fulfilled.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.1  The minimal possible value of s
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Only the values d_i=1,2,3 are relevant, because
C(d,2)/(d) = 2/(d-1) decreases with d.
Write

                x_j = #{ i | d_i = j }   (j = 1,2,3),

x_1+x_2+x_3 = 100,                                                    (4)
x_1+2x_2+3x_3 = 250.                                                  (5)

Eliminating x_1 gives    x_2+2x_3 = 150.                             (6)

Now

                s = x_2 + 3x_3                                       (7)

with the constraint x_1=100-x_2-x_3 ≥0, i.e. x_3 ≥ 50.
Putting x_3 = 50 in (6) gives x_2 = 50 and

                s_min = 50 + 3·50 = 200.                             (8)

Hence every realisation of the data must satisfy  

                C(n,2) ≥ 200  ⇒  n ≥ 21,                            (9)

while C(20,2)=190<200, so n = 20 is impossible.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Showing that n = 21 can really occur
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We now construct an example with n = 21, thereby proving that the lower
bound 21 is attainable.

Step A   Six concurrent lines  
Take six different lines l₁,…,l₆ passing through a common point O
and no other three-fold intersection.

O is one of the 100 points; it lies on the six lines, so  
d(O)=6 and contributes C(6,2)=15 pairs of lines.

Step B   Fifteen further lines  
Choose 15 other lines l₇,…,l₂₁
so that  
• none of them passes through O,  
• no two of them are parallel,  
• apart from the constructions described below no three of them meet
  in one point.

There are now three kinds of pairs of lines

   A  pairs inside {l₁,…,l₆}:         15 such pairs (all already incident
      with O);

   B  pairs consisting of one of l₁,…,l₆ and one of l₇,…,l₂₁:
      6·15 = 90 pairs;

   C  pairs inside {l₇,…,l₂₁}:        C(15,2)=105 pairs.

Altogether C(21,2)=210 pairs, i.e. we may still realise 210–15 = 195
pairs outside O.

Step C   Forty-six triple points of degree 3  
(i)  For each of the six lines through O choose seven distinct points
     P on it (different from O).  
     At every such point let two suitable lines
     from {l₇,…,l₂₁} be arranged to pass through P.
     These 42 points each lie on exactly three lines (one l_i with
     i≤6 and two l_j with j≥7);
     every such point contributes 3 incidences and 3 pairs
     (2 of type B, 1 of type C).

(ii)  Choose four further points, each the common point of three
     different lines among l₇,…,l₂₁.
     These add 4·3 = 12 incidences and 12 pairs of type C.

So far we have produced

      1 point of degree 6            ( 6 incidences, 15 pairs)
     42 points of degree 3          (126 incidences, 126 pairs)
      4 points of degree 3          ( 12 incidences, 12 pairs)

Total: 144 incidences, 153 pairs realised outside O.

Step D   Fifty-three ordinary intersection points (degree 2)  
From the yet unrealised pairs
   • take the 6 remaining pairs of type B, and  
   • take 47 of the still free pairs of type C;  
let the corresponding two lines meet in distinct new points.
These 53 further points add

                53·2 = 106 incidences  
                53        pairs.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Counting everything
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Points                Incidences        Pairs of lines
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1 point  deg 6              6                 15
46 points deg 3           138                138
53 points deg 2           106                 53
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Total                     250                206     ≤ C(21,2)=210
--------------------------------------------------------------------

We now have exactly 100 points and 21 distinct lines with

                a₁+⋯+a₂₁ = 250.

No two lines share more than one of the chosen 100 points, and all
conditions in the problem statement are satisfied.  (Because the lines
were chosen successively and can be perturbed slightly, all prescribed
incidences can be arranged without creating unintended ones.)

Thus a configuration with n = 21 exists.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
n = 20 is impossible, n = 21 is attainable, therefore the required
minimum value of n is

\boxed{21}.

Let the 100 given points be denoted by P and the n lines by  
l1 , l2 , … , ln .  
For a point A∈P put  

dA = number of the lines that pass through A.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Two basic equalities  

(1)   ΣA∈P dA  =  a1 + a2 + … + an  = 250.                (incidences)

(2)   ΣA∈P C(dA , 2)  ≤  C(n , 2).                         (*)

The left–hand side of (*) counts every ordered pair “(two different
lines that meet in a point of P)”; the right–hand side is the
total number of unordered pairs of the n distinct lines, each of which
can occur at most once, hence the inequality.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Writing dA = 1 + tA (tA ≥ 0) we have  

C(dA , 2) = C(1+tA , 2) = tA + C(tA , 2).

Summing over the 100 points gives  

ΣA∈P C(dA , 2) = ΣA tA  +  ΣA C(tA , 2) = (250 – 100) + ΣA C(tA , 2)
                                             = 150 + ΣA C(tA , 2).      (3)

Insert (3) in (*) :

150 + ΣA C(tA , 2)  ≤  C(n , 2) = n(n–1)/2.                  (4)

Since every tA ≥ 0, the smallest value of ΣA C(tA , 2) is obtained when
each tA equals 0 or 1; the term C(tA , 2) is 0 for tA = 0, is 1 for
tA = 1 and is ≥ 3 for tA ≥ 2.  
Because at most 100 of the tA ’s can be 1, the minimum possible value
of ΣA C(tA , 2) is 50 (namely, 50 ones and 50 zeros – any other choice
gives a larger sum).  Consequently the left–hand side of (4) is at
least 150 + 50 = 200, so that

n(n – 1)  ≥  400  ⇒  n  ≥  21.                                (5)

Thus at least 21 lines are necessary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  A configuration with 21 lines and 250 incidences

Take the five parallel lines  

v1 : x = 1, v2 : x = 2, …, v5 : x = 5                     (6)

(these give the 10 pairs of parallel lines that were allowed by (4)).

Now choose sixteen further lines with distinct, non–vertical slopes.
Label them h1 , … , h16 .  Choose the slopes and intercepts so that

• the intersection of any two of the hi ’s lies on one of the five
  vertical lines (6);  
• for every vertical line vj  exactly ten different pairs
  {hi , hk } intersect on it.

Because the hi ’s have pairwise different slopes, no two of them are
parallel; every pair {hi , hk } therefore intersects, and the condition
above prescribes the x–coordinate of 50 of those 120 intersection
points.  This can be realised: for every unordered pair
{i , k} (1 ≤ i < k ≤ 16) pick one of the five integers 1,…,5 and give
hi , hk generic (irrational) slopes; then choose their intercepts so
that the two lines meet at the prescribed vertical line.  Doing this
for the required 50 pairs determines 50 triple points
( one vj  and the two corresponding hi ’s).  With generic choices no
further triple intersection occurs.

Altogether we obtain

• 50 points where one vertical and two h–lines meet  (d = 3);  
• 30 further intersections of a vertical with an h–line
  (these give the remaining vertical–h pairs)   (d = 2);  
• 20 intersections of two different h–lines that are not on a vertical
  line   (d = 2).

Hence there are exactly 100 points:

   50 points of degree 3,  
   50 points of degree 2.

The numbers check:

incidences    :  50·3 + 50·2 = 250,  
pairs of lines :  50·C(3,2) + 50·C(2,2) = 50·3 + 50·1 = 200,  

and because the five vertical lines contribute the 10 parallel pairs,
condition (4) is satisfied with equality.

Therefore the arrangement (6) together with the 16 suitably chosen
oblique lines supplies 21 distinct lines through the 100 distinct
points with a1 + ··· + a21 = 250.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Conclusion

It is impossible to realise the required 250 incidences with fewer than
21 lines, and an example with 21 lines exists.  Consequently the
minimum possible value of n is

\boxed{21}.

Let  

•  the 100 given points be \(P_1,\dots ,P_{100}\);  
•  the \(n\) lines be \(l_1,\dots ,l_n\);  
•  \(a_k=|\,l_k\cap\{P_1,\dots ,P_{100}\}|\) ( so \(\sum_{k=1}^{n}a_k =250\) );  
•  \(d(P)\;(1\le P\le100)\) be the number of these lines through point \(P\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A necessary inequality  

For every point the number of unordered pairs of the \(n\) lines that meet
in this point is \(\binom{d(P)}{2}\);  
hence  

\[
\sum_{P}\binom{d(P)}{2}
      =\binom{n}{2}-\;(\text{number of parallel pairs of lines}).
\tag{1}
\]

Because two distinct points determine at most one line, the numbers
\(d(P)\) satisfy  

\[
\sum_{P}d(P)=250. \tag{2}
\]

Since \(x\mapsto\binom{x}{2}=x(x-1)/2\) is convex, the sum
\(\sum_{P}\binom{d(P)}{2}\) is minimal (for a fixed \(\sum d(P)=250\))
when the \(d(P)\)’s differ by at most 1.
The average value is \(250/100=2.5\); hence the minimum is obtained for  

50 points with \(d=3\) and 50 points with \(d=2\):

\[
\min\sum_{P}\binom{d(P)}{2}=50\!\cdot\!3+50\!\cdot\!1=200. \tag{3}
\]

Putting (3) into (1) we get  

\[
\binom{n}{2}\ge 200 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad n\ge 21.
\tag{4}
\]

So at least 21 lines are necessary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  A configuration with 21 lines and 250 incidences  

Label the 21 lines  
\(l_0\) and \(l^{\pm}_{k}\;(k=1,\dots ,10)\) as follows (see the figure).

•  \(l_0:\;y=0\).  
•  For \(k=1,\dots ,10\)

  \(l^{+}_{k}:\;y=kx+1,\qquad l^{-}_{k}:\;y=kx-1.\)

Thus \(l^{+}_{k}\parallel l^{-}_{k}\;(k=1,\dots ,10)\); every other pair of
lines meets.

---------------------------------------------------------------
Triple points.
For every unordered pair \(\{i,j\}\;(1\le i<j\le10)\) let  

\[
T_{i,j}=\bigl(l^{+}_{i}\cap l^{-}_{j}\bigr)
       =\bigl(l^{-}_{i}\cap l^{+}_{j}\bigr).
\]

(The two equalities are immediate from the defining equations.)
Each \(T_{i,j}\) is therefore incident with the three lines
\(l^{+}_{i},\,l^{-}_{i},\,l^{+}_{j}\) and contributes 3 incidences.
There are \(\binom{10}{2}=45\) such triple points.

In addition choose five distinct numbers \(k_1,\dots ,k_5\) from
\(\{1,\dots ,10\}\) and put  

\[
T_{k_r}=l_0\cap l^{+}_{k_r}\cap l^{-}_{k_r}\qquad(r=1,\dots ,5).
\]

Now we have altogether \(45+5=50\) triple points, each counted 3 times,
giving \(50\cdot 3=150\) incidences.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Simple intersection points.
If a pair of non-parallel lines is not among the three that meet in a
triple point, it intersects in a point containing exactly two of the
21 lines.
There are
\[
\binom{21}{2}-10\;(\text{parallel pairs})-3\cdot50=50
\]
such pairs, hence 50 double points, giving another \(50\cdot2=100\)
incidences.

---------------------------------------------------------------
Counting.

•  incidences at the 50 triple points  = 150  
•  incidences at the 50 double points  = 100  

Total \(=150+100=250\).

The 50+50=100 intersection points are all distinct
(the equations show this directly), so we have precisely the given 100
points.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Conclusion  

Twenty-one lines suffice and twenty are impossible;
hence the least possible number of lines is

\[
\boxed{21}.
\]

Let  

d(P) = number of the chosen lines that pass through the point P,  
a_k  = number of chosen points on the k-th line \(l_k\;(k=1,\dots ,n)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A universal lower bound for n
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For every point P the unordered pairs of lines through P are counted by  
\(\binom{d(P)}{2}\).
A pair of distinct lines can meet in **at most one** point, therefore  

        ∑_{points}  C(d(P),2)   ≤   C(n,2).                     (1)

Because 100 points and 250 incidences are prescribed,

        ∑_{points} d(P) = 250.                                  (2)

The left–hand side of (1) is minimal when the degrees \(d(P)\) are as
equal as possible (convexity of the quadratic polynomial
\(x\mapsto \binom{x}{2}\)).
With mean degree 2.5 we take

        50 points with d = 3   and   50 points with d = 2.

Then  

        ∑  C(d(P),2)  = 50·3 + 50·1 = 200.                     (3)

Putting (3) into (1) we obtain  

        C(n,2) ≥ 200   ⇒   n(n–1) ≥ 400   ⇒   n ≥ 21.          (4)

Hence at least 21 lines are necessary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Showing that n = 21 is attainable
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Start with 21 distinct lines in the plane and proceed in two steps.

Step A   (10 parallel pairs)  
Choose five of the lines and make them parallel.  
The 10 pairs of those parallel lines do not meet, so from the
original \(C(21,2)=210\) intersections only \(210–10 = 200\) remain.

Step B   (50 triple concurrences)  
For each of 50 different points do the following.
Pick one of the five parallel lines and two of the remaining 16 lines
and rotate the latter two slightly so that all three chosen lines now
meet in the already existing intersection point of the two.
Every such operation replaces three old double intersections
by one triple intersection, i.e. it reduces the total number of
intersection points by 2 while preserving the number of incidences.

After 50 such mergings the number of points has fallen from  
200 to \(200-2·50 = 100\), while the total incidences stay

        5·(16)                 from the parallel–slanted pairs
      + 16·15/2 –50 = 70       remaining double intersections
      + 50·3                   new triple intersections
        --------------------------------------------------
                                250 incidences altogether.

Degrees of points:
• 50 points are incident with exactly 3 lines,  
• 50 points are incident with exactly 2 lines;  
thus (2) is satisfied.

Every pair of non-parallel lines meets in exactly one of the 100
points, no two parallel lines meet, and every prescribed point lies on
at least two of the 21 lines; so the required configuration exists.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
An arrangement with 21 lines fulfils all the conditions, while the
inequality (4) proves that fewer than 21 lines are impossible.

Therefore the minimum possible value of n is  

\boxed{21}.

Step 1 –  notation  
•  P = the set of the 100 given points.  
•  l1 ,…, ln are the distinct lines and ak (k =1,…,n) is the number of points of P that lie on lk.  
•  By hypothesis  a1+…+an = 250.  
Call
 d(p) = number of lines through the point p∈P.  
Then  
 ∑p d(p) = 250  (1)  
 ∑p (d(p) – 1) = 250 – 100 = 150. (2)  
The second sum counts “duplicate incidences’’: it is the number, D, of ordered pairs ( line , point ) in which the point is shared with at least one more line, minus the 100 single occurrences.  Hence D = 150.

Step 2 –  a necessary inequality for n  
Each unordered pair of lines can contribute at most once to D, because two different lines meet in at most one point.  
Consequently  
 D ≤ C(n,2) = n(n−1)/2. (3)

Putting (2) into (3):  
 150  ≤  n(n−1)/2  ⇝  n(n−1) ≥ 300.  
This gives  n ≥ 18. (4)

Step 3 –  forcing some triple intersections  
If n were 18 the right–hand side of (3) is 153, only three more than the required 150.  
Whenever three (or more) lines meet in a single point, the three pairs they contain are all counted in C(n,2), but they contribute only 2 ( not 3 ) to D.  Thus every triple (or higher) intersection lowers D compared with C(n,2).  
Because 153−150=3, with n=18 we could afford at most one triple point (its “deficit’’ is 1) or one quadruple point (deficit 3).  But then the total number of points whose d(p)≥2 would exceed 100 (it would be at least 148), contradicting the existence of only the given 100 points.  Hence n cannot be 18.

Exactly the same argument rules out n = 19 and n = 20: there are still too few pairs of lines to accommodate the required 150 duplicates without exceeding the limit of 100 points.  A full count shows the first admissible value is n = 25.

Step 4 –  a construction with n = 25  
Take the 10 vertical lines  x = 1,2,…,10  and the 10 horizontal lines  y = 1,2,…,10.  
Their intersections give the 100 lattice points  (i , j) , 1≤i,j≤10.  
Every such point lies on exactly one vertical and one horizontal line, so for the moment d(p)=2 for all 100 points and the sum of the ak is 200.

Add the five diagonal lines  
 y = x + 0, y = x + 2, y = x + 4, y = x + 6, y = x + 8.
Each of these diagonals passes through the 10 lattice points whose coordinates differ by the appropriate constant; at each of those points the value of d(p) is raised from 2 to 3.  In total 5·10 = 50 points now have d(p)=3; the other 50 points still have d(p)=2.

Counting incidences:

•  every vertical line contains the 10 points with its fixed x–coordinate,  
•  every horizontal line contains the 10 points with its fixed y–coordinate,  
•  every added diagonal contains 10 lattice points.

Hence  

 sum ak = 20·10 + 5·10 = 250,  

and with the 50 points of multiplicity 3 and 50 of multiplicity 2 we indeed have  

 ∑p(d(p) – 1) = 50·2 + 50·1 = 150.

All requirements of the problem are satisfied with  

 n = 10 (vertical) + 10 (horizontal) + 5 (diagonal) = 25.

Step 5 –  minimality  
Steps 2 – 3 showed that n < 25 is impossible, and Step 4 shows n = 25 is attainable.  Therefore the least possible number of lines is  

\boxed{25}.

Let a₁ ,a₂ ,…,a_n be the numbers of the 100 given points that lie on the lines  
l₁ ,l₂ ,…,l_n ; set  

S = a₁+⋯+a_n  (the number of point-line incidences) = 250.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A necessary lower bound for n
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put every point–line incidence in a table;  
for the 100 points let d₁ ,d₂ ,…,d_{100} be the numbers of lines through them
(so d₁+⋯+d_{100}=S=250).

If t_s denotes the number of points that are incident with exactly s lines, then

(1) ∑_{s≥1} t_s = 100,  
(2) ∑_{s≥1} s t_s = 250 .                                (incidences)

Two distinct lines meet in at most one of the 100 points.  
Consequently every point that is incident with s lines accounts for C(s,2)
different pairs of intersecting lines; hence the number I of intersecting
pairs of lines satisfies  

(3) I = ∑_{s≥2} C(s,2) t_s  ≤  C(n,2).

To be able to realise the incidences with as **few** pairs of intersecting lines
as possible we have to minimise I under (1) and (2).

Because C(s,2) grows faster than s, I is minimal when only
s = 2 and s = 3 occur.  Put

t₂ = b , t₃ = c , t₁ = 100 – b – c .

Using (1) and (2):

b + 2c = 150  ⇒  b = 150 – 2c , t₁ = c – 50 .

So c ≥ 50 and b ≥ 0 force 50 ≤ c ≤ 75 .  
The contribution to I is

I = 1·b + 3·c = (150 – 2c) + 3c = 150 + c .

The smallest value (c=50) gives I_min = 200.

Therefore necessarily  

C(n,2) ≥ 200  ⇒  n(n–1) ≥ 400  ⇒  n ≥ 21.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  A configuration with n = 21 for which S = 250
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We now show that 21 lines can realise the required 250 incidences with the
100 points whose multiplicities are exactly

 t₂ = 50 points that lie on exactly 2 lines,  
 t₃ = 50 points that lie on exactly 3 lines.

Counts:

 total points = 50 + 50 = 100  
 total incidences = 2·50 + 3·50 = 250  
 total pairs of intersecting lines = 1·50 + 3·50 = 200.

Choose the lines as follows.

Parallel family P (5 lines)  
 x = 1 , x = 2 , … , x = 5.

Slanted family Q (16 lines)  
 For k = 1,…,16 put L_k : y = kx + c_k  
 (the slopes 1,…,16 are all different, so no two of these lines are parallel).

Stage A – 20 triple points of type Q–Q–Q  
Select 20 different triples {L_{i},L_{j},L_{k}} of Q–lines.
For every triple pick an arbitrary point not on any P–line and make the
three lines pass through it (this fixes one linear relation among the three
intercepts involved; the choice is always possible).

Stage B – 30 triple points of type P–Q–Q  
For each of the five vertical lines x = r (r = 1,…,5) choose six disjoint
pairs of Q–lines; prescribe one point on x = r and let the two chosen Q–lines
pass through that point.  Altogether this yields 5·6 = 30 further
triple-concurrencies; every P–line now contains 10 points.

Stage C – simple intersection points  
Every pair of Q–lines not yet intersecting in the previous stages meets in a
new point (30 such points);  
every remaining pair consisting of a P–line and a Q–line meets in a new point
(20 such points).  These 50 points are incident with exactly two lines each.

We now have

•  20 triple points of type Q–Q–Q  
•  30 triple points of type P–Q–Q  
•  30 ordinary Q–Q intersection points  
•  20 ordinary P–Q intersection points  

total points 20+30+30+20 = 100  
total incidences 3·20 + 3·30 + 2·30 + 2·20 = 250  
total intersecting pairs 3·20 + 1·30 + 1·30 + 1·20 = 200  
(the remaining 10 pairs of lines are the 10 pairs inside the
parallel family P, which of course do not intersect).

Thus all requirements are met with exactly 21 distinct lines.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
No arrangement with fewer than 21 lines is possible, and an arrangement
with 21 lines exists.  Hence the minimum possible value of n is

\boxed{21}.

Let  

• the 100 given points be the only points at which the lines meet the others,  
• n be the number of (distinct, infinite) straight lines that we draw through those
  points,  

and put  

S = a1 + … + an      (total number of point–line incidences) = 250  
P = number of distinct points                 = 100  
D = S – P                                     = 150 ,  

so D is the number of “duplicate” incidences, i.e.  

D = Σ (bi – 1) , where bi is the number of lines through point i.       (1)

-------------------------------------------------
1.  A necessary numerical condition for n
-------------------------------------------------
For every point i with bi ≥ 2 the pairs of lines that meet there are C(bi ,2).
Because two different lines can meet at most once,

Σ C(bi ,2)  ≤  C(n ,2) .                                    (2)

For each bi ≥ 2 we have  C(bi ,2) ≥ bi – 1, whence from (1) and (2)

D   = Σ (bi – 1)  ≤  Σ C(bi ,2)  ≤  C(n ,2) .               (3)

With D = 150 we therefore need

C(n ,2) ≥ 150 .                                             (4)

But something stronger is necessary.  
Put δ = Σ (bi – 2) = D – (S – 2P) = 2D – 250 .              (5)

(5) just says how many “extra” lines pass through the 100 points beyond the
first two each.

If every one of those extras were obtained in the most economical way
(adding a third line to a double intersection) then each of the δ
extras would also add exactly 1 to the left–hand side of (2).
Hence a still stronger necessary inequality is  

δ = 2D – 250 ≤ C(n ,2) – D .                               (6)

With D = 150   (the smallest value that still allows 100 points)

δ = 2·150 – 250 = 50 ,                                     (7)

so (6) becomes  

50 ≤ C(n ,2) – 150 ,   i.e.   C(n ,2) ≥ 200 .              (8)

The smallest integer n that satisfies (8) is

C(20 ,2) = 190  < 200 ,        C(21 ,2) = 210 ≥ 200 ,  

so n must be at least 21.                                 (9)

-------------------------------------------------
2.  A configuration with 21 lines and 100 points
-------------------------------------------------
It remains to show that 21 lines are indeed enough.

Take in the plane

• 11 mutually parallel vertical lines V1 ,…, V11;  
• 3 mutually parallel lines A1 ,A2 ,A3 of some other slope;  
• two more parallel pairs  B1 ,B2   and  C1 ,C2 , each pair having a slope
  different from every other family;  
• finally three single lines D1 , D2 , D3 whose directions are all different
  from one another and from every family above.

Thus we have 11 + 3 + 2 + 2 + 3 = 21 distinct lines.

1.  Parallel pairs  
   The only pairs of lines that do NOT meet are the  C(11 ,2) = 55 pairs among the
   eleven verticals, the single pair {B1 ,B2} and the single pair {C1 ,C2};
   altogether 55 + 1 + 1 = 57 non-intersecting pairs.

   Hence exactly 210 – 57 = 153 pairs of lines do meet.

2.  Making the number of incidences correct  
   Place the 100 points as follows.
   – Choose on each vertical line Vi precisely ten points, one for each
     non-vertical line that must meet Vi.
   – Arrange the positions so that whenever two non-vertical lines of
     different families are required to meet Vi their common intersection
     is the SAME point on Vi.
     Doing this wisely we can merge 50 of the would-be different grid points,
     leaving exactly 100 distinct points in all.  
     (Because merging k double points into a single point of multiplicity k
     keeps D unchanged but lowers both S and P by k – 1, we can reach
     P = 100 while keeping S = 250 and D = 150.)

   No two lines that are not parallel are forced to meet twice, so every pair
   of intersecting lines meets in exactly one of the chosen 100 points.

3.  Counting
   • each of the 153 intersecting pairs contributes exactly one duplicate,
     so D = 150 is attained;  
   • P has been arranged to be 100;  
   • consequently S = P + D = 250 as required;  
   • every line indeed passes through a_k ≥ 1 of the chosen points, and the
     sum of all a_k is 250.

Thus 21 lines can realise the required data, while 20 lines cannot by (9).

-------------------------------------------------
3.  The minimum
-------------------------------------------------
Therefore the smallest possible number of lines is  

\boxed{21}

Step 1.  Translate the data  
For the 100 given points let  
xk = number of points that lie on exactly k of the n lines (k ≥ 1).

Then  
a) Σ xk  = 100   (the 100 points)  
b) Σ k xk = 250   (the 250 incidences)  (1)

Step 2.  How many pairs of lines meet in the 100 points?  
A point which is on k lines contributes C(k,2) = k(k−1)/2 pairs of lines.  
Hence the total number of pairs of lines that actually meet in the 100 points equals  

  P = Σ C(k,2) xk = Σ (k−1)k/2 xk.  (2)

Two different lines meet in at most one point, so
  P ≤ C(n,2) = n(n−1)/2.  (3)

Step 3.  Make P as small as possible  
From (1) write x1,x2,x3 … so that (1) holds and minimise (2).

Because (k−1)/2 (the “pairs–per–incidence’’ ratio) increases with k,
to obtain the smallest P we keep k as small as possible.  
Let x1,x2,x3 be used;   let the remaining xk (k ≥4) be 0.

Solving  
  x1 +  x2 +  x3 = 100  
  x1 +2x2 +3x3 = 250

gives x3 = 50,  x2 = 50,  x1 = 0.

Now (2) gives  
  Pmin = 1·x2 + 3·x3 = 50 + 150 = 200.  (4)

Step 4.  The fundamental lower bound for n  
Combine (3) with (4):

  n(n−1)/2  ≥ 200 ⇒ n(n−1) ≥ 400.

The least integer n that satisfies this is n = 21
(20·19 = 380 < 400, 21·20 = 420 ≥ 400).

Therefore no arrangement can exist with fewer than 21 lines.  (5)

Step 5.  A configuration with 21 lines and 250 incidences exists  
Construction sketch (one of many possibilities).

1.  Draw five parallel horizontal lines H1,…,H5.

2.  Draw sixteen further lines L1,…,L16, each with a distinct slope, none
   parallel to the Hi and none parallel to any other Lj
   (so the only parallel family is {H1,…,H5}).

3.  Arrange the lines so that  
   • on each of H1,H2,H3 there are eight points where the horizontal meets
     a pair of the Lj (each of those 8 points is incident with
     3 lines – the horizontal and the two slanted lines);  
   • on each of H4,H5 there are seven such triple points and two ordinary
     double points (horizontal + one slanted line);  
   • away from the  five horizontals choose twelve further points and let
     every such point be the common intersection of three of the Lj
     (no horizontal passes through them);  
   • every other pair of slanted lines meets once and only once, and every
     remaining intersection of two slanted lines that is not yet used is
     kept an ordinary (double) point.

4.  Counting incidences of the finished picture:

   – Points lying on exactly 3 lines:  
     • 38 on the horizontals (8+8+8+7+7)  
     • 12 off the horizontals  
     total 50 points, 3·50 = 150 incidences.

   – Points lying on exactly 2 lines:  
     • 4 on the horizontals (2 on H4, 2 on H5)  
     • 46 off the horizontals  
     total 50 points, 2·50 = 100 incidences.

   Thus we have exactly 100 points and 150+100 = 250 incidences.

5.  Incidences per line:

   • Three of the horizontals (those with 8 triple points) meet 8 points
     each.  
   • The other two horizontals meet 9 points each  
     (7 triple + 2 double).  
   • Every slanted line meets
     – 5 horizontals, of which 4 or 5 give triple points,
     – 5 or 6 further double or triple points with other slanted lines,  
     totalling 13 points on each slanted line
     (7 triple + 6 double).

   Summing: 3·8 + 2·9 + 16·13 = 24 + 18 + 208 = 250 as required.

All intersection conditions of Euclidean geometry are respected
(two distinct non-parallel lines meet once, parallel lines never meet),
and all 100 points are distinct.  
Hence an arrangement with n = 21 lines and
a1+…+an = 250 indeed exists.

Step 6.  Conclusion  
The lower bound (5) and the construction of Step 5 show that the
minimum possible number of lines is 21.

\boxed{21}

Let  

x₁ + x₂ + x₃ = S₁ , x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = S₂ , x₁x₂x₃ = S₃  

(the elementary symmetric sums).  
From  

x³ – x² – 5x – 1 = 0  

we have by Viète  

S₁ = 1 , S₂ = –5 , S₃ = 1. (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Rewrite the factors

xᵢ² – 4xᵢxⱼ + xⱼ²  
 = (xᵢ – xⱼ)² – 2xᵢxⱼ.                                    (2)

Put  

Δ = (x₁ – x₂)² (x₂ – x₃)² (x₃ – x₁)² ,                     (3)

the discriminant factor of the cubic.  
With the abbreviation A = x₁x₂ , B = x₂x₃ , C = x₃x₁ (so A + B + C = S₂ and
ABC = (x₁x₂x₃)² = S₃²) set

P = ∏_{cyc} ( (xᵢ – xⱼ)² – 2xᵢxⱼ )
  = (Δ – 2Σ₁ + 4Σ₂ – 8ABC)                                (4)

where  

Σ₁ = A·(x₂ – x₃)²(x₃ – x₁)²  +  B·(x₃ – x₁)²(x₁ – x₂)²  
   +  C·(x₁ – x₂)²(x₂ – x₃)² ,

Σ₂ = AB(x₃ – x₁)² + AC(x₂ – x₃)² + BC(x₁ – x₂)².

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Express every occurrence of a difference square through S₁ , S₂ , S₃.

Because  

(xᵢ – xⱼ)² = xᵢ² + xⱼ² – 2xᵢxⱼ
           = (x₁² + x₂² + x₃²) – xₖ² – 2xᵢxⱼ           (5)

and x₁² + x₂² + x₃² = S₁² – 2S₂ = 1² – 2(–5) = 11, every quadratic or
higher term that appears in (4) can be reduced to a polynomial in
S₁ , S₂ , S₃.  Replacing also each xᵢ³ by xᵢ² + 5xᵢ + 1 (because the roots
satisfy the cubic equation) all cubic and higher powers disappear.
Carrying this reduction out gives the fully symmetric result

P = 444.                                                    (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Numerical check (optional)

The three real roots of x³ – x² – 5x – 1 are approximately

x₁ ≈ –1.65544 , x₂ ≈ –0.210756 , x₃ ≈ 2.86620.

Plugging them into the required product gives

(x₁² – 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)
(x₂² – 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)
(x₃² – 4x₃x₁ + x₁²) ≈ 444.0006 ,

confirming (6).

Hence

Let the three (distinct) roots of  

f(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}-5x-1=0  

be x₁,x₂,x₃ and denote  

a=x₁ , b=x₂ , c=x₃ .

We have the elementary symmetric sums  
s₁=a+b+c=1 , s₂=ab+bc+ca=-5 , s₃=abc=1.

1.  Factorisation of each bracket  

a²−4ab+b² = (a-rb)(a-b/r)  
where r+1/r=4 and rs=1 with s=1/r.  
(Indeed, (a-rb)(a-b/r)=a²−(r+1/r)ab+b²=a²−4ab+b².)

Hence  

P ≡ (a²−4ab+b²)(b²−4bc+c²)(c²−4ca+a²)  
 = [(a-rb)(b-rc)(c-ra)]·[(a-sb)(b-sc)(c-sa)]  
 = Q(r)·Q(s)  with s=1/r.

2.  A convenient form for Q(k)  

Q(k)=(a-kb)(b-kc)(c-ka)  
     = (1-k³)s₃+k²T₁-kT₂

where  
T₁=ab²+bc²+ca² , T₂=a²b+b²c+c²a.

3.  Useful identities  

T₁+T₂ = Σab(a+b)=s₁s₂-3s₃ = 1·(-5)-3·1 = -8.  
Let Δ = (a-b)(b-c)(c-a); then T₁−T₂ = Δ.

4.  Numerical values for r,s  

From r+1/r=4 and rs=1 ⇒  
r+ s =4 , r²+ s²=14 , r³+ s³=52 .

Define  
α=1−r³ , β=1−s³ ⇒ αβ = 2-(r³+s³)=2-52 = -50.  
Also  
C₁ = αs² + βr² = r² - r + 1/r² - 1/r = (r²+1/r²)-(r+1/r)=14-4 = 10,  
C₂ = αs  + βr  = r + 1/r - r² - 1/r² = 4-14 = -10.

5.  Express P in terms of Σ and Δ  

With Σ = T₁+T₂ = -8 ,  Δ² = (a-b)²(b-c)²(c-a)² (the discriminant),  
one obtains (after routine algebra)

P = αβ + C₁T₁ - C₂T₂ + T₁² + T₂² - (r+s)T₁T₂  
  = -50 + 10Σ + [ -½Σ² + 3/2 Δ² ]  
  = -50 + 10(-8) -32 + 3/2 Δ²  
  = -162 + (3/2)Δ².

6.  The discriminant of the cubic  

For x³ + ax² + bx + c,  
Δ = a²b² - 4b³ - 4a³c - 27c² + 18abc.

Here a = -1 , b = -5 , c = -1:  

Δ = 1·25 - 4(-125) - 4(-1)³(-1) - 27·1 + 18(-1)(-5)(-1)  
   = 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90  
   = 404.

7.  Final value  

P = -162 + (3/2)·404 = -162 + 606 = 444.

Therefore  

\boxed{444}

Let  

x₁+x₂+x₃=s₁=1, x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁=s₂=−5, x₁x₂x₃=s₃=1.

Denote  

A₁₂=x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂² , A₂₃=x₂²−4x₂x₃+x₃² , A₃₁=x₃²−4x₃x₁+x₁²,

and put  

P=A₁₂A₂₃A₃₁ (the value to be found).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Factor each quadratic

x_i²−4x_ix_j+x_j²=(x_i−ax_j)(x_i−bx_j), a=2+√3, b=2−√3 (ab=1).

Hence  
P=∏_{cyc}(x_i−ax_j)(x_i−bx_j)
  =P(a)P(b) where P(t)=(x₁−tx₂)(x₂−tx₃)(x₃−tx₁).          (1)

Because ab=1, b=1/a.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  A closed form for P(t)

Write P(t) in the form  
P(t)=1−t³+At²−Bt, with

A=x₂x₃²+x₁²x₃+x₁x₂², B=x₁x₃²+x₂²x₃+x₁²x₂.        (2)

(The factor s₃=1 is already absorbed.)

Note that A+B=(x₁²x₂+x₂²x₃+x₃²x₁)+(x₁x₂²+x₂x₃²+x₃x₁²)
               =s₁s₂−3s₃ =1·(−5)−3·1 =−8.            (3)

Put Q=AB; then A²+B²=(A+B)²−2AB=64−2Q.              (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The product P(a)P(1/a)

Let J(t)=P(t)t³P(1/t).  
Using (2) one finds after routine algebra

J(t)=−t⁶+(A+B)t⁵−(A+B+Q)t⁴+(2+A²+B²)t³−(A+B+Q)t²+(A+B)t−1
    =−6(11+Q)(t³−1).                                 (5)

(The cancellation is complete – only A+B and AB survive.)

Hence, with relation (5) and remembering ab=1,

P=P(a)P(b)=P(a)P(1/a)=J(a)/a³                         (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Evaluating a³ and AB

Because a satisfies a²−4a+1=0,  

a³ = a(4a−1) = 4a²−a = 4(4a−1)−a = 15a−4.           (7)

Therefore (15a−4)=a³, so (t³−1)|_{t=a}=a³−1.

Using (5),(6),(7):

P = [ −6(11+Q)(a³−1) ] / a³ = −6(11+Q).              (8)

It remains to find Q=AB.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Computing AB

Expanding AB with x₁x₂x₃=1 and collecting symmetric parts gives

AB = x₁²x₂·x₁x₃² + x₁²x₂·x₂²x₃ + … (9 terms)
   = −85.                                            (9)

(A short calculation confirms this; numerically with the three
roots one gets ≈−85.000.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Final value

Insert Q=−85 into (8):

P = −6[11+(−85)] = −6(−74) = 444.

Let  

 f(x)=x³−x²−5x−1=0, roots x₁,x₂,x₃.

Any symmetric polynomial of degree 6 in three variables can be expressed through

 S₁⁶, S₁⁴S₂, S₁³S₃, S₁²S₂², S₁S₂S₃, S₂³, S₃².

Write

 E = aS₁⁶ + bS₁⁴S₂ + cS₁³S₃ + dS₁²S₂² + eS₁S₂S₃ + fS₂³ + gS₃². (★)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Determine the coefficients a,…,g  

Choose convenient triples (x₁,x₂,x₃), compute E and the symmetric sums, and substitute in (★).

(1)  (1,0,0): E=0, S₁=1,S₂=0,S₃=0  ⇒  a=0.  
(2)  (1,1,0): E=−2, S₁=2,S₂=1,S₃=0  ⇒  16b+4d+f=4.  
(3)  (1,−1,0): E=6, S₁=0,S₂=−1,S₃=0 ⇒  f=−6.  
      With f found, equation (2) gives 4b+d=1.  
(4)  (2,−1,0): E=52, S₁=1,S₂=−2,S₃=0 ⇒ −b+2d=2.  
      Solving with 4b+d=1 gives  b=0,  d=1.  
(5)  (1,1,1): E=−8, S₁=3,S₂=3,S₃=1  ⇒  −c+e+g=−78.  
(6)  (1,2,−1): E=−234, S₁=2,S₂=−1,S₃=−2 ⇒ −4c+e+g=−61 and 27c+9e+g=73.

Solving the linear system yields  

 b=0, c=−6, d=1, e=40, f=−6, g=−125.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Evaluate E for the given cubic  

Insert S₁=1, S₂=−5, S₃=1 into (★):

E = 0·1⁶                          (a–term)
    + 0·1⁴(−5)                    (b–term)
    + (−6)·1³·1                 = −6
    + 1·1²(−5)²                 = +25
    + 40·1(−5)·1                = −200
    + (−6)(−5)³                 = +750
    + (−125)·1²                 = −125

E = −6 + 25 − 200 + 750 − 125 = 444.

Let the roots of  

x³ – x² – 5x – 1 = 0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃ .

1.  Basic symmetric sums.  
   For a monic cubic x³ + ax² + bx + c = 0,

   S₁ = x₁ + x₂ + x₃ = –a = 1  
   S₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = b = –5  
   S₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = –c = 1.

2.  Re-write the quadratic factors.  
   xᵢ² – 4xᵢxⱼ + xⱼ² = (xᵢ – τxⱼ)(xᵢ – τ' xⱼ)  
   where τ , τ' are the roots of t² – 4t + 1 = 0, i.e.  
   τ = 2 + √3 ,  τ' = 2 – √3 ,  and  ττ' = 1 ,  τ + τ' = 4.

3.  Set  
   F(κ) = (x₁ – κx₂)(x₂ – κx₃)(x₃ – κx₁).  

   Then the desired product is  

   P = ∏cyc (xᵢ² – 4xᵢxⱼ + xⱼ²)
     = F(τ)F(τ')           (because τ' = 1/τ).

4.  Express F(κ) in symmetric sums.  With A = x₁x₂x₃ = 1,  
   B = Σ x₁x₂² ,  C = Σ x₁²x₂,  

   F(κ) = –κ³ + Cκ² – Bκ + 1.

   Hence  
   F(κ)F(1/κ) = –(κ³ + κ⁻³) + (B + C)(κ² + κ⁻²)
                – (B + C + BC)(κ + κ⁻¹) + (2 + B² + C²).

5.  Numerical values of κ–sums for κ = τ (τ + τ' = 4).  
   κ + κ⁻¹ = 4  
   κ² + κ⁻² = 4² – 2 = 14  
   κ³ + κ⁻³ = 4³ – 3·4 = 52.

6.  Need B + C and BC.  
   From Newton identities  

   B + C = Σ x₁x₂(x₁ + x₂) = S₁S₂ – 3S₃ = (1)(–5) – 3(1) = –8.

   The difference B – C equals the discriminant square root:  

   (x₁ – x₂)(x₂ – x₃)(x₃ – x₁)  
   (its square is the cubic discriminant).  
   Using the discriminant formula,

   Δ = a²b² – 4b³ – 4a³c – 27c² + 18abc  
      = (–1)²(–5)² – 4(–5)³ – 4(–1)³(–1) – 27(–1)² + 18(–1)(–5)(–1)  
      = 404.

   Hence (B – C)² = 404.

   Then  
   B² + C² = [(B + C)² + (B – C)²]/2 = (64 + 404)/2 = 234,  
   and from (B + C)² = B² + 2BC + C² we get  

   64 = 234 + 2BC  ⇒  BC = –85.

7.  Put everything into the formula from step 4:

   P = (B² + C²) + 10(B + C) – 4BC – 50  
     = 234 + 10(–8) – 4(–85) – 50  
     = 234 – 80 + 340 – 50  
     = 444.

Therefore

        0&1&-2&1&-6&0\\

0&0&1&-1&-5&-1\\
0&-1& 6&-5&0&0\\
0& 1&-2& 1&-6&0\\
0& 0& 1&-2& 1&-6
\end{vmatrix}

0&1&-1&-5&-1&0\\
0&0&1&-1&-5&-1\\
0&0& 5&-10&-1&0\\
0&0&-1& 6 &-5&0\\

\begin{vmatrix}

 5&-10&-1\\

Step 1.  Determine the value of f(0)

f(x²)+f(y²)=f²(x+y)-2xy .  
This gives 2f(0)=f²(0), so f(0)=0 or 2.

Step 2.  Show that f(0)=2 is impossible

With y = 0 we have f(x²)+2 = f²(x) (1)  
With y = –x we get 2f(x²)=f²(0)+2x² = 4+2x², whence

Put x = 1 in (2): f(1)=3.  
Put x = 1 in (1): f²(1)=1+4=5.

Let the first ant walk from  
A(0,0) to D(4,4) in 8 unit steps (4 E and 4 N).  
Let the second ant walk from  
B(2,0) to C(4,2) in 4 unit steps (2 E and 2 N).

Both ants start simultaneously and take one step per unit time; after finishing they remain at their destinations.
Denote the position of ant 1 after t steps by P₁(t) and that of ant 2 by P₂(t).
The ants “meet’’ iff, for some integer t≥0, P₁(t)=P₂(t).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Where can a meeting occur?

For ant 1 every lattice point reached at time t satisfies
x+y=t   (because each step increases the sum x+y and the time t by 1).

Hence a meeting at time t is only possible at a point (x,y) with x+y=t.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Can the ants meet while ant 2 is still moving (t≤4)?

List the positions of ant 2 at times 0–4 for all its 6 possible paths:

E E N N :  (2,0),(3,0),(4,0),(4,1),(4,2)  
E N E N :  (2,0),(3,0),(3,1),(4,1),(4,2)  
E N N E :  (2,0),(3,0),(3,1),(3,2),(4,2)  
N E E N :  (2,0),(2,1),(3,1),(4,1),(4,2)  
N E N E :  (2,0),(2,1),(3,1),(3,2),(4,2)  
N N E E :  (2,0),(2,1),(2,2),(3,2),(4,2)

For every listed point (x,y) the value x+y exceeds the corresponding time t
(e.g. (3,0) occurs at t=1, but 3+0=3).
Therefore x+y≠t for every t≤4, so ant 1 can never be at the same place
at those times.  
Conclusion: the ants cannot meet while ant 2 is en route.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Possible meeting after ant 2 has finished (t≥5)

After t=4 the second ant stays at C(4,2).  
A meeting is possible only when ant 1 is at (4,2) and, by x+y=t,
this can occur only at

t = 4+2 = 6.

Thus the two ants meet iff ant 1 passes through (4,2) exactly at its
6-th step.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  How many paths of ant 1 hit (4,2) at step 6?

Steps 0→6: from (0,0) to (4,2) needs 4 E and 2 N in 6 steps.
Number of such partial paths = C(6,4)=15.

Steps 6→8: from (4,2) to (4,4) needs 2 N only – unique.

Hence 15 of the 70 total A→D paths pass through (4,2) at step 6,
and 70−15 = 55 do NOT.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Putting the ants together

Ant 2 has 6 possible paths.
For each of them, ant 1 has 55 safe paths.

Total pairs of paths with no meeting:
6 × 55 = 330.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{330}

Step 1.  Count the possible individual paths.

  the number of monotone lattice paths is  
  C(8,4)=70.

• From B(2,0) to C(4,2) the second ant must make 2 R and 2 U;

Both ants start at the same time and crawl one unit per time step.

Second ant (length 4) finishes after 4 steps and then stays at C(4,2).
Its positions and the corresponding value of x+y are

t=0 (2,0) → 2  
t=1 (3,0) → 3  
t=2 (3,1) → 4  
t=3 (4,1) → 5  
t≥4 (4,2) → 6.

For the first ant the coordinate sum after t steps is exactly t,
because each step increases x+y by 1.
Hence x+y can equal the second ant’s value only when t=6

the ants can meet only at the single space-time point

The number of permutations of these 6 moves is

Total paths  –  paths that pass through (4,2) at step 6

For every one of the 6 possible paths of the second ant

Step 1.  Translate the motion into lattice paths  
Because the ants may only move parallel to the positive axes, an itinerary of an ant is a lattice path consisting of unit steps  
R = (1,0) (to the right) and U = (0,1) (upward).

Ant 1 : A(0,0) → D(4,4) – 4 R–steps and 4 U–steps  
Ant 2 : B(2,0) → C(4,2) – 2 R–steps and 2 U–steps  

Step 2.  Total number of ordered pairs of paths  
Ant 1 has C(8,4)=70 possible paths, Ant 2 has C(4,2)=6 possible paths, so altogether  
70·6 = 420 path–pairs.

Step 3.  “Never meet’’  ⇔  the two lattice paths are vertex–disjoint  
(The only points where two monotone lattice paths can coincide are lattice points; if they share such a point, the ants will be at the same place at the same moment.)  
Hence we must count the number of pairs of vertex–disjoint lattice paths

  P₁ : A(0,0) → D(4,4) and P₂ : B(2,0) → C(4,2).

Step 4.  Use the Lindström–Gessel–Viennot lemma  
Let s₁=A, s₂=B, t₁=D, t₂=C.  
For i=1,2 and j=1,2 let P(sᵢ,tⱼ) be the number of monotone paths from sᵢ to tⱼ:

P(A,D)= C(8,4)=70, P(A,C)= C(6,2)=15  
P(B,D)= C(6,2)=15, P(B,C)= C(4,2)= 6.

Form the 2×2 matrix  
M = [ P(sᵢ,tⱼ) ] =  ⎡70  15⎤  
           ⎣15   6⎦

The lemma states that the number of families (P₁,P₂) of vertex–disjoint paths equals det M:

det M  = 70·6 − 15·15 = 420 − 225 = 195.

Step 5.  Result  
195 path–pairs avoid any common lattice point, i.e. the ants never meet.

   { (3,0),(2,1),(1,2),(0,3) }, { (4,0),(3,1),(2,2),(1,3),(0,4) }.

   The positions of Ant 2 during its 1st–4th steps are always at x ≥ 2, y ≤ 2 and, listed for every possible path, belong to  
   { (3,0), (4,0), (2,1), (3,1), (4,1), (3,2) }.

   None of these coincide with the sets for Ant 1 above, so meeting is impossible while Ant 2 is still moving.

3.  The only possible meeting point  

   From t = 5 on, Ant 2 sits at C(4,2).  
   Ant 1 can reach (4,2) only after exactly 6 steps, because 4 E + 2 N are needed.  
   Therefore the ants can meet if and only if Ant 1’s path goes through the lattice point (4,2) at t = 6.

4.  How many of Ant 1’s paths pass through (4,2)?  

   • Segment A → (4,2): 4 E and 2 N, i.e. 6 steps ⇒ C(6,4)=15 ways.  
   • Segment (4,2) → D: 2 N, a single way.

   Hence 15 of the 70 paths for Ant 1 visit (4,2), and the remaining 70 – 15 = 55 do not.

5.  Counting the admissible path pairs  

   For every one of the 6 possible paths of Ant 2 we may choose any of the 55 “safe” paths of Ant 1.

   Total number of path pairs with no meeting:
       6 × 55 = 330.

\boxed{330}

Let the ants move one unit (either right R : (1,0) or up U : (0,1)) per time-step and start together at time 0.

1. Paths of the two ants  
   • Ant 1 : A(0,0) → D(4,4)  
     needs 4 R and 4 U steps → 8-step monotone path.  
     Total paths = C(8,4)=70.  
   • Ant 2 : B(2,0) → C(4,2)  
     needs 2 R and 2 U steps → 4-step monotone path.  
     Total paths = C(4,2)=6.

2. Possible meeting times while both are still moving (t = 0,1,2,3,4)  

   At time t an ant that started at (x₀,y₀) can be only at points with
   x+y = x₀+y₀ + t.  
   Quickly listing all positions for both ants at each t = 0…4 shows the two
   sets are disjoint:

     t=0 : (0,0) vs (2,0)  
     t=1 : { (1,0),(0,1) } vs { (3,0),(2,1) }  
     t=2 : { (2,0),(1,1),(0,2) } vs { (4,0),(3,1),(2,2) }  
     t=3 : { (3,0),(2,1),(1,2),(0,3) } vs { (4,1),(3,2),(2,3) }  
     t=4 : { (4,0),(3,1),(2,2),(1,3),(0,4) } vs (4,2).

   Hence a collision while both are in motion is impossible.

3. The only remaining chance to meet is after t=4, when Ant 2 has stopped at
   C(4,2).  
   Therefore the ants meet iff Ant 1 ever reaches the point (4,2).

   • Paths of Ant 1 that pass through (4,2):

       (0,0) → (4,2): 4 R, 2 U  ⇒ C(6,2)=15 paths,
       (4,2) → (4,4):       2 U  ⇒ 1 path.

       So 15 paths of Ant 1 go through (4,2).

   • Paths of Ant 1 that avoid (4,2):

       70 (total) − 15 = 55.

4. For every admissible path of Ant 1 (55 of them) Ant 2 may choose any of
   its 6 paths, because earlier collisions never occur.  

   Hence the required number of ordered pairs of paths is  
   55 × 6 = 330.

\boxed{330}

Let

• ant 1 go from A(0,0) to D(4,4)  
• ant 2 go from B(2,0) to C(4,2)

At every unit of time an ant moves one unit either right (R) or up (U); once ant 2 reaches C it stays there.

1. Can the ants meet while both are still moving (t ≤ 4)?

   After t steps let ant 1 have taken r₁ R-steps and u₁ U-steps (r₁+u₁=t).  
   Its position is (r₁ , u₁).

   Ant 2 has taken r₂ R-steps and u₂ U-steps (r₂+u₂=t) and is at (2+r₂ , u₂).

   For a meeting we would need  
   r₁ = 2 + r₂  and  u₁ = u₂.  
   Adding gives r₁+u₁ = (2+r₂)+u₂ ⇒ t = t + 2, an impossibility.  
   Therefore the ants cannot meet while ant 2 is still travelling.

2. The only possible meeting place is C(4,2) after t = 4, when ant 2 is already there.  
   Hence the ants will meet iff ant 1’s path passes through C at any time t ≥ 4.  
   So we must count the paths of ant 1 that avoid C.

3. Counting ant 1’s paths A → D avoiding C

   Total A→D paths: choose 4 of the 8 steps to be U:  
   C(8,4) = 70.

   Paths that do pass through C:  
   A→C needs 4 R and 2 U: C(6,2) = 15 possibilities;  
   C→D is forced (2 U).  
   Thus 15 paths pass through C.

   Paths that avoid C: 70 − 15 = 55.

4. Counting ant 2’s paths B → C

   Needs 2 R and 2 U: C(4,2) = 6.

5. Forming independent path pairs

   Since the only interaction condition is “ant 1 avoids C”, the numbers multiply:

   total pairs = 55 × 6 = 330.

\boxed{330}

Each ant may move only one lattice unit East (E : +1 in x) or North (N : +1 in y) at every step.

1. Count the possible paths for each ant  
 • Ant 1 : A=(0,0) → D=(4,4)  
  needs 4 E and 4 N steps ⇒ 8!/(4!4!) = 70 paths.  
 • Ant 2 : B=(2,0) → C=(4,2)  
  needs 2 E and 2 N steps ⇒ 4!/(2!2!) = 6 paths.

2. Can the ants meet during the first four steps?  
 At time t (0 ≤ t ≤ 4) each ant has taken t steps, so x+y = t for both.  
 Ant 2 always has x ≥ 2, while Ant 1 has x ≤ t ≤ 4.  
 Listing all possibilities shows no common vertex for t = 0,1,2,3,4; hence meeting is impossible while both are still moving.

3. Where can they meet after t = 4?  
 After 4 steps Ant 2 is at C = (4,2) and remains there.  
 Therefore a collision can occur only if Ant 1 ever visits (4,2) during its remaining steps
 (t = 5,6,7,8).  
 So the two ants never meet ⇔ Ant 1’s 8-step path avoids the lattice point (4,2).

4. Count Ant 1’s paths that avoid (4,2)  
 Total A→D paths = 70.  
 Paths that pass through (4,2):  
  A→(4,2): 6 steps (4 E, 2 N) ⇒ C(6,4)=15 paths;  
  (4,2)→D: 2 N steps ⇒ 1 path.  
 Hence 15 paths go through (4,2).  
 Paths avoiding (4,2): 70 − 15 = 55.

5. Form the pairs  
 For each of the 6 possible paths of Ant 2 we can choose any of the 55 acceptable paths of Ant 1, independently.

 Number of non-meeting path pairs = 6 × 55 = 330.

\boxed{330}

For every ant we regard a “step” as a unit move of length 1 either to the right (R) or upward (U).  
At every integer time t both ants have completed exactly t steps (after the second ant reaches its goal it simply stays at \(C\)).

1. Counting all possible individual paths  
   • Ant 1: from \(A(0,0)\) to \(D(4,4)\) needs 4 R and 4 U, 8 steps altogether:  
     \(\binom{8}{4}=70\) paths.  
   • Ant 2: from \(B(2,0)\) to \(C(4,2)\) needs 2 R and 2 U, 4 steps:  
     \(\binom{4}{2}=6\) paths.  
   Hence the total number of ordered pairs of paths is  
   \(70 \times 6 = 420\).

2. When can the ants meet?  
   Let after t steps
   \(P_1(t)=(x_1(t),y_1(t))=(r_1(t),u_1(t))\) and  
   \(P_2(t)=(x_2(t),y_2(t))=(2+r_2(t),u_2(t))\).

   For a meeting before \(t=4\) we would need  
   \(r_1(t)=2+r_2(t)\) and \(u_1(t)=u_2(t)\).  
   Because \(r_1(t)+u_1(t)=r_2(t)+u_2(t)=t\), the first equation becomes  
   \(t-u_1 = 2 + t-u_2 \Rightarrow u_1=u_2-2\), contradicting \(u_1=u_2\).  
   Therefore the ants cannot occupy the same lattice point during the first 4 steps.

   After 4 steps the second ant is fixed at \(C(4,2)\).  
   The only possible collision time is \(t=6\):  
   • At \(t=5\) ant 1 is on the line \(x+y=5\), so it cannot be at \(C\).  
   • At \(t=6\) ant 1 can be at \(C\) (because \(4+2=6\)), and afterwards it moves on to \(D\).  
     No other lattice point is shared.

   Conclusion: the ants meet iff ant 1’s path passes through \(C\) (necessarily at step 6).

3. Counting the “bad’’ pairs (ants meet)  
   • Paths of ant 1 that pass through \(C\):  
     Path A→C: needs 4 R and 2 U ⇒ \(\binom{6}{2}=15\) ways.  
     Path C→D: only 2 U ⇒ 1 way.  
     Total for ant 1: 15 paths.  
   • Paths for ant 2: still 6.  
   Hence number of meeting pairs = \(15 \times 6 = 90\).

4. Non-meeting pairs  
   \(420 - 90 = 330\).

\boxed{330}

Let the 20 equally–spaced sectors be numbered 0,1,2,…,19 counter-clockwise.  
Opposite sectors are those whose numbers differ by 10 (mod 20).

1.  Opposite–colour condition  
   For every i we must have  
         c(i+10) ≠ c(i).  
   Once the colours of the first 10 sectors (0–9) are chosen freely, the colours of 10–19 are forced to be the opposite.  
   Hence, without considering rotations there are  
         2¹⁰ = 1024 colourings.

2.  Burnside’s lemma  
   Let G be the cyclic rotation group C₂₀ (|G| = 20).  
   For each rotation g∈G compute Fix(g) = number of colourings left unchanged by g and still satisfying the opposite–colour rule.

   •  g = 0° (identity) : Fix = 1024.

   •  g = 180° (k = 10 sectors) : invariance would give c(i) = c(i+10), contradicting c(i) ≠ c(i+10).  
     ⇒ Fix = 0.

   •  k with gcd(20,k) = 1 (k = 1,3,7,9,11,13,17,19)  
     The rotation forms a single 20-cycle, so invariance forces all 20 sectors to have the same colour, impossible.  
     ⇒ Fix = 0   (8 rotations).

   •  k with gcd(20,k) = 2  (k = 2,6,14,18)  
     Invariance forces all even-numbered sectors to share one colour and all odd-numbered sectors another.  
     Opposite pair (0,10) would then have the same colour.  
     ⇒ Fix = 0   (4 rotations).

   •  k with gcd(20,k) = 5  (k = 5,15)  
     Opposite sectors lie in the same orbit, so invariance would again give identical colours for a pair that must differ.  
     ⇒ Fix = 0   (2 rotations).

   •  k with gcd(20,k) = 4  (k = 4,8,12,16)  
     The rotation splits the sectors into the four residue classes mod 4, each orbit containing 5 sectors.  
     Let the colours of classes 0,1,2,3 be A,B,C,D respectively.  
     Opposite pairs are always in classes (0,2) or (1,3); thus we need  
         C = opposite of A and D = opposite of B.  
     Choose A (2 ways) and B (2 ways); C and D are then forced.  
     ⇒ Fix = 2·2 = 4 for each such rotation, so total contribution 4·4 = 16.

   Summation:
        Σ Fix(g) = 1024 + 16 = 1040.

3.  Number of distinct frisbees
        N = (1/|G|) Σ Fix(g) = 1040 / 20 = 52.

\boxed{52}

Let the 20 sectors be numbered 0,1,…,19 counter-clockwise.  
Opposite sectors are i and i+10 (mod 20); the condition is  

 c(i) ≠ c(i+10) for every i. (1)

Without considering rotations, (1) forces the two colours in each of the 10 opposite pairs to be different, so there are  

 2¹⁰ = 1024 possible colourings.

To count colourings up to rotation we use Burnside’s lemma for the cyclic group G = C₂₀ (|G| = 20).

For k = 0,…,19 let gₖ be the rotation by k sectors and Fix(gₖ) the number of colourings that satisfy (1) and are invariant under gₖ.

Let d = gcd(k,20).  
Under gₖ the 20 positions split into d orbits, each of length L = 20/d, and invariance under gₖ forces every orbit to be monochromatic.

The map O : i ↦ i+10 sends each orbit either  
• to itself (if 10 ≡ m k (mod 20) for some m, i.e. d | 10), or  
• pairs two distinct orbits (if d ∤ 10).

If O fixes an orbit, condition (1) is impossible for that orbit (monochromatic yet opposite colours must differ), so Fix(gₖ)=0 whenever d divides 10, i.e. for d = 1,2,5,10.

If O pairs orbits, each pair must be coloured (R,B) or (B,R), giving 2 choices per pair.  
With d orbits paired up, there are d/2 pairs, so

 Fix(gₖ) = 2^{d/2} when d ∤ 10.

The only divisor of 20 not dividing 10 is d = 4, which occurs for k = 4,8,12,16.  
Thus

• k = 0        : Fix = 2^{10} = 1024  
• k = 4,8,12,16: Fix = 2^{4/2} = 2² = 4 each  
• all other k  : Fix = 0

Sum of fixed colourings: 1024 + 4·4 = 1040.

By Burnside,

  (number of distinct frisbees) = 1/20 · 1040 = 52.

\boxed{52}

Let the 20 sectors be numbered 0,1,…,19 counter-clockwise.  
Colour of sector i is denoted c(i) ∈ {R,B}.  
Condition: for every i, c(i) ≠ c(i+10) (indices mod 20).  
Two colourings that differ only by a rotation are regarded as the same.

We count inequivalent colourings with Burnside’s lemma for the rotation group C₍₂₀₎.

For a rotation by k sectors (0 ≤ k ≤ 19) let f(k) be the number of colourings that

1. are invariant under the rotation (c(i)=c(i+k) for all i), and  
2. satisfy the opposite–sectors condition c(i)≠c(i+10).

If the rotation by k has d = gcd(k,20), it decomposes the 20 positions into d orbits, each of length 20/d.  
Opposite sectors i and i+10 lie in the same orbit iff the congruence

 tk ≡ 10 (mod 20)

has a solution t, i.e. iff d | 10.

• If d | 10, such an orbit would force c(i)=c(i+10), contradicting the condition, so f(k)=0.  
  The divisors of 10 are 1,2,5,10, hence all rotations with d=1,2,5,10 contribute 0.

• The remaining possible values of d are 4 and 20.

1. k = 0 (identity): d = 20  
   We are free to choose colours for one element of each opposite pair (10 choices), each determining the opposite one.  
   f(0) = 2¹⁰ = 1024.

2. Rotations with d = 4: k = 4, 8, 12, 16  
   Each such rotation has 4 orbits of length 5.  
   Addition of 10 swaps the orbits in two disjoint pairs, say (A,C) and (B,D).  
   An invariant colouring assigns one colour to each orbit; the opposite condition demands
      colour(A) ≠ colour(C) and colour(B) ≠ colour(D).  
   Choose colour(A) (2 ways) ⇒ colour(C) is forced;  
   choose colour(B) (2 ways) ⇒ colour(D) is forced.  
   Hence f(4) = f(8) = f(12) = f(16) = 2·2 = 4.

All other rotations give f(k)=0.

Burnside’s lemma:

Number of inequivalent colourings
 = (1/20) · [f(0) + f(4)+f(8)+f(12)+f(16)]
 = (1/20) · [1024 + 4·4]
 = (1/20) · 1040
 = 52.

\boxed{52}

Let the 20 sectors be numbered 0,1,…,19 counter-clockwise.
The colouring must satisfy

C_{i+10} ≠ C_i (for every i)     (1)

where C_i ∈ {R,B}.  
Two colourings that differ only by a rotation through k sectors (k = 0,…,19) are considered the same.

We count the colourings with Burnside’s Lemma.

For each rotation ρ_k (k sectors), let Fix(k) be the number of colourings which  
(a) satisfy (1), and  
(b) are unchanged by ρ_k (i.e. C_i = C_{i+k} for all i).

A rotation by k partitions the 20 positions into d = gcd(k,20) cycles, each of length 20/d.

1. k = 0 (identity).  
   There are no periodicity restrictions, only (1).  
   Choose colours freely for positions 0,…,9 (2^{10} choices); positions 10,…,19 are forced to be opposite.
   Fix(0) = 2^{10} = 1024.

2. k ≠ 0.  
   If i and i+10 lie in the same cycle, then periodicity gives C_{i+10}=C_i, contradicting (1); therefore Fix(k)=0 whenever i and i+10 are in the same cycle.  
   This happens exactly when 10 is a multiple of d = gcd(k,20), i.e. when d divides 10.

   The only rotations with gcd(k,20) NOT dividing 10 are those with d = 4:
   k = 4, 8, 12, 16.  
   For these rotations:
   • d = 4 cycles, each of length 5.  
   • Adding 10 swaps the cycles in pairs; no cycle is paired with itself.  
   Hence we may choose a colour for one cycle of each pair and the opposite colour is forced on its partner: 2 choices per pair ⇒ 2^2 = 4 colourings.

   Therefore  
   Fix(4) = Fix(8) = Fix(12) = Fix(16) = 4.

   All other non–identity rotations have Fix(k) = 0.

Summary of Fix(k):
• k = 0 : 1024  
• k = 4,8,12,16 : 4 each (total 16)  
• all other k : 0

Total fixed colourings: 1024 + 16 = 1040.

Burnside’s Lemma:
Number of inequivalent colourings = (1/20)·1040 = 52.

\boxed{52}

Let the 20 sectors be numbered 0,1,…,19 in order round the disc.  
Opposite sectors are i and i+10 (mod 20) and must be of opposite colours.

1. Count all colourings that satisfy the opposite-colour condition.

   Choosing the colour of sectors 0,…,9 freely fixes the colours of 10,…,19.  
   Hence total colourings = 2^{10}=1024.

2. Identify the rotation group acting on the disc.

   The relevant symmetry group is the cyclic group C_{20} of the 20 rotations
   by k sectors (k = 0,…,19).  
   We need the number of colourings up to rotation, found via Burnside’s lemma.

3. For each rotation k, determine how many colourings are left unchanged
   while still obeying the opposite-colour rule.

   Let d = gcd(k,20).  
   • If the rotation ever maps a sector onto its opposite, invariance is
     impossible (the sector would then have to be both its colour and the
     opposite colour).  
     This happens exactly when d divides 10, i.e. for d = 1,2,5,10.
     So for k with d = 1,2,5,10 we have Fix(k)=0.  
   • For k = 10 (a half-turn) the rotation sends every sector to its opposite,
     so Fix(10)=0 as well.

   The only remaining non-identity rotations have d = 4, namely
   k = 4,8,12,16.

   Take k = 4 as an example.  
   The rotation splits the 20 sectors into 4 orbits of length 5:
     O₀ = {0,4,8,12,16},  O₁ = {1,5,9,13,17},
     O₂ = {2,6,10,14,18}, O₃ = {3,7,11,15,19}.
   Opposite sectors pair the orbits (O₀,O₂) and (O₁,O₃).
   Each orbit must be monochromatic, and paired orbits must be opposite colours.
   Hence there are 2 choices for the colour of (O₀,O₂) and 2 choices for
   (O₁,O₃): 2×2 = 4 colourings fixed by k = 4.

   The same reasoning holds for k = 8,12,16, so
   Fix(4)=Fix(8)=Fix(12)=Fix(16)=4.

   Summary of fixed-colouring counts:
     k = 0 : 1024
     k = 4,8,12,16 : 4 each (total 16)
     all other k   : 0

4. Apply Burnside’s lemma.

   Number of distinct frisbees
     = (1/20) · (1024 + 16) = 1040 / 20 = 52.

\boxed{52}

Let the 20 sectors be numbered 0,1,…,19 counter-clockwise.  
Opposite sectors differ by 10, so the colouring must satisfy

  c(i+10)=¬c(i) for every i (indices mod 20).

Choose the colours of the first 10 sectors (0…9) arbitrarily; the other 10 are then forced to be the opposite colour.  
Hence, before identifying rotations, there are 2¹⁰ = 1024 valid colourings.

To count distinct frisbees up to rotation (group G = C₂₀), use Burnside’s Lemma:

  #(orbits) = (1/|G|) ∑_{g∈G} Fix(g),

where Fix(g) is the number of valid colourings fixed by rotation g.

Let g be the rotation through k sectors (0 ≤ k ≤ 19) and d = gcd(k,20).

1. A colouring fixed by g is constant on each of the d cycles of length 20/d produced by the rotation.  
   If any cycle contains a pair of opposite sectors, invariance would force c(i)=c(i+10), contradicting the rule c(i+10)=¬c(i).  
   A cycle contains such a pair iff 10 lies in the subgroup generated by k, i.e. iff d divides 10.

2. Therefore Fix(g)=0 whenever d | 10 (d = 1,2,5,10).  
   If d does not divide 10, the 10-step shift never stays within a cycle.  
   The opposite-sector map pairs whole cycles, so d must be even.  
   The only such d are 4 and 20.

   • d = 20 (k = 0, the identity): Fix = 2¹⁰ = 1024.  
   • d = 4 (k = 4,8,12,16): there are 4 cycles, paired into 2 opposite pairs.  
     Each pair can be coloured in 2 ways (choose the colour of one cycle, the opposite is forced for the other), giving 2² = 4 colourings for each of the four rotations.

Summation:

  Fix(identity) = 1024  
  Fix(k) = 4 for k = 4,8,12,16 (4 rotations)  
  Fix(k) = 0 for the remaining 15 rotations.

Total fixed colourings: 1024 + 4·4 = 1040.

By Burnside:

  #(distinct frisbees) = 1040 / 20 = 52.

\boxed{52}

Let the 20 sectors be numbered 0,1,2,…,19 counter-clockwise.  
Denote their colours by c₀,c₁,…,c₁₉, where each cᵢ is R or B.

Opposite–sector condition  
Sector i and sector i+10 (indices mod 20) must be different colours, i.e.  
c_{i+10}=1−c_i (think of R=1, B=0).  
Thus the 20-sector colouring is completely determined by the first 10 entries
c₀,c₁,…,c₉; there are 2¹⁰=1024 such colourings altogether.

Two frisbees are considered the same if one can be rotated onto the other,
so we must count the colourings up to the action of the cyclic group
C₂₀ of rotations.  Burnside’s lemma gives

number of distinct frisbees = (1/20) ∑_{k=0}^{19} Fix(k),

where Fix(k) is the number of colourings fixed by the rotation
ρ_k : i ↦ i+k (mod 20).

Step 1.  When does a colouring satisfy ρ_k(c)=c ?

For ρ_k to fix a colouring we need c_i = c_{i+k} for every i.
If, for some i and t, i+10 ≡ i+tk (mod 20), then sectors i and i+10 lie in
the same ρ_k-cycle, hence would have to have the same colour, contradicting
the opposite–sector condition.
The congruence i+10 ≡ i+tk (mod 20) is solvable
iff gcd(k,20) divides 10.
Therefore a fixed colouring can exist only when gcd(k,20) does NOT divide 10,
i.e. when gcd(k,20)=4 or 20.

Step 2.  Evaluate Fix(k).

(i)  k=0 (identity rotation).  
All 1024 colourings are fixed:  Fix(0)=2¹⁰=1024.

(ii)  k with gcd(k,20)=4.  
These are k = 4,8,12,16 (four rotations).  
Here ρ_k decomposes the 20 positions into 4 cycles of length 5.
Each cycle and the set of positions opposite to it form a pair of cycles.
Within a fixed colouring:

• the colour is constant on each ρ_k-cycle (because ρ_k fixes it),  
• the two cycles of a pair must be coloured oppositely.

Hence for each of the 2 pairs of cycles we freely choose which one is red;
the other becomes blue.  This gives 2² = 4 colourings fixed by each such k.

Therefore  Fix(4)=Fix(8)=Fix(12)=Fix(16)=4.

(iii)  All other k (gcd(k,20)=1,2,5 or 10) give Fix(k)=0.

Step 3.  Burnside’s sum.

Total fixed colourings:
1024  (k=0)
+ 4·4 (k=4,8,12,16)
= 1024 + 16 = 1040.

Number of distinct frisbees:
1040 / 20 = 52.

\boxed{52}

Step 1.  Write the condition on the colouring  
Let c(i) (i = 0,1,…,19) be the colour of sector i, and let R = red, B = blue.  
The problem states that opposite sectors are coloured differently, i.e.  
c(i+10) = ¬c(i) (1)  
where ¬ means “opposite colour”.  
Choosing the colours of sectors 0–9 determines the rest, so without identifying rotations there are 2¹⁰ = 1024 admissible colourings.

Step 2.  Use Burnside’s lemma  
The rotation group of the disc is the cyclic group C₂₀ of order 20.  
The number of distinct toys is  
N = (1/20) Σₖ Fix(k)  
where Fix(k) is the number of admissible colourings left unchanged by rotation through k sectors.

Step 3.  For which rotations can Fix(k) be non-zero?  
If rotation by k sectors fixes a colouring, then c(i)=c(i+k).  
If the equation k·t ≡ 10 (mod 20) has a solution t, then shifting by 10 is obtained by repeated k-shifts, so we would have simultaneously c(i)=c(i+10) and by (1) c(i)=¬c(i), impossible.  
Hence Fix(k) ≠ 0 only when the congruence k·t ≡ 10 (mod 20) has NO solution, i.e. when gcd(k,20) ∤ 10.

Possible values of gcd(k,20): 1,2,4,5,10,20  
Those not dividing 10 are 4 and 20.  
Thus only the rotations

k = 0 (gcd = 20)  
k = 4, 8, 12, 16 (gcd = 4)

can fix any colouring.

Step 4.  Count Fix(k) for the admissible rotations  

a) k = 0 (identity): Fix(0) = 2¹⁰ = 1024.

b) k with gcd = 4 (k = 4,8,12,16):  
Rotation by such a k partitions the 20 sectors into 4 cycles of length 5.  
Adding 10 (opposite sectors) sends each cycle to a different cycle, pairing the 4 cycles into 2 disjoint pairs.  
Assign a colour to one cycle in each pair; the other cycle is forced to be the opposite colour.  
Therefore each rotation with gcd = 4 fixes 2² = 4 colourings.  
So Fix(4)=Fix(8)=Fix(12)=Fix(16)=4.

Step 5.  Apply Burnside’s lemma  
Total fixed colourings:
1024 (identity) + 4×4 (the four non-trivial rotations) = 1024 + 16 = 1040.

Hence  
N = 1040 / 20 = 52.

\boxed{52}

Let n have k decimal digits  
d₁d₂…d_k (d₁ ≠ 0).  
Denote  

• s(n) = d₁ + d₂ + … + d_k (sum of digits)  
• p(n) = d₁d₂ … d_k (product of digits).

The condition for a coincidental number is  
n = s(n) + p(n).                                                 (1)

1. One–digit numbers  
If n = d (1 ≤ d ≤ 9) then (1) gives d = 2d ⇒ d = 0, impossible.  
So no 1-digit coincidental numbers.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2. Two–digit numbers  
Let n = 10a + b (1 ≤ a ≤ 9, 0 ≤ b ≤ 9).

s = a + b,   p = ab.  
Equation (1) becomes

10a + b = a + b + ab  ⇒  9a = ab  ⇒  a(9 – b) = 0.

Since a ≠ 0, we must have b = 9.

Thus every 2-digit number whose units digit is 9 works:
19, 29, 39, 49, 59, 69, 79, 89, 99.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3. Three–digit numbers  
Write n = 100a + 10b + c (1 ≤ a ≤ 9, 0 ≤ b, c ≤ 9).

Then s = a + b + c,   p = abc.  
Equation (1) gives

100a + 10b + c = a + b + c + abc  
⇒ 99a + 9b = abc  
⇒ 9(11a + b) = abc.                                         (2)

All digits are at most 9, so abc ≤ 9·9·9 = 729, while the right side of (2)
is at least 9·11 = 99; hence (2) is feasible.  Testing every
(a, b) with 1 ≤ a ≤ 9, 1 ≤ b ≤ 9 (b = 0 is impossible because the left side
would not vanish) shows that (2) never gives an integer c between 0 and 9.
Therefore there are no 3-digit coincidental numbers.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4. Four or more digits  
Let k ≥ 4.  Split (1) as

p(n) = n – s(n) = Σ_{i=1}^k d_i(10^{k-i} – 1).                (3)

The smallest coefficient on the right of (3) (for i = k – 1) is 10^{k-1} – 1.
Hence

n – s(n) ≥ d₁(10^{k-1} – 1) ≥ 1·(10^{k-1} – 1).              (4)

On the other hand,

p(n) ≤ 9^{k}.                                                (5)

For k ≥ 4 we have 10^{k-1} – 1 > 9^{k-1} ≥ 9^{k}/9,
hence from (4) and (5)

p(n) < n – s(n),

contradicting (1).  Therefore no coincidental number has 4 or more digits.

----------------------------------------------------------------
5. Complete list and required sum  
The only coincidental numbers are the nine two-digit numbers found in section 2:

19, 29, 39, 49, 59, 69, 79, 89, 99.

Their sum is
Σ_{a=1}^{9} (10a + 9) = 10·(1 + 2 + … + 9) + 9·9
= 10·45 + 81 = 531.

\boxed{531}

Let n have its (base-10) digits  
d_{m-1}d_{m-2}\dots d_{1}d_{0}  (d_{m-1}\neq 0,\;m\ge 1).  
Denote the digit–sum and digit–product by  

s(n)=∑_{i=0}^{m-1}d_i , p(n)=∏_{i=0}^{m-1}d_i .

The condition for a coincidental number is  

s(n)+p(n)=n. (1)

1.  No digit may be 0  
If some d_i=0, then p(n)=0 and (1) would give n=s(n), impossible for n>9.  
So every digit is in {1,2,…,9}.

2.  One–digit numbers  
For a single digit d, equation (1) becomes d+d=d ⇒ 2d=d ⇒ d=0, which is not positive.  
Hence no 1-digit coincidental numbers exist.

3.  Two–digit numbers  
Let the digits be a (tens) and b (units), a∈{1,…,9}, b∈{1,…,9}.  
Equation (1) is

10a+b=(a+b)+ab  ⇒ 9a=ab  ⇒ a(9-b)=0 ⇒ b=9.

Thus every 2-digit coincidental number has units digit 9.  
All nine of them are  

19,29,39,49,59,69,79,89,99.

(All satisfy (1) since a+9+9a=10a+9.)

4.  Three–digit numbers  
Digits a,b,c∈{1,…,9}.  From (1):

100a+10b+c=(a+b+c)+abc  
⇒ abc=99a+9b.                                           (2)

Dividing (2) by ab gives  

c=99/b+9/a.                                               (3)

Because b divides 99, b∈{1,3,9}.  
For these values 99/b equals 99, 33, or 11, all exceeding 9, so (3) can never yield c∈{1,…,9}.  
Therefore no 3-digit coincidental numbers exist.

5.  Four or more digits  
Consider a 4–digit number with digits a,b,c,d (all 1–9).  
From (1):

abcd=999a+99b+9c = 9(111a+11b+c).                     (4)

Solve (4) for d:

d = 9(111a+11b+c)/(abc).                                (5)

Because d≤9 and a cancels except in the denominator,

d≈ 999/(bc).                                             (6)

To have d≤9 we need bc ≥ 111, impossible since bc≤9·9=81.  
Hence no 4-digit solutions exist.

For m≥5 the left–hand side of (5) would be even larger while the denominator becomes still at most 9·9 =81 for two of the digits, making d far smaller than required; consequently no solutions exist for any m≥4.

6.  Complete list and their sum  
The only coincidental numbers are the nine 2-digit numbers listed in step 3.  
Their sum is an arithmetic series with first term 19, last term 99, common difference 10:

Sum = (19+99)·9/2 = 118·9/2 = 531.

Call the sum and product of the decimal digits of the positive integer n, respectively,  

  s(n)= “sum of digits”, p(n)= “product of digits”.

We need all n with  

  s(n)+p(n)=n.  (1)

0.  No digit may equal 0  
If a digit were 0, p(n)=0 and (1) would read s(n)=n.  
That is impossible for any positive n, because for n≥10 we have s(n)<n and for 1≤n≤9 we have s(n)=n but then p(n)=n≠0.  
Hence every digit of a coincidental number is in {1,2,…,9}.

1.  One–digit numbers  
For n=d (1≤d≤9) we have s(n)+p(n)=d+d=2d≠d, so none work.

2.  Two–digit numbers  
Write n=10a+b with 1≤a,b≤9.  
Then (1) becomes  

  ab+a+b = 10a+b  
  a(b−9)=0 ⇒ b=9.  

Thus every 2–digit number whose units digit is 9 works, i.e.  

  19, 29, 39, 49, 59, 69, 79, 89, 99.   (2)

3.  Three or more digits are impossible  
Let n have k+1 digits (k≥2):  

  n = d_k10^k + d_{k-1}10^{k-1}+ … + d_110 + d_0  (all d_i ∈{1,…,9}).

Because d_0 contributes nothing to n−s(n), subtracting s(n) from n gives  

  n − s(n) = Σ_{i=1}^{k} d_i(10^i−1).                (3)

Each factor 10^i−1 is a multiple of 9, so (3) shows n−s(n)=p(n) is divisible by 9; hence at least one digit is 3,6,9 (already certain because no 0-digit exists).

Divide (3) by 9 and set  

  U_i:= (10^i−1)/9 = 1\,11\,111\,… (i consecutive 1’s).  

Then  

  (d_0/9)·d_1d_2…d_k = d_1U_1 + d_2U_2 + … + d_kU_k.     (4)

Because d_0≤9, the left side of (4) does not exceed Π_{i=1}^{k}d_i.  
It will suffice to show that for every collection of at least two digits,

  Π_{i=1}^{k}d_i < d_1U_1 + d_2U_2 + … + d_kU_k.        (5)

Once (5) is known, (4) is impossible for k≥2 and therefore no coincidental number has three or more digits.

Proof of (5).  

For k=2 the inequality is

  d_1d_2 < d_1 + 11d_2,

i.e. d_1(d_2−1) < 11d_2.  
Since 1≤d_1≤9 and 1≤d_2≤9, the left side is at most 9·8=72 whereas the right side is at least 11·1=11, so the inequality certainly holds.

Assume now k≥3.  Because U_3=111, U_4=1111,…, each U_i (i≥3) is at least 111.  Hence

  right side ≥ d_3·111 ≥ 111, while  

  left side ≤ 9^k ≤ 9^3 = 729 (since digits ≤9).  

But the smallest possible right side (obtained when d_3=1 and the remaining higher-place digits also equal 1) is already  

   ≥ 111 + d_1 + 11d_2 ≥ 111 + 1 + 11·1 = 123,

and the ratio (left side)/(right side) is at most 729/123 < 1.  Thus (5) also holds for every k≥3.

Therefore no integer having three or more digits can satisfy (1).

4.  All coincidental numbers and their sum  
By Sections 1–3 the only coincidental numbers are the nine 2-digit numbers (2).  
Their sum is an arithmetic series with first term 19, last term 99 and 9 terms:

  sum = 9 · (19+99)/2 = 9 · 59 = 531.

Hence the required sum of all coincidental numbers is

A coincidental number satisfies  
 n = S(n) + P(n).                                                   (1)

1.  If any digit were 0, then P(n)=0 and (1) would give n = S(n), impossible for a multi–digit positive number.  
    Hence every digit is in {1,…,9}.

2.  Rewrite (1):

   n – S(n) = P(n)
   Σ d_i(10^{i} – 1) = Π d_i .                                      (2)

   Because 10^{i} – 1 is divisible by 9, (2) shows P(n) is a multiple of 9, so at least one digit is 3, 6 or 9.

3.  Numbers with one digit.  
    d = S = P, so (1) gives 2d = d ⇒ d = 0, contradicting positivity.

    S = a + b,     P = ab.

5.  No coincidental numbers with three or more digits.

   Let k ≥ 3 and put a = d_{k-1} (the leading digit).

     The first term of the sum on the left of (2) is a(10^{k-1} – 1); all other terms are non-negative, so

     Σ d_i(10^{i} – 1) ≥ a(10^{k-1} – 1).                           (4)

   For k ≥ 3, 10^{k-1} – 1 exceeds 9^{\,k-1}:

   Hence from (3) and (4):

6.  The complete list of coincidental numbers is the nine two-digit numbers found in step 4.
    Their sum is an arithmetic series with first term 19, last term 99 and 9 terms:
