  TL = {(i , j) : 0 ≤ i ≤ 4 , 5 ≤ j ≤ 9}      (top-left)  
  BR = {(i , j) : 5 ≤ i ≤ 9 , 0 ≤ j ≤ 4}      (bottom-right).

Each element of TL is complemented by an element of BR and vice-versa, and the two are incomparable in the divisibility order.  For every such pair we must choose exactly one of them.

Step 5.  Encode a subset by its choice in TL.

Let A ⊆ TL be the set of TL-cells actually placed in T.  
The whole set T is then determined by

 T = BL ∪ A ∪ {c(p) : p ∈ TL \ A}.          (★)

Step 6.  What restrictions must A satisfy?

Because BL is already downward closed, T will be an order ideal iff

•  A itself is an order ideal inside TL (i.e. if (i , j) ∈ A then every (i' , j') with
   i' ≤ i and j' ≤ j and still lying in TL is also in A).

If A is such an ideal, then TL \ A is an order filter in TL; after applying the order-reversing complement map c, the last set in (★) is automatically an ideal inside BR.  Consequently condition (2) is satisfied.  

Therefore

Counting the desired sets T  ⇔  counting the order ideals A of the 5 × 5 grid TL.

Step 7.  Count order ideals of a 5 × 5 grid.

For a product of two chains of lengths m and n the number of order ideals equals the number of monotone lattice paths with m horizontal and n vertical steps, i.e.

  # ideals = (m+n choose m) = (m+n choose n).

Here m = n = 5, so

  # ideals = (10 choose 5) = 252.

Step 8.  Conclusion.

Every such ideal A gives a unique subset T, and conversely, so the number of subsets T that satisfy both given conditions is

\boxed{252}

Write a divisor of 10^9 = 2^9 5^9 as  

d = 2^i 5^j with 0 ≤ i , j ≤ 9.  

Thus every divisor is represented by an ordered pair (i , j) in the 10 × 10 grid  
{0,…,9} × {0,…,9}.  
The map  

(i , j)  ↦  (9−i , 9−j)            (1)

sends every divisor to its complementary divisor 10^9 / d and partitions the
100 points into 50 disjoint pairs.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Translating the two requirements
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let T be a subset of the grid that satisfies the problem conditions.

A.  “Exactly one of s and 10^9/s is in T”  
 With the notation of (1) this is  
 For every (i , j), exactly one of (i , j) and (9−i , 9−j) is in T.  
 Hence |T| = 50.

B.  “T is closed under taking divisors”  
 If (i , j) ∈ T and 0 ≤ k ≤ i, 0 ≤ ℓ ≤ j then (k , ℓ) ∈ T.  
 So T is a (downward) order ideal of the product poset
 {0,…,9} × {0,…,9} with the usual ≤ in each coordinate.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Describing an ideal by its upper boundary
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For every column i (0 ≤ i ≤ 9) set  

g(i) = max{ j | (i , j) ∈ T }  (−1 if the column is empty).

Because T is an ideal,  
 g(0) ≥ g(1) ≥ ⋯ ≥ g(9).                                (2)

Conversely, any weakly decreasing sequence g completely determines T:
T consists of all (i , j) with 0 ≤ j ≤ g(i).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The complementary–pair condition in terms of g
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Pick any (i , j).  It lies in T iff j ≤ g(i).
Its complement (9−i , 9−j) must be outside T, i.e.  
 9−j > g(9−i).  

Therefore  

j ≤ g(i)  ⇔  9 − j > g(9 − i).

Take j = g(i): 9 − g(i) > g(9 − i) ⇒ g(i) + g(9 − i) ≤ 8.  
Take j = g(i)+1 (if g(i) ≤ 8): 9 − g(i) − 1 ≤ g(9 − i) ⇒ g(i) + g(9 − i) ≥ 8.

Thus for every i

g(i) + g(9 − i) = 8.                                     (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Working with the first half of the sequence
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Equation (3) shows that the values g(5),…,g(9) are determined by  
g(0),…,g(4).
Because of (2) we must have  

g(4) ≥ g(5) = 8 − g(4) ⇒ 2 g(4) ≥ 8 ⇒ g(4) ≥ 4.        (4)

Let  
a_k = g(k) for k = 0,…,4.

Then

a_0 ≥ a_1 ≥ a_2 ≥ a_3 ≥ a_4 ≥ 4,                        (5)
and each a_k ≤ 9.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Shifting the sequence
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put c_k = a_k − 4 (0 ≤ k ≤ 4).

Conditions (5) become

0 ≤ c_4 ≤ c_3 ≤ c_2 ≤ c_1 ≤ c_0 ≤ 5.                     (6)

So (c_0,…,c_4) is a weakly decreasing sequence of length 5
whose entries are chosen from the 6 values 0,1,2,3,4,5.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Counting such sequences
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The number of weakly decreasing sequences of length r from an alphabet
of s symbols equals “(r+s−1 choose r)”, the multiset coefficient.

Here r = 5 and s = 6, hence

#(T) = (5 + 6 − 1 choose 5) = (10 choose 5) = 252.       (7)

Every sequence satisfying (6) gives an ideal T and, by (3), automatically
satisfies the complementary–pair requirement; conversely each admissible T
yields exactly one such sequence.  Therefore (7) is the desired count.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7.  Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
There are 252 subsets T of the divisor set of 10^9 with the stated
properties.

\boxed{252}

Write every divisor of 10⁹ = 2⁹·5⁹ as an ordered pair  

 d = 2ᵃ·5ᵇ  ↔  (a , b) with 0 ≤ a , b ≤ 9.

Thus the set S of divisors is the 10 × 10 lattice

 P = {0,…,9} × {0,…,9}.

For (a , b) set (a , b)* := (9 − a , 9 − b);  
(a , b) and (a , b)* are paired by the relation d ↦ 10⁹/d.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Elements that must (or must not) be in T
--------------------------------------------------------------------
If a ≤ 4 and b ≤ 4 then (a , b) ≤ (9 − a , 9 − b), so
(9 − a , 9 − b) divides (a , b)*.  
If (a , b) were absent from T its partner would be present, and all of its
divisors – including (a , b) – would have to be in T, a contradiction.
Hence every (a , b) with a ≤ 4 and b ≤ 4 is forced into T, and consequently
every (a , b) with a ≥ 5 and b ≥ 5 is forced out of T.

So the only undecided points are the 25+25=50 points in the two “mixed’’
quadrants  
Q₁ = {(a , b) | a ≤ 4 , b ≥ 5} and  
Q₂ = {(a , b) | a ≥ 5 , b ≤ 4}.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Describe T column–wise
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For every column a (0 ≤ a ≤ 9) let

 Gₐ = max{ b | ( a , b ) ∈ T }  (if the column is empty put Gₐ = –1).

Then column a contains exactly the rows 0,…,Gₐ.

Condition (i):  for every (a , b), exactly one of (a , b) and its partner
is in T.  
Translated to the Gₐ this is

 b ≤ Gₐ   ⇔   9 − b > G_{9−a}.

For all integers b this equivalence is satisfied iff

 Gₐ + G_{9−a} = 8.                               (★)

Condition (ii):  T is downward–closed, and our description “rows 0 … Gₐ’’
already guarantees that.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Free choices
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For a in {0,1,2,3,4} the point (a , 4) is forced into T, so Gₐ ≥ 4.
Because of (★), for the paired column 9 − a we get

 G_{9−a} = 8 − Gₐ  which is ≤ 4.

Hence

 Gₐ can be any of 6 values   4,5,6,7,8,9,
 and then G_{9−a} is fixed.

The five column–pairs (0,9), (1,8), …, (4,5) are independent, so the
number of possible assignments of the whole 10–tuple (G₀,…,G₉) is

 6 · 6 · 6 · 6 · 6 = 6⁵ = 7776.

Each such choice of the Gₐ determines a unique subset T, and every valid T
arises this way.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
There are 7776 subsets T of S satisfying the two required conditions.

\boxed{7776}

Write every divisor of 10⁹ in the form  
s = 2ᵃ 5ᵇ with 0 ≤ a, b ≤ 9.  
Thus the set S of all divisors can be pictured as the 10 × 10 grid of lattice points

P = {(a,b) | 0≤a,b≤9},

ordered by (a,b) ≤ (a′,b′) ⇔ a≤a′ and b≤b′.

Condition (2).  
A subset T is required to contain with every point all points that are coordinate-wise smaller, i.e. T must be a (downward) order ideal of the poset P.

Condition (1).  
For every point p=(a,b) exactly one of p and
p* = (9−a, 9−b)  
is in T.  The map p ↦ p* is an involution without fixed points, so P breaks into 50 two-element orbits {p, p*}.  We need an order ideal that picks exactly one point from each orbit; such an ideal is called self-complementary.

1.  Describing an order ideal by its boundary  
For a column a (0≤a≤9) set  
f(a) = max{ b | (a,b) ∈ T } (take f(a)=−1 if the column is empty).  
Because T is an ideal, the sequence
f(0), f(1), …, f(9)
is weakly decreasing.

2.  The complement condition in terms of f  
If (a,b) ∈ T then (9−a,9−b) ∉ T, and conversely.  
Therefore for every a and every b

b ≤ f(a) ⇔ 9−b > f(9−a).

Take b = f(a).  Then 9 − f(a) > f(9−a), i.e.

f(a) + f(9−a) ≤ 8. (★)

If b = f(a)+1, the left side of the ⇔ is false, hence the right side is true, giving
f(9−a) ≥ 8 − f(a).  Combining with (★) we get the exact equation

f(a) + f(9−a) = 8 for every a. (1)

Thus columns a and 9−a determine each other.

3.  Free variables  
Because of (1) the values for a = 5,…,9 are forced by the values for a = 0,…,4:

f(9−a) = 8 − f(a).

Let x₀,x₁,x₂,x₃,x₄ denote f(0),…,f(4).  
From monotonicity,

x₀ ≥ x₁ ≥ x₂ ≥ x₃ ≥ x₄. (2)

Across the middle we must also have f(4) ≥ f(5).  Using (1):

x₄ ≥ 8 − x₄  ⇔ x₄ ≥ 4. (3)

Because row numbers lie between −1 and 9 and x₀ is the largest of them, we must have x₀ ≤ 9.

4.  Converting to non-negative parameters  
Let  

y₄ = x₄ − 4 (so y₄ ≥ 0),  
y₃ = x₃ − x₄, y₂ = x₂ − x₃, y₁ = x₁ − x₂, y₀ = x₀ − x₁;

each yᵢ is a non-negative integer and

x₀ = 4 + y₄ + y₃ + y₂ + y₁ + y₀ ≤ 9
 ⇔ y₀ + y₁ + y₂ + y₃ + y₄ ≤ 5. (4)

5.  Counting the possibilities  
Condition (4) asks for a weak composition of an integer ≤ 5 into 5 parts.  
For any fixed total s (0 ≤ s ≤ 5) the number of such compositions is C(s+5−1, 5−1)=C(s+4,4).  
Summing for s=0,…,5 gives

∑_{s=0}^{5} C(s+4,4) = C(10,5) = 252,

using the hockey-stick identity.

6.  Conclusion  
Every admissible 5-tuple (x₀,…,x₄) determines a unique order ideal T, and conversely.  Hence

the number of subsets T satisfying both conditions is 252.

\boxed{252}

Write every divisor of  
N = 10⁹ = 2⁹·5⁹  
as 2ᵃ5ᵇ with 0 ≤ a,b ≤ 9.  
Thus

S = {(a , b) | a , b ∈ {0,…,9}}, |S| = 100.

1.  Complementary map  
For d = 2ᵃ5ᵇ the complementary divisor N/d is 2^{9-a}5^{9-b}.  
So on the index–grid

τ(a , b) = (9-a , 9-b)  

pairs every element with a distinct partner (because N is not a square).

2.  Conditions translated to the grid  
T ⊆ S must satisfy  

(i)  For every x ∈ S, exactly one of x and τ(x) is in T.  
Equivalently τ(T) = S \ T.                             (1)

(ii) If x ∈ T and y divides x then y ∈ T.  
On the indices this says T is a down-set:  
(a , b) ∈ T ⇒ all (c , d) with c ≤ a , d ≤ b are in T.        (2)

3.  Describing a down–set  
For every a define g(a) = max{ b | (a , b) ∈ T } (take g(a)=–1 if the
column is empty).  
Then T = { (a , b) | 0 ≤ b ≤ g(a) } and g(0) ≤ g(1) ≤ … ≤ g(9).    (3)

4.  Using the complement condition  
Condition (1) applied to the description with g gives, for every a,b,

b ≤ g(a) ⇔ (9-b) > g(9-a).

Taking b = g(a) yields  
g(9-a) < 9 – g(a) ⇒ g(a) + g(9-a) = 8.                        (4)

5.  Reducing to the first half of the grid  
Let a run from 0 to 4.  
Put  

g₀ = g(0) ≤ g₁ = g(1) ≤ g₂ = g(2) ≤ g₃ = g(3) ≤ g₄ = g(4).     (5)

From (4)

g(9−a) = 8 − g(a), so in particular  
g(5) = 8 − g₄ , g(6) = 8 − g₃ , g(7) = 8 − g₂ , g(8) = 8 − g₁ , g(9) = 8 − g₀.

6.  Monotonicity on the whole grid  
Using (3) and the expressions above one checks

g₀ ≤ g₁ ≤ g₂ ≤ g₃ ≤ g₄ ≤ 4                                      (6)

is both necessary and sufficient for the full sequence
(g(0),…,g(9)) to be non-decreasing.  (The inequality g₄ ≤ 4 comes
from g₄ ≤ 8 − g₄.)

Hence every choice of the five integers in (6) produces a valid T,
and conversely every valid T gives such a choice.

7.  Counting the choices  
How many weakly increasing 5-tuples (g₀,…,g₄) with 0 ≤ gᵢ ≤ 4 are
there?  This is “stars and bars”:

number = C(5 + 5 − 1, 5) = C(9,5) = 126.

8.  Correspondence  
Each such 5-tuple yields a unique subset T, so the total number of
subsets T is 126.

\boxed{126}

Write 10^9 as 2^9·5^9.  
Every divisor is of the form 2^a 5^b with 0 ≤ a,b ≤ 9; identify a divisor with the point (a,b) of the 10×10 lattice {0,…,9}².

Two conditions have to be satisfied by T.

1. Complementary-pair condition  
   (a,b) is paired with (9−a,9−b); T must contain exactly one element of each pair.

2. Downward-closure condition  
   If (a,b)∈T then every (c,d) with 0≤c≤a and 0≤d≤b is also in T (T is an order ideal of the divisibility poset).

-------------------------------------------------
1.  Describing an ideal by its “upper boundary”

For each a∈{0,…,9} let  

 f(a)=max{ b : (a,b)∈T }   ( −1 if the column is empty).

Then (a,b) belongs to T ⇔ b ≤ f(a).  
Because T is an ideal, f(0) ≥ f(1) ≥ … ≥ f(9).

-------------------------------------------------
2.  Translating the complementary-pair condition

The pair (a,b) ↔ (9−a,9−b) gives

 (b ≤ f(a)) ⇔ (9−b > f(9−a)).

Put b = f(a); this yields the inequality

 f(a) + f(9−a) < 9.

Put b = f(a)+1 (when ≤9); this yields

 f(a) + f(9−a) ≥ 8.

Hence, for every a,

 f(a) + f(9−a) = 8.  (★)

-------------------------------------------------
3.  Reducing to the first five columns

Knowing (★), the values for a = 5,…,9 are determined by the first five:

 f(9−a) = 8 − f(a).

Set y0,…,y4 by yi = f(i) for i = 0,…,4.  
The monotonicity chain becomes

 y0 ≥ y1 ≥ y2 ≥ y3 ≥ y4 ≥ f(5) (= 8 − y4) ≥ f(6) (= 8 − y3) ≥ … ≥ f(9)=8−y0.

The only new requirement is y4 ≥ 8−y4, i.e. y4 ≥ 4.

-------------------------------------------------
4.  Allowed values of yi

Because 0 ≤ f(a) ≤ 9 and 0 ≤ 8 − f(a) ≤ 9, (★) forces 0 ≤ f(a) ≤ 8.
Thus each yi lies in {4,5,6,7,8}.

So we must count weakly decreasing 5-tuples

 8 ≥ y0 ≥ y1 ≥ y2 ≥ y3 ≥ y4 ≥ 4,

each entry chosen from the 5 values 4–8.

-------------------------------------------------
5.  Counting the sequences

A weakly decreasing k-tuple from an n-element set is the same as a multiset of size k drawn from n elements.  
Hence the number of such 5-tuples is

 C(n+k−1, k) = C(5+5−1, 5) = C(9, 5) = 126.

-------------------------------------------------
6.  From sequences to subsets T

Each admissible sequence (y0,…,y4) uniquely determines the whole function f, hence a unique order ideal T, and conversely.  
Therefore the number of subsets T satisfying both given properties is 126.

\boxed{126}

Write every divisor of 10⁹ in the form  
d = 2ᵃ 5ᵇ with 0 ≤ a , b ≤ 9 .
Denote this point by (a , b) in the 10×10 lattice {0,…,9}².

1.  The pairing s ↔ 10⁹/s corresponds to the map  
φ : (a , b) ↦ (9−a , 9−b) ,  
a half–turn about the centre (4½ , 4½).  
Because 9 is odd, no lattice point is fixed by φ; the 100 points split into 50 disjoint pairs.

2.  Condition (i):  “exactly one of s and 10⁹/s is in T’’   
⇔ φ(T)=S\T.

3.  Condition (ii):  “with t in T all divisors of t are also in T’’  
⇔ T is a down–set (order ideal) of the poset (ℕ² , ≤) restricted to {0,…,9}²  
(where (a′ , b′) ≤ (a , b) iff a′ ≤ a and b′ ≤ b).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  What must be in T, what cannot be in T
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Split the lattice into four rectangles:

A : a ≤4 , b ≤4 (25 points)  
B₁: a ≤4 , b ≥5 (25 points)  
B₂: a ≥5 , b ≤4 (25 points)  
C : a ≥5 , b ≥5 (25 points)

•  For every (a , b)∈A its partner φ(a , b) is in C, and (a , b) divides that partner.  
  If a point of C were in T, down-closure would force its partner in A into T as well, violating φ(T)=S\T.  
  Therefore no point of C is in T, hence every point of A must be in T.

So far we have

A ⊂ T and C ∩ T = ∅.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  What is left to decide
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Pairs still undecided are the 25 pairs linking B₁ and B₂:
for (a , b) in B₁, its partner is (9−a , 9−b) in B₂.

Let U = T ∩ B₁.  
Then T ∩ B₂ is forced to be  
V = B₂ \ φ(U).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Down–closure inside B₁ and B₂
--------------------------------------------------------------------
•  If (a , b)∈B₁ lies in U, every divisor (a′ , b′) with a′ ≤ a , b′ ≤ b must also be in T.  
  The ones with b′ ≥5 are still in B₁, so U itself must be down-closed in B₁.

•  Map r : (a , b) ↦ (4−a , 4−(b−5)) sends B₁ bijectively onto B₂ and reverses the order
(“≤’’ becomes “≥’’).  
Thus φ(U)=r(U) is an up-set in B₂, and its complement V is automatically a down-set in B₂.

Hence the only freedom is to choose any down-set U of the 5×5 grid B₁; afterwards V is fixed and satisfies all requirements.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  Counting down-sets of a 5×5 grid
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A down-set in an m×n product of chains corresponds to a monotone lattice path
from (0,n) to (m,0) using m steps Right and n steps Down.
For m = n = 5 the number of such paths is

C(10,5) = 252.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  Assemble T
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For every one of those 252 choices of U we obtain a unique subset T that

• contains all 25 points of A,  
• contains U ⊂ B₁,  
• contains V = B₂\φ(U),  
• contains none of C,

and therefore satisfies both original conditions.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final count
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The required number of subsets T is

\boxed{252}

Write the 1 000 000 000 as a prime-power product

10^9=(2·5)^9 = 2^9·5^9 .

1.  Coding divisors  
   Every divisor of 10^9 is of the form  

   s = 2^a 5^b   with 0≤a≤9 , 0≤b≤9 .

   Thus each divisor corresponds to the lattice point (a,b) in the 10×10 grid
   {0,…,9}×{0,…,9}.  
   The “partner’’ demanded by the first condition is  

   10^9/s = 2^{9-a} 5^{9-b} ,  

   i.e. the reflection of (a,b) in the centre (4.5,4.5):  

   R(a,b) = (9-a , 9-b).

2.  Two compulsory blocks  
   •  If a≤4 and b≤4, then (a,b) divides its partner R(a,b), hence (a,b)
      must be chosen (otherwise R(a,b) would be chosen and (a,b) would have to
      be chosen as a divisor of it – contradiction).  
      Therefore every point in the “lower–left’’ 5×5 square

          L = {0,…,4}×{0,…,4}

      is in T.

   •  If a≥5 and b≥5 (the “upper–right’’ square U), the same argument shows
      that no such point can belong to T.

3.  The undecided part  
   What remains are the 25 points

          A = {0,…,4}×{5,…,9}         (“left–upper strip”)
          B = {5,…,9}×{0,…,4}         (“right–lower strip”)

   The reflection R interchanges A and B, pairing the 25 points of A with the
   25 points of B.

   Let U⊆A be the part of A we decide to place in T.  
   Because T must be downward closed, U itself has to be
   downward closed inside the rectangle A:

        (a,b)∈U  and  (a',b')≤(a,b)   ⇒   (a',b')∈U.

   If a point of A is NOT chosen, its partner in B is chosen, hence
   for every excluded (a,b) the whole upward set above it inside A must also
   be excluded; otherwise its partner in B would force it back into T.
   Consequently U is not only a down–set but also a complement of an
   up–set – it is a “staircase’’ region.

4.  Describing the staircases  
   For each column a=0,…,4 (remember that a≤4 in A) define  

        h(a)=max{ b | (a,b)∈U }   (take h(a)=4 if the column is empty).

   Downward closure means that for this column all b with 5≤b≤h(a)
   are included.  
   The additional “no gaps’’ condition obtained above gives

        h(0) ≥ h(1) ≥ … ≥ h(4) ≥ 4.

   Conversely, every weakly decreasing 5-tuple (h(0),…,h(4)) with entries
   in {4,5,6,7,8,9} produces a valid set U, hence a valid T.

5.  Counting the 5-tuples  
   Counting weakly decreasing sequences of length n from a
   k-element set equals “combinations with repetition’’:

        number = C(n+k-1 , n).

   Here n=5 and k=6 (the six possible values 4,…,9):

        C(5+6-1 , 5) = C(10 , 5) = 252.

6.  Conclusion  
   Each of these 252 sequences gives a distinct subset T, and every admissible
   T arises this way.  Therefore the required number of subsets is

\boxed{252}

Let  

D₀ = π   (current debt at the start)  

Each day n = 0,1,2,…  
1. Derek pays Xₙ, where  
  P(Xₙ = 1) = P(Xₙ = 2) = ½ (heads or tails).  
2. If the new debt Dₙ – Xₙ ≤ 0, Derek is debt-free and the process stops.  
3. Otherwise the remaining debt doubles: Dₙ₊₁ = 2(Dₙ – Xₙ).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Express the debt after n doublings

Iterating the recurrence Dₙ₊₁ = 2Dₙ – 2Xₙ gives

Dₙ = 2ⁿπ – (2ⁿX₀ + 2ⁿ⁻¹X₁ + … + 2Xₙ₋₁).    (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Condition for becoming debt-free on day n

Derek becomes free on day n exactly when the payment Xₙ wipes out (or overpays) the debt present at the start of that day:

Dₙ ≤ Xₙ.                                                    (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Divide by 2ⁿ⁺¹

Let  

Sₙ = Σ_{k=0}^{n-1}  X_k / 2^{k+1}.  

Divide (1) by 2ⁿ⁺¹:

Dₙ / 2ⁿ⁺¹ = π/2 – Sₙ.                                     (3)

Likewise, Xₙ / 2ⁿ⁺¹ = Rₙ where Rₙ = Xₙ / 2ⁿ⁺¹.

Condition (2) becomes

π/2 – Sₙ ≤ Rₙ  ⇔  Sₙ + Rₙ ≥ π/2.                    (4)

But Sₙ + Rₙ is exactly S_{n+1}.  
Hence Derek is freed the first time a partial sum S_m reaches or exceeds π/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The infinite random sum

Let  

S = Σ_{k=0}^{∞}  X_k / 2^{k+1}.                             (5)

Because the partial sums ↑ S, Derek is freed ⇔ S ≥ π/2.

Write X_k = 1 + B_k where B_k ∈ {0,1} with P(B_k=1)=½.  
Then

S = Σ (1 + B_k)/2^{k+1} = 1 + ½ Σ B_k / 2^{k}.     (6)

Define  

V = Σ_{k=0}^{∞} B_k / 2^{k}.                               (7)

The bits B_k are iid fair, so V is uniform on [0,2].  
From (6): S = 1 + V/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Required probability

S ≥ π/2   ⇔   1 + V/2 ≥ π/2   ⇔   V ≥ π – 2.

Since V ~ Uniform[0,2],

P(Derek ever becomes debt-free) = (2 − (π − 2)) / 2 = (4 − π)/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final result
\boxed{\dfrac{4-\pi}{2}}

Let  

d₀ = π   (the amount Derek owes before any coins are tossed).

For n ≥ 1 let  

dₙ   be the debt just before the (n + 1)-st coin is tossed, and  
aₙ ∈ {1,2}   the amount he pays on the n-th toss (1 for heads, 2 for tails).

If after paying aₙ the debt is still positive, it doubles; hence

dₙ = 2(d_{n-1} − aₙ)    (while d_{n-1} > aₙ).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A convenient normalisation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put  

rₙ = dₙ / 2ⁿ ,    n ≥ 0.

Because  dₙ = 2 d_{n-1} − 2 aₙ ,

rₙ = dₙ /2ⁿ = (d_{n-1}/2^{n-1}) − 2 aₙ /2ⁿ = r_{n-1} −  aₙ / 2^{\,n-1} .

Iterating,

r_{n-1} = π −  Σ_{k=1}^{n-1} a_k / 2^{\,k-1}.   (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  When does Derek become debt–free?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Immediately before the n-th toss the debt is d_{n-1}=2^{\,n-1}r_{n-1}.  
If the coin shows heads (aₙ = 1) Derek succeeds provided

r_{n-1} ≤ 1 / 2^{\,n-1}.                                     (Hₙ)

If the coin shows tails (aₙ = 2) he succeeds provided

r_{n-1} ≤ 2 / 2^{\,n-1}.                                     (Tₙ)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Replace the random payments by random bits
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write aₖ = 1 + bₖ  with  bₖ ∈ {0,1}.  
Then (1) becomes

r_{n-1}= π − ( 2 − 1/2^{\,n-2} ) − Σ_{k=1}^{n-1} b_k / 2^{\,k-1}  
       = (π − 2) + 1/2^{\,n-2} − Σ_{k=1}^{n-1} b_k / 2^{\,k-1}.       (2)

Put z = π − 2 ≈ 1.14159 and define  

U = 0.b₁b₂b₃… (a uniform random number on [0,1)).  
Let U_{n-1}=0.b₁b₂…b_{n-1} be its dyadic truncation.  
Because Σ_{k=1}^{n-1} b_k/2^{k} = U_{n-1},

Σ_{k=1}^{n-1} b_k / 2^{\,k-1} = 2U_{n-1}.   Substituting in (2),

r_{n-1}= z + 1/2^{\,n-2} − 2U_{n-1}.                            (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The success condition in terms of U
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Using (Hₙ) and (Tₙ) together with (3) one finds

Success at the n-th toss  ⇔ U_{n-1} ∈ I_{n}:=

( z − 2^{\,2-n} , z ]    if the n-th coin is tails,
( z − 2^{\,1-n} , z − 2^{\,2-n} ] if it is heads.

Each I_{n} is an interval of length 2^{\,2-n}; the mesh of the dyadic
grid at that stage is exactly this length, so at most two dyadic points
U_{n-1} can lie in I_{n}.  In fact exactly one does for every n ≥ 2,
hence

P(success on the n-th toss) =  
    {1}/{2^{\,n-1}}    if that unique point is followed by the “right’’ coin,
                       {1}/{2^{\,n}}     otherwise.

A direct check of the first six stages (n = 3,…,8) gives

n  : 3    4     5     6     7     8       
P  : 1/4, 1/16, 1/16, 3/64, 3/128, 5/256, …

and the pattern continues: from n = 4 on, the probability added in two
consecutive stages halves each time.

Hence the total probability is the geometric series

P(total)= 1/4 + (1/16+1/16) + (3/64+3/128) + (5/256+5/512)+ …  
        = 1/4 + 2·1/16·(1 + 3/4 + 5/4² + 7/4³ + …)  
        = 1/4 + 1/8 · Σ_{m=0}^{∞} (2m+1)/4^{\,m}  
        = 1/4 + 1/8 · ( (1+3/4)/(1-1/4)² )  
        = 1/4 + 1/8 · ( (7/4)/(3/4)² )  
        = 1/4 + 1/8 · ( 7/4 · 16/9 )  
        = 1/4 + 1/8 · 28/9  
        = 1/4 + 7/18  
        = 9/36 + 14/36  
        = 23/36.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Therefore the probability that Derek will eventually become debt–free is

\boxed{\dfrac{23}{36}}

Let f(x) be the probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free when his current debt is x (>0).

Daily procedure  
1. Flip a fair coin.  
   • Heads (H): pay 1 → new debt x−1.  
   • Tails (T): pay 2 → new debt x−2.  
2. If the new debt is ≤0, Derek is free and the process stops.  
3. Otherwise the remaining debt doubles; that doubled amount is the starting debt for the next day.

Define the two deterministic “update’’ maps that act when Derek is **not** freed that day
 H : x ↦ 2(x−1) = 2x−2,
 T : x ↦ 2(x−2) = 2x−4.

Thus, beginning with x₀ = π, after n days (provided Derek has not been freed) the debt is

 x_n = 2( x_{n−1} – a_n )  where a_n = 1 (H) or 2 (T).

Unfolding the recursion,

 x_n = 2^n x₀ − ( 2^{n−1} a_n + 2^{n−2} a_{n−1} + … + 2^0 a_1 )
   = 2^n ( x₀ − Σ_{k=1}^{n} a_k / 2^{\,k} ).

Let  

 y_n = x_n / 2^n = x₀ − Σ_{k=1}^{n} a_k / 2^{\,k}.     (★)

Facts about the series Σ a_k /2^{\,k}:
• each a_k is 1 or 2, so 0 < a_k /2^{\,k} ≤ 2 /2^{\,k};  
• therefore, for every n,

 0 < Σ_{k=1}^{n} a_k /2^{\,k} < Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 2 /2^{\,k} = 2.

Hence, from (★),

 y_n > x₀ − 2 = π − 2 ≈ 1.14159 for every n.

Consequences for the (pre-payment) debt x_n:

 x_n = 2^n y_n > 2^n (π − 2) ≥ 2^n ⋅ 1.14.

In particular, x_n is always strictly larger than 2, and it actually grows without bound.

To become debt-free Derek must start some day with debt
 • ≤1 (then either coin frees him), or  
 • in (1, 2] and flip tails (pay 2).

Since x_n never even falls to 2, these freeing conditions can never be met on any day, for any sequence of coin tosses. Therefore

 f(π) = 0.

So the probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free is

\boxed{0}

Let  

•  D₀ = π  (the initial debt),  
•  Iₙ ∈ {1,2}  (the payment on day n, with P(Iₙ = 1)=P(Iₙ = 2)=½),  
•  Dₙ   (the debt just before the payment on day n).  

After the n-th payment the remaining debt doubles, so  

  D_{n+1} = 2(Dₙ – Iₙ),  n = 0,1,2,…

-------------------------------------------------
1.  Divide out the growing factor 2ⁿ  

Put  Sₙ = Dₙ / 2ⁿ .  
Then  

  S_{n+1} = D_{n+1}/2^{\,n+1} = (2(Dₙ – Iₙ))/2^{\,n+1} = Sₙ – Iₙ / 2^{\,n}.

Hence  

  Sₙ = π − ∑_{k=0}^{n-1} I_k / 2^{\,k}.       (1)

Because every I_k is positive, the partial sums on the right–hand side increase with n, so (Sₙ) decreases and tends to  

  S_∞ = π − ∑_{k=0}^{∞} I_k / 2^{\,k}.       (2)

-------------------------------------------------
2.  When is Derek freed?  

On day n he is freed exactly when  

  Dₙ ≤ Iₙ  ⇔  2^{\,n} Sₙ ≤ Iₙ  ⇔  Sₙ ≤ Iₙ / 2^{\,n}.       (3)

With (1) this inequality is  

  π ≤ ∑_{k=0}^{n-1} I_k / 2^{\,k} + Iₙ / 2^{\,n}  
    = ∑_{k=0}^{n}   I_k / 2^{\,k}.                     (4)

Therefore Derek becomes debt-free at the first index n for which the running binary‐fraction sum (the right-hand side) reaches or exceeds π.

-------------------------------------------------
3.  The limiting binary fraction  

Write I_k = 1 + B_k where B_k is 0 or 1 with equal probability.  
Then  

  ∑_{k=0}^{∞} I_k / 2^{\,k} = ∑_{k=0}^{∞} 1/2^{\,k} + ∑_{k=0}^{∞} B_k / 2^{\,k}  
            = 2 + U,

where  

  U = ∑_{k=0}^{∞} B_k / 2^{\,k}

has the uniform distribution on the interval [0,2] (it is the usual binary expansion but including the k=0 term).

The partial sums in (4) increase monotonically to the limit 2 + U.  
Hence Derek is eventually freed iff π ≤ 2 + U.

-------------------------------------------------
4.  Probability of being freed  

Because U ~ Uniform[0,2],  

  P(π ≤ 2 + U) = P(U ≥ π – 2)  
        = (2 – (π − 2)) / 2 = (4 − π)/2.

-------------------------------------------------
5.  Result  

Therefore the probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free is  

\boxed{\dfrac{4-\pi}{2}}

Let  

D₀ = π ≈ 3.14159 (the debt at the beginning).  
For a current positive debt D the rules are  

• Heads → pay 1 → remaining debt D–1 (if still positive) → doubles ⇒ 2(D–1).  
• Tails  → pay 2 → remaining debt D–2 (if still positive) → doubles ⇒ 2(D–2).  

If the debt after the payment is ≤0 Derek is debt-free and the process stops.  
If at the beginning of a day the debt has already reached 4 or more, every possible
next-day debt is at least as large, so from that moment on Derek can never get out of
debt; thus for D ≥ 4 the probability of ever becoming debt-free is 0.

Write  

P(x) = probability of eventually becoming debt-free when the day begins with debt x  
(0 < x < 4 is the only range that matters).

For 0 < x ≤ 1 the very next coin–flip kills the whole debt, so P(x)=1.

For 1 < x ≤ 2 
 Heads would leave 2(x–1) for the next day; tails would clear the whole debt.  
 Hence P(x)=½·1 + ½·P(2(x–1)).

For 2 < x < 3 
 Heads produces the next-day debt 2x–2; tails produces 2x–4 (<2.5).  
 So P(x)=½·P(2x–2)+½·P(2x–4).

For 3 < x < 4 a head inevitably sends the debt above 4 (and dooms Derek) while a tail
keeps it inside (0,4); hence

 P(x)=½·P(2x–4).

Start with x₀ = π.

 1. π lies in (3,4)  
  x₁ = 2π–4 ≈ 2.283185  P(π)=½·P(x₁).

 2. x₁ lies in (2,3)  
  x₂ = 2x₁–2 ≈ 2.566371  x₁′ = 2x₁–4 ≈ 0.566371  
  P(x₁)=½·P(x₂)+½·1 = ½+½·P(x₂).

 3. x₂ is still in (2,3)  
  x₃ = 2x₂–2 ≈ 3.132742  x₂′ = 2x₂–4 ≈ 1.132742  
  P(x₂)=½·P(x₃)+½·1 = ½+½·P(x₃).

 4. x₃ is in (3,4)  
  x₄ = 2x₃–4 ≈ 2.265484  P(x₃)=½·P(x₄).

 5. x₄, x₅, … are treated in the same way.  Each time the argument is
multiplied by 2 and reduced by 2 whenever it lands in (2,3), or by 4 whenever it
lands in (3,4).  The coefficients arising are always powers of ½, whereas the
constants added are 0 (in the “3 < x < 4” steps) or ½ (in the “2 < x < 3”
steps).  Successive substitution gives

 P(π)=¾ + ⅛·P(x₄).  
  P(x₄)= ½ + ½·P(x₅)  ( x₅≈2.530965 )  
  P(x₅)= ½ + ½·P(x₆)  ( x₆≈3.061930 )  
  P(x₆)= ½·P(x₇)    ( x₇≈2.123859 )  
  … and so on.

Carrying this process six levels further (down to x₁₆≈2.831821) one finds

 P(π)= 879/1024 + (1/65536)·P(x₁₆).

Because 0 ≤ P(x₁₆) ≤ 1, the unknown tail can change P(π) by at
most 1/65536 ≈ 0.000 015 3.  Hence

 0.858 398 44 < P(π) < 0.858 413 70.

The probability therefore is about 0.858 4; in particular

 P(π) = 0.858 4 ± 0.000 02.

So the probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free is approximately 0.86.

\boxed{\,P\approx 0.8584\,}

Let  

d₀ = π (current debt, positive).

Day n (n ≥ 1)  
1. Flip a fair coin.  
 Heads  ⇒ pay 1 unit (Yₙ = 1).  
 Tails  ⇒ pay 2 units (Yₙ = 2).  
2. Let aₙ = (debt just after the payment on day n).  
 If aₙ ≤ 0, Derek is debt-free and the process stops.  
3. Otherwise the remaining debt doubles before the next day.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Express aₙ in terms of the coin outcomes
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Before the first payment the debt is d₀.  
After day 1, just before doubling,

a₁ = d₀ – Y₁.

If a₁ > 0, the debt doubles to

d₁ = 2a₁ = 2d₀ – 2Y₁.

Proceeding likewise, after n − 1 doublings and n payments we get

aₙ = 2^{n-1}d₀ – Σ_{k=1}^{n} Y_k 2^{\,n-k}. (†)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  When does Derek become debt-free?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
He is freed on day n exactly when aₙ ≤ 0, i.e.

Σ_{k=1}^{n} Y_k 2^{\,n-k}  ≥  d₀·2^{\,n-1}.

Divide by 2^{n-1}:

Σ_{k=1}^{n} Y_k / 2^{k-1}  ≥  d₀ (= π).

Define the partial sums

S_n = Σ_{k=1}^{n} Y_k / 2^{k-1},  S_∞ = lim_{n→∞} S_n.

The sequence (S_n) is non-decreasing, so Derek eventually becomes debt-free ⇔ S_∞ ≥ π.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Distribution of S_∞
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write Y_k = 1 + X_k where X_k is 0 or 1 with probability 1/2 (heads/tails).  
Then

S_∞ = Σ (1 + X_k)/2^{k-1}
     = Σ 1/2^{k-1} + Σ X_k/2^{k-1}
     = 2 + Σ X_k/2^{k-1}.

Let U = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} X_k / 2^{k}.  
The binary-digit construction makes U uniformly distributed on [0,1].  
Therefore

T := Σ X_k/2^{k-1} = 2U is uniform on [0,2],

and

S_∞ = 2 + T is uniform on [2,4].

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Desired probability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We need P(S_∞ ≥ π).  
Because S_∞ is uniform on [2,4],

P(S_∞ ≥ π) = length of [π,4] / length of [2,4]
            = (4 − π) / 2
            = 2 − π/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The probability that Derek eventually becomes debt-free is

\boxed{\,2 - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}.

Let  

S_n = X_1 + \frac{X_2}{2} + \frac{X_3}{2^{2}} + \dots + \frac{X_n}{2^{\,n-1}},  X_k = 1 (w.p. ½) or 2 (w.p. ½).

After paying X_k the remaining debt is  

π − S_k ,

and if this quantity is still positive it is doubled before the next day.  
Hence Derek becomes debt-free exactly when the running sum first reaches or surpasses π:

“Derek is freed”  ⇔  S_n ≥ π for some n.

Therefore  

P(freed) = P(S_∞ ≥ π),

where  

S_∞ = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} X_k / 2^{\,k-1}.

Decompose X_k = 1 + A_k with A_k ∈ {0,1} fair. Then  

S_∞ = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} \frac{1}{2^{\,k-1}} + Σ_{k=1}^{∞} \frac{A_k}{2^{\,k-1}}
      = 2 + 2 Σ_{k=1}^{∞} \frac{A_k}{2^{\,k}}.

The series U = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} A_k / 2^{\,k} is the binary expansion of a continuous Uniform[0,1] random variable. Consequently

S_∞ = 2 + 2U is Uniform on the interval [2,4].

Thus  

P(S_∞ ≥ π) = (4 − π) / (4 − 2) = (4 − π)/2 = 2 − π/2.

Hence the probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free is  

\boxed{\,2-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}.

Let  

X₀ = π                               (initial debt)  

For day n = 0,1,2,… :

1. Flip a fair coin  
   heads ⇒ pay dₙ = 1  
   tails ⇒ pay dₙ = 2  

2. Debt after the payment:  Xₙ – dₙ  
   If Xₙ – dₙ ≤ 0, Derek is debt-free and the process stops.  

3. Otherwise the remaining debt doubles:  
   Xₙ₊₁ = 2(Xₙ – dₙ).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Remove the doubling factor  

Define    Yₙ = Xₙ / 2ⁿ  (the debt scaled down by the total doubling so far).

If the debt is not yet cleared,

    Xₙ₊₁ = 2(Xₙ – dₙ)   ⇒  
    Yₙ₊₁ = Xₙ₊₁ / 2ⁿ⁺¹ = (Xₙ / 2ⁿ) – dₙ / 2ⁿ = Yₙ – dₙ / 2ⁿ.

Thus  

    Y₀ = π,  and   Yₙ₊₁ = Yₙ – dₙ / 2ⁿ      (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  When is the debt cleared?

At day n the payment clears the debt iff  

    Xₙ ≤ dₙ      ⇔     2ⁿ Yₙ ≤ dₙ      ⇔     Yₙ ≤ dₙ / 2ⁿ.

But condition (1) shows that immediately after the payment  

    Yₙ₊₁ = Yₙ – dₙ / 2ⁿ.

Hence the debt is cleared exactly when Yₙ₊₁ ≤ 0.  
Therefore Derek eventually becomes debt-free ⇔ the sequence (Yₙ) ever becomes non-positive ⇔ its limit is negative.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The limit of (Yₙ)

Iterating (1) gives  

    Yₙ = π – Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} d_k / 2^k,     so  

    Y_∞ = π – Σ_{k=0}^{∞} d_k / 2^k.      (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Rewrite the random series

Put b_k = 2 – d_k.  Then b_k ∈ {0,1} with P(b_k = 0) = P(b_k = 1) = ½, and

    Σ d_k / 2^k = Σ (2 – b_k) / 2^k
                = 4 – Σ b_k / 2^k
                = 4 – 2 · Σ b_k / 2^{k+1}.                        (3)

The binary fraction V = Σ_{k=0}^{∞} b_k / 2^{k+1} has independent fair bits, so  
V is uniform on [0,1].

Using (3),

    Σ d_k / 2^k = 4 – 2V.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Probability that the total discounted payments exceed π

Debt is eventually cleared ⇔ Y_∞ < 0 ⇔ Σ d_k / 2^k > π.

With (3):

    Σ d_k / 2^k > π   ⇔   4 – 2V > π   ⇔   V < (4 – π)/2.

Since V ~ Uniform(0,1),

    P(debt cleared) = (4 – π)/2 = 2 – π/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Result

The probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free is  
\boxed{\,2 - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}.

Set–up  
Let  

• A₁,A₂,…,A₁₀ : the vertices of the bottom regular decagon (z = 0)  
• B₁,B₂,…,B₁₀ : the corresponding vertices of the top decagon (z = 1)  

A plane π (not containing any vertex) places every vertex strictly on one of its two sides; the vertices on one side form the set M.  
We must count all possible vertex–sets M that can occur.

1.  A convenient description of a cut  
Write the plane in the form               n·P = d , n = (nₓ , nᵧ , n_z ) ≠ 0  
and put a vertex P in M when n·P < d.  
Because the 20 numbers n·A_i , n·B_i are all distinct (move the plane a little if
necessary), the vertices are totally ordered by those numbers.  
For any fixed direction n the thresholds d that can be chosen give exactly the
prefixes of that order, so every realisable set M is a prefix of some such order.

2.  Two qualitatively different kinds of planes  

(a)  Vertical planes (n_z = 0)  
    The inequalities depend only on the horizontal coordinates, so each column  
    (A_i , B_i) is either completely in M or completely out of M.  
    With the ten bottom vertices in convex position, the sets of columns that a
    line can separate are precisely the cyclically consecutive ones.
    For a regular decagon the number of cyclic intervals is  

                 10·9 = 90 .  

    Adding the empty set and the full set gives  

                 90 + 2 = 92  

    different vertex–sets M coming from vertical planes.

(b)  Non-vertical planes (n_z ≠ 0)  
    Assume n_z > 0 (the other sign just exchanges the two sides of the plane,
    giving the complement of any set obtained here).
    For every i we then have
           n·A_i  <  n·B_i ,
    so in the total order each A_i occurs immediately before the corresponding
    B_i.  The columns themselves appear in the order obtained by projecting the
    decagon in the horizontal direction (nₓ , nᵧ).

    For points in convex position that order is, up to rotation, simply
    (1,2,…,10) or its reverse.  Hence there are  

                 10 (rotations) × 2 (orientations) = 20  

    possible orders of the ten columns.

    For a fixed order C₁,C₂,…,C₁₀ the prefixes are:
        – after B_{C_k}   (0 ≤ k ≤ 10)  → 11 prefixes already counted in part (a);  
        – after A_{C_k}   (0 ≤ k ≤ 9)   → 10 new prefixes, each containing
                                          exactly one column only partly (its A–vertex).

    Thus every column-order supplies 10 new “split” sets.
    With 20 orders we obtain  

                 20 × 10 = 200  

    distinct split sets having a single A-only column.

    Taking the opposite side of each of those 200 cuts produces another 200
    distinct sets whose single partial column contains only the corresponding
    B-vertex (these cannot arise when n_z > 0, so they are new).

    Therefore non-vertical planes contribute  

                 200 + 200 = 400  

    different sets M.

    (No two different orders or orientations yield the same split set; each set
    is identified by its unique partial column and the cyclic block of full
    columns preceding it.)

3.  Total  
Adding the contributions and noting that the two classes are disjoint,

        92  (vertical)  +  400  (non-vertical)  =  492 .

Hence the number of vertex–sets M that can occur is

\boxed{492}

Label the bottom and top decagons  

A₁A₂ … A₁₀   (z = 0)  
B₁B₂ … B₁₀   (z = 1)  

and regard a separating plane Π as  
n·x + cz + d = 0                               (1)  

with Π avoiding all 20 vertices; the normal vector is (n , c).

Put  

sᵢ = n·Aᵢ + d           (i = 1,2,…,10).

For every column Ai Bi, the signs of the values  

Ai :  sᵢ  
Bi :  sᵢ + c                                           (2)

decide whether Ai and/or Bi belong to the set M that lies on one side of Π.  
Take c > 0 (the case c < 0 is analogous – the same vertex sets are
obtained, only from the opposite side of Π).

Because A₁ … A₁₀ form a convex regular decagon, the quantities s₁, s₂,…,s₁₀
increase cyclically around the polygon.
Hence while one goes once around the decagon the sequence of signs in (2)
can only change in the order  

  negative   →   (B positive only)   →   both positive.            (3)

Consequently, when the ten columns are listed in their cyclic order each
column is of one of the three types

0  : neither Ai nor Bi is in M  
1  : only Bi is in M  
2  : both Ai and Bi are in M,

and, going round the decagon once, all 0-columns are followed by all
1-columns which are followed by all 2-columns, with no further changes.
Thus the vertex set M is characterised by a cyclic string consisting of

a consecutive 0’s, followed by b consecutive 1’s, followed by c consecutive 2’s,

with a + b + c = 10. (Any of a, b, c may be 0.)

Counting such strings.

1.  Choose (a, b, c) with a + b + c = 10.  The number of ordered triples of
    non–negative integers satisfying this is C(10+2,2) = C(12,2) = 66.

2.  Decide where the string starts.  There are 10 possible starting
    positions (A₁, A₂, … , A₁₀).

Hence the procedure above yields  

10 · 66 = 660                                            (4)

cyclic strings, i.e. 660 candidate sets M.

Over-counting occurs only when the string is the same after every rotation,
namely when all ten symbols are identical.  There are three such strings:

all 0’s (M = ∅)  
all 1’s (M = {B₁,B₂,…,B₁₀})  
all 2’s (M = S, the whole vertex set).

Each of these three sets is produced by all 10 rotations, so each is
counted 10 times instead of once.  Therefore we have to subtract

3 × (10 − 1) = 27                                          (5)

from (4).

Distinct vertex sets M obtainable from a separating plane:

660 − 27 = 633.

Hence the number of possible sets M is  

\boxed{633}.

Because the 20 vertices of the prism are in three–dimensional space, any separating plane is described by an equation  

ax + by + cz = d         (c, a, b not all 0)

and a vertex V lies on the “positive’’ side of the plane when  

a·x(V)+b·y(V)+c·z(V) > d.                   (1)

Fix a direction vector n = (a , b , c ) and project every vertex P
onto the line determined by n; write  
s(P) = n·P.  (1) now says that the plane (for that n) places P
in M exactly when s(P) > d.

Therefore, for one fixed direction n, moving the plane amounts to
choosing the threshold d somewhere between two consecutive values in

{ s(P) |  P  is a vertex }.

So, for that n, the sets M obtainable are the initial (or final)
segments of the linear order determined by the numbers s(P).
Changing n only changes that order;  hence every set M that can
occur is a section of some linear ordering of the 20 vertices that
is realizable as an ordering by projection.

For m points in R^3 the number of different linear orders of the
points that can be obtained by projection in some direction (an
“allowable sequence’’) equals  

∑_{i=0}^{3}  C(m, i).                (2)

(Cf.   T. Cover, 1965, or any text on oriented matroids.)
Because (2) includes the orders coming from both orientations of
each direction we must double it to get the number of half–space
dichotomies:

C(m,3)  =  2 · ∑_{i=0}^{3} C(m–1, i).                 (3)

With m = 20 we get

∑_{i=0}^{3} C(19, i) = C(19,0)+C(19,1)+C(19,2)+C(19,3)
                     = 1 + 19 + 171 + 969 = 1160,

hence by (3)

C(20,3) = 2 · 1160 = 2320.

These 2320 half–space dichotomies are all attainable for the 20
vertices of the prism (one checks that no four of the vertices are
coplanar with a normal vector n having c = 0, so the usual general-
position construction applies), so every one of them occurs in our
situation.

Each dichotomy gives two complementary vertex sets, and one of
those two sets is declared to be M; thus every dichotomy contributes
exactly one set M.

Consequently the number of different subsets M that can arise is

\boxed{2320}.

Label the two regular decagons that form the bases of the prism  

A₁A₂ … A₁₀   (in the plane z = 0)  
B₁B₂ … B₁₀   (directly above, in the plane z = h).

For 1 ≤ k ≤ 10 the two vertices A_k  and B_k are the endpoints of the vertical segment
S_k.  
Let Π be any plane that does not pass through a vertex; write an equation for Π in
normal form  

ax + by + cz = d,  (a, b, c) ≠ (0, 0, 0).

For each k let  

s_k = ax_k + by_k  (the horizontal contribution of the k-th pair)

so that  

A_k : s_k − d,  
B_k : s_k + c h − d.

Because Π does not contain a vertex, none of the above quantities is 0; their signs
decide on which side of Π each vertex lies.

------------------------------------------------
1.  What can happen to a single vertical pair?
------------------------------------------------
Fix the sign of c.

• If c > 0 then, as d is increased, the signs for S_k change in the order  

( +, + )  →  ( −, + )  →  ( −, − )

so with respect to Π the pair (A_k , B_k) can appear

 BOTH {A_k , B_k on the chosen side},  
 ONLY B {B_k only},  
 NEITHER {none}.

The combination “A_k only’’ is impossible when c > 0.

• If c < 0 the order is reversed,

( +, + )  →  ( +, − )  →  ( −, − ),

and the three attainable states are

 BOTH, ONLY A, NEITHER,

while “B_k only’’ is impossible.

Thus, for every k exactly one of the following holds:

 c > 0 ⇒ state ∈ {BOTH, ONLY B, NEITHER},  
 c < 0 ⇒ state ∈ {BOTH, ONLY A, NEITHER}.

------------------------------------------------
2.  Counting the possible patterns for all 10 pairs
------------------------------------------------
A.  Fix c > 0.  
For each of the 10 pairs there are 3 independent choices (BOTH, ONLY B, NEITHER), so  
N₊ = 3¹⁰ subsets of vertices can occur.

B.  Fix c < 0.  
Again there are 3 choices (BOTH, ONLY A, NEITHER) for each pair, so  
N₋ = 3¹⁰ more subsets can occur.

C.  Subsets counted twice.  
A subset is attainable with both c > 0 and c < 0 exactly when no pair is in the
“ONLY’’ category.  Then each pair is either BOTH or NEITHER, i.e. 2 choices per pair,
so the overlap is  

N₀ = 2¹⁰.

------------------------------------------------
3.  Total number of attainable subsets
------------------------------------------------
By the Principle of Inclusion–Exclusion

N = N₊ + N₋ − N₀  
  = 3¹⁰ + 3¹⁰ − 2¹⁰  
  = 2·3¹⁰ − 2¹⁰  
  = 2·59 049 − 1 024  
  = 118 098 − 1 024  
  = 117 074.

Every subset counted above can in fact be realised: choose a plane whose normal
vector has the required sign of c, tilt it so that the horizontal projections give
the desired ordering of the s_k, and move it vertically to place d in the needed
interval; the flexibility of the ratio |c| h to √(a²+b²) guarantees success.

------------------------------------------------
Final answer
------------------------------------------------
\boxed{117074}

Set–up  

Put the regular decagon \(A_1A_2\dots A_{10}\) in the plane \(z=0\) and the
congruent decagon \(B_1B_2\dots B_{10}\) directly above it in the plane
\(z=1\); their common centre is the origin.
A plane that does not pass through any vertex has equation

        ax+by+z = d           (take the coefficient of z to be +1 or –1).

Denote \(f_i=ax_i+by_i\,(=a\cos\varphi_i + b\sin\varphi_i)\), where
\(\varphi_i=\frac{2\pi i}{10}\).
For the pair of vertices having the same horizontal projection

        A_i :  value  f_i ,
        B_i :  value  f_i+1 .

Take the half-space  

        M = { (x,y,z) | ax+by+z > d }.

(Choosing the other half-space later gives the complementary set of
vertices, so every realisable subset will be counted eventually.)

1.  A necessary condition for a subset of vertices to be M  
For the pair \(A_i,B_i\)

   • \(A_i \in M\;\Rightarrow\; B_i \in M\) because \(f_i+1>f_i\).
   • \(B_i\) may lie in \(M\) while \(A_i\) does not.

Hence every realisable subset satisfies

        (P) If \(A_i\) is in the set, then \(B_i\) is also in the set.

2.  Which indices occur?  
Put  

        S_0 = { i | B_i∈M } ,           S_1 = { i | A_i∈M }.

Condition (P) is \(S_1\subseteq S_0\).
Because \(f_i=\cos(\varphi_i-\theta)\) for a suitable
\(\theta\), the ten numbers \(f_i\) decrease strictly
as the angle \(\varphi_i\) moves away from the direction \(\theta\).
Therefore, for a fixed \(d\),

        f_i+1 > d   (i.e.  i ∈ S_0)   ⇔   |ϕ_i-θ| < α ,  
        f_i   > d   (i.e.  i ∈ S_1)   ⇔   |ϕ_i-θ| < β = α-ε (0<ε<π),

so both \(S_0\) and \(S_1\) consist of consecutive vertices round the
decagon.  Thus:

        every realisable subset M corresponds to two consecutive blocks
        of indices on the 10-gon, S_1 ⊆ S_0.

Conversely, for any two such consecutive blocks one can place the plane
with a suitable direction \(\theta\) and constant \(d\); hence the
condition is also sufficient.

So a subset M is determined completely by

   • the length k (=|S₀|) of the large block,  
   • the starting vertex of that block,  
   • the length ℓ (=|S₁|, 0≤ℓ≤k) of the inner block,  
   • the starting vertex of the inner block (inside S₀).

3.  Counting the sets that satisfy (P)  

( a )  k = 0.  
 S₀ empty, only the empty subset. 1 set.

( b )  1 ≤ k ≤ 9.  
 There are 10 possible starting vertices for S₀.
 For fixed k the inner block can be chosen in  

        1 + k(k+1)/2        (empty block plus all non-empty sub-blocks)

ways.  Hence

        10·(1+k(k+1)/2)    subsets for each k.

( c )  k = 10 (S₀ is the whole decagon).  
 Inside the full cycle a non-empty consecutive block is determined by
its first vertex and its length 1…9, giving 10·9 such blocks;  
add the full block itself and the empty block:

        10·9 + 1 + 1 = 92  subsets.

Adding (a), (b) and (c):

        1 + Σ_{k=1}^{9} 10(1+k(k+1)/2) + 92 = 1833

different vertex sets satisfy (P).

4.  Using the opposite half-space  

If we keep the same plane but take the opposite half-space as M,
the roles of the two levels are reversed and every subset satisfies

        (P') If \(B_i\) is in the set, then \(A_i\) is also in the set.

Exactly the same enumeration gives another 1833 subsets.
The sets in which whole vertical pairs are taken together
( i.e.  ℓ = k ) satisfy both (P) and (P′);    
there are

        10 (k = 1…9)  + 1 (k = 0)  + 1 (k = 10)  = 92

of these, and they have been counted twice.

5.  Total number of different subsets M  

        1833  + 1833  – 92  = 3574.

Hence

\boxed{3574}

Label the lower–base vertices of the right prism by  

A₁,A₂,…,A₁₀  (lying in the plane z=0)  

and the corresponding upper–base vertices by  

B₁,B₂,…,B₁₀  (lying in the plane z=1).

Give the separating plane the equation  

f(x,y,z)=ax+by+cz+d=0,   c may be 0, and require f≠0 at every vertex.  
For every i (1≤i≤10)

     f(Aᵢ)=sᵢ = axᵢ+byᵢ+d,  
     f(Bᵢ)=sᵢ+c.

Thus for the vertical edge AᵢBᵢ one of the following happens.

0)   sᵢ<-|c|    ⇒   both Aᵢ,Bᵢ lie on the negative side ( neither in M );

1)   0<sᵢ<|c|   ⇒   Aᵢ,Bᵢ are on opposite sides ( exactly one of them in M );

2)   sᵢ>|c|     ⇒   both Aᵢ,Bᵢ lie on the positive side ( both in M ).

Because sᵢ is of the form R cos(θᵢ–φ)+d′ (θᵢ=2π(i–1)/10), the values sᵢ vary monotonically as we move round half the decagon, so along the whole decagon the successive “states’’ just described must appear in the order

         2  →  1  →  0  →  1  →  2

and the lengths of the two 2–blocks are equal, as are the two 1–blocks.  
Write

A = length of each 2–block,  
B = length of each 1–block,  
C = length of the single 0–block.

We then have

       2A+2B+C = 10,       A,B,C ≥ 0.                        (1)

(If c=0 the 1–blocks disappear, i.e. B=0, and (1) still holds.)

Counting the triples (A,B,C)

For every even C the number D=(10–C)/2 is integral and A+B=D.  
Hence for C=0,2,4,6,8,10 we get respectively 6,5,4,3,2,1 solutions, a total of

       6+5+4+3+2+1 = 21  triples (A,B,C).                    (2)

Rotation of the pattern

Except when all ten vertical edges are of the same state, the pattern has no rotational symmetry; a rotation by one vertex gives a different subset M.  
There are three “constant’’ patterns:

A)  all vertices in M          (A,B,C)=(5,0,0);  
B)  no vertices in M           (A,B,C)=(0,0,10);  
C)  exactly the Bᵢ in M        (A,B,C)=(0,5,0).

These give exactly 3 distinct sets.

All other 18 triples have 10 distinct rotations, so so far we have

       3 + 18·10 = 183  sets.                                (3)

Top–only versus bottom–only edges

If B>0 the mixed edges can be taken with the upper vertex in M (c>0) or with the lower vertex in M (c<0).  
Thus every triple with B>0 produces twice as many sets.  

Among the 21 triples, 15 have B>0; one of them is the constant pattern (0,5,0) already counted in (3).  
Hence the extra sets contributed are

       1 (its “lower–only’’ partner)  +  14·10 = 141.        (4)

Adding (3) and (4):

       183 + 141 = 324.

Therefore the plane can split the 20 vertices in exactly

\boxed{324}

different ways.

Label the bottom base  
A₁,A₂,…,A₁₀  in counter-clockwise order in the plane z = 0 and the top base  
B₁,B₂,…,B₁₀  directly above them in the plane z = 1.  
Let a plane be given by  

  ax + by + cz + d = 0 ,  c ≠ 0 if the plane is not vertical.

For every i (1 ≤ i ≤ 10) put  

  fᵢ = ax(Aᵢ)+by(Aᵢ)+d .

Then  
  Aᵢ is on the positive side  ⇔  fᵢ > 0 ,  
  Bᵢ is on the positive side  ⇔  fᵢ + c > 0 .

Take c > 0 (the case c < 0 will give exactly the complements of the sets obtained with c > 0, and complements are counted separately).  
For a fixed i three situations are possible

0.  fᵢ < −c   (Aᵢ,Bᵢ both negative)  
1. −c < fᵢ < 0  (Aᵢ negative, Bᵢ positive)  
2.  fᵢ > 0   (Aᵢ,Bᵢ both positive)

Because fᵢ is a linear function, as one goes once around the regular decagon the values change monotonically with respect to the two parallel lines f = 0 and f = –c; therefore along the cyclic order of indices the ten vertical edges appear in the consecutive pattern

  0 … 0   1 … 1   2 … 2 ,

i.e. a block of type 0, followed by a block of type 1, followed by a block of type 2 (any of the three blocks is allowed to be empty).

Choose a starting vertex of the decagon (10 choices) and let

n₀ = length of the 0-block, n₁ = length of the 1-block, n₂ = length of the 2-block, n₀+n₁+n₂ = 10 .

The number of ordered triples (n₀,n₁,n₂) of non–negative integers with sum 10 is
C(10+2,2) = C(12,2) = 66.

Hence there are 10·66 = 660 different labelled patterns.  
The three uniform patterns

 all 0 (n₀=10), all 1 (n₁=10), all 2 (n₂=10)

are each produced 10 times (one for every choice of the starting vertex) but correspond to only one subset of vertices, so they contribute 9 duplicate counts each.  
After removing these 27 duplicates we obtain

 N = 660 – 27 = 633

distinct subsets M that arise with c > 0.

Among them the 92 subsets with n₁ = 0 (no split pairs; both vertices of every chosen vertical edge are on the same side) already contain their own complements, while the remaining 633 − 92 = 541 subsets do not.  
Adding the complements of these 541 subsets (obtained by taking c < 0) gives the total number of possible vertex sets

 Total = 633 + 541 = 1174.

Therefore the number of distinct sets M that can occur is

\boxed{1174}.

Label the 20 vertices of the right prism by  
A₁,A₂,…,A₁₀ (bottom regular decagon) and  
B₁,B₂,…,B₁₀ (the corresponding top vertices).

For any plane Π not passing through a vertex, let M be the set of vertices lying on one side of Π
(the other side contains the complementary set M̅).  
The problem asks for the number of different vertex–sets M that can occur.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A convenient description of the possible sets M
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write the indices 1,2,…,10 cyclically round the decagon.
Going round the prism only three mutually exclusive situations can occur for every index i:

0  : neither Aᵢ nor Bᵢ lies in M,  
1  : only Aᵢ lies in M,  
2  : only Bᵢ lies in M,  
3  : both Aᵢ and Bᵢ lie in M.

Because the function f(θ)=a cosθ+b sinθ+d that governs the signed distance of the vertical
edge (AᵢBᵢ) from Π is a sinusoid, it crosses the levels 0 and –1 at most twice in one revolution.
Hence, as we run round the indices, the four symbols 0,1,3 (or 0,2,3) can appear only in the
order

0 … 1 … 3 … 1 … 0           or          0 … 2 … 3 … 2 … 0.

Thus, for some contiguous arc I of indices (length k, 1≤k≤9)

• all indices outside I are of type 0;  
• inside I there is one contiguous sub-arc J (possibly empty or equal to I) of type 3;  
• the remaining part I\J is entirely of type 1 (if the “1–3–1” pattern is realised) or of
  type 2 (if the “2–3–2” pattern is realised).

So every set M has the following structure.

Type A   (pattern 1–3–1)  
Choose a contiguous arc I (length k, 1≤k≤9);  
choose a contiguous sub-arc J⊆I (length m, 0≤m≤k).  
For i∈I\J put Aᵢ∈M, Bᵢ∉M; for i∈J put both Aᵢ,Bᵢ∈M.

Type B   (pattern 2–3–2)  
Exactly the same choice of I and J, but now  
for i∈I\J put Bᵢ∈M, Aᵢ∉M; for i∈J put both Aᵢ,Bᵢ∈M.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Counting the Type A sets
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Fix a length k (1≤k≤9).

Number of different arcs I of length k on the decagon = 10.

Inside such an arc the number of contiguous sub-arcs J
(including the empty one and the whole I) is

1  (empty)   +   the k(k+1)/2 non-empty sub-arcs  
= k(k+1)/2 + 1.

Hence, for this k the number of Type A sets is  
10 · ( k(k+1)/2 + 1 ).

Sum over k=1,…,9:

Σ_{k=1}^{9} ( k(k+1)/2 + 1 )  
= (1+3+6+10+15+21+28+36+45) + 9  
= 165 + 9 = 174.

Therefore
#(Type A) = 10 · 174 = 1740.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Counting the Type B sets
--------------------------------------------------------------------
By symmetry the same number is obtained:

#(Type B) = 1740.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Subsets counted twice
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A set is produced by both patterns only when J = I
(i.e. every index in I contributes both vertices, type 3).
Those are the subsets that consist of complete vertical pairs only.

For a convex decagon, a line (vertical plane) can separate t consecutive pairs
from the remaining 10–t pairs for any t = 1,…,9, and two directions give the same unordered partition.  
The number of such “full-pair” subsets is therefore

10·9/2 = 45.

Each of those 45 subsets has been counted once in Type A and once in Type B,
so they must be subtracted once.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Total number of distinct vertex sets M
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Total = #(Type A) + #(Type B) – (double counts)  
        = 1740 + 1740 – 45  
        = 3435.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{3435}

Step 1.  Remove the centre  
The centre of the big hexagon is a lattice point, but if it is chosen as a vertex the centre cannot lie strictly inside the triangle.  
Hence we work with  

 n = total lattice points – the centre  
   = (3s² + 3s + 1) – 1  with s = 4  
   = (3·16 + 12 + 1) – 1 = 61 – 1 = 60.

Step 2.  Put the 60 points into 6 equal 60°‐sectors  
Because of the 60° rotational symmetry, each sector (sextant) contains exactly 10 points.  
A half–plane through the centre consists of 3 consecutive sextants, so each such half–plane contains 30 points.

Step 3.  Triangles that fail to contain the centre  
A triangle fails iff all of its vertices lie in some half–plane (i.e. in 3 consecutive sextants).

Let
A = triangles whose three vertices lie in a single sextant  
B = triangles whose vertices lie in exactly two consecutive sextants  
C = triangles whose vertices lie in three consecutive sextants (and in no smaller union).

Counts:

Single sextant: C(10,3)=120, so |A| = 6·120 = 720.  

Two consecutive sextants: C(20,3)=1140, subtract the 2·120 triangles from A that lie entirely in one sextant, giving 900 per pair, hence |B| = 6·900 = 5400.

Half–plane (3 sextants): C(30,3)=4060, hence Σ|half–plane| = 6·4060 = 24 360.

A triangle from A is counted 3 times in that last sum, one from B is counted twice, one from C once.  
Therefore

failing triangles  
= 24 360 – |B| – 2|A|  
= 24 360 – 5 400 – 1 440  
= 17 520.

Step 4.  Degenerate triangles not yet discarded  
All collinear triples are already among the 17 520 except the ones that lie on a line through the centre and use points on both sides of the centre; these have angular spread exactly 180°, so they were not removed.

Along each of the three axial directions the line contains 4 points on each side of the centre.  
For one axis:

total triples on the 8 non-central points = C(8,3)=56  
all-on-one-side triples = 2·C(4,3)=8  
so crossing-the-centre triples = 56 – 8 = 48.

With the three axes this gives 3·48 = 144 such degenerate triples.

Step 5.  Triangles with the centre strictly inside  
Total triples from the 60 points: C(60,3) = 34 220  
Subtract the 17 520 failing triples and then the 144 remaining degenerate ones:

34 220 – 17 520 – 144 = 16 556.

\boxed{16556}

Let O be the centre of the regular hexagon and let the six 60°–sectors determined by the six radii through the vertices of the hexagon be  
T1 ,T2 ,…,T6 (indices taken mod 6).  
Every lattice point different from O lies in exactly one of the Ti.  
Because the hexagon has side 4, each Ti is an equilateral triangle of side 4 whose lattice–point total is  

|Ti| = 1+2+3+4 = 10 .

Hence  
|S| = 1 + 6·10 = 61,              |S\{O}| = 60 .                  (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Triangles that DO NOT contain O
--------------------------------------------------------------------
O is inside a triangle iff its three vertices are not contained in any closed half–plane through O, i.e. they are not contained in any set of three consecutive sectors Ti, Ti+1, Ti+2.

Thus a triangle misses O iff its vertices lie in (and are **minimally** contained in) an arc of one, two, or three consecutive sectors.  
These three mutually–exclusive possibilities are counted below.

A.  All three vertices in one sector  
      6·C(10,3) = 6·120 = 720.

B.  Vertices in two consecutive sectors (both sectors used)  
      for one ordered pair (Ti ,Ti+1) :  
           C(10,2)·C(10,1) + C(10,1)·C(10,2) = 45·10·2 = 900.  
      Six such pairs ⇒ 6·900 = 5400.

C.  Vertices in three consecutive sectors (all three sectors used)  
      for one triple (Ti ,Ti+1 ,Ti+2) : 10·10·10 = 1000.  
      Six such triples ⇒ 6·1000 = 6000.

Hence

Nout = 720 + 5400 + 6000 = 12 120 .                       (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Triangles that CONTAIN O or have O on a side
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Total (non–degenerate) triangles with vertices in S

Ntot = C(61,3) = 35 990 .                                   (3)

Subtracting (2) gives

N★ = Ntot − Nout = 35 990 − 12 120 = 23 870.               (4)

N★ counts all triangles not of type A,B,C, i.e. every triangle
whose three vertices are spread over more than three consecutive
sectors.  All such triangles surround O except the ones that
contain an antipodal pair of points (p , −p); in those triangles O
lies on the side p(−p) and must be excluded.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Triangles that have an antipodal pair
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Fix a non-zero lattice vector v = (x,y,z) with x+y+z=0 and let  
M = max(|x|,|y|,|z|).

Along the line ℓ(v) through O in direction v the lattice points
inside the hexagon are
       t v  for t = −⌊4/M⌋,…,−1,0,1,…,⌊4/M⌋;

so

|ℓ(v)| = 2⌊4/M⌋ + 1 .                                       (5)

For each pair { t v , −t v } (t ≥ 1) the number of third vertices
that give a non-degenerate triangle is |S| − |ℓ(v)|.

All 60 non-zero points fall into 30 antipodal pairs, distributed as
follows (primitive means gcd = 1).

(a)  M = 1  : 3 axial directions, t = 1,2,3,4  ⇒  12 pairs  
     |ℓ| = 9 ⇒ 12·(61−9) = 12·52 = 624.

(b)  primitive M = 2  (directions (2,−1,−1) and rotations)  
     3 directions, t = 1,2 ⇒ 6 pairs  
     |ℓ| = 5 ⇒ 6·(61−5) = 6·56 = 336.

(c)  primitive M = 3  : 12 points on the radius-3 ring → 6 pairs  
     |ℓ| = 3 ⇒ 6·(61−3) = 6·58 = 348.

(d)  primitive M = 4  : 12 points on the radius-4 ring → 6 pairs  
     |ℓ| = 3 ⇒ 6·(61−3) = 6·58 = 348.

Total triangles that contain an antipodal pair

Napo = 624 + 336 + 348 + 348 = 1 656.                       (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Triangles that contain O strictly in their interior
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Subtract (6) from (4):

Ninside = N★ − Napo = 23 870 − 1 656 = 22 214.              (7)

Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{22214}

Step 1.  The lattice points  

Put the hexagon in an axial (cube-) coordinate system  
x+y+z=0 with integer x,y,z.  
The big hexagon of side 4 is the set |x|,|y|,|z|≤4.  
The number of lattice points is  

 N = 3·4·5 + 1 = 61.  

There is one point at the origin O (the centre),  
6·r points on each “ring’’ |x|,|y|,|z| = r (r = 1,2,3,4),  
so besides O we have 60 points.  
Call the set of those 60 points S₀ (= S\{O}).

Step 2.  Opposite–direction pairing of the 60 points  

For every P∈S₀ the opposite point −P is also in S₀,  
and both lie on the same line through O.  
Thus S₀ breaks up into 30 pairs {R,−R}.  
The number of points on a ray R and on its opposite −R is the same;  
write that common number as k_R (1≤k_R≤4).

Step 3.  A half–plane count  

Label the 60 points by their polar angle in (−π,π].  
For a point P let m(P) be the number of other points whose angle is
between the angle of P (inclusive) and that angle +π (exclusive).  
A triangle fails to contain O iff all its three vertices lie in such a
half–plane; when the angles are listed round the circle each triangle in
this situation is counted exactly once, namely with the vertex that
comes first in the circular order.

Fix a ray R.  
A half-plane that starts with direction R contains  

 • all k_R points on R except the chosen vertex P itself;  
 • for every other pair {S,−S} exactly one whole ray, hence exactly k_S
   points, because the two rays are separated by π.

For the 28 rays different from ±R the sum of the k-values is 60−2k_R,
so the half-plane contributes half of that, i.e. 30−k_R points.
Hence for every point P lying on the ray R

 m(P) = (k_R−1) + (30−k_R) = 29.

Crucially, m(P)=29 is the same for every one of the 60 points.

Step 4.  Triples lying in a half–plane  

For each of the 60 points P the number of unordered pairs among the
m(P)=29 followers is C(29,2)=406.  
Therefore the number H of triples of points of S₀ that
lie in some open half-plane through O is  

 H = 60 · 406 = 24 360.

Step 5.  Total non-degenerate triples of S₀  

Total triples not using O:

 C(60,3) = 60·59·58 / 6 = 34 220.

Collinear triples are automatically contained in a half-plane, so they
are already included in H and need no separate subtraction.

Step 6.  Triangles that contain the centre  

A triangle formed with vertices in S₀ contains O in its interior
iff it is not counted in H.  Hence

 T = 34 220 − 24 360 = 9 860.

Step 7.  Triangles using the centre as a vertex  

If O itself is chosen as one of the three vertices, the centre is on the
boundary, not in the interior, so such triples are not to be counted.
Therefore the answer is exactly T.

Final result  

\boxed{9860}

Put the centre of the hexagon at the origin O and describe every lattice vertex by its polar angle θ (0 ≤ θ<360°).  
There are

|S| = 3·4·5+1 = 61  

lattice points, one of which is O itself; the other N = 60 points come in 30 opposite pairs (P, –P).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Triangles whose vertices are not O  
(The only triangles that can contain O strictly in their interior are the ones whose three vertices are among the N=60 outer points.)

Total triples of outer points  
        T = C(60,3) = 34 220.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Triples that do NOT contain O  

A triangle fails to contain O exactly when its three vertices all lie in some open half-plane bounded by a line through O, i.e. when their polar angles all lie in an interval of length 180°.

Fix one outer vertex P and look at the 180° interval that starts at the ray OP and sweeps counter–clockwise.  

•  The point –P lies on the boundary at 180°, so it is not included.  
•  The remaining 58 points (other than P and –P) split into 29 opposite pairs, and exactly one point of every pair lies in the chosen half–plane.  
•  Along the ray OP itself there are three more points (distance 2,3,4) which are also in the half–plane.

Hence for every outer vertex P the half–plane contains

    k = 29 + 3 = 29  

(other) vertices.  (The calculation is identical for every P, so k is constant.)

With P fixed, the other two vertices of a triangle that lies in this half–plane can be chosen in C(k,2)=C(29,2)=406 ways.

Therefore the total number of triples of outer points that lie in some half–plane is

    N_out = 60 · 406 = 24 360.

(Each such triple is produced exactly once, by the half–plane whose initial ray contains its vertex with the smallest angle.)

All degenerate (collinear) triples are counted in N_out, so they need not be treated separately.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Triangles that DO contain O  

The desired number is obtained by subtracting the triples that miss O:

    N_in = T – N_out  
          = 34 220 – 24 360  
          = 9 860.

Answer

\boxed{9860}

Let the regular hexagon of side 4 be centred at the origin and be drawn on the standard triangular lattice.  
Its side 4 means that every lattice point whose “hex–distance’’ to the origin is ≤ 4 lies in (or on) the hexagon.  
Using the well–known formula for such a hexagonal cluster, the set  

S = { lattice points lying in / on the hexagon }  

contains  

	|S| = 3·4·(4+1)+1 = 60+1 = 61  

points – the origin and 60 other points situated on six concentric “rings’’ about the origin.

Because we are asked for triangles that contain the centre strictly in their interior, the centre itself cannot be one of the vertices, so every relevant triangle is formed by three of the 60 non-central points.  Denote those 60 points by P1 , … , P60 in counter–clockwise order around the origin.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Reformulating the question
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For a triple {Pi , Pj , Pk } the centre O is inside △PiPjPk  iff  the three points are **not** contained in any (closed) half–plane whose boundary passes through O.  
Thus

(number of desired triangles)
	=  (all non–degenerate triples of the 60 points)
	   – (triples that lie in some half-plane through O).        …(1)

No three of the 60 points are collinear unless they lie on the same line through the origin, and such triples are automatically excluded by the “strictly interior’’ requirement, so “non-degenerate’’ simply means “three different points’’.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Counting all triples
--------------------------------------------------------------------
There are        C(60,3) = 34 220                                        …(2)
different 3-point subsets of the 60 points.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  How many triples fit into a half-plane through O?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Label the 60 points P1 , … , P60 cyclically.  
For each i let ki be the number of other points that lie in the 180° angle
(from Pi, going counter–clockwise) whose vertex is O.
If two further points Pj , Pk both lie in that angle, the triple
{Pi , Pj , Pk } is contained in a half-plane through O, so the number of unwanted triples with “first’’ vertex Pi is C(ki,2).

Hence
        (unwanted triples) = Σ_{i=1}^{60} C(ki,2).                      …(3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Determining the ki

Because the 60 points are distributed with perfect 6–fold symmetry, the ki are the same for symmetric positions; nevertheless they are not all equal because of points situated exactly 180° from Pi.

Place the lattice so that the 0°, 60°, …, 300° rays are lattice directions.
Along each such ray there are exactly 4 lattice points (at distances 1,2,3,4).
Hence for every point that lies on one of the six main lattice rays there are
4 opposite points 180° away, whereas for every other point there is exactly
one opposite point.

Counting how often “180°’’ occurs:

• there are 6 rays,  
• on each ray there are 4 points on the “positive’’ side and 4 on the “negative’’,
so the number M of unordered opposite pairs is

        M = 6 · 4 · 4 / 2 = 48.

For any of the 60·59 ordered pairs (Pi , Pj ) (i≠j)
exactly one of the two directions Pi→Pj or Pj→Pi
is ≤180°, except when the two points are opposite, in which case **both**
directions are 180°.  Hence

        Σ ki  =  C(60,2)  +  M  =  1770 + 48  = 1818.                 …(4)

Thus the mean value of the ki is 1818 / 60 = 30.3.

A point on a main ray has 4 opposite points, so its k equals 30+?,
whereas every other point has k = 30.  One finds

        24 points (those on the 6 rays) have ki = 31,
        36 points have ki = 30.                                      …(5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Evaluating the Σ C(ki,2)

Using (5):

Σ C(ki,2) = 24·C(31,2) + 36·C(30,2)
          = 24·465   + 36·435
          = 11 160   + 15 660
          = 26 820.                                                 …(6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Putting everything together

From (1), (2) and (6):

(number of triangles containing O)
      = 34 220  –  26 820
      = 16 700.

Let O be the centre of the regular hexagon.  
The 61 lattice points in the hexagon consist of

• O itself,  
• 60 non-central points which lie on 36 distinct rays through O.

Along any ray the lattice points are equally spaced, and the numbers of points on a ray are

 4 points on each of the 6 axis directions (|a|=1),  
 2 points on each of 6 directions whose primitive vector has max-coordinate 2,  
 1 point on each of the remaining 24 directions.

Hence

 24 points lie on axis rays,  
 12 points lie on “k = 2’’ rays,  
 24 points lie on “k = 1’’ rays.

A triangle misses O iff its three vertices lie in some open half–plane bounded by a line through O.  
For every non-central point P let m(P) be the number of other lattice points that are swept out when a ray is rotated clockwise from P through an angle strictly less than 180°.  
Every triangle missing O is counted exactly once by selecting its “first’’ vertex P and observing that the other two vertices lie among those m(P) points.  
Thus the number of unwanted triangles is  

 K = ΣP C(m(P),2).

To find the m-values note the cyclic order of the 36 rays.  
Travelling clockwise from a vertex on an axis ray meets

 5 further rays of its own sextant,  
 all 6 rays of the next sextant,  
 the first 6 rays of the third sextant,

for a total of 17 rays.  Counting points on those 17 rays gives

 m = 26 for every point on an axis ray,  
 m = 28 for the 12 points on k = 2 rays,  
 m = 29 for the 24 points on k = 1 rays.

Hence  

 K = 24·C(26,2) + 12·C(28,2) + 24·C(29,2)  
    = 24·325 + 12·378 + 24·406

    = 22 080.

Step 1.  The lattice and the number of points

All such integer triples are the 61 lattice points of the hexagon.  
The origin (0,0,0) is the centre; the other 60 points occur in 30 antipodal pairs.

For each direction the lines are indexed by k = −4, … , 4 and contain

Thus the number of non-degenerate triangles is

Step 7.  Triangles that DO contain the centre
