Step 1.  Fundamental properties of the family {Aₙ}

Because  
|Aᵢ∩Aⱼ| = gcd(i ,j),  
setting i = j shows that  

 |Aₙ| = gcd(n ,n) = n for every n ≥ 1.   (1)

Moreover, if i divides j then gcd(i ,j)=i, so |Aᵢ∩Aⱼ| = |Aᵢ|, whence  

 i | j ⇒ Aᵢ ⊂ Aⱼ.   (2)

For each n write

 Bₙ = Aₙ \bigcup_{d|n,\,d<n} A_d

(the elements that first appear in Aₙ).  
Because of (1) and (2) these Bₙ are pairwise disjoint and

 |Bₙ| = |Aₙ| − Σ_{d|n, d<n}|B_d| = n − Σ_{d|n, d<n}|B_d|.   (3)

By Möbius inversion (3) gives |Bₙ| = φ(n), the Euler totient function.  
Thus a convenient concrete model is

 for every n, create φ(n) new elements, put them into Aₙ and
 into every A_k with n|k.               (4)

With this model every admissible family of sets is obtained by choosing
distinct positive integers to label the elements of every Bₙ.

Step 2.  The eight relevant groups

For the problem we only need the sets whose indices divide 250 = 2·5³:

 1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 125, 250.

For these indices the sizes of the “new-element’’ groups are

 φ(1)=1, φ(2)=1, φ(5)=4, φ(10)=4,
 φ(25)=20, φ(50)=20, φ(125)=100, φ(250)=100.  (5)

Hence altogether 250 distinct integers have to be assigned to
the eight groups in (5).

Denote the groups by

 G₁(1), G₂(1), G₃(4), G₄(4), G₅(20), G₆(20), G₇(100), G₈(100),

where the number in parentheses is the size |G_k|=φ(⋯).

Step 3.  What determines max A_d

With any ordering of G₁,…,G₈ let S_t be the total number of elements
placed up to and including the t-th group in that order.
For a divisor d, max A_d equals S_t where G_t
is the last group whose index divides d.

Thus the value of max A_d depends only on the relative order of
the groups that do or do not divide d.

Step 4.  Choosing the best order

Whenever two consecutive groups G_a (earlier) and G_b (later) are interchanged,
the change in the objective

 F = Σ_{d|250} max A_d

is

 Δ = |G_b|·(# sets containing G_a but not G_b)
     – |G_a|·(# sets containing G_b but not G_a).   (6)

If Δ>0, the original order is better; if Δ<0 the interchange is better.
Applying (6) to every pair of neighbours shows successively that

 G₁ « G₂ « G₃ « G₄ « G₅ « G₆ « G₇ « G₈

(where « means “must precede’’) minimises F.  Any adjacent interchange
in this chain gives Δ>0, hence this linear order is globally optimal.

Step 5.  Labelling the elements

Give the elements of G₁ the label 1, of G₂ the label 2,
and continue consecutively so that every earlier group receives smaller
labels than every later group.  The cumulative totals S_t are then

 S₁ = 1
 S₂ = 2
 S₃ = 6
 S₄ = 10
 S₅ = 30
 S₆ = 50
 S₇ = 150
 S₈ = 250.   (7)

Step 6.  The eight maxima

Using (7):

A₁ : max = 1  
A₂ : max = 2  
A₅ : max = 6  
A₁₀ : max = 10  
A₂₅ : max = 30  
A₅₀ : max = 50  
A₁₂₅ : max = 150  
A₂₅₀ : max = 250.

Step 7.  Minimum possible sum

Adding,

 1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Because every adjacent interchange of groups increases the total,
no other assignment can produce a smaller value; hence 499 is minimal.

\boxed{499}

Let the universe of elements be partitioned into pairwise–disjoint blocks  

  B₁ , B₂ , B₃ , …  

and put every element of the block B_d into the sets A_n whose indices are multiples of d:
 A_n = ⋃_{d | n} B_d .

Then  
|A_i ∩ A_j| = Σ_{d|gcd(i,j)} |B_d| .  
To satisfy |A_i ∩ A_j| = gcd(i,j) for all i , j we need the arithmetic function  

f(d) = |B_d| , with Σ_{d|n} f(d) = n.

By Möbius inversion f = μ * id, hence f(d)=φ(d).  
Thus every block B_d must contain exactly φ(d) elements.

────────────────────────────────────────
The eight divisors of 250 = 2·5³ are

1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 125, 250,

and the corresponding block sizes are

|B₁|=1, |B₂|=1, |B₅|=4, |B₁₀|=4, |B₂₅|=20, |B₅₀|=20, |B₁₂₅|=100, |B₂₅₀|=100,
whose total is 250.

────────────────────────────────────────
Strategy for minimising Σ max A_d (d | 250)

1.  Put the smallest available numbers into the blocks that belong to the
    most sets, so that those sets get as small a maximum as possible;
    keep the numbers inside each block consecutive and as small as the
    disjointness condition allows.

2.  Proceed in the order  
    B₁, B₂, B₅, B₁₀, B₂₅, B₅₀, B₁₂₅, B₂₅₀,  
    assigning the first unused positive integers each time.

The explicit choice can be

• B₁  = {  1 }  
• B₂  = {  2 }  
• B₅  = {  3, 4, 5, 6 }  
• B₁₀ = {  7, 8, 9,10 }  
• B₂₅ = { 11,…,30 } (20 numbers)  
• B₅₀ = { 31,…,50 } (20 numbers)  
• B₁₂₅= { 51,…,150} (100 numbers)  
• B₂₅₀= {151,…,250} (100 numbers)

From these we have

A₁   max =  1  
A₂   max =  2  
A₅   max =  6  
A₁₀  max = 10  
A₂₅  max = 30  
A₅₀  max = 50  
A₁₂₅ max =150  
A₂₅₀ max =250.

────────────────────────────────────────
Why nothing smaller is possible

• |A₁|=1 ⇒ max A₁ ≥1.  
• A₂ contains 1 plus another new element ⇒ max A₂ ≥2.  
• A₅ needs four new elements distinct from 1 and the element of B₂; the
  smallest they can be force max A₅ ≥6.  
• With these ten distinct elements already used, A₁₀ still needs four more,
  so max A₁₀ ≥10.  
• After A₁₀ is formed, only 19 unused numbers remain below 30, but A₂₅
  needs 20 of them ⇒ max A₂₅ ≥30.  
• Similar counting shows max A₅₀ ≥50, max A₁₂₅ ≥150 and max A₂₅₀ ≥250.

Thus the list of maxima (1,2,6,10,30,50,150,250) is simultaneously minimal,
and the above construction attains it.

────────────────────────────────────────
Therefore the minimum possible value of  

  Σ_{d|250} max A_d = 1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250 = 499.

Step 1.  A useful construction  
Work with two primes only, 2 and 5, because 250 = 2·5³.  
For every non–negative integer a let  

C2(0) = {α₁} (size 1), C2(1) = {α₁ , α₂} (size 2)  
and for every non–negative b let  

C5(0) = {β₁}, C5(1) = {β₁ , … , β₅},  
C5(2) = {β₁ , … , β₂₅}, C5(3) = {β₁ , … , β₁₂₅}.  

Put the universe  
U = {(α_i , β_j ) : i ∈ {1,2}, j ∈ {1,…,125}} (250 ordered pairs).  

For any positive integer n write n = 2ᵃ5ᵇ (a = 0 or 1, 0 ≤ b ≤ 3) and define  

A_n = C2(a) × C5(b).

Then |A_n| = 2ᵃ5ᵇ = n and  
|A_i ∩ A_j| = 2^{min(a_i , a_j)} 5^{min(b_i , b_j)} = gcd(i , j),  
so the required condition is satisfied.

Step 2.  Giving the 250 elements the best possible labels 1,…,250  
Only the eight sets with n | 250 matter; their elements are the pairs with  
j ≤ 125 (all) and, for the sets whose 2–part is 1, also i = 2.

We list the 250 pairs in the following order.  
• First the ten pairs coming from the first five columns, in the pattern  
  R₁₁ , R₂₁ , R₁₂ , R₁₃ , R₁₄ , R₁₅ , R₂₂ , R₂₃ , R₂₄ , R₂₅  
  (R₁k means the pair with α₁ and β_k, R₂k the one with α₂ and β_k).  
• Then the remaining 20 pairs with i = 1 and j = 6,…,25 (indices 11…30).  
• Then the 20 pairs with i = 2 and j = 6,…,25 (indices 31…50).  
• Finally the 100 pairs with i = 1 and j = 26,…,125 (indices 51…150)  
  followed by the 75 remaining pairs (i = 2, j = 26,…,125) – indices 151…250.

Step 3.  The eight maxima

A₁  (size 1) max = 1  
A₂  (α =1, b =0) pairs (1, 2) max = 2  
A₅  (α =0, b =1) pairs 1,3,4,5,6 max = 6  
A₁₀ pairs of first five columns max = 10  
A₂₅ row α₁ , j ≤25 max = 30  
A₅₀ rows α₁,α₂ , j ≤25 max = 50  
A₁₂₅ row α₁ , j ≤125 max = 150  
A₂₅₀ all pairs max = 250

Step 4.  The value of the required sum  
1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Step 5.  Proving minimality  
• A₁ obviously needs element 1.  
• To have |A₂| = 2 we need two different integers, so max A₂ ≥ 2.  
• Because max A₂ = 2 occupies index 2, the fifth element of A₅ cannot be
  earlier than index 6, so max A₅ ≥ 6.  
• With six elements already used, the 10 elements of A₁₀ force max A₁₀ ≥ 10.  
• The 25 elements of A₂₅ must avoid the 5 indices already taken by α₂,
  therefore max A₂₅ ≥ 30.  
• A₅₀ contains 50 distinct elements, hence its maximum is at least 50.  
• Because the 25 elements with i = 2 (needed by A₅₀) occupy
  25 of the first 50 indices, the 125 row-1 elements cannot finish before
  index 150, so max A₁₂₅ ≥ 150.  
• Obviously max A₂₅₀ ≥ 250.

Thus any family of sets satisfies
Σ_{d|250} max A_d ≥ 1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250 = 499,
and the construction above attains this bound.

Therefore the minimum possible value of
∑_{d | 250} max A_d is

\boxed{499}.

Step 1.  A convenient way to produce sets with  
|A_i| = i and |A_i∩A_j| = gcd(i ,j)

For every positive integer n let  
B_n be a collection of φ(n) distinct symbols and put each symbol of B_n in the
sets A_k with n | k.
Because  
|A_k| = Σ_{n|k} φ(n) = k and  
|A_i∩A_j| = Σ_{n|gcd(i ,j)} φ(n) = gcd(i ,j),  
this construction works, and (because of the inclusion A_d ⊂ A_m whenever d|m) every family (A_i) that satisfies the condition can be obtained this way.  

For the divisors of 250 (=2·5³) we have

n : 1   2   5   10   25   50   125   250  
φ(n):1   1   4    4    20    20    100    100

Thus, inside the eight sets we are interested in, the elements that can occur are exactly the 250 symbols contained in the eight groups B_n (n|250) listed above.

Step 2.  A lower bound for Σ_{d|250} max A_d  

Because A_250 contains all 250 symbols we always have   C_250 := max A_250 ≥ 250.

Consider the pair {A_1}.  It contains only the single element of B_1, so necessarily  
C_1 = 1.

Consider the pair {A_2 , A_5}.  
Either the element of B_2 is placed before the four elements of B_5 or after them.
• If it is placed first, A_2 is finished at time 2 but A_5 cannot be completed before time 6.  
• If it is placed after the four B_5–elements, A_5 finishes at time 5 but A_2 not before time 6.  
Thus C_2 + C_5 ≥ 8.

Now the pair {A_10 , A_25}.  
To finish A_10 we must place the four symbols of B_10, to finish A_25 the twenty
symbols of B_25.  Whichever group is scheduled last delays its own set by the size
of that group plus the six common earlier symbols (B_1 , B_2 , B_5).  A simple count shows that the least possible value is obtained when the four B_10 symbols are scheduled before the twenty B_25 symbols; hence C_10 + C_25 ≥ 40.

Finally the pair {A_50 , A_125}.  
All twenty symbols of B_50 are needed for A_50 and all one-hundred symbols of B_125 are needed for A_125.  
Placing the twenty B_50 symbols first makes A_50 finish at time 50 and A_125 at
time 150; any other order gives a larger total finishing time, so
C_50 + C_125 ≥ 200.

Adding the unavoidable contribution 250 coming from A_250 we obtain the universal lower bound

C_1 + (C_2+C_5) + (C_10+C_25) + (C_50+C_125) + C_250
          ≥ 1 + 8 + 40 + 200 + 250 = 499.               (1)

Step 3.  An order that attains the lower bound  

List the 250 symbols in the following order.

B_1 (1 symbol)  
B_2 (1 symbol)  
B_5 (4 symbols)  
B_10 (4 symbols)  
B_25 (20 symbols)  
B_50 (20 symbols)  
B_125 (100 symbols)  
B_250 (100 symbols)

In this sequence the eight sets finish at the moments

A_1 : 1  
A_2 : 2  
A_5 : 6  
A_10 : 10  
A_25 : 30  
A_50 : 50  
A_125 : 150  
A_250 : 250

The sum of these finishing times is exactly

1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Step 4.  Conclusion  

Inequality (1) shows that no family (A_i) can give a smaller value, and the order displayed in Step 3 shows that 499 can indeed be achieved.  Hence the minimum possible value of

Σ_{d|250} max A_d

is

\boxed{499}.

Construction of the sets  

For every positive integer n create a set Eₙ of φ(n) new, distinct positive integers and put each element of Eₙ into every A_k with n | k.  
(Thus the Eₙ’s are pairwise disjoint and an element of Eₙ is in A_k iff n divides k.)

Then for every k

 A_k = ⋃_{n|k} Eₙ              and              |A_k| = Σ_{n|k} φ(n) = k,

and for any i, j

 |A_i ∩ A_j| = Σ_{n|gcd(i,j)} φ(n) = gcd(i,j),

so the required gcd–intersection condition is satisfied.

Only the sets Eₙ with n dividing 250 affect the eight sets we care about; the others can be numbered later with larger integers and will not influence the maxima in A_d for d | 250.

Choose the labels for the eight relevant groups as follows:

E₁   : { 1 }            (φ(1)=1)  
E₂   : { 2 }            (φ(2)=1)  
E₅   : { 3,4,5,6 }      (φ(5)=4)  
E₁₀ : { 7,8,9,10 }     (φ(10)=4)  
E₂₅ : { 11,…,30 }      (φ(25)=20)  
E₅₀ : { 31,…,50 }      (φ(50)=20)  
E₁₂₅: { 51,…,150 }     (φ(125)=100)  
E₂₅₀: { 151,…,250 }    (φ(250)=100)

(Every integer 1–250 is used exactly once, so the labels are all distinct.)

Maxima of the eight required sets

A₁     = {1}                      ⇒ max A₁ = 1  
A₂     = {1,2}                    ⇒ max A₂ = 2  
A₅     = {1,3,4,5,6}              ⇒ max A₅ = 6  
A₁₀   = {1,…,10}                 ⇒ max A₁₀ = 10  
A₂₅   = {1,3,4,5,6,11,…,30}      ⇒ max A₂₅ = 30  
A₅₀   = {1,…,50}                 ⇒ max A₅₀ = 50  
A₁₂₅  = {1,3,4,5,6,11,…,30,51,…,150} ⇒ max A₁₂₅ = 150  
A₂₅₀  = {1,…,250}                ⇒ max A₂₅₀ = 250

Hence

∑_{d|250} max A_d  = 1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Minimality

•  max A₁ ≥ 1 and max A₂ ≥ 2 (size reasons).  
•  Because A₂ and A₅ intersect only in A₁, A₅ needs four elements other than the two in A₂, forcing max A₅ ≥ 6.  
•  Using similar counting arguments (how many numbers ≤m are unavailable to A_d) one sees that the least possible maxima for A₁₀, A₂₅, A₅₀, A₁₂₅ and A₂₅₀ are 10, 30, 50, 150 and 250 respectively.

Thus no construction can give a smaller maximum for any of the eight sets, so 499 is the minimum possible value.

Step 1.  A convenient model for the sets  
Give every positive integer n a “basic set’’ of ϕ(n) distinct elements.  
Put each of those elements into exactly the sets A_k with n | k.

With this construction  
|A_i| = Σ_{n|i} ϕ(n) = i,  
and therefore |A_i∩A_j| = Σ_{n|gcd(i,j)} ϕ(n) = gcd(i,j),  
so all the required conditions are satisfied.  
Conversely, those conditions force |A_i| = i, hence any valid family of
sets contains exactly the i elements that correspond to the divisors of i in
this model, merely with different labels.  Thus we may speak of “the element
for the divisor n of i’’ without loss of generality.

For the eight divisors of 250 we have

 d  1 2 5 10 25 50 125 250  
ϕ(d) 1 1 4  4  20  20  100  100

Altogether there are 250 elements, exactly those that lie in A₍₂₅₀₎.

Step 2.  Lower bounds for the eight maxima M_d = max A_d

• M₁ ≥ 1 (obvious).  
• M₂ ≥ 2 (two different elements are needed).  
• M₅ ≥ 6  
  (If max A₅ ≤5 then A₅ = {1,2,3,4,5}.  
   A₂ contains the “1–element’’ and another element, so A₂ would overlap
   A₅ in two elements, contradicting |A₂∩A₅| =1.)  
• M₁₀ ≥ 10 (10 different elements cannot all be ≤9).  
• M₂₅ ≥ 30  
  (Among the first 29 integers, five elements – the extra element of A₂ and
   the four elements of A₁₀ not in A₂₅ – are not in A₂₅.  
   Hence A₂₅ can supply at most 24 of its 25 elements from {1,…,29}.)  
• M₅₀ ≥ 50 (as above, 50 different elements need numbers up to at least 50).  
• M₁₂₅ ≥ 150  
  (Within 1,…,149 there are 1+4+20 = 25 elements that are not in A₁₂₅
   – the extra element of A₂, the four extra elements of A₁₀ and the
   20 elements of A₅₀.  Only 149-25 =124 places remain for the 125 elements of
   A₁₂₅, impossible.)  
• M₂₅₀ = 250 (A₂₅₀ contains every element).

Thus each maximum is bounded below as

M₁,M₂,M₅,M₁₀,M₂₅,M₅₀,M₁₂₅,M₂₅₀ ≥ 1,2,6,10,30,50,150,250.

Step 1.  A convenient model for the sets  
Put all 250-th complex roots of unity into one big set Ω.  
For every positive integer n let  

  A_n = { ζ ∈ Ω : ζ^n = 1 }.  

Because the n-th roots of unity form a cyclic group of order n,

 |A_i| = i and |A_i ∩ A_j| = gcd(i , j)

for all positive integers i , j.  
So the required intersection property is satisfied.  
(It remains only to attach distinct positive‐integer “labels’’ to the elements of Ω.)

Step 2.  How many new elements does each divisor contribute?  
For every divisor d of 250 let  

 B_d = { ζ ∈ Ω : the order of ζ is exactly d }.  

Then |B_d| = φ(d) and the sets B_d (d | 250) partition Ω.

Step 3.  Labelling strategy  
Process the divisors of 250 in increasing order

 1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 125, 250,

and give the elements of B_d the smallest unused positive labels when d is being processed.

Step 4.  Minimal possible maximum for each A_d  
Suppose, while we are about to deal with a divisor d, the total number of labels already used is T.  
Among those T labels, precisely  

 Σ_{e | d, e processed earlier} φ(e)

belong to A_d.  
Therefore we still need  

 d – Σ_{e | d, e earlier} φ(e)

new labels for A_d, and they will be the next consecutive labels T+1, … , T + (required).  
Hence the largest label occurring in A_d (denoted M_d) is

 M_d = T + d – Σ_{e | d, e earlier} φ(e).        (★)

This value is forced: no other labelling can make the maximum smaller, because the earlier T
labels are fixed and at most the Σ part of them lie in A_d.

Step 5.  Compute the forced maxima successively  

 d = 1 : T = 0     M_1 = 0 + 1        = 1  
 d = 2 : T = 1     M_2 = 1 + (2 – 1) = 2  
 d = 5 : T = 2     M_5 = 2 + (5 – 1) = 6  
 d = 10 : T = 6     M_10 = 6 + (10 – 6) = 10  
 d = 25 : T = 10    M_25 = 10 + (25 – 5) = 30  
 d = 50 : T = 30    M_50 = 30 + (50 – 30) = 50  
 d = 125 : T = 50   M_125 = 50 + (125 – 25) = 150  
 d = 250 : T = 150  M_250 = 150 + (250 – 150) = 250  

Step 6.  The required sum  
Σ_{d | 250} M_d  
  = 1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250  
  = 499.

Step 7.  Realisation  
The labelling procedure described in Step 3 achieves exactly these maxima, so the lower
bounds in (★) are attainable.

Consequently the minimum possible value of  
 Σ_{d | 250} max A_d  
is

\boxed{499}.

Step 1.  A convenient description of the family  

For every positive integer g let Sg be a set, put  

 Ag  =  ⋃g|n  Sg .                             (1)

If the Sg are pairwise disjoint then  
|Ai| = Σg|i |Sg| , |Ai∩Aj| = Σg|gcd(i,j) |Sg| .   

Thus the condition |Ai∩Aj| = gcd(i , j) will hold once  

 Σg|n |Sg| = n                                  (2)

is satisfied for every n.  
By Möbius inversion the unique solution of (2) is |Sg| = φ(g), Euler’s totient.  
Hence we may (and will) assume that  

 |S1|=1, |S2|=1, |S5|=4, |S10|=4, |S25|=20,  
 |S50|=20, |S125|=100, |S250|=100,             (3)

and that the eight sets Sg (g|250) are pairwise disjoint.

Step 2.  What has to be minimised  

For d|250 we have Ad = ⋃g|d  Sg .  
Let Md be the greatest element of Ad ; then the required sum is Σd|250 Md.  

Because the Sg are disjoint, Ad contains exactly d elements.  
Write  
 T(d)= {g|250 : g∤ d}.      
The d elements of Ad must be chosen among the first Md positive integers,  
but the Σg∈T(d) φ(g) elements that lie in the Sg with g∈T(d) are forbidden.  
Hence  

 Md is the least integer N for which  
  N − Σg∈T(d) φ(g)  ≥  d.                     (4)

Step 3.  Computing the eight minima  

Keep placing the elements of the eight sets Sg in increasing order, always using  
the smallest still–unused numbers; this attains the minimum in (4) for every d.  
The placements and resulting maxima are:

• S1 needs 1 element → {1}            M1 = 1  
• S2 needs 1 element → {2}            M2 = 2  
• S5 needs 4 elements → {3,4,5,6}     M5 = 6  
• S10 needs 4 elements → {7,8,9,10}   M10 = 10  
• S25 needs 20 elements → {11,…,30}   M25 = 30  
• S50 needs 20 elements → {31,…,50}   M50 = 50  
• S125 needs 100 elements → {51,…,150}  M125 = 150  
• S250 needs 100 elements → {151,…,250} M250 = 250  

To see that each Md is indeed minimal, apply (4):  

– For d=1,2 the bound is obvious.  
– For d=5 the number 2 is forbidden, so at least six numbers are needed.  
– For d=10 nothing else is forbidden inside {1,…,10}, so M10=10.  
– For d=25 the five forbidden numbers 2,7,8,9,10 force M25≥30.  
– For d=50 we already have 30 admissible numbers; 20 further ones are needed, so M50≥50.  
– For d=125 the 25 forbidden numbers ≤149 force M125≥150.  
– For d=250 the 150 allowed numbers ≤250 suffice, so M250=250.  

Thus the eight minima are

 M1=1, M2=2, M5=6, M10=10, M25=30, M50=50, M125=150, M250=250.

Step 4.  The minimal total  

Σd|250   Md = 1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Therefore the smallest possible value of  
 Σ_{d|250} max Ad is 

\boxed{499}.

Let the three congruent circles be 𝛾₁ , 𝛾₂ , 𝛾₃ , each of radius  

r = 2, and let their centres be P₁ , P₂ , P₃.  
Since they are tangent internally to Ω( O, 11 ),  

OP₁ = OP₂ = OP₃ = 11 – 2 = 9.

Put θ for the (smaller) central angle ∠P₁OP₂ between two centres;  
set

c = cos θ , s = sin θ , A = 1 – c.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The chord length that a tangent line determines
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Take the external common tangent to 𝛾₁ and 𝛾₂ (the line touches both
circles on the same side of the line).  
Give the line the unit-normal vector ​n
so that the two tangency conditions are

n·P₁ – c₀ = r,  n·P₂ – c₀ = r.                     (1)

Subtracting gives n·(P₁ – P₂) = 0, i.e. n ⟂ (P₁P₂).  
Choosing the particular perpendicular

n = (sin θ , 1 – cos θ)/‖(sin θ , 1 – cos θ)‖
    = (s , A)/√(s² + A²) = (s , A)/√(2A)

makes n a unit vector.

Hence

n·P₁ = 9s /√(2A)      and      c₀ = n·P₁ – r.

The distance from O to the line is therefore

D = |c₀| = |9s /√(2A) – 2|.                         (2)

Because the line cuts Ω, the chord it forms has length

L = 2√[11² – D²] = 2√(121 – D²).                   (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Using the given chord length 4√30
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For two of the chords L = 4√30, so with (3),

121 – D² = 120  ⇒  D = 1.                          (4)

Equation (2) with |…| = 1 gives two possibilities

9s /√(2A) – 2 = 1  or  9s /√(2A) – 2 = –1,

i.e.

9s /√(2A) = 3  or  9s /√(2A) = 1.                 (5)

But

s²/(2A) = (1 – c²)/(2(1 – c)) = (1 + c)/2,

so (5) is equivalent to

(1 + c)/2 = (1/3)²  or  (1 + c)/2 = (1/9)²,

giving

cos θ = –7/9                 or                cos θ = –79/81.   (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Which of the two angles occurs?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let B = 9s /√(2A).  
If cos θ = –7/9 then B = 3 (> 2), whence c₀ = B – 2 = +1 and
O lies on the side of the line opposite the two small circles.

If cos θ = –79/81 then B = 1 (< 2), whence c₀ = –1 and
O is on the same side of the line as the two circles,
so O would lie outside the triangle of chords.

Since the problem states that O is inside that triangle,
only the first possibility is admissible:

cos θ = –7/9,  θ ≈ 141.79°.                       (7)

Thus two of the three centre–to–centre angles are θ.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The third centre angle
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let the three consecutive centre angles about O be θ, θ, φ.
Because they add to 360°,

φ = 360° – 2θ ⇒ cos φ = cos 2θ = 2cos²θ – 1
                = 2(49/81) – 1 = 17/81.          (8)

Hence

Aφ = 1 – cos φ = 64/81,  sφ = sin φ = 56√2 /81.  (9)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  The remaining chord
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For φ we have, from (2),

Bφ = 9 sφ /√(2Aφ)
    = (9·56√2 /81)/(√(128/81))
    = (56√2 /9)/(8√2 /9) = 7,

so  Dφ = |7 – 2| = 5.

Insert this in (3):

L₃ = 2√(11² – 5²) = 2√(121 – 25)
    = 2√96 = 2·4√6 = 8√6.                         (10)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The length of the third chord is

\boxed{8\sqrt6}.

Let  

• Ω be the circle with centre O and radius R = 11  
• A , B , C be the centres of the three congruent inner circles (radius r = 2)  

Since each small circle is tangent to Ω,  
OA = OB = OC = R – r = 9.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Distance from O to the chord that is tangent to two circles  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For any two of the little circles (centres P and Q) let θ be the acute
central angle ∠POQ ( 0 < θ < π ).  
Write

s = sin(θ/2), c = cos(θ/2), so s² + c² = 1.

The internal common tangent to the two circles is also a chord of Ω.
Using vector–geometry (let n be a unit normal to the chord) one finds

n · (P − Q) = 2 r , h = n · O = distance from O to the chord.

Solving these constraints gives

h(θ) = 9 c · √(1 − (2/(9 s))² )   (★)

and the chord length is

L(θ) = 2 √( R² − h(θ)² ).   (★★)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The two equal chords
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Two of the chords have the same length 4√30.
From (★★):

4√30 = 2 √(121 − h² )  ⇒  h = 1.

Set h = 1 in (★):

9 c · √(1 − (2/(9 s))² ) = 1.

With s² = t this becomes

81 (1−t) (1 − 4/(81t)) = 1
⇔ 81 t² − 84 t + 4 = 0
⇔ t = sin²(θ/2) = (14 ± 4√10)/27.

To make the three angles round O satisfy  θ + θ + θ₁ = 2π
with every consecutive angle < π, the larger root is required:

sin²(θ/2) = (14 + 4√10)/27.

Hence

s² = (14 + 4√10)/27, c² = (13 − 4√10)/27,
2t − 1 = 2s² − 1 = 1 + 8√10  27
so cos θ = −0.974…, and θ ≈ 165.6°.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The third angle
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let θ be the equal angles ∠AOB = ∠AOC; then

θ₁ = 2π − 2θ   and   θ₁/2 = π − θ.

Hence

sin(θ₁/2) = sin θ = 2 s c,
cos(θ₁/2) = −cos θ = 2s² − 1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Distance from O to the third chord
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Using (★) with θ → θ₁/2:

h₃ = 9 (2s²−1) √(1 − (1/(9 s c))² ).

With
s² = (14 + 4√10)/27, c² = (13 − 4√10)/27,

one obtains after simplification

h₃² = (463 − 130√10)/18.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Length of the third chord
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Finally,

L₃ = 2 √( R² − h₃² )
    = 2 √( 121 − (463 − 130√10)/18 )
    = 2 √( (1715 + 130√10)/18 ).

Because 1715 + 130√10 = (5 + 13√10)²,

L₃ = 2 · 1/(3√2) · (5 + 13√10)
    = (5 + 13√10)√2 / 3
    = (5√2 + 26√5)/3.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{\dfrac{5\sqrt2 + 26\sqrt5}{3}}

Let the large circle Ω have centre O and radius R = 11.  
Each small circle has radius r = 2, so the centres A, B, C of the three small circles lie on the circle with centre O and radius 11−2 = 9:
|A| = |B| = |C| = 9.   (1)

For every pair of centres, the chord of Ω that is tangent to both corresponding circles is an external common tangent to those two equal circles.  
Hence, for the pair (A,B) there is a unit normal vector n₁ such that

n₁·x = h₁                         (line ℓ₁ , the chord AB)  (2)
with  
distance(O,ℓ₁) = |h₁| = 1    (given chord length 4√30)  (3)  
and, because ℓ₁ is tangent to both circles of radius 2,

n₁·A = n₁·B = h₁ + 2 = 3.  (4)

Choose coordinates so that n₁ = (1,0); then ℓ₁ is the vertical line x = 1, and from (4)

A = (3, −6√2),  B = (3, 6√2).        (5)

(The two possible signs for the y–coordinate are chosen so that O lies inside the triangle formed by the three chords.)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––

Chord AC  
Let n₂ = (cos θ, sin θ) be the unit normal to the chord through A and C.  
Conditions analogous to (3)–(4) give

n₂·A = 3                                        (6)  
cos θ − 2√2 sin θ = 1    (from (5) and (6))  (7)

Solving (7) yields sin θ = − 4√2 / 9 , cos θ = −7/9,  
so

n₂ = ( −7/9 , −4√2 / 9 ),     t₂ = ( 4√2 / 9 , −7/9 ) (perpendicular to n₂).   (8)

Since n₂ ⟂ (C−A), write C = A + k t₂.  
Because |C| = 9, substituting in |C|² = 81 gives

k² + 2k (A·t₂) = 0 ⇒ k = −2(A·t₂) = −12√2.             (9)

From (5), (8) and (9)

C = ( 3 − 32/3 ,   −6√2 + 28√2/3 ) = ( −23/3 , 10√2 / 3 ).    (10)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––

Chord BC  
Vector v = C − B = ( −32/3 , −8√2 / 3 ).  
A unit normal to BC is

n₃ = v⊥ / |v⊥| = ( −1/3 , 4/(3√2) )  (where v⊥ = (v_y , −v_x)).    (11)

Check that n₃·B = n₃·C:

n₃·B = (−1/3)(3) + (4/(3√2))(6√2) = −1 + 8 = 7  
n₃·C = (−1/3)(−23/3) + (4/(3√2))(10√2/3) = 7.                (12)

Thus the chord BC (line ℓ₃) is

n₃·x = h₃ ,  with h₃ = n₃·B − 2 = 7 − 2 = 5.                (13)

The perpendicular distance from O to ℓ₃ is therefore d₃ = h₃ = 5.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––

Length of the chord BC  
For a chord at distance d from the centre of a circle of radius R, its length is  
L = 2√(R² − d²).

Hence

L₃ = 2√(11² − 5²) = 2√(121 − 25) = 2√96 = 8√6.               (14)

Therefore, the length of the third chord is

\boxed{8\sqrt{6}}

Let  

• Ω be the circle with centre O and radius R = 11.  
• A, B, C be the centres of the three small circles (each of radius r = 2).  
  Because every small circle is internally tangent to Ω, OA = OB = OC = R – r = 9,  
  so A, B, C lie on the circle γ with centre O and radius 9.

For every pair of small circles there is an external common tangent that meets Ω in a
chord; these three tangents form the sides of a triangle that contains O.  
Call the tangent to the pair (A,B) the line ℓ_AB, etc.

--------------------------------------------------
1.  Determining ℓ_AB

Let us place coordinates so that  
O = (0,0), A = (−4√5 , 1), B = ( 4√5 , 1).

Indeed |A| = |B| = √( (4√5)^2 + 1^2 ) = √(80 + 1) = 9.

For equal radii the external common tangent is parallel to AB and 2 units from it.
AB is the line y = 1, hence the nearer external tangent is

  ℓ_AB : y = −1.

Its distance from O is 1, so the chord cut off on Ω has length

 L_AB = 2√(R² − 1²) = 2√(121−1) = 2√120 = 4√30.

Thus ℓ_AB is one of the given chords.

--------------------------------------------------
2.  Finding the position of C using the second chord of length 4√30

The second chord of length 4√30 is tangent to the circles at A and C,
so the line ℓ_AC must also be 1 unit from O.
Let p be the distance from O to the line AC (joining the centres).
Because ℓ_AC is 2 units from AC, |p − 2| = 1, whence p = 1 or 3.
The case p = 1 would put AC parallel to ℓ_AB and gives C = B, which is impossible,
so p = 3.

Let C = (x, y) (with x² + y² = 81) and impose “distance from O to AC equals 3”.
Using the determinant formula for the distance from the origin to a line,

 |A_x y − A_y x| / |AC| = 3,

i.e.

 |−4√5 y − x| = 3√( (x + 4√5)² + (y − 1)² ). (∗)

Together with x² + y² = 81, equation (∗) yields (after clearing radicals
and simplifying)

 81y⁴ − 4y³ − 474y² + 636y − 239 = 0.

Factoring gives (y − 1)³ (81y + 239) = 0, and y ≠ 1, so

 y = −239/81.

Substituting this y back into (∗) gives

 x = 1540/(81√5).

Therefore

 C = ( 1540 /(81√5), −239/81 ).

--------------------------------------------------
3.  The third tangent ℓ_BC and its distance from O

Vectors OB and OC allow us to find the distance p_BC from O to BC:

 p_BC = |B_x C_y − B_y C_x| / |BC| = 79/9.

The two lines parallel to BC and tangent to the two equal circles are
2 units on either side of BC; the nearer one to O is therefore

 h = p_BC − 2 = 79/9 − 18/9 = 61/9

units from O.  
That line is ℓ_BC, the third side of the triangle.

--------------------------------------------------
4.  Length of the third chord

The length L of a chord of Ω at distance h from O is

 L = 2√(R² − h²).

With R = 11 and h = 61/9,

 h² = (61/9)² = 3721/81,  
 R² − h² = 121 − 3721/81 = 6080/81.

Hence

 L_BC = 2√(6080/81) = (2/9)√6080
    = (2/9)·4√380 = (8/9)·2√95
    = 16√95 / 9.

--------------------------------------------------
Answer

The length of the third chord is  

\boxed{\dfrac{16\sqrt{95}}{9}}

Let  

• Ω be the circle with centre O and radius R = 11  
• r   = 2 be the radius of each of the three congruent interior circles  
• A, B, C be the centres of the three small circles (so OA = OB = OC = 11 − 2 = 9)

For every pair of small circles the side of the triangle is the common external tangent to the two circles; hence that side is

1. parallel to the segment joining the two centres, and  
2. exactly r = 2 units nearer to O than the line through the two centres.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The two equal sides
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The two equal chords have length  

L = 4√30 ⇒ half–length = 2√30.

For a chord in Ω the relation between its half–length ℓ and its distance d from O is  
ℓ² + d² = R² = 11² = 121.

Thus for the equal sides  
d = √(121 − (2√30)²) = √(121 − 120) = 1.

Hence the distance from O to each of the two corresponding lines through centres is

d(O, line AB) = d + r = 1 + 2 = 3.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Central angle between A and B
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For points A, B on the circle OA = OB = 9, the distance from O to the chord AB is

d(O, AB) = 9 cos(θ/2),   θ = ∠AOB.

Therefore  
9 cos(θ/2) = 3  ⇒  cos(θ/2) = 1/3.

Let θ₁ = ∠AOB; then θ₁/2 = arccos(1/3).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Disposition of the three centres
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because the two equal chords correspond to pairs (A,B) and (B,C), we have  
∠AOB = ∠BOC = θ₁.

With the three centres arranged around O,

θ₁ + θ₁ + θ₂ = 360°  ⇒  θ₂ = 360° − 2θ₁.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Distance from O to AC
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For the third pair (A,C),

d(O, AC) = 9 cos(θ₂/2)
          = 9 cos[(360° − 2θ₁)/2]
          = 9 cos(180° − θ₁)
          = 9(−cos θ₁).

Using cos θ₁ = 2 cos²(θ₁/2) − 1 = 2(1/3)² − 1 = 2/9 − 1 = −7/9,

d(O, AC) = 9·(7/9) = 7.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Distance from O to the third chord
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The side tangent to the circles centred at A and C lies r = 2 units nearer to O than line AC, so

d₃ = d(O, AC) − r = 7 − 2 = 5.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Length of the third chord
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Finally,

(L₃/2)² + d₃² = 121
⇒ (L₃/2)² = 121 − 25 = 96
⇒ L₃ = 2·√96 = 2·4√6 = 8√6.

\boxed{8\sqrt{6}}

Let  

• Ω be the circle with centre O and radius R = 11,  
• C₁ , C₂ , C₃ the centres of the three congruent inner circles (radius r = 2).

Because every inner circle is tangent to Ω, each Cᵢ is 11 – 2 = 9 units from O; hence C₁ , C₂ , C₃ lie on the circle |OC| = 9.

------------------------------------------------
1.  Distance from O to the line through two centres  
------------------------------------------------
For two points U, V on |OU| = |OV| = 9 with central–angle Δ = ∠UOV,

distance from O to the line UV is

d = |OU × OV| / |U – V|
  = (9·9 |sinΔ|) / (9·2|sin(Δ/2)|)
  = 9 |cos(Δ/2)|.                (1)

------------------------------------------------
2.  Relating a chord length to that distance  
------------------------------------------------
The chord of Ω tangent to the two inner circles whose centres are U and V is parallel to UV and is displaced 2 units toward O (the nearer of the two exterior tangents).  
Thus the perpendicular distance from O to the chord is

s = d – 2   (because d > 2 here).                (2)

Its length is

L = 2√(R² – s²) = 2√(11² – s²).                  (3)

------------------------------------------------
3.  Using the given equal chords  
------------------------------------------------
Two of the chords have length 4√30.  
From (3):

4√30 = 2√(121 – s²)  ⇒  121 – s² = 120  ⇒  s = 1.

With (2):  s = d – 2 = 1  ⇒  d = 3.

Insert d = 3 into (1):

3 = 9 |cos(Δ/2)|  ⇒  |cos(Δ/2)| = 1/3
 ⇒  Δ = 2 arccos(1/3).

Hence the angular separations about O are

Δ₁₂ = Δ₂₃ = 2 arccos(1/3).

Because the three angles around the full circle sum to 2π,

Δ₃₁ = 2π – 2Δ = 2π – 4 arccos(1/3).

------------------------------------------------
4.  Distance for the remaining pair  
------------------------------------------------
Let θ = arccos(1/3).  Then Δ = 2θ and

Δ₃₁/2 = π – Δ/2 = π – θ – θ = π – 2θ.

So (1) gives for the remaining pair (C₃ , C₁):

d₃₁ = 9 |cos(Δ₃₁/2)|
     = 9 |cos(π – 2θ)|
     = 9 |–cos(2θ)|
     = 9·(2cos²θ – 1)
     = 9·(2·(1/3)² – 1)
     = 9·(2/9 – 1)
     = 9·(–7/9) = 7.                         (4)

------------------------------------------------
5.  Length of the third chord  
------------------------------------------------
Using (2) and (3) again:

s = d₃₁ – 2 = 7 – 2 = 5,

L = 2√(11² – 5²)
  = 2√(121 – 25)
  = 2√96
  = 2·4√6
  = 8√6.

------------------------------------------------
6.  Result  
------------------------------------------------
The length of the third chord is

\boxed{8\sqrt{6}}

Let  

•  Ω be the circle with centre O and radius 11.  
•  A, B, C be the centres of the three small circles (each radius 2).

Because every small circle is internally tangent to Ω,  

OA = OB = OC = 11 − 2 = 9,

so A, B, C all lie on the circle of radius 9 centred at O.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Locating A, B, C

Put O at the origin and let the points be

A = (9,0), B = (9 cos φ, 9 sin φ), C = (9 cos φ, −9 sin φ)

so that AB = AC (the configuration will then give two equal chords).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The chords and their distances from O

For any pair of circles, the chord of Ω that is tangent to both circles is parallel to the segment joining their centres and is r = 2 units closer to O than that segment (otherwise O would lie outside the triangle of chords).  
If h₀ is the distance from O to the side of ΔABC, the distance h from O to the corresponding chord is

h = h₀ − 2.                               (†)

The length of such a chord is

L = 2√(11² − h²).                       (‡)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The equal chords (those to AB and AC)

Distance from O to the line AB:

Area(OAB) = ½⋅OA⋅OB⋅sin φ = ½⋅AB⋅h₀  
⇒ h₀ = (81 sin φ)/(AB).

Since AB = 18 sin(φ/2) and sin φ = 2 sin(φ/2) cos(φ/2),

h₀ = 9 cos(φ/2).

Thus, using (†), h = 9 cos(φ/2) − 2.

We are told that the two equal chords have length 4√30.  Using (‡):

4√30 = 2√(121 − h²) ⇒ h² = 1 ⇒ |9 cos(φ/2) − 2| = 1.

Hence 9 cos(φ/2) − 2 = 1  (the other sign would give overlapping circles),
so

cos(φ/2) = 1/3  ⇒  sin(φ/2) = 2√2/3  ⇒  sin φ = 2 sin(φ/2) cos(φ/2) = 4√2/9.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The third chord (tangent to B and C)

Segment BC is vertical:

BC = 18 sin φ = 18·(4√2/9) = 8√2.

Its x-coordinate is 9 cos φ, and since

cos φ = 2 cos²(φ/2) − 1 = 2·(1/3)² − 1 = −7/9,

the line BC is x = −7, so h₀ (distance from O to BC) is 7.

Applying (†):

h = h₀ − 2 = 7 − 2 = 5.

Finally, by (‡) the length of the third chord is

L = 2√(11² − 5²) = 2√(121 − 25) = 2√96 = 8√6.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer:

\boxed{8\sqrt6}

Let  

• Ω be the circle with centre O and radius R = 11.  
• A , B , C be the centres of the three inner circles (all of radius r = 2).  
 Because the small circles are internally tangent to Ω,  

  OA = OB = OC = R – r = 9.                                               (1)  

• α , β , γ be the (counter-clockwise) arguments of OA, OB, OC.  
 Put  

  θ = ∠AOB ,        ( so |AB| = 18 sin (θ⁄2) )                  (2)

(The two equal chords will turn out to come from pairs (A, B) and (B, C), so
∠BOC = θ also, while ∠AOC = 360° – 2θ.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The length of a chord of Ω that is tangent to two small circles
--------------------------------------------------------------------

For any pair of centres P , Q with ∠POQ = φ (0° < φ ≤ 180°) let L(φ) be the
length of the chord of Ω that is tangent to the two circles centred at P and
Q and that lies closer to O (the “internal” common tangent).

The line l that realises this chord

• passes through the midpoint M of the segment PQ,  
• makes an angle with PQ whose tangent is  

  tan ψ = 2r /|PQ| = 4 /(18 sin (φ⁄2)),  

• hence the distance from O to l is  

  h(φ)= 9·sin φ ⁄√{ (18 sin (φ⁄2))² + 4² }                            (3)

(A short vector computation gives (3); the numerator is the area factor
|OM × PQ|, the denominator is |PQ cos ψ|.)

Because the chord is in Ω, its length is

  L(φ) = 2√{ R² – h(φ)² }.                                          (4)



-------------------------------------------------
2.  Fixing θ with the information on the equal chords
-------------------------------------------------

The two equal chords have length 4√30, so for φ = θ the length (4) equals
4√30.  Inserting R = 11 and r = 2 in (3) and (4) gives

 2√{121 – h(θ)²} = 4√30 ⇒ h(θ) = 1.                               (5)

Putting h(θ)=1 in (3) one obtains

 162 sin (θ⁄2) cos (θ⁄2) = √{324 sin²(θ⁄2)+16}.                      (6)

Writing s = sin²(θ⁄2) turns (6) into

 6561 s² – 6480 s + 4 = 0.                                          (7)

Equation (7) has two roots; the one compatible with two non–overlapping
circles is

5.  Express sin β and cos β from (5):

  ∠APC ≈ arccos 0.2756 ≈ 74°.

Step 2  Expressing the three equal lengths  

Using the law of cosines in the three small triangles we obtain  

BP² = 1 + p² − 2p cos20° …………………… (1)  
PQ² = p² + q² − 2pq cos46° ……………… (2)  
QC² = 1 + q² − 2q cos26° ………………… (3)

The hypothesis BP = PQ = QC means (1) = (2) = (3).  
Equating (1) and (2) and then (2) and (3) gives the system

q² − 2 cos46° p q + 2 cos20° p − 1 = 0        … (A)  
p² − 2 cos46° p q + 2 cos26° q − 1 = 0        … (B)

Solving (A)–(B) (routine elimination) yields  

p = 2 cos26° cos46° − 1 ≈ 0.581 and q = 1 − 2 cos20° cos46° ≈ 0.669.

In ΔAPC the angle between AP (20°) and AC (92°) is 72°, so

PC² = 1 + p² − 2p cos72°.                … (4)

Substituting p = 2 cos26° cos46° − 1 in (4) and simplifying with the identities  

cos72° = 2 cos²36° − 1, cos26° cos46° = (cos20°+cos72°)/2  

one finds  

PC = 2 sin20° cos26° ≈ 0.989.

Finally, in ΔAPC the cosine of the required angle θ = ∠APC is

cos θ = (AP² + PC² − AC²)/(2·AP·PC)  
       = (p² + PC² − 1)/(2p·PC).

Replacing p and PC by the expressions already obtained and using
standard trigonometric identities produces

cos θ = cos74°.

Hence θ = 74°.

Therefore

1.  Length AP  

Quadrilateral ABHP is cyclic.  
The inscribed angle subtending AB at H equals 180° − γ, so  
∠APB = 180° − γ.  
Hence in ΔABP  

∠A = α , ∠P = 180° − γ , ∠B = γ − α.

By the Law of Sines  

AP = AB · sin(γ − α)/sin γ = c·sin(γ − α)/sin γ = 4.     (1)

2.  Length AQ  

Similarly, A, C, H, Q are cyclic and  
∠AQC = 180° − β.  
In ΔACQ  

∠A = α , ∠Q = 180° − β , ∠C = β − α,

so  

AQ = AC · sin(β − α)/sin β = b·sin(β − α)/sin β = 5.     (2)

3.  Using the circum-radius R  

By the Law of Sines in ΔABC  

c = 2R sin γ , b = 2R sin β , a = 2R sin 2α = 7.

Relations (1) and (2) become  

4 = 2R sin(γ − α), 5 = 2R sin(β − α), R = 7/(2 sin 2α).     (3)

Eliminate R from (3):

4 sin 2α = 7 sin(γ − α),                        (4)  
5 sin 2α = 7 sin(β − α).                        (5)

Since β = 180° − γ − 2α, β − α = 180° − γ − 3α, and  
sin(β − α) = sin(γ + 3α).  
Dividing (5) by (4) gives  

5/4 = sin(γ + 3α) / sin(γ − α).                 (6)

Let t = γ − α.  
Then γ + 3α = t + 4α and (6) yields  

sin(t + 4α) = (5/4) sin t.                      (7)

Using (4): sin t = (4/7) sin 2α.  

Solve (7) with this relation (routine trigonometry gives)

sin 2α = √65 / (2√23), sin t = 2√65 / (7√23), cos t = √867 /(7√23).

In ΔABM (with M on the circumcircle ABC) the angle at M equals γ, so  

AM / sin(∠ABM) = AB / sin γ ⇒  
AM = c·sin(∠ABM)/sin γ = c·sin(α + γ)/sin γ.

Because c = 2R sin γ, we get  

AM = 2R sin(α + γ) = 7·sin(α + γ)/sin 2α.     (8)

Now  

sin(α + γ) = sin(2α + t)  
= sin 2α cos t + cos 2α sin t  
= (√65 /(2√23))·(√867 /(7√23)) + (3√3 /(2√23))·(2√65 /(7√23))  
= √195 /14.

Substituting this and sin 2α into (8):

AM = 7·(√195 /14) ÷ (√65 /(2√23))  
  = (7/14)·2·√195 √23 /√65  
  = √(195·23 /65) = √69.

\boxed{\sqrt{69}}

α = ∠BAC, θ = α/2, k = cos θ, s = sin θ, a = BC = 7,

-------------------------------------------------
1.  Distances from A to the three circles
-------------------------------------------------
For any circle through A with centre O and radius R, the second intersection of
the bisector with that circle is the point whose distance from A is  

t = 2 (AO projected on the bisector) = 2 Oₓ .

All three circles – (ABC), (ABH) and (ACH) – have the same radius R (because
R_{ABH}=R_{ACH}=R_{ABC}=R).  Hence

AP = 2 O₁ₓ, AQ = 2 O₂ₓ, AM = 2 Oₓ,               (1)

where O, O₁, O₂ are the centres of (ABC), (ABH), (ACH) respectively.

-------------------------------------------------
2.  The circum-centre O of △ABC
-------------------------------------------------
Since OA = OB = R and OC = R,

O·B = c²/2,  O·C = b²/2.

Solving,

O = (x, y) with  
x = (b + c)/(4k), y = (c − b)/(4s).

Hence (1) gives  

AM = 2x = (b + c)/(2k).                          (2)

-------------------------------------------------
3.  Centres O₁ and O₂ of (ABH) and (ACH)
-------------------------------------------------
Both O and O₁ lie on the perpendicular bisector of AB; with equal radii they
are the two points on that line at distance R from A, situated on opposite
sides of AB.  Writing the perpendicular bisector in parametric form and using
|O| = |O₁| = R one finds

O₁ₓ = [4c k² − (b + c)] / (4k),  
O₂ₓ = [4b k² − (b + c)] / (4k).

Using (1) and the data AP = 4, AQ = 5:

4 = (4c k² − (b + c))/(2k),  
5 = (4b k² − (b + c))/(2k).                       (3)

-------------------------------------------------
4.  Solving for b, c in terms of k
-------------------------------------------------
From (3):

8k = 4c k² − (b + c),  
10k = 4b k² − (b + c).

Subtracting gives (b − c) = 1/(2k);  
adding gives (b + c) = 9k /(2k² − 1).            (4)

-------------------------------------------------
5.  Using BC = 7
-------------------------------------------------
Length BC in the chosen coordinates is  

a² = (c − b)² k² + (b + c)² s²
    = (b − c)² k² + (b + c)² (1 − k²).

Insert (4) and a = 7:

49 = (1/4) + 81 k²(1 − k²)/(2k² − 1)².

Let t = k².  The equation becomes  

65 = 108 t(1 − t)/(2t − 1)².                     (5)

-------------------------------------------------
6.  Finding k²
-------------------------------------------------
(5) simplifies to  

t² − t + 65/368 = 0  ⇒  t = [1 ± √(27/92)]/2.

Since 2t − 1 must be positive (b + c > 0), take

t = k² = (1 + √(27/92))/2
      = 1/2 + (3√3)/(4√23).                      (6)

-------------------------------------------------
7.  Computing AM
-------------------------------------------------
From (2) and (4):

AM = (b + c)/(2k) = 9 / [2(2k² − 1)].

But 2k² − 1 = √(27/92) = 3√3 /(2√23), so

AM = 9 / ( 2·3√3 /(2√23) )
    = 9√23 /(3√3)
    = 3 √(23/3)
    = √69.

-------------------------------------------------
Final result
-------------------------------------------------
\boxed{\sqrt{69}}

Let the side–lengths opposite \(A,B,C\) be  
\(BC=a=7,\;CA=b,\;AB=c\) and denote \(\angle BAC = 2\alpha\;(\alpha>0)\).

1.  Put \(A\) at the origin and \(AB\) on the positive \(x\)-axis.  
    In this system the internal angle–bisector is the ray
    \[
    \ell: A+\lambda(\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha),\qquad\lambda>0 .
    \]
    For convenience write \(P,Q,M\) on \(\ell\) as  
    \(P\!(4\cos\alpha,4\sin\alpha),\;Q\!(5\cos\alpha,5\sin\alpha),\;
      M\!(m\cos\alpha,m\sin\alpha)\) where \(m=AM\) is the quantity sought.

2.  Circles through \(A\).

    Every circumcircle through \(A\) has equation
    \(x^{2}+y^{2}+Dx+Ey=0\).
    For the circumcircle \((ABC)\) we get \(D=-c\) and
    \(E=(c\cos2\alpha-b)/\sin2\alpha\).  Substituting the coordinates of
    \(M\) gives
    \[
      m+\bigl(D\cos\alpha+E\sin\alpha\bigr)=0 .
    \]
    A short computation shows
    \(D\cos\alpha+E\sin\alpha=-(b+c)/(2\cos\alpha)\), hence
    \[
    AM=m=\dfrac{b+c}{2\cos\alpha}. \tag{1}
    \]

3.  Use of the cyclic quadrilaterals \(ABHP\) and \(ACHQ\).

    Because \(ABHP\) is cyclic,  
    \(\angle APB=\angle AHB = 180^{\circ}-C\).
    In \(\triangle ABP\) we then have  
    \[
    \frac{c}{\sin C}=\frac{AP}{\sin(C-\alpha)}
      \Longrightarrow c=\frac{4\sin C}{\sin(C-\alpha)}. \tag{2}
    \]
    Similarly, from \(ACHQ\) being cyclic,
    \(\angle AQ C = 180^{\circ}-B\) and
    \[
    b=\frac{5\sin B}{\sin(B-\alpha)}. \tag{3}
    \]

4.  Introduce the circumradius \(R\).

    By the Law of Sines for \(\triangle ABC\)
    \[
       \frac{a}{\sin 2\alpha}= \frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}=2R,
       \qquad\text{so } b=K\sin B,\;c=K\sin C,
       \;K=\frac{7}{\sin2\alpha}. \tag{4}
    \]

5.  Equating (2)+(3) with (4) yields
    \[
      K=\frac{4}{\sin(C-\alpha)}=\frac{5}{\sin(B-\alpha)}. \tag{5}
    \]
    From (5) we obtain
    \[
      4\sin(B-\alpha)=5\sin(C-\alpha), \tag{6}
    \]
    \[
      4\sin2\alpha=7\sin(C-\alpha),\qquad
      5\sin2\alpha=7\sin(B-\alpha). \tag{7}
    \]

6.  Let \(x=B-\alpha,\;y=C-\alpha\).
    Then \(x+y=B+C-2\alpha=180^{\circ}-4\alpha\) and
    \[
      \sin x=\frac{5\sin2\alpha}{7},\qquad
      \sin y=\frac{4\sin2\alpha}{7}. \tag{8}
    \]
    With \(s=\sin2\alpha\) the identity
    \[
      2\sqrt{1-s^{2}}\sqrt{49-25s^{2}}=-1+10s^{2}
    \]
    obtained from (8) simplifies to  
    \(196-296s^{2}=1-20s^{2}\), giving
    \[
       s^{2}=\frac{65}{92},\qquad
       \sin2\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{65}{92}},\;
       \cos2\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{27}{92}}=\frac{3\sqrt3}{2\sqrt{23}}. \tag{9}
    \]

7.  Determining \(AM\).

    From (4) and the sum–to–product identity
    \(\sin B+\sin C=2\cos\alpha\cos\!\dfrac{B-C}{2}\),
    equation (1) becomes
    \[
       AM=\frac{7(\sin B+\sin C)}{2\cos\alpha\sin2\alpha}
          =\frac{7\cdot2\cos\alpha\cos\!\frac{B-C}{2}}
                 {2\cos\alpha\sin2\alpha}
          =\frac{7\cos\!\frac{B-C}{2}}{\sin2\alpha}. \tag{10}

    Using (8) again,
    \(\sin x+\sin y =\dfrac{9\sin2\alpha}{7}
      =2\sin\!\Bigl(\frac{x+y}{2}\Bigr)\cos\!\Bigl(\frac{x-y}{2}\Bigr)
      =2\cos2\alpha\cos\!\frac{B-C}{2}\).
    Hence
    \[
      \cos\!\frac{B-C}{2}=\frac{9\sin2\alpha}{14\cos2\alpha}.
    \]
    Substitute this and (9) into (10):
    \[
      AM=\frac{7}{\sin2\alpha}\cdot
          \frac{9\sin2\alpha}{14\cos2\alpha}
        =\frac{9}{2\cos2\alpha}
        =\frac{9}{2}\cdot\frac{2\sqrt{23}}{3\sqrt3}
        =\frac{3\sqrt{23}}{\sqrt3}=\sqrt{69}.
    \]

• the vertex A is at the origin O,  
• the internal bisector of ∠BAC is the positive x–axis,  
• ∠BAC = 2θ, so that AB makes an angle +θ and AC an angle −θ with the x–axis,  
• AB = c and AC = b.  

Thus  

B(cos θ , sin θ) with |AB| = c  
C(cos θ , −sin θ) with |AC| = b.  

Set  

a = BC = 7 , d₁ = c − b , d₂ = c + b .

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Coordinates of the orthocenter H  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A short trigonometric computation (using the fact that BH ⟂ AC and CH ⟂ AB) gives  

H_x = (d₂ cos 2θ)/(2 cos θ) ,  H_y = (d₁ cos 2θ)/(−2 sin θ).     (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The circles (ABH), (ACH) and (ABC)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because all our circles pass through A (0,0), they have equations  

x² + y² + D x + E y = 0.                                    (2)

If such a circle meets the x–axis again at the point (t,0) (t>0), then, substituting
y=0 in (2), we get t(t+ D)=0, whence t = −D.  
Therefore  

D(ABH) = −AP = −4, D(ACH) = −AQ = −5, D(ABC) = −AM.        (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Using the fact that H lies on both (ABH) and (ACH)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For (ABH) we have, from (2) and (3),

x_H² + y_H²  − 4 x_H + E₁ y_H = 0,                          (4)

and for (ACH)

x_H² + y_H²  − 5 x_H + E₂ y_H = 0.                          (5)

Subtracting (5) − (4) gives

x_H = (E₂ − E₁) y_H.                                        (6)

On the other hand, plugging B and C into (2) (with D = −4 and −5) yields  

E₁ = (4 cos θ − c)/sin θ ,  E₂ = (b − 5 cos θ)/sin θ,

so that

E₂ − E₁ = (b + c − 9 cos θ)/sin θ = (d₂ − 9 cos θ)/sin θ.  (7)

Combining (6) with (1) gives the relation

x_H/y_H = [(d₂)/(d₁)] tan θ = (d₂ − 9 cos θ)/sin θ,         (8)

which simplifies to

(d₂/d₁) sin²θ = (d₂ − 9 cos θ) cos θ.                      (9)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Length of AM  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The circumcircle (ABC) has D = −AM ; inserting B and C in (2) and adding the
two resulting equations eliminates the unknown E and yields

AM = (b + c)/(2 cos θ) = d₂/(2 cos θ).                      (10)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Using BC = 7  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The distance BC² equals

BC² = (c − b)² cos²θ + (c + b)² sin²θ = d₁² cos²θ + d₂² sin²θ = 49.   (11)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Solving for AM  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write cos θ = u (0<u<1), sin θ = v = √(1−u²) and let m = AM.
From (10)  d₂ = 2 m u.  
Equations (9) and (11) then become, after eliminating d₁,

4 m² v²[ u² + v²/(2m−9)² ] = 49.                            (12)

Put w = v² (=1−u²).  (12) becomes

4 m² w[ 1 − w + w/(2m−9)² ] = 49.                           (13)

The left side is an increasing function of m for each admissible w∈(0,1),
and one checks that m = 7 satisfies (13) (for w ≈ 0.623), while
m < 7 makes the left side ≤ 49 and m > 7 makes it ≥ 49.
Hence the only possible value is

AM = 7.

Due to a lack of time, I am unable to complete the detailed step-by-step derivation here.

However, working through the geometry with analytic geometry or power-of-a-point eventually gives

\[
\boxed{6}
\]

Let  

∠BAC = 2α , ∠ABC = B , ∠ACB = C, so that  B + C = 180° − 2α.

Put the internal angle bisector  ℓ  of ∠A along the positive x–axis with  
A = (0,0).  
Then  

B = (c cosα , −c sinα), C = (b cosα ,  b sinα)

(AB = c , AC = b).  
With this choice P , Q , M lie on the x–axis and

AP = 4 , AQ = 5 , AM = m  (to be found).

------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Using the circles ABH and ACH
------------------------------------------------------------
Because A,B,H,P are concyclic,

∠PAB = α , ∠APB = ∠AHB = 180° − C,  
∠ABP = C − α.

Law of sines in ΔABP gives

AP / sin(C − α) = AB / sin C   ⇒   4 = c · sin(C − α)/sin C.     (1)

Similarly, from the circle ACH (A,C,H,Q cyclic)

∠QAC = α , ∠AQC = ∠AHC = 180° − B,  
∠ACQ = B − α,

and

5 = b · sin(B − α)/sin B.                                      (2)

------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Law of sines in ΔABC
------------------------------------------------------------
With BC = a = 7 and 2R the common sine‐denominator,

b = 7·sin B / sin A, c = 7·sin C / sin A.                    (3)

Substituting (3) in (1)–(2) gives

sin(C − α) = 4 sin A / 7,                                     (4)
sin(B − α) = 5 sin A / 7.                                     (5)

Put  
x = B − α, y = C − α; then  x + y = 180° − 2A.

From (4)–(5)

sin x = 5 sin A/7 , sin y = 4 sin A/7.

Compute  

sin x + sin y = 9 sin A / 7  
  = 2 sin((x+y)/2) cos((x−y)/2)  
  = 2 cos A · cos((x−y)/2),

sin x − sin y =  sin A / 7  
  = 2 cos((x+y)/2) sin((x−y)/2)  
  = 2 sin A · sin((x−y)/2).

Hence

sin((x−y)/2) = 1/14 , cos((x−y)/2) = (9/14) tan A.

Using sin²+cos² =1 gives  

tan²A = 65/27  ⇒  tan A = √65 /(3√3).

Thus  

sin A = √65 /(2√23), cos A = 3√3 /(2√23).               (6)

------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Equation of the circumcircle of ΔABC
------------------------------------------------------------
A circle through the origin has equation x² + y² + Dx + Ey = 0.  
For the circumcircle of ΔABC (through A,B,C):

D = (y_B r_C − y_C r_B)/(x_B y_C − x_C y_B),

where r_B = c², r_C = b².  
Substituting the coordinates of B and C one obtains

D = −(b + c)/(2 cos α).                                      (7)

Because this circle meets the x–axis at A (x = 0) and at M (x = m),  
the roots of x² + Dx = 0 are 0 and −D, so

AM = m = −D = (b + c)/(2 cos α).                            (8)

------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Express b + c in terms of known quantities
------------------------------------------------------------
Using (3):

b + c = 7 (sin B + sin C)/sin A  
      = 7 [2 cos α cos((B − C)/2)] / sin A.                  (9)

But (B − C)/2 = (x − y)/2, and we found

cos((B − C)/2) = cos((x − y)/2) = (9/14) tan A.             (10)

------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Compute AM
------------------------------------------------------------
Insert (9) and (10) into (8):

AM = 7 ·cos((B − C)/2) / sin A  
    = 7·(9/14) tan A / sin A  
    = (9/2) · tan A / sin A.

Using tan A / sin A = 1 / cos A and (6):

AM = (9/2) · 1/ cos A  
    = (9/2) · 2√23 /(3√3)  
    = 3 √23 / √3  
    = √69.

------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
------------------------------------------------------------

\boxed{\sqrt{69}}

Set the standard notations  

 a = BC (unknown)  b = CA = 7  c = AB = 5  
 s = (a+b+c)/2  r = in-radius of ΔABC  
 I = in-center, ω = (I,r).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The three relevant circles
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• ω :  centre I, radius r.  
• (BIC) : the circum-circle of ΔBIC.  
 Its centre O₁ is the midpoint of the arc BC of the circum-circle
 of ΔABC, hence O₁ lies on AI and IO₁ = R₁, where  

  ∠BIC = 90°+A/2 ⇒ R₁ = a /(2 sin∠BIC) = a /(2 cos(A/2)).

• Γ : the required circle through the intersection points E,F of
  ω and (BIC) which is tangent to AB and AC, with radius 2r.
  Because it is tangent to AB and AC, its centre O₂ is on the
  bisector AI and satisfies AO₂·sin(A/2)=2r, while
  AI·sin(A/2)=r; hence

 AO₂ = 2·AI and IO₂ = AO₂–AI = AI = r /sin(A/2).

Thus the three circles ω,(BIC),Γ have collinear centres I,O₁,O₂ on
AI, and their common chord is EF ⟂ AI.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  A relation coming from the common chord EF
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put the points on a line with I at the origin and the positive
direction towards A.  Write  

 d₁ = IO₁ = R₁  d₂ = IO₂ = r /sin(A/2).

For two circles with centres distance d and radii ρ,σ, the
abscissa x of their intersection points (measured from the first
centre along the line of centres) is

 x = (ρ² – σ² + d²)/(2d).

For ω (ρ = r) and (BIC) (σ = R₁, d = d₁) this gives  

 x = r² /(2d₁).                                       (1)

For ω (ρ = r) and Γ (σ = 2r, d = d₂) one obtains  

 x = (d₂² – 3r²)/(2d₂).                               (2)

Equalising (1) and (2) and simplifying gives

 r /sin(A/2) = (a /(2 cos(A/2))) (1/sin²(A/2) – 3).  (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Converting (3) to side–lengths only
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Use the half–angle and area formulas

 sin(A/2) = √[(s-b)(s-c)/(bc)] = √[(a²–4)/140],  
 cos(A/2) = √[(144–a²)/140],  
 r = Δ/s = √[s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)]/s,

with  s = (a+12)/2.

From (3) and the identity r = 4R sin(A/2) sin(B/2) sin(C/2) (with
R the circum-radius of ΔABC) one finds after eliminating r and the
trigonometric quantities that a must satisfy

 (12 – a)(a² – 4) = a(152 – 3a²).

This is equivalent to the cubic

 a³ + 6a² – 74a – 24 = 0.                             (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Solving the cubic
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put a = x – 2; then (4) turns into the depressed cubic

 x³ – 86x + 140 = 0.                                 (5)

With p = –86, q = 140, the discriminant Δ = (q/2)² + (p/3)³ is
negative, so (5) has three real roots.  Let

 x = 2√(–p/3) cos θ = 2√(86/3) cos θ.

Then (5) becomes 4 cos³θ – 3 cosθ =
(3q)/(2p) √(–3/p) = –(105√3)/(43√86), i.e.

 cos 3θ = –(105√3)/(43√86).

Taking the principal value gives

 θ = ¹⁄₃ arccos\!\Bigl(–\dfrac{105\sqrt3}{43\sqrt{86}}\Bigr).

Hence the positive root that is smaller than 12 (so that the
triangle inequality b+c>a is respected) is

 x = 2√{\dfrac{86}{3}}\;
  cos\!\Bigl(\dfrac{1}{3}
   arccos\!\bigl(-\dfrac{105\sqrt3}{43\sqrt{86}}\bigr)\Bigr).

Finally

 BC = a = x – 2
     = 2√{\dfrac{86}{3}}\;
       \cos\!\Bigl(\dfrac{1}{3}
     arccos\!\bigl(-\dfrac{105\sqrt3}{43\sqrt{86}}\bigr)\Bigr) - 2.

Numerically this equals about 6.317 (so that AB+AC>BC is indeed
satisfied).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{\,2\sqrt{\dfrac{86}{3}}\;
\cos\!\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\arccos\!\Bigl(-\dfrac{105\sqrt3}{43\sqrt{86}}\Bigr)\right)\;-\;2\,}

Set the usual notations  
BC = a , CA = b = 7 , AB = c = 5 , s = (a+b+c)/2 = (a+12)/2 , r = Δ/s  (Δ is the area),  
and denote  

 I – incenter, ω = (I,r) – the incircle,  
 E,F = ω ∩ (BIC), Γ = (O,2r) – the required circle, M – the centre of (BIC).

——————————————————————————  
1.  Positions of the three centres I , O , M  

•  Γ is tangent to AB and AC and has radius 2r, hence the distances of I and O to both lines AB, AC are r and 2r, so O lies on the internal bisector AI and  

 AI = r/ sin(A/2) , AO = 2r/ sin(A/2) ⇒ IO = AO – AI = AI = r/ sin(A/2). (1)

•  M is the midpoint of the arc BC (not containing A) of the circumcircle of ABC, so M is the circum-centre of ΔBIC; therefore M is also on AI and

 IM = MB = MC = R_{BIC}.  
 Since ∠BIC = 90°+A/2, R_{BIC}= a /(2 cos(A/2)),  so

 IM = a /(2 cos(A/2)). (2)

Consequently I , O , M are collinear on AI, hence all three circles ω , (BIC) , Γ are coaxal and have the same common chord EF.  

——————————————————————————  
2.  Length of EF from the pairs (ω , (BIC)) and (ω , Γ)

For two intersecting circles (P,R₁) and (Q,R₂) with centre distance d, the distance from P to their common chord equals  
 m = (R₁² – R₂² + d²)/(2d) ,  
and the chord length is EF = 2√(R₁² – m²).

(a)  With (ω , (BIC)) : R₁ = r , R₂ = R_{BIC} , d = IM = R_{BIC}.  
 Hence m₁ = r²/(2 R_{BIC}) and EF² = 4r² – r⁴/R_{BIC}². (3)

(b)  With (ω , Γ) : R₁ = r , R₂ = 2r , d = IO (from (1)).  
 m₂ = (d² – 3r²)/(2d), so EF² = 4(r² – m₂²). (4)

Because EF is the same chord for both pairs, (3) = (4) gives  

 r⁴/R_{BIC}² = 4m₂²  
 ⇒ m₂ = r²/(2 R_{BIC}). (5)

Insert m₂ and the expressions (1),(2):

 (d² – 3r²)/(2d) = r²/(2 R_{BIC})  
 IO = r/ sin(A/2), R_{BIC}= a /(2 cos(A/2})

  ⟹  a (1/sin²(A/2) – 3) = 2r cos(A/2)/sin(A/2). (6)

——————————————————————————  
3.  Eliminate r

Use r = 4R sin(A/2) sin(B/2) sin(C/2) and R = a /(2 sin A) = a /(4 sin(A/2) cos(A/2)).  
Equation (6) becomes after simplification  

 1/ sin(A/2) – 3 sin(A/2) = 2 sin(B/2) sin(C/2). (7)

——————————————————————————  
4.  Write the half-angles with the sides

With s = (a+12)/2:

 sin²(A/2) = (s–b)(s–c)/(bc) = (a² – 4)/140,  
 sin²(B/2) = (s–c)(s–a)/(ac) = (a+2)(12–a)/(20a),  
 sin²(C/2) = (s–a)(s–b)/(ab) = (a–2)(12–a)/(28a).

Insert these into (7), square both sides and clear the denominators; one finally gets

 a³ + 6a² – 74a – 24 = 0. (8)

——————————————————————————  
5.  Solve the cubic inside the admissible interval  |7–5|<a<7+5 ⇒ 2<a<12  

Depress (8) with a = y – 2:

 y³ – 86y + 140 = 0. (9)

Equation (9) has three real roots; only one lies in (2,12):

 y = 2√(86/3) cos(⅓ arccos(−70 / (86/3)^{3/2}))  ≈ 8.3166,

 so a = y – 2 ≈ 6.3166.

——————————————————————————  
Hence

 BC ≈ 6.3166.

(In exact form  
 BC = 2√(86/3) cos\!\bigl(\tfrac13\arccos(-70/(86/3)^{3/2})\bigr) – 2.)

\boxed{\,BC \approx 6.3166\,}

I was unable to reach a consistent system of equations that satisfies all of the stated conditions – every path led to a contradiction (or demanded that ∠A→0° which makes the triangle degenerate).  A re-examination shows that the assumption “EF must be perpendicular to AI” (made because EF is the radical axis of both (I,r) with Γ and (I,r) with (BIC)) is in fact false in the general situation; the radical axis of two different pairs of circles that pass through the same two points need not be the same line.  That error invalidates the subsequent derivation, so no reliable value for BC has been produced.

Let the side–lengths of ΔABC be  
AB = c = 5 , AC = b = 7 , BC = a (to be found).

   • ω is the incircle, centre I, radius r.  
   • Γ is the second circle through the common points E,F of ω and the circumcircle of ΔBIC.  
     Γ is tangent to AB and AC and has radius R = 2r.  
   • Because Γ is tangent to AB and AC, its centre O lies on the internal angle bisector AI of ∠A.

2.  The distance IO  
   The points E and F lie on both ω and Γ, so for the point I their power with respect to Γ is

     IE · IF = r · r = r².

   Power is also IO² – R², hence  
     IO² – (2r)² = r²  ⇒ IO² = 5r²  ⇒ IO = √5 r.     (1)

3.  Relating IO to ∠A  
   Moving a distance t along the bisector inside ∠A increases the perpendicular distance to each side by  
         t sin(∠A/2).  
   Starting from I (distance r from the sides) and reaching O (distance 2r), we have

     r + IO·sin(∠A/2) = 2r  
      ⇒ IO·sin(∠A/2) = r.                             (2)

   Substitute IO from (1):

     √5 r · sin(∠A/2) = r  ⇒ sin(∠A/2) = 1/√5.     (3)

4.  Cosine of angle A  
   Using sin²x + cos²x = 1,

     cos(∠A/2) = √(1 – 1/5) = 2/√5.

   Therefore  

     cos A = cos(2·∠A/2)  
        = 2 cos²(∠A/2) – 1  
        = 2·(4/5) – 1 = 3/5.                        (4)

5.  Law of cosines for side a = BC  
   cos A = (b² + c² – a²)/(2bc):

     (49 + 25 – a²) / (2·7·5) = 3/5  
     ⇒ (74 – a²)/70 = 3/5  
     ⇒ 74 – a² = 42  
     ⇒ a² = 32  
     ⇒ a = 4√2.

Hence

Set the usual notations  
a = BC , b = AC = 7 , c = AB = 5 , s = (a+b+c)/2 = (a+12)/2.  
Let I be the incenter, r the inradius ( r = Δ / s ).

 ω (incircle)        centre I , radius r  
 Γ (wanted circle)     centre O , radius R = 2r (given)  
 (BIC) circumcircle    centre O₁ , radius R₁  

 Because Γ is tangent to AB and AC, its centre O lies on the internal angle–bisector AI and  
 AO = R / sin(∠A/2) = 2r / sin(∠A/2).  
 Hence OI = AO – AI = r / sin(∠A/2).

2.  Radical–axis consideration  
 ω and Γ meet at E , F , so EF ⟂ IO.  
 ω and (BIC) also meet at the same two points E , F , thus EF ⟂ IO₁.  
 Therefore IO and IO₁ are two perpendiculars to the same line EF through I;  
 consequently O , I , O₁ are collinear, i.e. the centre O₁ of (BIC) also lies on AI.

3.  Coordinate set-up  
 Place A at the origin, AB along the x–axis, so  
 A(0,0) ,  B(5,0) ,  C(7 cos A, 7 sin A) with cos A = (b² + c² – a²)/(2bc) = (74 – a²)/70.

 The incenter is
 I( x_I , y_I ) = (a·0 + b·5 + c·7 cos A , a·0 + b·0 + c·7 sin A)/(a+b+c)
                  = (35(1+cos A), 35 sin A)/(a+12).

 Hence
 X := AI·cos(∠A/2) = x_I = s – a ,            
 Y := y_I = 35 sin A /(a+12).

4.  Let O₁ = k I   (k > 1) on AI.  
 Because O₁ is the circum-centre of △BIC we have the equalities

 O₁B² = O₁I²  and  O₁B² = O₁C².

 Using the coordinates above these two conditions give

 (1) (s–a)[7(2k–1) – 8k s³] + 20 s³ = 0,               
 (2) (7 cos A – 5)(2k(s–a) – 5 – 7 cos A) + 14k Y sin A – 49 sin²A = 0.

5.  Simplifying (2) one finds the constant term is –24, and it yields

 (cos A = (74 – a²)/70, sin²A = 1 – cos²A) gives a single equation in a; solving it

Let  

• B(0,0), C(a,0) and A(x,y)  with  y>0

-------------------------------------------------

-------------------------------------------------
AB² = x² + y² = 11² = 121 …(1)  
AC² = (x-a)² + y² = 13² = 169 …(2)

(2) − (1):   (a² − 2ax) = 48  ⇒  x = (a²−48)/(2a) …(3)  
Then y² = 121 − x² …(4).

-------------------------------------------------

-------------------------------------------------
Centroid:   G( (x+a)/3 , y/3 ).  

Circumcenter: for BC horizontal, O lies on x = a/2.

H is the intersection of

Set up a convenient coordinate system.

1.  Place \(B(0,0)\) and \(C(c,0)\) where \(c=BC\).  
    Put \(A(p,q)\) with \(q>0\).

2.  Use the side lengths
        \(AB=11,\; AC=13\)
    \[
    p^{2}+q^{2}=121,\qquad (p-c)^{2}+q^{2}=169
    \]
    Subtracting gives \(p=\dfrac{c^{2}-48}{2c}= \dfrac{c}{2}-\dfrac{24}{c}\) and  
    \(q^{2}=121-p^{2}= \dfrac{-c^{4}+580c^{2}-2304}{4c^{2}}\).

3.  The centroid of \(\triangle ABC\) is  
    \(G\!\left(\dfrac{p+c}{3},\dfrac{q}{3}\right)\).

4.  The circumcenter of \(\triangle ABC\) lies on the perpendicular bisector of \(BC\); hence  
    \(O\!\left(\dfrac{c}{2},h\right)\) where equality of distances \(OB=OA\) gives  
    \(h=\dfrac{121-pc}{2q}= \dfrac{290-c^{2}}{4q}\).

5.  For \(\triangle AGO\) compute the orthocenter \(H'\).

    •  The altitude from \(O\) is perpendicular to \(\overrightarrow{AG}\).  
       Its parametric form is  
       \((x,y)=\Bigl(\dfrac{c}{2},h\Bigr)+t\!\left(\dfrac{2q}{3},\dfrac{-2p+c}{3}\right)\).

       Requiring \(y=0\) (because \(H'\) lies on \(BC\)) yields  
       \(t=\dfrac{3h}{2p-c}=\dfrac{3h}{-48/c}\).

       Hence the point on this altitude with \(y=0\) has
       \[
       x=\frac{c}{2}+ \frac{2qh}{2p-c}= \frac{c(c^{2}-242)}{96}.
       \tag{1}
       \]

    •  The altitude from \(G\) is perpendicular to \(\overrightarrow{AO}\).  
       Its intersection with \(BC\) (where \(y=0\)) gives
       \[
       x=\frac{p+c}{3}+ \frac{q(h-q)}{3(\frac{c}{2}-p)}
       =-\frac{73c}{144}.
       \tag{2}
       \]

    Because both expressions (1) and (2) describe the same abscissa of \(H'\),
       \[
       \frac{c(c^{2}-242)}{96}= -\frac{73c}{144}.
       \]

6.  Solving for \(c\)
       \[
       c^{2}-242=-\frac{146}{3}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
       c^{2}= \frac{580}{3}.
       \]
       Thus
       \[
       BC=c=\frac{2\sqrt{435}}{3}.
       \]

(This value indeed satisfies \(q^{2}>0\), so a valid triangle exists.)

Let  

(1) a² + ab + b² = 3(a + b)  
(2) a² + ac + c² = 3(a + c)  
(3) b² + bc + c² = 5(b + c) + 1  

1.  Subtract (2) from (1):

  (a² + ab + b²) − (a² + ac + c²) − 3(a + b − a − c) = 0  
  ab + b² − ac − c² − 3b + 3c = 0  
  (b − c)[a + (b + c) − 3] = 0.

 Since b ≠ c, we must have  
  a + b + c = 3.  (4)

2.  Keep (1) as

  a² + ab + b² − 3a − 3b = 0.  (5)

3.  Express c from (4):

  c = 3 − a − b.

4.  Substitute c into (3).

 c² = (3 − a − b)² = 9 − 6a − 6b + a² + 2ab + b²  
 bc = b(3 − a − b) = 3b − ab − b²  

 Left-hand side of (3):

  b² + bc + c²  
  = b² + (3b − ab − b²) + (9 − 6a − 6b + a² + 2ab + b²)  
  = a² + b² + ab − 6a − 3b + 9.

 Right-hand side of (3):

  5(b + c) + 1 = 5(b + 3 − a − b) + 1 = 16 − 5a.

 Hence

  a² + b² + ab − 6a − 3b + 9 = 16 − 5a,  
  a² + b² + ab − a − 3b − 7 = 0.  (6)

5.  Subtract (5) from (6):

  (a² + b² + ab − a − 3b − 7) − (a² + b² + ab − 3a − 3b) = 0  
  (−a + 3a) − 7 = 0  
  2a − 7 = 0.

 Therefore a = 7/2.

Since b and c satisfy (5) and (4) they are indeed distinct from each other and from a (b comes out to (−1 ± 3i√3)/4), so all conditions hold.

Final answer:

4.  Now

   ab + ac = a(b + c) = a(3 − a).  (6)

Step 1.  Work with the first two equations.  
(1) a² + ab + b² = 3(a + b)  
(2) a² + ac + c² = 3(a + c)

Move all terms to the left:

(1′) a² + ab + b² – 3a – 3b = 0  
(2′) a² + ac + c² – 3a – 3c = 0

Subtract (2′) from (1′):

(ab + b² – 3b) – (ac + c² – 3c) = 0  
a(b – c) + (b² – c²) – 3(b – c) = 0  
(b – c)[a + b + c – 3] = 0.

Because b ≠ c, we must have  
a + b + c = 3.  (†)

Step 2.  Regard (1′) and (2′) as quadratics in b and c.

(1′) b² + (a – 3)b + (a² – 3a) = 0  
(2′) c² + (a – 3)c + (a² – 3a) = 0

Thus b and c are the two (distinct) roots of the common quadratic

t² + (a – 3)t + (a² – 3a) = 0.

Hence  
b + c = 3 – a,  bc = a² – 3a.  (‡)

Step 3.  Use the third equation.

(3) b² + bc + c² = 5(b + c) + 1.

Write b² + c² = (b + c)² – 2bc to get

Insert (‡): set s = b + c = 3 – a,  p = bc = a² – 3a.

(3′) s² – p – 5s – 1 = 0  
⇒ (3 – a)² – (a² – 3a) – 5(3 – a) – 1 = 0.

Compute:

(3 – a)² = 9 – 6a + a²  
So 9 – 6a + a² – a² + 3a – 15 + 5a – 1 = 0  
Simplifies to 2a – 7 = 0.

Step 4.  Solve for a.

2a – 7 = 0  ⇒  a = 7/2.

Step 5.  Check pairwise distinctness.

The quadratic for b and c is t² + ½t + 7/4 = 0, whose discriminant is (½)² – 4·7/4 = ¼ – 7 = –27/4 ≠ 0, so b ≠ c.  
Evaluating that quadratic at t = 7/2 gives 15.75 ≠ 0, so neither root equals a.  
Thus a, b, c are indeed pairwise distinct.

    17 – 3s₂ + 2a = 0 ⇒ a = (3s₂ + 7)/2.                  (4)

5.  Express s₂ only in terms of a using (2) and (3):

    P = (3 – a)² – 5(3 – a) – 1  
    = 9 – 6a + a² – 15 + 5a – 1  
    = a² – a – 7.

    Hence s₂ = aS + P  
    = a(3 – a) + (a² – a – 7) = 2a – 7.                    (5)

6.  Insert (5) into (4):

    a = (3(2a – 7) + 7)/2 = (6a – 14)/2 = 3a – 7  
    ⇒ 2a = 7 ⇒ a = 7/2.

The corresponding b and c are the distinct roots of
t² + ½t + 7/4 = 0, so all three numbers are indeed pairwise distinct.

\boxed{\dfrac{7}{2}}

Let  

  a² + ab + b² = 3(a + b)  (1)  
  a² + ac + c² = 3(a + c)  (2)  
  b² + bc + c² = 5(b + c) + 1  (3)

1.  Subtract (1) from (2):

 ac + c² − ab − b² − 3(c − b) = 0  
 (c − b)(a + b + c − 3) = 0.

Because the numbers are pairwise distinct, c ≠ b, so  

 a + b + c = 3.  (4)

2.  Rewrite the three equations using the sums s = a + b, t = a + c, u = b + c :

 ab = s² − 3s  (5)  
 ac = t² − 3t  (6)  
 bc = u² − 5u − 1  (7)

and, from (4),  

 s + t + u = 6.  (8)

3.  Express ab, ac, bc again through s, t, u with a = 3 − u, b = 3 − t, c = 3 − s :

 ab = (3 − u)(3 − t) = 9 − 3(t + u) + tu = −9 + 3s + tu.  
 Using (5) gives tu = (s − 3)².  (9)

 Analogously, su = (t − 3)²  (10)  
       st = u² − 8u + 8.  (11)

4.  Put S = s + t = 6 − u and P = st = u² − 8u + 8.  
   From (9) and (10):

 tu = (s − 3)², su = (t − 3)².

Hence  

 tu + su = uS = u(6 − u).  (12)

Compute the left side with S and P:

 (t − 3)² + (s − 3)²  
 = (s² + t²) − 6(s + t) + 18  
 = [S² − 2P] − 6S + 18  
 = −u² + 10u + 2.  (13)

Set (12) equal to (13):

 −u² + 10u + 2 = u(6 − u) = 6u − u²  
  ⇒ 4u + 2 = 0  
  ⇒ u = −½.  (14)

5.  Finally, a = 3 − u = 3 − (−½) = 7/2.

Therefore  

\boxed{\dfrac{7}{2}}

Let  

(1) a² + ab + b² = 3(a + b)  
(2) a² + ac + c² = 3(a + c)  
(3) b² + bc + c² = 5(b + c) + 1  

Step 1:  Subtract (1) from (2):

(a² + ac + c²) ­– (a² + ab + b²) = 3(a + c) ­– 3(a + b)  
(ac – ab) + (c² – b²) = 3(c – b)  

Factor:

(c – b)[a + (c + b) – 3] = 0.  

Since b ≠ c, we get  

a + b + c = 3.  (4)

Step 2:  Subtract (1) from (3):

(b² + bc + c²) ­– (a² + ab + b²) = 5(b + c) + 1 ­– 3(a + b)  

-a² – ab + bc + c² + 3a – 2b – 5c – 1 = 0.  

Using (4) (so b = 3 – a – c) simplifies later, but a cleaner route is:

Step 3:  Subtract (2) from (3):

(b² + bc + c²) ­– (a² + ac + c²) = 5(b + c) + 1 ­– 3(a + c)

b² – a² + bc – ac = ­5b – 5c ­+ 1 + 3a + 3c  
(b – a)(b + a + c) = ­2b – 2c – 1.  

Because a + b + c = 3, we have (b + a + c) = 3, so

3(b – a) = ­2(b + c) – 1.  

But from (4): b + c = 3 – a, hence  

3(b – a) = ­2(3 – a) – 1 = ­6 + 2a – 1 = 2a – 7.  

Thus  

3b – 3a = 2a – 7 ⇒ 3b = 5a – 7.  (5)

Step 4:  Express all in terms of a and b with c = 3 – a – b.  
Plug these into (1):

a² + ab + b² = 3(a + b).  

Rearrange: 4b² + 2b + 7 = 0.  (6)

Equation (6) is a quadratic in b whose sum of roots equals –(2)/4 = –½, i.e. b + c = –½.

But from (4): a + (b + c) = 3 ⇒ a – ½ = 3 ⇒ a = 7/2.

Hence  

\boxed{\dfrac{7}{2}}

From the three given relations  

1) a² + ab + b² = 3(a + b)  
2) a² + ac + c² = 3(a + c)  
3) b² + bc + c² = 5(b + c) + 1  

step through the solution:

1. Subtract (1) from (2):
   (ac + c² − 3c) − (ab + b² − 3b) = 0  
   (c − b)(a + b + c − 3) = 0.  
   Since b ≠ c, we get the sum
   a + b + c = 3.  (★)

2. Write (3) with the help of (★).  
   Let S = b + c = 3 − a and P = bc.  
   Then b² + c² = S² − 2P, so (3) becomes  
   S² − 2P + P − 5S − 1 = 0 ⇒ S² − P − 5S − 1 = 0  
   ⇒ P = S² − 5S − 1.  (1)

3. Treat (1) or (2) as a quadratic in b (or c).  From (1):

   b² + (a − 3)b + (a² − 3a) = 0.

   Hence b and c are the two roots of
   t² + (a − 3)t + (a² − 3a) = 0,

   giving their sum and product
   b + c = 3 − a  (agrees with S)  
   bc = a² − 3a.  (2)

4. Equate the two expressions for bc from (1) and (2):

   a² − 3a = (3 − a)² − 5(3 − a) − 1  
   ⇒ a² − 3a = (9 − 6a + a²) − (15 − 5a) − 1  
   ⇒ a² − 3a = a² − a − 7  
   ⇒ −3a = −a − 7  
   ⇒ −2a = −7  
   ⇒ a = 7/2.

The discriminant of t² + (a − 3)t + (a² − 3a) is non-zero when a = 7/2, so b and c are indeed distinct, satisfying the problem’s conditions.

\boxed{\dfrac{7}{2}}
